n180011: sn See N. C. Habel, вАЬThe Narrative Art of Job,вАЭ JSOT 27 (1983): 101-11; J. J. Owens, вАЬPrologue and Epilogue,вАЭ RevExp 68 (1971): 457-67; and R. Polzin, вАЬThe Framework of the Book of Job,вАЭ Int 31 (1974): 182-200.
n180012: sn The book of Job is one of the major books of wisdom literature in the Bible. But it is a different kind of wisdom. Whereas the book of Proverbs is a collection of the short wisdom sayings, Job is a thorough analysis of the relationship between suffering and divine justice put in a dramatic poetic form. There are a number of treatises on this subject in the ancient Near East, but none of them are as thorough and masterful as Job. See J. Gray, вАЬThe Book of Job in the Context of Near Eastern Literature,вАЭ ZAW 82 (1970): 251-69; S. N. Kramer, вАЬMan and His God, A Sumerian Variation on the вАШJobвАЩ Motif,вАЭ VTSup 3 (1953): 170-82. While the book has fascinated readers for ages, it is a difficult book, difficult to translate and difficult to study. Most of it is written in poetic parallelism. But it is often very cryptic, it is written with unusual grammatical constructions, and it makes use of a large number of very rare words. All this has led some scholars to question if it was originally written in Hebrew or some other related Semitic dialect or language first. There is no indication of who the author was. It is even possible that the work may have been refined over the years, but there is no evidence for this either. The book uses a variety of genres (laments, hymns, proverbs, and oracles) in the various speeches of the participants. This all adds to the richness of the material. And while it is a poetic drama using cycles of speeches, there is no reason to doubt that the events represented here do not go back to a real situation and preserve the various arguments. Several indications in the book would place JobвАЩs dates in the time of the patriarchs. But the composition of the book, or at least its final form, may very well come from the first millennium, maybe in the time of the flowering of wisdom literature with Solomon. We have no way of knowing when the book was written, or when its revision was completed. But dating it late in the intertestamental period is ruled out by the appearance of translations and copies of it, notably bits of a Targum of Job in the Dead Sea Scrolls. Among the general works and commentaries, see A. Hurvitz, вАЬThe Date of the Prose Tale of Job Linguistically Reconsidered,вАЭ HTR 67 (1974): 17-34; R. H. Pfeiffer, вАЬThe Priority of Job over Isaiah 40-55, вАЭ JBL 46 (1927): 202ff. The book presents many valuable ideas on the subject of the suffering of the righteous. Ultimately it teaches that one must submit to the wisdom of the Creator. But it also indicates that the shallow answers of JobвАЩs friends do not do justice to the issue. Their arguments that suffering is due to sin are true to a point, but they did not apply to Job. His protests sound angry and belligerent, but he held tenaciously to his integrity. His experience shows that it is possible to live a pure life and yet still suffer. He finally turns his plea to God, demanding a hearing. This he receives, of course, only to hear that God is sovereignly ruling the universe. Job can only submit to him. In the end God does not abandon his sufferer. For additional material, see G. L. Archer, The Book of Job; H. H. Rowley, вАЬThe Book of Job and Its Meaning,вАЭ BJRL 41 (1958/59): 167-207; J. A. Baker, The Book of Job; C. L. Feinberg, вАЬThe Book of Job,вАЭ BSac 91 (1934): 78-86; R. Polzin and D. Robertson, вАЬStudies in the Book of Job,вАЭ Semeia 7 (Missoula: Scholars Press, 1977).
n180013: tn The Hebrew construction is literally вАЬa man was,вАЭ using вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б „Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО ( Њish hayah) rather than a preterite first. This simply begins the narrative.
n180014: sn The term Uz occurs several times in the Bible: a son of Aram (Gen 10:23), a son of Nahor (Gen 22:21), and a descendant of Seir (Gen 36:28). If these are the clues to follow, the location would be north of Syria or south near Edom. The book tells how JobвАЩs flocks were exposed to Chaldeans, the tribes between Syria and the Euphrates (1:17), and in another direction to attacks from the Sabeans (1:15). The most prominent man among his friends was from Teman, which was in Edom (2:11). Uz is also connected with Edom in Lamentations 4:21. The most plausible location, then, would be east of Israel and northeast of Edom, in what is now North Arabia. The LXX has вАЬon the borders of Edom and Arabia.вАЭ An early Christian tradition placed his home in an area about 40 miles south of Damascus, in Baashan at the southeast foot of Hermon.
n180015: tn In Hebrew the defining relative clause (вАЬwhose name was JobвАЭ) is actually an asyndetic verbless noun-clause placed in apposition to the substantive (вАЬa manвАЭ); see GKC 486 ¬І155.e.
n180016: sn The name вАЬJobвАЭ is mentioned by Ezekiel as one of the greats in the pastвАФNoah, Job, and Daniel (14:14). The suffering of Job was probably well known in the ancient world, and this name was clearly part of that tradition. There is little reason to try to determine the etymology and meaning of the name, since it may not be Hebrew. If it were Hebrew, it might mean something like вАЬpersecuted,вАЭ although some suggest вАЬaggressor.вАЭ If Arabic it might have the significance of вАЬthe one who always returns to God.вАЭ
n180017: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (tam) has been translated вАЬblamelessвАЭ (so NIV, NLT, NASB). The verbal root вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (tamam) means вАЬto be blameless, complete.вАЭ The word is found in Gen 25:27 where it describes Jacob as вАЬeven-tempered.вАЭ It also occurs in Ps 64:5 (64:4 ET) and Prov 29:10. The meaning is that a person or a thing is complete, perfect, flawless. It does not mean that he was sinless, but that he was wholeheartedly trying to please God, that he had integrity and was blameless before God.
n180018: tn The word вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО (yashar, вАЬuprightвАЭ) is complementary to вАЬblameless.вАЭ The idea is вАЬupright, just,вАЭ and applies to his relationships with others (see Pss 25:21; 37:37).
n180019: sn These two expressions indicate the outcome of JobвАЩs character. вАЬFearing GodвАЭ and вАЬturning from evilвАЭ also express two correlative ideas in scripture; they signify his true pietyвАФhe had reverential fear of the Lord, meaning he was a truly devoted worshiper who shunned evil.
n1800110: sn The numbers used in the chapter, seven, three, and five, carry the symbolism in the Bible of perfection and completeness (see J. J. Davis, Biblical Numerology). JobвАЩs вАЬseven sonsвАЭ are listed first because in the East sons were considered more valuable than daughters (recall Ruth, who is вАЬbetter than seven sonsвАЭ [Ruth 4:15]).
n1800111: tn The verb begins the sentence: вАЬand there were born.вАЭ This use of the preterite with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive, especially after the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah, вАЬto beвАЭ), is explanatory: there was a manвА¶and there was born to himвА¶вАЭ (IBHS 551-52 ¬І33.2.2b).
n1800112: tn The word means вАЬcattle, livestock, possessionsвАЭ (see also Gen 26:14). Here it includes the livestock, but also the entire substance of his household.
n1800113: tn Or вАЬamounted to,вАЭ вАЬtotaled.вАЭ The preterite of вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah, вАЬto beвАЭ) is sometimes employed to introduce a total amount or an inventory (see Exod 1:5; Num 3:43).
n1800114: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷≤„С÷ї„У÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО ( њavuddah, вАЬservice of household servantsвАЭ) indicates that he had a very large body of servants, meaning a very large household.
n1800116: tn The expression is literally вАЬsons of the east.вАЭ The use of the genitive after вАЬsonsвАЭ in this construction may emphasize their nature (like вАЬsons of belialвАЭ); it would refer to them as easterners (like вАЬsons of the southвАЭ in contemporary American English). BDB 869 s.v. вАП„І÷ґ„У÷ґ„ЭвАО says вАЬdwellers in the east.вАЭ
n1800117: tn The perfect verb with the вАП„ХвАО (vav), вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷Є„Ь÷∞„Ы„Х÷ЉвАО (vehalekhu, вАЬthey wentвАЭ) indicates their characteristic action, actions that were frequently repeated (GKC 335-36 ¬І112.dd).
n1800119: tn The sense is cryptic; it literally says вАЬhouseвАФa manвАФhis day.вАЭ The word вАЬhouseвАЭ is an adverbial accusative of place: вАЬin the house.вАЭ вАЬManвАЭ is the genitive; it also has a distributive sense: вАЬin the house of each man.вАЭ And вАЬhis dayвАЭ is an adverbial accusative: вАЬon his day.вАЭ The point is that they feasted every day of the week in rotation.
n1800120: tn The use of вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (qara Њ, вАЬto call, inviteвАЭ) followed by the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) usually has the force of вАЬto summon.вАЭ Here the meaning would not be so commanding, but would refer to an invitation (see also 1 Kgs 1:19, 25, 26).
n1800121: tn Normally cardinal numerals tend to disagree in gender with the numbered noun. In v. 2 вАЬthree daughtersвАЭ consists of the masculine numeral followed by the feminine noun. However, here вАЬthree sistersвАЭ consists of the feminine numeral followed by the feminine noun. The distinction appears to be that the normal disagreement between numeral and noun when the intent is merely to fix the number (3 daughters as opposed to 2 or 4 daughters). However, when a particular, previously known group is indicated, the numeral tends to agree with the noun in gender. A similar case occurs in Gen 3:13 (вАЬthree wivesвАЭ of NoahвАЩs sons).
n1800122: tn The verse begins with the temporal indicator вАЬand it happenedвАЭ or вАЬand it came to pass,вАЭ which need not be translated. The particle вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki, вАЬwhenвАЭ) with the initial verbal form indicates it is a temporal clause.
n1800123: tn The verb is the Hiphil perfect of вАП„†÷Є„І÷Ј„£вАО (naqaf, вАЬgo aroundвАЭ), here it means вАЬto make the roundвАЭ or вАЬcomplete the circuitвАЭ (BDB 668-69 s.v. II вАП„†÷Є„І÷Ј„£вАО Hiph). It indicates that when the feasting had made its circuit of the seven sons, then Job would sanctify them.
n1800124: tn The form is a preterite with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. The same emphasis on repeated or frequent action continues here in this verse. The idea here is that Job would send for them, because the sanctification of them would have consisted of washings and changes of garments as well as the sacrifices (see Gen 35:2; 1 Sam 16:5).
n1800126:
tn The first verb could also be joined with the next to form a verbal hendiadys: вАЬhe would rise early and he would sacrificeвАЭ would then simply be вАЬhe would sacrifice early in the morningвАЭ (see M. Delcor, вАЬQuelques cas de survivances du vocabulaire nomade en h√©breu biblique,вАЭ VT 25 [1975]: 307-22). This section serves to explain in more detail how Job sanctified his children.
sn In the patriarchal society it was normal for the father to act as priest for the family, making the sacrifices as needed. Job here is exceptional in his devotion to the duty. The passage shows the balance between the greatest earthly rejoicing by the family, and the deepest piety and affection of the father.
n1800127: tn The text does not have вАЬaccording toвАЭ; the noun вАЬnumberвАЭ is an accusative that defines the extent of his actions (GKC 373-74 ¬І118.e, h).
n1800128: tn The clause stands as an accusative to the verb, here as the direct object introduced with вАЬperhapsвАЭ (IBHS 645-46 ¬І38.8d).
n1800129: tn Heb вАЬsons,вАЭ but since the three daughters are specifically mentioned in v. 4, вАЬchildrenвАЭ has been used in the translation. In this patriarchal culture, however, it is possible that only the sons are in view.
n1800130: tn The Hebrew verb is вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (barakh), which means вАЬto bless.вАЭ Here is a case where the writer or a scribe has substituted the word вАЬcurseвАЭ with the word вАЬblessвАЭ to avoid having the expression вАЬcurse God.вАЭ For similar euphemisms in the ancient world, see K. A. Kitchen, Ancient Orient and Old Testament, 166. It is therefore difficult to know exactly what Job feared they might have done. The opposite of вАЬblessвАЭ would be вАЬcurse,вАЭ which normally would convey disowning or removing from blessing. Some commentators try to offer a definition of вАЬcurseвАЭ from the root in the text, and noting that вАЬcurseвАЭ is too strong, come to something like вАЬrenounce.вАЭ The idea of blaspheming is probably not meant; rather, in their festivities they may have said things that renounced God or their interest in him. Job feared this momentary turning away from God in their festivities, perhaps as they thought their good life was more important than their religion.
n1800131: tn The imperfect expresses continual action in past time, i.e., a customary imperfect (GKC 315 ¬І107.e).
n1800132: sn The text draws the curtain of heaven aside for the reader to understand the background of this drama. God extols the virtue of Job, but Satan challenges the reasons for it. He receives permission to try to dislodge Job from his integrity. In short, God is using Job to prove SatanвАЩs theory wrong.
n1800133: tn The beginning Hebrew expression вАЬand there wasвАФthe dayвАЭ indicates that вАЬthere came a day whenвАЭ or more simply вАЬthe day came when.вАЭ It emphasizes the particular day. The succeeding clause is then introduced with a preterite with the with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive (see E. Dhorme, Job, 5).
n1800134: sn The вАЬsons of GodвАЭ in the OT is generally taken to refer to angels. They are not actually вАЬsonsвАЭ of Elohim; the idiom is a poetic way of describing their nature and relationship to God. The phrase indicates their supernatural nature, and their submission to God as the sovereign Lord. It may be classified as a genitive that expresses how individuals belong to a certain class or type, i.e., the supernatural (GKC 418 ¬І128.v). In the pagan literature, especially of Ugarit, вАЬthe sons of GodвАЭ refers to the lesser gods or deities of the pantheon. See H. W. Robinson, вАЬThe Council of Yahweh,вАЭ JTS 45 (1943): 151-57; G. Cooke, вАЬThe Sons of (the) God(s),вАЭ ZAW 76 (1964): 22-47; M. Tsevat, вАЬGod and the Gods in the Assembly,вАЭ HUCA 40-41 (1969/70): 123-37.
n1800135: tn The preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal) in this construction after a verb of standing or going means вАЬbeforeвАЭ (GKC 383 ¬І119.cc).
n1800136: sn The word means вАЬadversaryвАЭ or with the article вАЬthe adversaryвАЭвАФhere the superhuman adversary or Satan. The word with the article means that the meaning of the word should receive prominence. A denominative verb meaning вАЬto act as adversaryвАЭ occurs. Satan is the great accuser of the saints (see Zech 3 where вАЬSatan was standing there to вАШsatanizeвАЩ Joshua the priestвАЭ; and see Rev 12 which identifies him with the Serpent in Genesis). He came among the angels at this time because he is one of them and has access among them. Even though fallen, Satan has yet to be cast down completely (see Rev 12).
n1800137: sn Throughout the book of Job exchanges between speakers are stated as вАЬ[someone] answered and said.вАЭ However, when the Lord speaks, the formula is usually just вАЬhe said.вАЭ The rhetorical function in Job is likely to show that God initiates and others respond to him. The text only describes the Lord as вАЬansweringвАЭ when he responds to Job in 38:1; 40:1, 6. That God вАЬrespondsвАЭ to Job shows his merit.
n1800138: tn The imperfect may be classified as progressive imperfect; it indicates action that although just completed is regarded as still lasting into the present (GKC 316 ¬І107.h).
n1800139: tn Heb вАЬanswered the Lord and saidвАЭ (also in v. 9). The words вАЬand saidвАЭ here and in v. 9 have not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1800140: tn The verb вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„ШвАО (shut) means вАЬto go or rove aboutвАЭ (BDB 1001-2 s.v.). Here the infinitive construct serves as the object of the preposition.
n1800141:
tn The Hitpael (here also an infinitive construct after the preposition) of the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh) means вАЬto walk to and fro, back and forth, with the sense of investigating or reconnoitering (see e.g. Gen 13:17).
sn As the words are spoken by Satan, there is no self-condemnation in them. What they signify is the swiftness and thoroughness of his investigation of humans. The good angels are said to go to and fro in the earth on behalf of the suffering righteous (Zech 1:10, 11; 6:7), but Satan goes seeking whom he may devour (1 Pet 5:8).
n1800142:
tn The Hebrew has вАЬhave you placed your heart on Job?вАЭ This means вАЬdirect your mind toвАЭ (cf. BDB 963 s.v. I вАП„©„В„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО 2.b).
sn The question is undoubtedly rhetorical, for it is designed to make Satan aware of Job as God extols his fine qualities.
n1800143: tn The Hebrew conjunction вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) need not be translated in this case or it might be taken as emphatic (cf. IBHS 665 ¬І39.3.4e): вАЬCertainly there is no one like him.вАЭ
n1800144: tn The same expressions that appeared at the beginning of the chapter appear here in the words of God. In contrast to that narrative report about Job, the emphasis here is on JobвАЩs present character, and so the participle form is translated here as a gnomic or characteristic present (вАЬturnsвАЭ). It modifies вАЬmanвАЭ as one who is turning from evil.
n1800145: tn The Hebrew form has the interrogative вАП„ФвАО (he) on the adverb вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (khinnam, вАЬgratisвАЭ), a derivative either of the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan, вАЬto be gracious, show favorвАЭ), or its related noun вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen, вАЬgrace, favorвАЭ). The adverb has the sense of вАЬfree; gratis; gratuitously; for nothing; for no reasonвАЭ (see BDB 336 s.v. вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО). The idea is that Satan does not disagree that Job is pious, but that Job is loyal to God because of what he receives from God. He will test the sincerity of Job.
n1800146: tn The use of the independent personal pronoun here emphasizes the subject of the verb: вАЬHave you not put up a hedge.вАЭ
n1800147: tn The verb вАП„©„В„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷∞вАО (sukh) means вАЬto hedge or fence up, aboutвАЭ something (BDB 962 s.v. I вАП„©„В„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷∞вАО). The original idea seems to have been to surround with a wall of thorns for the purpose of protection (E. Dhorme, Job, 7). The verb is an implied comparison between making a hedge and protecting someone.
n1800148: sn Here the verb вАЬblessвАЭ is used in one of its very common meanings. The verb means вАЬto enrich,вАЭ often with the sense of enabling or empowering things for growth or fruitfulness. See further C. Westermann, Blessing in the Bible and the Life of the Church (OBT).
n1800149: tn Or вАЬsubstance.вАЭ The herds of livestock may be taken by metonymy of part for whole to represent possessions or prosperity in general.
n1800150: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (parats) means вАЬto break through.вАЭ It has the sense of abundant increase, as in breaking out, overflowing (see also Gen 30:30 and Exod 1:12).
n1800151: tn The particle вАП„Р„Х÷Љ„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њulam, вАЬbutвАЭ) serves to restrict the clause in relation to the preceding clause (IBHS 671-73 ¬І39.3.5e, n. 107).
n1800152:
tn The force of the imperatives in this sentence are almost conditionalвАФif God were to do this, then surely Job would respond differently.
sn The two imperatives (вАЬstretch outвАЭ and вАЬstrikeвАЭ) and the word вАЬhandвАЭ all form a bold anthropomorphic sentence. It is as if God would deliver a blow to Job with his fist. But the intended meaning is that God would intervene to destroy JobвАЩs material and physical prosperity.
n1800153: sn The formula used in the expression is the oath formula: вАЬif not to your face he will curse youвАЭ meaning вАЬhe will surely curse you to your face.вАЭ Satan is so sure that the piety is insincere that he can use an oath formula.
n1800154: tn See the comments on Job 1:5. Here too the idea of вАЬrenounceвАЭ may fit well enough, but the idea of actually cursing God may not be out of the picture if everything Job has is removed. Satan thinks he will denounce God.
n1800155: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) introduces a foundational clause upon which the following volitional clause is based.
n1800156: tn The versions add a verb here: вАЬdelivered toвАЭ or вАЬabandoned toвАЭ the hand of Satan.
n1800157: tn Heb вАЬin your hand.вАЭ The idiom means that it is now SatanвАЩs to do with as he pleases.
n1800158: tn The Hebrew word order emphatically holds out JobвАЩs person as the exception: вАЬonly upon him do not stretch forth your hand.вАЭ
n1800159: tn The Targum to Job adds вАЬwith permissionвАЭ to show that he was granted leave from GodвАЩs presence.
n1800160: sn So Satan, having received his permission to test JobвАЩs sincerity, goes out from the LordвАЩs presence. But Satan is bound by the will of the Most High not to touch Job himself. The sentence gives the impression that SatanвАЩs departure is with a certain eagerness and confidence.
n1800161: sn The series of catastrophes and the piety of Job is displayed now in comprehensive terms. Everything that can go wrong goes wrong, and yet Job, the pious servant of Yahweh, continues to worship him in the midst of the rubble. This section, and the next, will lay the foundation for the great dialogues in the book.
n1800162: tn The Targum to Job clarifies that it was the first day of the week. The fact that it was in the house of the firstborn is the reason.
n1800163: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (Job) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1800164: tn The use of the verb вАЬto beвАЭ with the participle gives emphasis to the continuing of the action in the past (GKC 360 ¬І116.r).
n1800165:
tn The LXX has вАЬthe spoilers spoiled themвАЭ instead of вАЬthe Sabeans swooped down.вАЭ The translators might have connected the word to вАП„©„Б÷∞÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (shavah, вАЬto take captiveвАЭ) rather than вАП„©„Б÷∞„С÷Є„РвАО (sheva Њ, вАЬSabeansвАЭ), or they may have understood the name as general reference to all types of Bedouin invaders from southern Arabia (HALOT 1381 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷∞„С÷Є„РвАО 2.c).
sn The name вАЬShebaвАЭ is used to represent its inhabitants, or some of them. The verb is feminine because the name is a place name. The Sabeans were a tribe from the Arabian peninsula. They were traders mostly (6:19). The raid came from the south, suggesting that this band of Sabeans were near Edom. The time of the attack seems to be winter since the oxen were plowing.
n1800166: tn The Hebrew is simply вАЬfellвАЭ (from вАП„†÷Є„§÷Ј„ЬвАО, nafal). To вАЬfall uponвАЭ something in war means to attack quickly and suddenly.
n1800167: sn JobвАЩs servants were probably armed and gave resistance, which would be the normal case in that time. This was probably why they were вАЬkilled with the sword.вАЭ
n1800168: tn Heb вАЬthe edge/mouth of the swordвАЭ; see T. J. Meek, вАЬArchaeology and a Point of Hebrew Syntax,вАЭ BASOR 122 (1951): 31-33.
n1800169: tn The pleonasms in the verse emphasize the emotional excitement of the messenger.
n1800170: tn The particle вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО ( њod, вАЬstillвАЭ) is used with the participle to express the past circumstances when something else happened (IBHS 625-26 ¬І37.6d).
n1800171: tn The Hebrew expression is literally вАЬyet/this/speaking/and this/ arrived.вАЭ The sentence uses the two demonstratives as a contrasting pair. It means вАЬthis one was still speaking when that one arrivedвАЭ (IBHS 308-9 ¬І17.3c). The word вАЬmessengerвАЭ has been supplied in the translation in vv. 16, 17, and 18 for clarity and for stylistic reasons.
n1800172: sn The вАЬfire of GodвАЭ would refer to lightning (1 Kgs 18:38; 2 Kgs 1:12; cf. NAB, NCV, TEV). The LXX simply has вАЬfire.вАЭ The first blow came from enemies; the second from heaven, which might have confused Job more as to the cause of his troubles. The use of the divine epithet could also be an indication of the superlative degree; see D. W. Thomas, вАЬA Consideration of Some Unusual Ways of Expressing the Superlative in Hebrew,вАЭ VT 3 (1953): 209-24.
n1800173: tn Or вАЬfrom the sky.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheaven[s]вАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n1800174: sn The name may have been given to the tribes that roamed between the Euphrates and the lands east of the Jordan. These are possibly the nomadic Kaldu who are part of the ethnic Aramaeans. The LXX simply has вАЬhorsemen.вАЭ
n1800175: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ШвАО (pashat) means вАЬto hurl themselvesвАЭ upon something (see Judg 9:33, 41). It was a quick, plundering raid to carry off the camels.
n1800176: tn Heb вАЬwith the edge/mouth of the sword.вАЭ
n1800177: tn The use of the particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) in this sentence is deictic, pointing out with excitement the events that happened as if the listener was there.
n1800178: sn Both wind and lightning (v. 16) were employed by Satan as his tools. God can permit him such control over factors of the weather when it suits the divine purpose, but God retains ultimate control (see 28:23-27; Prov 30:4; Luke 8:24-25).
n1800179: tn The word вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷µ„С÷ґ„®вАО (me њever) is simply вАЬfrom the direction ofвАЭ; the word вАП„Ґ÷µ„С÷ґ„®вАО ( њever) indicates the area the whirlwind came across.
n1800180: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„І÷Є„ЭвАО (vayyaqom, вАЬand he aroseвАЭ) indicates the intentionality and the rapidity of the actions to follow. It signals the beginning of his response to the terrible news. Therefore, the sentence could be translated, вАЬThen Job immediately began to tear his robe.вАЭ
n1800181: sn It was the custom to tear the robe in a time of mourning, to indicate that the heart was torn (Joel 2:12). The вАЬgarment, mantelвАЭ here is the outer garment frequently worn over the basic tunic. See further D. R. Ap-Thomas, вАЬNotes on Some Terms Relating to Prayer,вАЭ VT 6 (1956): 220-24.
n1800182: sn In mourning one normally put off every adornment that enhanced or embellished the person, including that which nature provided (Jer 7:29; Mic 1:16).
n1800183: tn This last verb is the Hishtaphel of the word вАП„Ч÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (khavah; BDB 1005 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Є„ФвАО); it means вАЬto prostrate oneself, to cause oneself to be low to the ground.вАЭ In the OT it is frequently translated вАЬto worshipвАЭ because that is usually why the individual would kneel down and then put his or her forehead to the ground at the knees. But the word essentially means вАЬto bow down to the ground.вАЭ Here вАЬworshipвАЭ (although employed by several English translations, cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, CEV) conveys more than what is taking placeвАФalthough JobвАЩs response is certainly worshipful. See G. I. Davies, вАЬA Note on the Etymology of histahawah,вАЭ VT 29 (1979): 493-95; and J. A. Emerton, вАЬThe Etymology of histahawah,вАЭ OTS (1977): 41-55.
n1800184: tn The adjective вАЬnakedвАЭ is functioning here as an adverbial accusative of state, explicative of the state of the subject. While it does include the literal sense of nakedness at birth, Job is also using it symbolically to mean вАЬwithout possessions.вАЭ
n1800185: sn While the first half of the couplet is to be taken literally as referring to his coming into this life, this second part must be interpreted only generally to refer to his departure from this life. It is parallel to 1 Tim 6:7, вАЬFor we have brought nothing into this world and so we cannot take a single thing out either.вАЭ
n1800186: tn The two verbs are simple perfects. (1) They can be given the nuance of gnomic imperfect, expressing what the sovereign God always does. This is the approach taken in the present translation. Alternatively (2) they could be referring specifically to JobвАЩs own experience: вАЬYahweh gave [definite past, referring to his coming into this good life] and Yahweh has taken awayвАЭ [present perfect, referring to his great losses]. Many English versions follow the second alternative.
n1800187: sn Some commentators are troubled by the appearance of the word вАЬYahwehвАЭ on the lips of Job, assuming that the narrator inserted his own name for God into the story-telling. Such thinking is based on the assumption that Yahweh was only a national god of Israel, unknown to anyone else in the ancient world. But here is a clear indication that a non-Israelite, Job, knew and believed in Yahweh.
n1800188: tn The last clause is difficult to translate. It simply reads, вАЬand he did not give unseemliness to God.вАЭ The word вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (tiflah) means вАЬunsavorinessвАЭ or вАЬunseemlinessвАЭ in a moral sense. The sense is that Job did not charge God with any moral impropriety in his dealings with him. God did nothing worthless or tasteless. The ancient versions saw the word connected with вАЬfoolishnessвАЭ or вАЬstupidityвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„§÷µ„ЬвАО, tafel, вАЬto be tastelessвАЭ). It is possible that вАЬfollyвАЭ would capture some of what Job meant here. See also M. Dahood, вАЬHebrew-Ugaritic Lexicography XII,вАЭ Bib 55 (1974): 381-93.
n180021: tc This last purpose clause has been omitted in some Greek versions.
n180022: tn Heb вАЬanswered the Lord and saidвАЭ (also in v. 4). The words вАЬand saidвАЭ here and in v. 9 have not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n180023: tn See the note on this phrase in 1:7.
n180024: tn The form is the Hiphil participle, вАЬmake strong, seize, hold fast.вАЭ It is the verbal use here; joined with вАП„Ґ÷є„У÷ґ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО ( њodennu, вАЬyet heвАЭ) it emphasizes that вАЬhe still holds firmly.вАЭ The testing has simply strengthened Job in his integrity.
n180025: tn This is the same word used to describe Job as вАЬblameless, pure.вАЭ Here it carries the idea of вАЬintegrityвАЭ; Job remained blameless, perfect.
n180026: tn The vav (вАП„ХвАО) with the preterite is used here to express the logical conclusion or consequence of what was stated previously. God is saying that Job has maintained his integrity, so that now it is clear that Satan moved against him groundlessly (GKC 328 ¬І111.l).
n180027: tn The verb literally means вАЬto swallowвАЭ; it forms an implied comparison in the line, indicating the desire of Satan to ruin him completely. See A Guillaume, вАЬA Note on the Root bala`,вАЭ JTS 13 (1962): 320-23; and N. M. Sarna, вАЬEpic Substratum in the Prose of Job,вАЭJBL 76 (1957): 13-25, for a discussion of the Ugaritic deity Mot swallowing up the enemy.
n180028: sn Once again the adverb вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (khinnam, вАЬgratisвАЭ) is used. It means вАЬgraciously, gratis, free, without cause, for no reason.вАЭ Here the sense has to be gratuitously, for no reason.вАЭ The point of the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan, вАЬto be graciousвАЭ) and its derivatives is that the action is undeserved. In fact, they would deserve the opposite. Sinners seeking grace deserve punishment. Here, Job deserves reward, not suffering.
n180029: tn The form is the simple preterite with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. However, the speech of Satan is in contrast to what God said, even though in narrative sequence.
n1800210: tn The preposition вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (be њad) designates interest or advantage arising from the idea of protection for (вАЬfor the benefit ofвАЭ); see IBHS 201-2 ¬І11.2.7a.
n1800211: sn The meaning of the expression is obscure. It may come from the idea of sacrificing an animal or another person in order to go free, suggesting the expression that one type of skin that was worth less was surrendered to save the more important life. Satan would then be saying that Job was willing for others to die for him to go free, but not himself. вАЬSkinвАЭ would be a synecdoche of the part for the whole (like the idiomatic use of skin today for a person in a narrow escape). The second clause indicates that God has not even scratched the surface because Job has been protected. His вАЬskinвАЭ might have been scratched, but not his flesh and bone! But if his life had been put in danger, he would have responded differently.
n1800212: tc The LXX has вАЬmake full payment, pay a full priceвАЭ (LSJ 522 s.v. бЉРќЇѕДбљЈќљѕЙ).
n1800213: tn Heb вАЬIndeed, all that a man has he will give for his life.вАЭ
n1800214: sn The вАЬbones and fleshвАЭ are idiomatic for the whole person, his physical and his psychical/spiritual being (see further H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament, 26-28).
n1800215: sn This is the same oath formula found in 1:11; see the note there.
n1800216: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ„Х÷євАО (hinno) is literally, вАЬhere he is!вАЭ God presents Job to Satan, with the restriction on preserving JobвАЩs life.
n1800217: tn The LXX has вАЬI deliver him up to you.вАЭ
n1800219: sn The irony of the passage comes through with this choice of words. The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (shamar) means вАЬto keep; to guard; to preserve.вАЭ The exceptive clause casts Satan in the role of a saviorвАФhe cannot destroy this life but must protect it.
n1800220: tn The verb is вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (nakhah, вАЬstruck, smoteвАЭ); it can be rendered in this context as вАЬafflicted.вАЭ
n1800221: sn The general consensus is that Job was afflicted with a leprosy known as elephantiasis, named because the rough skin and the swollen limbs are animal-like. The Hebrew word вАП„©„Б÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (shekhin, вАЬboilвАЭ) can indicate an ulcer as well. Leprosy begins with such, but so do other diseases. Leprosy normally begins in the limbs and spreads, but Job was afflicted everywhere at once. It may be some other disease also characterized by such a malignant ulcer. D. J. A. Clines has a thorough bibliography on all the possible diseases linked to this description (Job [WBC], 48). See also HALOT 1460 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ„ЯвАО.
n1800223: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„УвАО (garad) is a hapax legomenon (only occurring here). Modern Hebrew has retained a meaning вАЬto scrape,вАЭ which is what the cognate Syriac and Arabic indicate. In the Hitpael it would mean вАЬscrape himself.вАЭ
n1800224: sn The disease required constant attention. The infection and pus had to be scraped away with a piece of broken pottery in order to prevent the spread of the infection. The skin was so disfigured that even his friends did not recognize him (2:12). The book will add that the disease afflicted him inwardly, giving him a foul breath and a loathsome smell (19:17, 20). The sores bred worms; they opened and ran, and closed and tightened (16:8). He was tormented with dreams (7:14). He felt like he was choking (7:14). His bones were racked with burning pain (30:30). And he was not able to rise from his place (19:18). The disease was incurable, but it would last for years, leaving the patient longing for death.
n1800225: tn The construction uses the disjunctive vav (вАП„ХвАО) with the independent pronoun with the active participle. The construction connects this clause with what has just been said, making this a circumstantial clause.
n1800226: sn Among the ashes. It is likely that the вАЬashesвАЭ refers to the place outside the city where the rubbish was collected and burnt, i.e., the ash-heap (cf. CEV). This is the understanding of the LXX, which reads вАЬdung-hill outside the city.вАЭ
n1800227: tn The versions have some information here that is interesting, albeit fanciful. The Targum calls her вАЬDinah.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬwhen a long time had passed.вАЭ But the whole rendering of the LXX is paraphrastic: вАЬHow long will you hold out, saying, вАШBehold, I wait yet a little while, expecting the hope of my deliverance?вАЩ for behold, your memorial is abolished from the earth, even your sons and daughters, the pangs and pains of my womb which I bore in vain with sorrows, and you yourself sit down to spend the night in the open air among the corruption of worms, and I am a wanderer and a servant from place to place and house to house, waiting for the setting sun, that I may rest from my labors and pains that now beset me, but say some word against the Lord and die.вАЭ
n1800228: sn See R. D. Moore, вАЬThe Integrity of Job,вАЭ CBQ 45 (1983): 17-31. The reference of JobвАЩs wife to his вАЬintegrityвАЭ could be a precursor of the conclusion reached by Elihu in 32:2 where he charged Job with justifying himself rather than God.
n1800229:
tn The verb is literally вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, (barakh, вАЬblessвАЭ). As in the earlier uses, the meaning probably has more to do with renouncing God than of speaking a curse. The actual word may be taken as a theological euphemism for the verb вАП„І÷і„Ь÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАО (qillel, вАЬcurseвАЭ). If JobвАЩs wife had meant that he was trying to justify himself rather than God, вАЬbless GodвАЭ might be translated вАЬspeak well of God,вАЭ the resolution accepted by God in 42:7-8 following JobвАЩs double confession of having spoken wrongly of God (40:3-5; 42:1-6).
sn The church fathers were quick to see here again the role of the wife in the temptationвАФshe acts as the intermediary between Satan and Job, pressing the cause for him. However, JobвАЩs wife has been demonized falsely. Job did not say that she was a foolish woman, only that she was speaking like one of them (2:10). Also, Job did not exclude her from sharing in his suffering (вАЬshould we receiveвАЭ). He evidently recognized that her words were the result of her personal loss and pain as well as the desire to see her husbandвАЩs suffering ended. When God gave instructions for the restoration of JobвАЩs friends because of their foolish words (42:7-9), no mention is made of any need for JobвАЩs wife to be restored.
n1800230: tn The imperative with the conjunction in this expression serves to express the certainty that will follow as the result or consequence of the previous imperative (GKC 324-25 ¬І110.f).
n1800232: tn The word вАЬfoolishвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„С÷Є„ЬвАО, naval) has to do with godlessness more than silliness (Ps 14:1). To be foolish in this sense is to deny the nature and the work of God in life its proper place. See A. Phillips, вАЬNEBALAвАФA Term for Serious Disorderly Unruly Conduct,вАЭ VT 25 (1975): 237-41; and W. M. W. Roth, вАЬNBL,вАЭ VT 10 (1960): 394-409.
n1800233: tn The verb вАП„І÷і„С÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАО (qibbel) means вАЬto accept, receive.вАЭ It is attested in the Amarna letters with the meaning вАЬreceive meekly, patiently.вАЭ
n1800234: tn The adverb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (gam, вАЬalso, evenвАЭ) is placed here before the first clause, but belongs with the second. It intensifies the idea (see GKC 483 ¬І153). See also C. J. Labuschagne, вАЬThe Emphasizing Particle GAM and Its Connotations,вАЭ Studia Biblica et Semitica, 193-203.
n1800235: tn The two verbs in this sentence, Piel imperfects, are deliberative imperfects; they express the reasoning or deliberating in the interrogative sentences.
n1800236:
tn A question need not be introduced by an interrogative particle or adverb. The natural emphasis on the words is enough to indicate it is a question (GKC 473 ¬І150.a).
sn The Hebrew words вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (tov, вАЬgoodвАЭ) and вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њ, вАЬevilвАЭ) have to do with what affects life. That which is good benefits people because it produces, promotes and protects life; that which is evil brings calamity and disaster, it harms, pains, or destroys life.
n1800237: tn Heb вАЬsin with his lips,вАЭ an idiom meaning he did not sin by what he said.
n1800238: sn See N. C. Habel, вАЬвАШOnly the Jackal is My Friend,вАЩ On Friends and Redeemers in Job,вАЭ Int 31 (1977): 227-36.
n1800239: tn Heb вАЬa man from his placeвАЭ; this is the distributive use, meaning вАЬeach man came from his place.вАЭ
n1800240: sn Commentators have tried to analyze the meanings of the names of the friends and their locations. Not only has this proven to be difficult (Teman is the only place that is known), it is not necessary for the study of the book. The names are probably not symbolic of the things they say.
n1800241: tn The verb can mean that they вАЬagreed together,вАЭ but it also (and more likely) means that they came together at a meeting point to go visit Job together.
n1800242: tn The verb вАЬto show griefвАЭ is вАП„†„Х÷Љ„УвАО (nud), and literally signifies вАЬto shake the head.вАЭ It may be that his friends came to show the proper sympathy and express the appropriate feelings. They were not ready for what they found.
n1800243: tn The second infinitive is from вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Є„ЭвАО (nakham, вАЬto comfort, consoleвАЭ in the Piel). This word may be derived from a word with a meaning of sighing deeply.
n1800244: tn Heb вАЬthey lifted up their eyes.вАЭ The idiom вАЬto lift up the eyesвАЭ (or вАЬto lift up the voiceвАЭ) is intended to show a special intensity in the effort. Here it would indicate that they were trying to see Job from a great distance away.
n1800245: tn The Hiphil perfect here should take the nuance of potential perfectвАФthey were not able to recognize him. In other words, this does not mean that they did not know it was Job, only that he did not look anything like the Job they knew.
n1800246: tn Heb вАЬthey tossed dust skyward over their heads.вАЭ
n1800247: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Р÷µ„СвАО (ke Њev) means вАЬpainвАЭвАФboth mental and physical pain. The translation of вАЬgriefвАЭ captures only part of its emphasis.
n1800248: sn The three friends went into a more severe form of mourning, one that is usually reserved for a death. E. Dhorme says it is a display of grief in its most intense form (Job, 23); for one of them to speak before the sufferer spoke would have been wrong.
n180031: sn The previous chapters (1-2) were prose narrative, this chapter, however, commences the poetic section of the book (chs. 3-41) containing the cycles of speeches.
n180032: sn The detailed introduction to the speech with вАЬhe opened his mouthвАЭ draws the readers attention to what was going to be said. As the introduction to the poetic speech that follows (3:3-26), vv. 1-2 continue the prose style of chapters 1-2. Each of the subsequent speeches is introduced by such a prose heading.
n180033: tn The verb вАЬcursedвАЭ is the Piel preterite from the verb вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (qalal); this means вАЬto be lightвАЭ in the Qal stem, but here вАЬto treat lightly, with contempt, curse.вАЭ See in general H. C. Brichto, The Problem of вАЬCurseвАЭ in the Hebrew Bible (JBLMS); and A. C. Thiselton, вАЬThe Supposed Power of Words in the Biblical Writings,вАЭ JTS 25 (1974): 283-99.
n180034: tn Heb вАЬhis dayвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB). The Syriac has вАЬthe day on which he was born.вАЭ The context makes it clear that Job meant the day of his birth. But some have tried to offer a different interpretation, such as his destiny or his predicament. For this reason the Syriac clarified the meaning for their readers in much the same way as the present translation does by rendering вАЬhis dayвАЭ as вАЬthe day he was born.вАЭ On the Syriac translation of the book of Job, see Heidi M. Szpek, Translation Technique in the Peshitta to Job (SBLDS).
n180035: tc The text has вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (vayya њan), literally, вАЬand he answered.вАЭ The LXX simply has вАЬsayingвАЭ for the entire verse. The Syriac, Targum, and Greek A agree with the MT. вАЬ[Someone] answered and saidвАЭ is phraseology characteristic of all the speeches in Job beginning with Satan in 1:9. No other portion of the OT employs this phraseology as often or as consistently.
n180036: tn The relative clause is carried by the preposition with the resumptive pronoun: вАЬthe day [which] I was born in itвАЭ meaning вАЬthe day on which I was bornвАЭ (see GKC 486-88 ¬І155.f, i).
n180037: tn The verb is the Niphal imperfect. It may be interpreted in this dependent clause (1) as representing a future event from some point of time in the pastвАФвАЬthe day on which I was bornвАЭ or вАЬwould be bornвАЭ (see GKC 316 ¬І107.k). Or (2) it may simply serve as a preterite indicating action that is in the past.
n180038: tn The MT simply has вАЬand the nightвАФit saidвА¶.вАЭ By simple juxtaposition with the parallel construction (вАЬon which I was bornвАЭ) the verb вАЬit saidвАЭ must be a relative clause explaining вАЬthe night.вАЭ Rather than supply вАЬin whichвАЭ and make the verb passive (which is possible since no specific subject is provided, but leaves open the question of who said it), it is preferable to take the verse as a personification. First Job cursed the day; now he cursed the night that spoke about what it witnessed. See A. Ehrman, вАЬA Note on the Verb  Њamar,вАЭ JQR 55 (1964/65): 166-67.
n180039: tn The word is вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, вАЬa manвАЭ). The word usually distinguishes a man as strong, distinct from children and women. Translations which render this as вАЬboyвАЭ (to remove the apparent contradiction of an adult being вАЬconceivedвАЭ in the womb) miss this point.
n1800310: sn The announcement at birth is to the fact that a male was conceived. The same parallelism between вАЬbrought forth/bornвАЭ and вАЬconceivedвАЭ may be found in Ps 51:7 HT (51:5 ET). The motifs of the night of conception and the day of birth will be developed by Job. For the entire verse, which is more a wish or malediction than a curse, see S. H. Blank, вАЬвАШPerish the Day!вАЩ A Misdirected Curse (Job 3:3),вАЭ Prophetic Thought, 61-63.
n1800311: tn The first two words should be treated as a casus pendens (see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 69), referred to as an extraposition in recent grammarians.
n1800312: sn This expression by Job is the negation of the divine decree at creationвАФвАЬLet there be light,вАЭ and that was the first day. Job wishes that his first day be darkness: вАЬAs for that day, let there be darkness.вАЭ Since only God has this prerogative, Job adds the wish that God on high would not regard that day.
n1800313: tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (darash) means вАЬto seek, inquire,вАЭ and вАЬto address someone, be concerned about somethingвАЭ (cf. Deut 11:12; Jer 30:14, 17). Job wants the day to perish from the mind of God.
n1800314: tn The verb is the Hiphil of вАП„Щ÷Є„§÷Ј„ҐвАО (yafa њ), which means here вАЬcause to shine.вАЭ The subject is the term вАП„†÷∞„Ф÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (neharah, вАЬlightвАЭ), a hapax legomenon which is from the verb вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„®вАО (nahar, вАЬto gleamвАЭ [see Isa 60:5]).
n1800315: sn The translation of вАП„¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (tsalmavet, вАЬshadow of deathвАЭ) has been traditionally understood to indicate a dark, death shadow (supported in the LXX), but many scholars think it may not represent the best etymological analysis of the word. The word may be connected to an Arabic word which means вАЬto be dark,вАЭ and an Akkadian word meaning вАЬblack.вАЭ It would then have to be repointed throughout its uses to вАП„¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„™вАО (tsalmut) forming an abstract ending. It would then simply mean вАЬdarknessвАЭ rather than вАЬshadow of death.вАЭ Or the word can be understood as an idiomatic expression meaning вАЬgloomвАЭ that is deeper than вАП„Ч÷є„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (khoshekh; see HALOT 1029 s.v. вАП„¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО). Since вАЬdarknessвАЭ has already been used in the line, the two together could possibly form a nominal hendiadys: вАЬLet the deepest darknessвА¶.вАЭ There is a significant amount of literature on this; one may begin with W. L. Michel, вАЬSLMWT, вАШDeep DarknessвАЩ or вАШShadow of DeathвАЩ?вАЭ BR 29 (1984): 5-20.
n1800316: tn The verb is вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (ga Њal, вАЬredeem, claimвАЭ). Some have suggested that the verb is actually the homonym вАЬpollute.вАЭ This is the reading in the Targum, Syriac, Vulgate, and Rashi, who quotes from Mal 1:7, 12. See A. R. Johnson, вАЬThe Primary Meaning of gaвАЩal,вАЭ VTSup 1 (1953): 67-77.
n1800317: tn The expression вАЬthe blackness of the dayвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„®÷µ„Щ „Щ„Х÷є„ЭвАО, kimrire yom) probably means everything that makes the day black, such as events like eclipses. Job wishes that all ominous darknesses would terrify that day. It comes from the word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (kamar, вАЬto be blackвАЭ), related to Akkadian kamaru (вАЬto overshadow, darkenвАЭ). The versions seem to have ignored the first letter and connected the word to вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (marar, вАЬbe bitterвАЭ).
n1800318: tn The verb is simply вАП„Ь÷Є„І÷Ј„ЧвАО (laqakh, вАЬto takeвАЭ). Here it conveys a strong sense of seizing something and not letting it go.
n1800319: tn The pointing of the verb is meant to connect it with the root вАП„Ч÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (khadah, вАЬrejoiceвАЭ). But the letters in the text were correctly understood by the versions to be from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (yakhad, вАЬto be combined, addedвАЭ). See G. Rendsburg, вАЬDouble Polysemy in Genesis 49:6 and Job 3:6, вАЭ CBQ 44 (1982): 48-51.
n1800320: sn The choice of this word for вАЬmoons,вАЭ вАП„Щ÷∞„®÷Є„Ч÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yerakhim) instead of вАП„Ч÷≥„У÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khodashim) is due to the fact that вАЬmonthвАЭ here is not a reference for which an exact calendar date is essential (in which case вАП„Ч÷є„У÷ґ„©„БвАО [khodesh] would have been preferred). See J. Segal, вАЬвАШyrhвАЩ in the Gezer вАШCalendar,вАЩвАЭ JSS 7 (1962): 220, n. 4. Twelve times in the OT вАП„Щ÷ґ„®÷Ј„ЧвАО (yerakh) means вАЬmonthвАЭ (Exod 2:2; Deut 21:13; 33:14; 1 Kgs 6:37, 38; 8:2; 2 Kgs 15:13; Zech 11:8; Job 3:6; 7:3; 29:2; 39:2).
n1800321: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) in this sentence focuses the readerвАЩs attention on the statement to follow.
n1800322: tn The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„УвАО (galmud) probably has here the idea of вАЬbarrenвАЭ rather than вАЬsolitary.вАЭ See the parallelism in Isa 49:21. In Job it seems to carry the idea of вАЬbarrenвАЭ in 15:34, and вАЬgloomyвАЭ in 30:3. Barrenness can lead to gloom.
n1800323: tn The word is from вАП„®÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (ranan, вАЬto give a ringing cryвАЭ or вАЬshout of joyвАЭ). The sound is loud and shrill.
n1800324: tn The verb is simply вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬto enterвАЭ). The NIV translates interpretively вАЬbe heard in it.вАЭ A shout of joy, such as at a birth, that вАЬentersвАЭ a day is certainly heard on that day.
n1800325:
tn Not everyone is satisfied with the reading of the MT. Gordis thought вАЬdayвАЭ should be вАЬsea,вАЭ and вАЬcursersвАЭ should be вАЬrousersвАЭ (changing  Њalef to  њayin; cf. NRSV). This is an unnecessary change, for there is no textual problem in the line (D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 71). Others have taken the reading вАЬseaвАЭ as a personification and accepted the rest of the text, gaining the sense of вАЬthose whose magic binds even the sea monster of the deepвАЭ (e.g., NEB).
sn Those who curse the day are probably the professional enchanters and magicians who were thought to cast spells on days and overwhelm them with darkness and misfortune. The myths explained eclipses as the dragon throwing its folds around the sun and the moon, thus engulfing or swallowing the day and the night. This interpretation matches the parallelism better than the interpretation that says these are merely professional mourners.
n1800326: tn The verb is probably вАЬexecrate, curse,вАЭ from вАП„І÷Є„С÷Ј„СвАО (qavav). But E. Ullendorff took it from вАП„†÷Є„І÷Ј„СвАО (naqav, вАЬpierceвАЭ) and gained a reading вАЬLet the light rays of day pierce it (i.e., the night) apt even to rouse LeviathanвАЭ (вАЬJob 3:8, вАЭ VT 11 [1961]: 350-51).
n1800327: tn The verbal adjective вАП„Ґ÷Є„™÷і„Щ„УвАО ( њatid) means вАЬready, prepared.вАЭ Here it has a substantival use similar to that of participles. It is followed by the Polel infinitive construct вАП„Ґ÷є„®÷µ„®вАО ( њorer). The infinitive without the preposition serves as the object of the preceding verbal adjective (GKC 350 ¬І114.m).
n1800328: sn Job employs here the mythological figure Leviathan, the monster of the deep or chaos. Job wishes that such a creation of chaos could be summoned by the mourners to swallow up that day. See E. Ullendorff, вАЬJob 3:8, вАЭ VT 11 (1961): 350-51.
n1800329: tn Heb вАЬthe stars of its dawn.вАЭ The word вАП„†÷ґ„©„Б÷ґ„£вАО (neshef) can mean вАЬtwilightвАЭ or вАЬdawn.вАЭ In this context the morning stars are in mind. Job wishes that the morning starsвАФthat should announce the dayвАФgo out.
n1800330: tn The verb вАЬwait, hopeвАЭ has the idea of eager expectation and preparation. It is used elsewhere of waiting on the Lord with anticipation.
n1800331: tn The absolute state вАП„Р÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЯвАО ( Њayin, вАЬthere is noneвАЭ) is here used as a verbal predicate (see GKC 480 ¬І152.k). The concise expression literally says вАЬand none.вАЭ
n1800332: sn The expression is literally вАЬthe eyelids of the morning.вАЭ This means the very first rays of dawn (see also Job 41:18). There is some debate whether it refers to вАЬeyelidsвАЭ or вАЬeyelashesвАЭ or вАЬeyeballs.вАЭ If the last, it would signify the flashing eyes of a person. See for the Ugaritic background H. L. Ginsberg, The Legend of King Keret (BASORSup), 39; see also J. M. Steadman, вАЬвАШEyelids of MornвАЩ: A Biblical Convention,вАЭ HTR 56 (1963): 159-67.
n1800333: tn The subject is still вАЬthat night.вАЭ Here, at the end of this first section, Job finally expresses the crime of that nightвАФit did not hinder his birth.
n1800334: sn This use of doors for the womb forms an implied comparison; the night should have hindered conception (see Gen 20:18 and 1 Sam 1:5).
n1800335: tn The Hebrew has simply вАЬmy belly [= womb].вАЭ The suffix on the noun must be objectiveвАФit was the womb of JobвАЩs mother in which he lay before his birth. See however N. C. Habel, вАЬThe Dative Suffix in Job 33:13, вАЭ Bib 63 (1982): 258-59, who thinks it is deliberately ambiguous.
n1800336: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО ( њamal) means вАЬwork, heavy labor, agonizing labor, struggleвАЭ with the idea of fatigue and pain.
n1800337: sn Job follows his initial cry with a series of rhetorical questions. His argument runs along these lines: since he was born (v. 10), the next chance he had of escaping this life of misery would have been to be still born (vv. 11-12, 16). In vv. 13-19 Job considers death as falling into a peaceful sleep in a place where there is no trouble. The high frequency of rhetorical questions in series is a characteristic of the book of Job that sets it off from all other portions of the OT. The effect is primarily dramatic, creating a tension that requires resolution. See W. Watson, Classical Hebrew Poetry, 340-41.
n1800338: tn The negative only occurs with the first clause, but it extends its influence to the parallel second clause (GKC 483 ¬І152.z).
n1800339: tn The two verbs in this verse are both prefix conjugations; they are clearly referring to the past and should be classified as preterites. E. Dhorme (Job, 32) notes that the verb вАЬI came outвАЭ is in the perfect to mark its priority in time in relation to the other verbs.
n1800340: tn The translation вАЬat birthвАЭ is very smooth, but catches the meaning and avoids the tautology in the verse. The line literally reads вАЬfrom the womb.вАЭ The second half of the verse has the verb вАЬI came out/forthвАЭ which does double duty for both parallel lines. The second half uses вАЬbellyвАЭ for the womb.
n1800341: tn The two halves of the verse use the prepositional phrases (вАЬfrom the wombвАЭ and вАЬfrom the belly I went outвАЭ) in the temporal sense of вАЬon emerging from the womb.вАЭ
n1800342:
tn The verb вАП„І÷і„У÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷і„ЩвАО (qiddemuni) is the Piel from вАП„І÷Є„У÷Ј„ЭвАО (qadam), meaning вАЬto come before; to meet; to prevent.вАЭ Here it has the idea of going to meet or welcome someone. In spite of various attempts to connect the idea to the father or to adoption rites, it probably simply means the motherвАЩs knees that welcome the child for nursing. See R. de Vaux, Ancient Israel, 42.
sn The sufferer is looking back over all the possible chances of death, including when he was brought forth, placed on the knees or lap, and breastfed.
n1800343: tn There is no verb in the second half of the verse. The idea simply has, вАЬand why breasts that I might suck?вАЭ
n1800344: sn The commentaries mention the parallel construction in the writings of Ashurbanipal: вАЬYou were weak, Ashurbanipal, you who sat on the knees of the goddess, queen of Nineveh; of the four teats that were placed near to your mouth, you sucked two and you hid your face in the othersвАЭ (M. Streck, Assurbanipal [VAB], 348).
n1800345: tn Heb вАЬthat I might suckle.вАЭ The verb is the Qal imperfect of вАП„Щ÷Є„†÷Ј„ІвАО (yanaq, вАЬsuckleвАЭ). Here the clause is subordinated to the preceding question and so functions as a final imperfect.
n1800346: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷Ј„™÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО ( њattah, вАЬnowвАЭ) may have a logical nuance here, almost with the idea of вАЬif that had been the caseвА¶вАЭ (IBHS 667-68 ¬І39.3.4f). However, the temporal вАЬnowвАЭ is retained in translation since the imperfect verb following two perfects вАЬsuggests what JobвАЩs present state would be if he had had the quiet of a still birthвАЭ (J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 95, n. 23). Cf. GKC 313 ¬І106.p.
n1800347: tn The copula on the verb indicates a sequence for the imperfect: вАЬand then I wouldвА¶.вАЭ In the second half of the verse it is paralleled by вАЬthen.вАЭ
n1800348: tn The text uses a combination of the perfect (lie down/sleep) and imperfect (quiet/rest). The particle вАП„Ґ÷Ј„™÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО ( њattah, вАЬnowвАЭ) gives to the perfect verb its conditional nuance. It presents actions in the past that are not actually accomplished but seen as possible (GKC 313 ¬І106.p).
n1800349: tn The last part uses the impersonal verb вАЬit would be at rest for me.вАЭ
n1800350: tn The difficult term вАП„Ч÷≥„®÷Є„С„Х÷є„™вАО (khoravot) is translated вАЬdesolate [places]вАЭ. The LXX confused the word and translated it вАЬwho gloried in their swords.вАЭ One would expect a word for monuments, or tombs (T. K. Cheyne emended it to вАЬeverlasting tombsвАЭ [вАЬMore Critical Gleanings in Job,вАЭ ExpTim 10 (1898/99): 380-83]). But this difficult word is of uncertain etymology and therefore cannot simply be made to mean вАЬroyal tombs.вАЭ The verb means вАЬbe desolate, solitary.вАЭ In Isa 48:21 there is the clear sense of a desert. That is the meaning of Assyrian huribtu. It may be that like the pyramids of Egypt these tombs would have been built in the desert regions. Or it may describe how they rebuilt ruins for themselves. He would be saying then that instead of lying here in pain and shame if he had died he would be with the great ones of the earth. Otherwise, the word could be interpreted as a metonymy of effect, indicating that the once glorious tomb now is desolate. But this does not fit the contextвАФthe verse is talking about the state of the great ones after their death.
n1800351: tn The expression simply has вАЬor with princes gold to them.вАЭ The noun is defined by the noun clause serving as a relative clause (GKC 486 ¬І155.e).
n1800352: tn Heb вАЬfilled their houses.вАЭ There is no reason here to take вАЬhousesвАЭ to mean tombs; the вАЬhousesвАЭ refer to the places the princes lived (i.e., palaces). The reference is not to the practice of burying treasures with the dead. It is simply saying that if Job had died he would have been with the rich and famous in death.
n1800353: tn The verb is governed by the interrogative of v. 12 that introduces this series of rhetorical questions.
n1800354: tn The verb is again the prefix conjugation, but the narrative requires a past tense, or preterite.
n1800355: tn Heb вАЬhidden.вАЭ The LXX paraphrases: вАЬan untimely birth, proceeding from his motherвАЩs womb.вАЭ
n1800356: tn The noun вАП„†÷µ„§÷ґ„ЬвАО (nefel, вАЬmiscarriageвАЭ) is the abortive thing that falls (hence the verb) from the womb before the time is ripe (Ps 58:9). The idiom using the verb вАЬto fallвАЭ from the womb means to come into the world (Isa 26:18). The epithet вАП„Ш÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (tamun, вАЬhiddenвАЭ) is appropriate to the verse. The child comes in vain, and disappears into the darknessвАФit is hidden forever.
n1800357: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷є„Ь÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њolelim) normally refers to вАЬnurslings.вАЭ Here it must refer to infants in general since it refers to a stillborn child.
n1800358: tn The relative clause does not have the relative pronoun; the simple juxtaposition of words indicates that it is modifying the infants.
n1800359: sn The reference seems to be death, or Sheol, the place where the infant who is stillborn is either buried (the grave) or resides (the place of departed spirits) and thus does not see the light of the sun.
n1800360: sn The wicked are the ungodly, those who are not members of the covenant (normally) and in this context especially those who oppress and torment other people.
n1800361: tn The parallelism uses the perfect verb in the first parallel part, and the imperfect opposite it in the second. Since the verse projects to the grave or Sheol (вАЬthereвАЭ) where the action is perceived as still continuing or just taking place, both receive an English present tense translation (GKC 312 ¬І106.l).
n1800362: tn Here the noun вАП„®÷є„Т„ЦвАО (rogez) refers to the agitation of living as opposed to the peaceful rest of dying. The associated verb вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ЦвАО (ragaz) means вАЬto be agitated, excited.вАЭ The expression indicates that they cease from troubling, meaning all the agitation of their own lives.
n1800363: tn The word вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷і„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yagia њ) means вАЬexhausted, weariedвАЭ; it is clarified as a physical exhaustion by the genitive of specification (вАЬwith regard to their strengthвАЭ).
n1800364: tn вАЬThereвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied from the context.
n1800365: tn The LXX omits the verb and translates the noun not as prisoners but as вАЬold menвАЭ or вАЬmen of old time.вАЭ
n1800366: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Є„†„Х÷ЉвАО (sha Њananu) is the Palel of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЯвАО (sha Њan) which means вАЬto rest.вАЭ It refers to the normal rest or refreshment of individuals; here it is contrasted with the harsh treatment normally put on prisoners.
n1800367: sn See further J. C. de Moor, вАЬLexical Remarks Concerning yahad and yahdaw,вАЭ VT 7 (1957): 350-55.
n1800368: tn Or вАЬtaskmaster.вАЭ The same Hebrew word is used for the taskmasters in Exod 3:7.
n1800369: tn The versions have taken the pronoun in the sense of the verb вАЬto be.вАЭ Others give it the sense of вАЬthe same thing,вАЭ rendering the verse as вАЬsmall and great, there is no difference there.вАЭ GKC 437 ¬І135.a, n. 1, follows this idea with a meaning of вАЬthe same.вАЭ
n1800370: tn The LXX renders this as вАЬunafraid,вАЭ although the negative has disappeared in some mss to give the reading вАЬand the servant that feared his master.вАЭ See I. Mendelsohn, вАЬThe Canaanite Term for вАШFree ProletarianвАЩ,вАЭ BASOR 83 (1941): 36-39; idem, вАЬNew Light on hupsu,вАЭ BASOR 139 (1955): 9-11.
n1800371: tn The plural вАЬmastersвАЭ could be taken here as a plural of majesty rather than as referring to numerous masters.
n1800372: sn Since he has survived birth, Job wonders why he could not have died a premature death. He wonders why God gives light and life to those who are in misery. His own condition throws gloom over life, and so he poses the question first generally, for many would prefer death to misery (20-22); then he comes to the individual, himself, who would prefer death (23). He closes his initial complaint with some depictions of his suffering that afflicts him and gives him no rest (24-26).
n1800373: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1800374:
tn The verb is the simple imperfect, expressing the progressive imperfect nuance. But there is no formal subject to the verb, prompting some translations to make it passive in view of the indefinite subject (so, e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV). Such a passive could be taken as a so-called вАЬdivine passiveвАЭ by which God is the implied agent. Job clearly means God here, but he stops short of naming him (see also the note on вАЬGodвАЭ earlier in this verse).
sn In vv. 11, 12, and 16 there was the first series of questions in which Job himself was in question. Now the questions are more general for all mankindвАФwhy should the sufferers in general have been afflicted with life?
n1800375: sn In v. 10 the word was used to describe the labor and sorrow that comes from it; here the one in such misery is called the вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷µ„ЬвАО ( њamel, вАЬlaborer, suffererвАЭ).
n1800376: tn The second colon now refers to people in general because of the plural construct вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷µ„Щ „†÷Є„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (mare nafesh, вАЬthose bitter of soul/lifeвАЭ). One may recall the use of вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (marah, вАЬbitterвАЭ) by Naomi to describe her pained experience as a poor widow in Ruth 1:20, or the use of the word to describe the bitter oppression inflicted on Israel by the Egyptians (Exod 1:14). Those who are вАЬbitter of soulвАЭ are those whose life is overwhelmed with painful experiences and suffering.
n1800377: tn The verse simply begins with the participle in apposition to the expressions in the previous verse describing those who are bitter. The preposition is added from the context.
n1800378: tn The verb is the Piel participle of вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (khakhah, вАЬto wait forвАЭ someone; Yahweh is the object in Isa 8:17; 64:3; Ps 33:20). Here death is the supreme hope of the miserable and the suffering.
n1800379: tn The verse simply has the form вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО ( Њen, вАЬthere is notвАЭ) with a pronominal suffix and a conjunctionвАФвАЬand there is not itвАЭ or вАЬand it is not.вАЭ The LXX and the Vulgate add a verb to explain this form: вАЬand obtain it not.вАЭ
n1800380:
tn The parallel verb is now a preterite with a vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive; it therefore has the nuance of a characteristic perfect or gnomic perfectвАФthe English present tense.
sn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (khafar) means вАЬto dig; to excavate.вАЭ It may have the accusative of the thing that is being sought (Exod 7:24), but here it is followed by a comparative min (вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО). The verse therefore describes the sufferers who excavate or dig the ground to find death, more than others who seek for treasure.
n1800381: tn Here too the form is the participle in apposition вАЬto him who is in miseryвАЭ in v. 20. It continues the description of those who are destitute and would be delighted to die.
n1800382: tn The Syriac has вАЬand gather themselves together,вАЭ possibly reading вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (gil, вАЬrejoicingвАЭ) as вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЬвАО (gal, вАЬheapвАЭ). Some have tried to emend the text to make the word mean вАЬheapвАЭ or вАЬmound,вАЭ as in a funerary mound. While one could argue for a heap of stones as a funerary mound, the passage has already spoken of digging a grave, which would be quite different. And while such a change would make a neater parallelism in the verse, there is no reason to force such; the idea of вАЬjubilationвАЭ fits the tenor of the whole verse easily enough and there is no reason to change it. A similar expression is found in Hos 9:1, which says, вАЬrejoice not, O Israel, with jubilation.вАЭ Here the idea then is that these sufferers would rejoice вАЬto the point of jubilationвАЭ at death.
n1800383: tn This sentence also parallels an imperfect verb with the substantival participle of the first colon. It is translated as an English present tense.
n1800384: tn The particle could be вАЬwhenвАЭ or вАЬbecauseвАЭ in this verse.
n1800385: sn The expression вАЬwhen they find a graveвАЭ means when they finally die. The verse describes the relief and rest that the sufferer will obtain when the long-awaited death is reached.
n1800386: tn This first part of the verse, вАЬWhy is light given,вАЭ is supplied from the context. In the Hebrew text the verse simply begins with вАЬto a manвА¶.вАЭ It is also in apposition to the construction in v. 20. But after so many qualifying clauses and phrases, a restatement of the subject (light, from v. 20) is required.
n1800387: sn After speaking of people in general (in the plural in vv. 21 and 22), Job returns to himself specifically (in the singular, using the same word вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО [gever, вАЬa manвАЭ] that he employed of himself in v. 3). He is the man whose way is hidden. The clear path of his former life has been broken off, or as the next clause says, hedged in so that he is confined to a life of suffering. The statement includes the spiritual perplexities that this involves. It is like saying that God is leading him in darkness and he can no longer see where he is going.
n1800388: tn The LXX translated вАЬto a man whose way is hiddenвАЭ with the vague paraphrase вАЬdeath is rest to [such] a man.вАЭ The translators apparently combined the reference to вАЬthe graveвАЭ in the previous verse with вАЬhiddenвАЭ
n1800389: tn The verb is the Hiphil of вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (sakhakh, вАЬto hedge inвАЭ). The key parallel passage is Job 19:8, which says, вАЬHe has blocked [вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„®вАО, gadar] my way so I cannot pass, and has set darkness over my paths.вАЭ To be hedged in is an implied metaphor, indicating that the pathway is concealed and enclosed. There is an irony in JobвАЩs choice of words in light of SatanвАЩs accusation in 1:10. It is heightened further when the same verb is employed by God in 38:8 (see F. I. Andersen, Job [TOTC], 109).
n1800390: tn For the prepositional вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne), the temporal meaning вАЬbeforeвАЭ (вАЬmy sighing comes before I eatвАЭ) makes very little sense here (as the versions have it). The meaning вАЬin place of, forвАЭ fits better (see 1 Sam 1:16, вАЬcount not your handmaid for a daughter of BelialвАЭ).
n1800391: sn The line means that JobвАЩs sighing, which results from the suffering (metonymy of effect) is his constant, daily food. Parallels like Ps 42:3 which says вАЬmy tears have been my bread/foodвАЭ show a similar figure.
n1800392: tn The word normally describes the вАЬroaringвАЭ of a lion (Job 4:10), but it is used for the loud groaning or cries of those in distress (Pss 22:1; 32:3).
n1800393: tn This second colon is paraphrased in the LXX to say, вАЬI weep being beset with terror.вАЭ The idea of вАЬpouring forth waterвАЭ while groaning can be represented by вАЬI weep.вАЭ The word вАЬfear, terrorвАЭ anticipates the next verse.
n1800394: tn The construction uses the cognate accusative with the verb: вАЬthe fear I feared,вАЭ or вАЬthe dread thing I dreadedвАЭ (вАП„§÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„У „§÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„У÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО, fakhad pakhadti). The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad) has the sense of вАЬdreadвАЭ and the noun the meaning вАЬthing dreaded.вАЭ The structure of the sentence with the perfect verb followed by the preterite indicates that the first action preceded the secondвАФhe feared something but then it happened. Some commentaries suggest reading this as a conditional clause followed by the present tense translation: вАЬIf I fear a thing it happens to meвАЭ (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 24). The reason for this change is that it is hard for some to think that in his prime Job had such fears. He did have a pure trust and confidence in the Lord (16:19; 29:18ff). But on the other hand, he did make sacrifices for his sons because he thought they might sin. There is evidence to suggest that he was aware that calamity could strike, and this is not necessarily incompatible with trust.
n1800395: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО ( Њatah) is Aramaic and is equivalent to the Hebrew verb вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬcome, happenвАЭ).
n1800396: tn The final verb is вАП„Щ÷Є„С÷є„РвАО (yavo Њ, вАЬhas comeвАЭ). It appears to be an imperfect, but since it is parallel to the preterite of the first colon it should be given that nuance here. Of course, if the other view of the verse is taken, then this would simply be translated as вАЬcomes,вАЭ and the preceding preterite also given an English present tense translation.
n1800397: tn The LXX вАЬpeaceвАЭ bases its rendering on вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (shalam) and not вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (shalah), which retains the original vav (вАП„ХвАО). The verb means вАЬto be quiet, to be at ease.вАЭ
n1800398: tn The verb is literally вАЬand I do/can not rest.вАЭ A potential perfect nuance fits this passage well. The word вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh, вАЬrestвАЭ) implies вАЬrestвАЭ in every sense, especially in contrast to вАП„®÷є„Т÷ґ„ЦвАО (rogez, вАЬturmoil, agitationвАЭ [vv. 26 and 17]).
n1800399: tn The last clause simply has вАЬand trouble came.вАЭ Job is essentially saying that since the trouble has come upon him there is not a moment of rest and relief.
n180041: sn The speech of Eliphaz can be broken down into three main sections. In 4:1-11 he wonders that Job who had comforted so many people in trouble, and who was so pious, should fall into such despair, forgetting the great truth that the righteous never perish under afflictionвАФcalamity only destroys the wicked. Then in 4:12-5:7 Eliphaz tries to warn Job about complaining against God because only the ungodly resent the dealings of God and by their impatience bring down his wrath upon them. Finally in 5:8-27 Eliphaz appeals to Job to follow a different course, to seek after God, for God only smites to heal or to correct, to draw people to himself and away from evil. See K. Fullerton, вАЬDouble Entendre in the First Speech of Eliphaz,вАЭ JBL 49 (1930): 320-74; J. C. L. Gibson, вАЬEliphaz the Temanite: A Portrait of a Hebrew Philosopher,вАЭ SJT 28 (1975): 259-72; and J. Lust, вАЬA Stormy Vision: Some Remarks on Job 4:12-16,вАЭ Bijdr 36 (1975): 308-11.
n180043: tn The verb has no expressed subject, and so may be translated with вАЬoneвАЭ or вАЬsomeone.вАЭ
n180044: tn The Piel perfect is difficult here. It would normally be translated вАЬhas one tried (words with you)?вАЭ Most commentaries posit a conditional clause, however.
n180045: tn The verb means вАЬto be weary.вАЭ But it can have the extended sense of being either exhausted or impatient (see v. 5). A. B. Davidson (Job, 29) takes it in the sense of вАЬwill it be too much for you?вАЭ There is nothing in the sentence that indicates this should be an interrogative clause; it is simply an imperfect. But in view of the juxtaposition of the first part, this seems to make good sense. E. Dhorme (Job, 42) has вАЬShall we address you? You are dejected.вАЭ
n180046: tn The construction uses a noun with the preposition: вАЬand to refrain with wordsвАФwho is able?вАЭ The Aramaic plural of вАЬwordsвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷і„Ь÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО, millin) occurs 13 times in Job, with the Hebrew plural ten times. The commentaries show that EliphazвАЩs speech had a distinctly Aramaic coloring to it.
n180047: tn The deictic particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) summons attention; it has the sense of вАЬconsider, look.вАЭ
n180048: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„®вАО (yasar) in the Piel means вАЬto correct,вАЭ whether by words with the sense of teach, or by chastening with the sense of punish, discipline. The double meaning of вАЬteachвАЭ and вАЬdisciplineвАЭ is also found with the noun вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО (musar).
n180049: tn The parallelism again uses a perfect verb in the first colon and an imperfect in the second, but since the sense of the line is clearly what Job has done in the past, the second verb may be treated as a preterite, or a customary imperfectвАФwhat Job repeatedly did in the past (GKC 315 ¬І107.e). The words in this verse may have double meanings. The word вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„®вАО (yasar, вАЬteach, disciplineвАЭ) may have the idea of instruction and correction, but also the connotation of strength (see Y. Hoffmann, вАЬThe Use of Equivocal Words in the First Speech of Eliphaz [Job IVвАУV],вАЭ VT 30 [1980]: 114-19).
n1800410: tn The вАЬfeeble handsвАЭ are literally вАЬhands hanging down.вАЭ This is a sign of weakness, helplessness, or despondency (see 2 Sam 4:1; Isa 13:7).
n1800411: tn Both verbs in this line are imperfects, and probably carry the same nuance as the last verb in v. 3, namely, either customary imperfect or preterite. The customary has the aspect of stressing that this was what Job used to do.
n1800412: tn The form is the singular active participle, interpreted here collectively. The verb is used of knees that give way (Isa 35:3; Ps 109:24).
n1800413:
tn The expression is often translated as вАЬfeeble knees,вАЭ but it literally says вАЬthe bowing [or вАЬtotteringвАЭ] knees.вАЭ The figure is one who may be under a heavy load whose knees begin to shake and buckle (see also Heb 12:12).
sn Job had been successful at helping others not be crushed by the weight of trouble and misfortune. It is easier to help others than to preserve a proper perspective when oneвАЩs self is afflicted (E. Dhorme, Job, 44).
n1800414: tn The sentence has no subject, but the context demands that the subject be the same kind of trouble that has come upon people that Job has helped.
n1800415: tn This is the same verb used in v. 2, meaning вАЬto be exhaustedвАЭ or вАЬimpatient.вАЭ Here with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive the verb describes JobвАЩs state of mind that is a consequence of the trouble coming on him. In this sentence the form is given a present tense translation (see GKC 329 ¬І111.t).
n1800416: tn This final verb in the verse is vivid; it means вАЬto terrify, dismayвАЭ (here the Niphal preterite). Job will go on to speak about all the terrors that come on him.
n1800417: tn The word вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (yir Њah, вАЬfearвАЭ) in this passage refers to JobвАЩs fear of the Lord, his reverential devotion to God. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 46) says that on the lips of Eliphaz the word almost means вАЬyour religion.вАЭ He refers to MoffattвАЩs translation, вАЬLet your religion reassure you.вАЭ
n1800418: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„°÷∞„Ь÷Є„™÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (kislatekha, вАЬyour confidenceвАЭ) is rendered in the LXX by вАЬfounded in folly.вАЭ The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„°÷ґ„ЬвАО (kesel) is вАЬconfidenceвАЭ (see 8:14) and elsewhere вАЬfolly.вАЭ Since it is parallel to вАЬyour hopeвАЭ it must mean confidence here.
n1800419:
tn This second half of the verse simply has вАЬyour hope and the integrity of your ways.вАЭ The expression вАЬthe perfection of your waysвАЭ is parallel to вАЬyour fear,вАЭ and вАЬyour hopeвАЭ is parallel to вАЬyour confidence.вАЭ This sentence is an example of casus pendens or extraposition: вАЬas for your hope, it is the integrity of your waysвАЭ (see GKC 458 ¬І143.d).
sn Eliphaz is not being sarcastic to Job. He knows that Job is a God-fearing man who lives out his faith in life. But he also knows that Job should apply to himself the same things he tells others.
n1800420: sn Eliphaz will put his thesis forward first negatively and then positively (vv. 8ff). He will argue that the suffering of the righteous is disciplinary and not for their destruction. He next will argue that it is the wicked who deserve judgment.
n1800421: tn The use of the independent personal pronoun is emphatic, almost as an enclitic to emphasize interrogatives: вАЬwho indeedвА¶.вАЭ (GKC 442 ¬І136.c).
n1800422: tn The perfect verb in this line has the nuance of the past tense to express the unique pastвАФthe uniqueness of the action is expressed with вАЬeverвАЭ (вАЬwho has ever perishedвАЭ).
n1800423: tn The adjective is used here substantivally. Without the article the word stresses the meaning of вАЬuprightness.вАЭ Job will use вАЬinnocentвАЭ and вАЬuprightвАЭ together in 17:8.
n1800424: tn The Niphal means вАЬto be hiddenвАЭ (see the Piel in 6:10; 15:18; and 27:11); the connotation here is вАЬdestroyedвАЭ or вАЬannihilated.вАЭ
n1800425: tn The perfect verb here represents the indefinite past. It has no specific sighting in mind, but refers to each time he has seen the wicked do this.
n1800426: sn The figure is an implied metaphor. Plowing suggests the idea of deliberately preparing (or cultivating) life for evil. This describes those who are fundamentally wicked.
n1800427: tn The LXX renders this with a plural вАЬbarren places.вАЭ
n1800429: tn The LXX in the place of вАЬbreathвАЭ has вАЬwordвАЭ or вАЬcommand,вАЭ probably to limit the anthropomorphism. The word is вАП„Ю÷і„†÷Љ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Ј„™вАО (minnishmat) comprising вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) + вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Ј„™вАО (nishmat, the construct of вАП„†÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО [neshamah]): вАЬfrom/at the breath of.вАЭ The вАЬbreath of GodвАЭ occurs frequently in Scripture.
