In Gen 2:7 it imparts life, but here it destroys it. The figure probably does indicate a divine decree from God (e.g., вАЬdepart from meвАЭ)вАФso the LXX may have been simply interpreting.
n1800430: sn The statement is saying that if some die by misfortune it is because divine retribution or anger has come upon them. This is not necessarily the case, as the NT declares (see Luke 13:1-5).
n1800431: tn The word вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) is now parallel to вАП„†÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (neshamah); both can mean вАЬbreathвАЭ or вАЬwind.вАЭ To avoid using вАЬbreathвАЭ for both lines, вАЬblastвАЭ has been employed here. The word is followed by вАП„Р÷Ј„§÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( Њappo) which could be translated вАЬhis angerвАЭ or вАЬhis nostril.вАЭ If вАЬnostrilвАЭ is retained, then it is a very bold anthropomorphism to indicate the fuming wrath of God. It is close to the picture of the hot wind coming off the desert to scorch the plants (see Hos 13:15).
n1800432: tn вАЬThere isвАЭ has been supplied to make a smoother translation out of the clauses.
n1800433: sn Eliphaz takes up a new image here to make the point that the wicked are destroyedвАФthe breaking up and scattering of a den of lions. There are several words for вАЬlionвАЭ used in this section. D. J. A. Clines observes that it is probably impossible to distinguish them (Job [WBC], 109, 110, which records some bibliography of those who have tried to work on the etymologies and meanings). The first is вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Щ÷µ„ФвАО ( Њaryeh) the generic term for вАЬlion.вАЭ It is followed by вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (shakhal) which, like вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„§÷і„Щ„®вАО (kefir), is a вАЬyoung lion.вАЭ Some have thought that the вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (shakhal) is a lion-like animal, perhaps a panther or leopard. KBL takes it by metathesis from Arabic вАЬyoung one.вАЭ The LXX for this verse has вАЬthe strength of the lion, and the voice of the lioness and the exulting cry of serpents are quenched.вАЭ
n1800435: tn The verb belongs to the subject вАЬteethвАЭ in this last colon, but it is used by zeugma (a figure of speech in which one word is made to refer to two or more other words, but has to be understood differently in the different contexts) of the three subjects (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 46-47).
n1800436: tn The word вАП„Ь÷Ј„Щ÷і„©„БвАО (layish) traditionally rendered вАЬstrong lion,вАЭ occurs only here and in Prov 30:30 and Isa 30:6. It has cognates in several of the Semitic languages, and so seems to indicate lion as king of the beasts.
n1800437: tn The form of the verb is the Qal active participle; it stresses the characteristic action of the verb as if a standard universal truth.
n1800438: tn The text literally has вАЬsons of the lioness.вАЭ
n1800439: tn The LXX of this verse offers special problems. It reads, вАЬBut if there had been any truth in your words, none of these evils would have fallen upon you; shall not my ear receive excellent [information] from him?вАЭ The major error involves a dittography from the word for вАЬsecret,вАЭ yielding вАЬtruth.вАЭ
n1800440: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„†÷Ј„СвАО (ganav) means вАЬto steal.вАЭ The Pual form in this verse is probably to be taken as a preterite since it requires a past tense translation: вАЬit was stolen for meвАЭ meaning it was brought to me stealthily (see 2 Sam 19:3).
n1800442: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷µ„Ю÷ґ„•вАО (shemets, вАЬwhisperвАЭ) is found only here and in Job 26:14. A cognate form вАП„©„Б÷і„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„ФвАО (shimtsah) is found in Exod 32:25 with the sense of вАЬa whisper.вАЭ In postbiblical Hebrew the word comes to mean вАЬa little.вАЭ The point is that Eliphaz caught just a bit, just a whisper of it, and will recount it to Job.
n1800443: tn Here too the word is rare. The form вАП„©„В÷∞„Ґ÷і„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (se Њippim, вАЬdisquietingsвАЭ) occurs only here and in 20:2. The form вАП„©„В÷Ј„®÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sar њappim, вАЬdisquieting thoughtsвАЭ), possibly related by dissimilation, occurs in Pss 94:19 and 139:23. There seems to be a connection with вАП„°÷∞„Ґ÷і„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (se њippim) in 1 Kgs 18:21 with the meaning вАЬdivided opinionвАЭ; this is related to the idea of вАП„°÷∞„Ґ÷і„§÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (se њippah, вАЬboughвАЭ). H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 47) concludes that the point is that like branches the thoughts lead off into different and bewildering places. E. Dhorme (Job, 50) links the word to an Arabic root (вАЬto be passionately smittenвАЭ) for the idea of вАЬintimate thoughts.вАЭ The idea here and in Ps 139 has more to do with anxious, troubling, disquieting thoughts, as in a nightmare.
n1800445: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„У÷Љ÷µ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (tardemah) is a вАЬdeep sleep.вАЭ It is used in the creation account when the Lord God caused a deep sleep to fall on Adam; and it is used in the story of Jonah when the prophet was asleep during the storm. The LXX interprets it to mean вАЬfear,вАЭ rendering the whole verse вАЬbut terror falls upon men with dread and a sound in the night.вАЭ
n1800446: tn The two words вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad, вАЬdreadвАЭ) and вАП„®÷∞„Ґ÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (re њadah, вАЬtremblingвАЭ) strengthen each other as synonyms (see also Ps 55:6).
n1800447: tn The subject of the Hiphil verb вАП„Ф÷і„§÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ„УвАО (hifkhid, вАЬdreadвАЭ) is вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad, вАЬtremblingвАЭ), which is why it is in the singular. The cognate verb intensifies and applies the meaning of the noun. BDB 808 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО Hiph translates it вАЬfill my bones with dread.вАЭ In that sense вАЬbonesвАЭ would have to be a metonymy of subject representing the framework of the body, so that the meaning is that his whole being was filled with trembling.
n1800448: tn The word вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) can be вАЬspiritвАЭ or вАЬbreath.вАЭ The implication here is that it was something that Eliphaz feltвАФwhat he saw follows in v. 16. The commentators are divided on whether this is an apparition, a spirit, or a breath. The word can be used in either the masculine or the feminine, and so the gender of the verb does not favor the meaning вАЬspirit.вАЭ In fact, in Isa 21:1 the same verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО (khalaf, вАЬpass on, throughвАЭ) is used with the subject being a strong wind or hurricane вАЬblowing across.вАЭ It may be that such a wind has caused EliphazвАЩs hair to stand on end here. D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 111) also concludes it means вАЬwind,вАЭ noting that in Job a spirit or spirits would be called вАП„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (refa Њim), вАП„Р÷ґ„Ь÷є„Ф÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њelohim) or вАП„Р„Х÷є„СвАО ( Њov).
n1800449: tn The verbs in this verse are imperfects. In the last verse the verbs were perfects when Eliphaz reported the fear that seized him. In this continuation of the report the description becomes vivid with the change in verbs, as if the experience were in progress.
n1800450: tn The subject of this verb is also вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh, вАЬspiritвАЭ), since it can assume either gender. The вАЬhair of my fleshвАЭ is the complement and not the subject; therefore the Piel is to be retained and not changed to a Qal as some suggest (and compare with Ps 119:120).
n1800451: tc The LXX has the first person of the verb: вАЬI arose and perceived it not, I looked and there was no form before my eyes, but I only heard a breath and a voice.вАЭ
n1800452: tn The imperfect verb is to be classified as potential imperfect. Eliphaz is unable to recognize the figure standing before him.
n1800453: sn The colon reads вАЬa silence and a voice I hear.вАЭ Some have rendered it вАЬthere is a silence, and then I hear.вАЭ The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (damam) does mean вАЬremain silentвАЭ (Job 29:21; 31:34) and then also вАЬcease.вАЭ The noun вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (demamah, вАЬcalmвАЭ) refers to the calm after the storm in Ps 107:29. Joined with the true object of the verb, вАЬvoice,вАЭ it probably means something like stillness or murmuring or whispering here. It is joined to вАЬvoiceвАЭ with a conjunction, indicating that it is a hendiadys, вАЬmurmur and a voiceвАЭ or a вАЬmurmuring voice.вАЭ
n1800454: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse express obvious truths known at all times (GKC 315 ¬І107.f).
n1800455: tn The word for man here is first вАП„Р÷±„†„Х÷є„©„БвАО ( Њenosh), stressing man in all his frailty, his mortality. This is paralleled with вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever), a word that would stress more of the strength or might of man. The verse is not making a great contrast between the two, but it is rhetorical question merely stating that no human being of any kind is righteous or pure before God the Creator. See H. Kosmala, вАЬThe Term geber in the OT and in the Scrolls,вАЭ VTSup 17 (1969): 159-69; and E. Jacob, Theology of the Old Testament, 156-57.
n1800456: tn The imperfect verb in this interrogative sentence could also be interpreted with a potential nuance: вАЬCan a man be righteous?вАЭ
n1800457: tn The classification of вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) as a comparative in this verse (NIV, вАЬmore righteous than GodвАЭ; cf. also KJV, ASV, NCV) does not seem the most probable. The idea of someone being more righteous than God is too strong to be reasonable. Job will not do thatвАФbut he will imply that God is unjust. In addition, Eliphaz had this vision before hearing of JobвАЩs trouble and so is not addressing the idea that Job is making himself more righteous than God. He is stating that no man is righteous before God. Verses 18-21 will show that no one can claim righteousness before God. In 9:2 and 25:4 the preposition вАЬwithвАЭ is used. See also Jer 51:5 where the preposition should be rendered вАЬbeforeвАЭ [the Holy One].
n1800458: sn In Job 15:14 and 25:4 the verb вАП„Щ÷і„Ц÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (yizkeh, from вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО [zakhah, вАЬbe cleanвАЭ]) is paralleled with вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷∞„У÷Љ÷Ј„ІвАО (yitsdaq, from вАП„¶÷Є„У÷µ„ІвАО [tsadeq, вАЬbe righteousвАЭ).
n1800459: tn The double question here merely repeats the same question with different words (see GKC 475 ¬І150.h). The second member could just as well have been connected with вАП„ХвАО (vav).
n1800460: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷µ„ЯвАО (hen) introduces a conditional clause here, although the older translations used вАЬbehold.вАЭ The clause forms the foundation for the point made in the next verse, an argument by analogyвАФif this be true, then how much more/less the other.
n1800461: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1800462: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„Р÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (ya Њamin), a Hiphil imperfect from вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman) followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet), means вАЬtrust in.вАЭ
n1800463: sn The servants here must be angels in view of the parallelism. The Targum to Job interpreted them to be the prophets. In the book we have already read about the вАЬsons of GodвАЭ who take their stand as servants before the Lord (1:6; 2:1), and Ps 104:4 identifies the angels as attendants (using вАП„©„Б÷Є„®÷Ј„™вАО, sharat).
n1800464: tn The verb вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim, вАЬsetвАЭ) with the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) has the sense of вАЬimputeвАЭ or вАЬattribute something to someone.вАЭ
n1800465: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ф÷≥„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (toholah) is a hapax legomenon, and so has created some confusion in the various translations. It seems to mean вАЬerror; folly.вАЭ The word is translated вАЬperversenessвАЭ in the LXX, but Symmachus connects it with the word for вАЬmadness.вАЭ Some commentators have repointed the word to вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ф÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tehillah, вАЬpraiseвАЭ) making the line read: вАЬhe finds no [cause for] praise in his angels.вАЭ Others suggest вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (tiflah, вАЬoffensiveness, sillinessвАЭ) a bigger change; this matches the idiom in Job 24:12. But if the etymology of the word is вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (halal, вАЬto be madвАЭ) then that change is not necessary. The feminine noun вАЬmadnessвАЭ still leaves the meaning of the line a little uncertain: вАЬ[if] he does not impute madness to his angels.вАЭ The point of the verse is that God finds flaws in his angels and does not put his trust in them.
n1800466: sn Those who live in houses of clay are human beings, for the human body was made of clay (Job 10:9; 33:6; and Isa 64:7). In 2 Cor 4:7 the body is an вАЬearthen vesselвАЭвАФa clay pot. The verse continues the analogy: houses have foundations, and the house of clay is founded on dust, and will return to dust (Gen 3:19; Ps 103:14). The reasoning is that if God finds defects in angels, he will surely find them in humans who are inferior to the angels because they are but dust. In fact, they are easily crushed like the moth.
n1800467: tn The imperfect verb is in the plural, suggesting вАЬthey crush.вАЭ But since there is no subject expressed, the verb may be given an impersonal subject, or more simply, treated as a passive (see GKC 460 ¬І144.g).
n1800468: tn The prepositional compound вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne) normally has the sense of вАЬbefore,вАЭ but it has been used already in 3:24 in the sense of вАЬlike.вАЭ That is the most natural meaning of this line. Otherwise, the interpretation must offer some explanation of a comparison between how quickly a moth and a human can be crushed. There are suggestions for different readings here; see for example G. R. Driver, вАЬLinguistic and Textual Problems: Jeremiah,вАЭ JQR 28 (1937/38): 97-129 for a change to вАЬbirdвАЩs nestвАЭ; and J. A. Rimbach, вАЬвАШCrushed before the MothвАЩ (Job 4:19),вАЭ JBL 100 (1981): 244-46, for a change of the verb to вАЬthey are pure before their Maker.вАЭ However, these are unnecessary emendations.
n1800469: tn The form вАП„Щ÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yukkattu) is the Hophal imperfect of the root вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„™÷Ј„™вАО (katat, вАЬto be pounded, pulverized, reduced to ashesвАЭ [Jer 46:5; Mic 1:7]). It follows the Aramaic formation (see GKC 182 ¬І67.y). This line appears to form a parallelism with вАЬthey are crushed like a moth,вАЭ the third unit of the last verse, but it has its own parallel idea in this verse. See D. J. A. Clines, вАЬVerb Modality and the Interpretation of Job 4:20, 21, вАЭ VT 30 (1980): 354-57.
n1800470: tn Or вАЬfrom morning to evening.вАЭ The expression вАЬfrom morning to eveningвАЭ is probably not a merism, but rather describes the time between the morning and the evening, as in Isa 38:12: вАЬfrom day to night you make an end of me.вАЭ
n1800471: sn The second colon expresses the consequence of this day-long reducing to ashesвАФthey perish forever! (see 20:7 and 14:20).
n1800472: tn This rendering is based on the interpretation that вАП„Ю÷і„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ „Ю÷µ„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mibbeli mesim) uses the Hiphil participle of вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim, вАЬsetвАЭ) with an understood object вАЬheartвАЭ to gain the idiom of вАЬtaking to heart, considering, regarding itвАЭвАФhence, вАЬwithout anyone regarding it.вАЭ Some commentators have attempted to resolve the difficulty by emending the text, a procedure that has no more support than positing the ellipses. One suggested emendation does have the LXX in its favor, namely, a reading of вАП„Ю÷є„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (moshia њ, вАЬone who savesвАЭ) in place of вАП„Ю÷µ„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mesim, вАЬone who setsвАЭ). This would lead to вАЬwithout one who saves they perish foreverвАЭ (E. Dhorme, Job, 55).
n1800473: tn The word вАП„Щ÷ґ„™÷ґ„®вАО (yeter, here with the suffix, вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„®÷Є„ЭвАО [yitram]) can mean вАЬwhat remainsвАЭ or вАЬrope.вАЭ Of the variety of translations, the most frequently used idea seems to be вАЬtheir rope,вАЭ meaning their tent cord. This would indicate that their life was compared to a tentвАФperfectly reasonable in a passage that has already used the image вАЬhouses of clay.вАЭ The difficulty is that the verb вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ҐвАО (nasa њ) means more properly вАЬto tear up; to uprootвАЭ and not вАЬto cut off.вАЭ A similar idea is found in Isa 38:12, but there the image is explicitly that of cutting the life off from the loom. Some have posited that the original must have said вАЬtheir tent peg was pulled upвАЭ as in Isa 33:20 (A. B. Davidson, Job, 34; cf. NAB). But perhaps the idea of вАЬwhat remainsвАЭ would be easier to defend here. Besides, it is used in 22:20. The wealth of an individual is what has been acquired and usually is left over when he dies. Here it would mean that the superfluous wealth would be snatched away. The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) would carry the meaning вАЬfromвАЭ with this verb.
n1800474:
tc The text of the LXX does not seem to be connected to the Hebrew of v. 21a. It reads something like вАЬfor he blows on them and they are witheredвАЭ (see Isa 40:24b). The Targum to Job has вАЬIs it not by their lack of righteousness that they have been deprived of all support?вАЭ
tn On the interpretation of the preposition in this construction, see N. Sarna, вАЬThe Interchange of the Preposition bet and min in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ JBL 78 (1959): 310-16.
n1800475: sn They die. This clear verb interprets all the images in these versesвАФthey die. When the house of clay collapses, or when their excess perishesвАФtheir life is over.
n1800476:
tn Heb вАЬand without wisdom.вАЭ The word вАЬattainingвАЭ is supplied in the translation as a clarification.
sn The expression without attaining wisdom is parallel to the previous without anyone regarding it. Both verses describe how easily humans perish: there is no concern for it, nor any sense to it. Humans die without attaining wisdom which can solve the mystery of human life.
n180051:
tn Some commentators transpose this verse with the following paragraph, placing it after v. 7 (see E. Dhorme, Job, 62). But the reasons for this are based on the perceived development of the argument and are not that compelling.
sn The imperative is here a challenge for Job. If he makes his appeal against God, who is there who will listen? The rhetorical questions are intended to indicate that no one will respond, not even the angels. Job would do better to realize that he is guilty and his only hope is in God.
n180052: tn The participle with the suffix could be given a more immediate translation to accompany the imperative: вАЬCall now! Is anyone listening to you?вАЭ
n180053: tn The LXX has rendered вАЬholy onesвАЭ as вАЬholy angelsвАЭ (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT). The LXX has interpreted the verb in the colon too freely: вАЬif you will see.вАЭ
n180054: sn The point being made is that the angels do not represent the cries of people to God as if mediating for them. But if Job appealed to any of them to take his case against God, there would be no response whatsoever for that.
n180055: tn One of the reasons that commentators transpose v. 1 is that the вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki, вАЬforвАЭ) here seems to follow 4:21 better. If people die without wisdom, it is folly that kills them. But the verse also makes sense after 5:1. He is saying that complaining against God will not bring deliverance (v. 1), but rather, by such impatience the fool will bring greater calamity on himself.
n180056: tn The two words for вАЬfoolish personвАЭ are common in wisdom literature. The first, вАП„Р÷±„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevil), is the fool who is a senseless person; the вАП„§÷Љ÷є„™÷ґ„ФвАО (poteh) is the naive and silly person, the simpleton, the one who is easily led astray. The direct object is introduced with the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) in this verse (see GKC 366 ¬І117.n).
n180057: tn The two parallel nouns are similar; their related verbs are also paralleled in Deut 32:16 with the idea of вАЬvexвАЭ and вАЬirritate.вАЭ The first word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„©„ВвАО (ka њas) refers to the inner irritation and anger one feels, whereas the second word вАП„І÷і„†÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (qin Њah) refers to the outward expression of the anger. In Job 6:2, Job will respond вАЬO that my impatience (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„©„ВвАО) were weighedвА¶.вАЭ
n180058: tn The use of the pronoun here adds emphasis to the subject of the sentence (see GKC 437 ¬І135.a).
n180059: tn This word is вАП„Р÷±„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevil), the same word for the вАЬsenseless manвАЭ in the preceding verse. Eliphaz is citing an example of his principle just givenвАФhe saw such a fool for a brief while appearing to prosper (i.e., taking root).
n1800510: tn A. B. Davidson argues that the verse does not mean that Eliphaz cursed his place during his prosperity. This line is metonymical (giving the effect). God judged the fool and his place was ruined; consequently, Eliphaz pronounced it accursed of God (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 36). Many emend the verb slightly to read вАЬand it was suddenly cursedвАЭ (вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷ї„Ы÷Ј„СвАО [vayyukhav] instead of вАП„Х÷Є„Р÷ґ„І÷Љ„Х÷є„СвАО [va Њeqqov]; see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 51).
n1800511: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse describe the condition of the accursed situation. Some commentators follow the LXX and take these as jussives, making this verse the curse that the man pronounced upon the fool. Rashi adds вАЬThis is the malediction with which I have cursed him.вАЭ That would make the speaker the one calling down the judgment on the fool rather than responding by observation how God destroyed the habitation of the fool.
n1800512: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Р„Х÷ЉвАО (yiddakke Њu) could be taken as the passive voice, or in the reciprocal sense (вАЬcrush one anotherвАЭ) or reflexive (вАЬcrush themselvesвАЭ). The context favors the idea that the children of the foolish person will be destroyed because there is no one who will deliver them.
n1800513: tn Heb вАЬin the gate.вАЭ The city gate was the place of both business and justice. The sense here seems to fit the usage of gates as the place of legal disputes, so the phrase вАЬat the place of judgmentвАЭ has been used in the translation.
n1800514: tn The text simply says вАЬand there is no deliverer.вАЭ The entire clause could be subordinated to the preceding clause, and rendered simply вАЬwithout a deliverer.вАЭ
n1800515: sn The hungry are other people, possibly the hungry poor to whom the wealthy have refused to give bread (22:7). The sons are so helpless that even the poor take their property.
n1800516: tn The MT reads вАЬwhose harvest the hungry eat up.вАЭ Some commentators want to follow the LXX and repoint вАП„І÷∞„¶÷і„Щ„®„Х÷євАО (qetsiro, вАЬhis harvestвАЭ) to вАП„І÷Є„¶÷∞„®„Х÷ЉвАО (qatseru, вАЬ[what] they have reapedвАЭ; cf. NAB). The reference as it stands in the MT seems to be to the image of taking root in v. 3; whatever took rootвАФthe prosperity of his lifeвАФwill not belong to him or his sons to enjoy. If the emendation is accepted, then the reference would be immediately to the вАЬsonsвАЭ in the preceding verse.
n1800517: tn The line is difficult; the Hebrew text reads literally вАЬand unto from thorns he takes it.вАЭ The idea seems to be that even from within an enclosed hedge of thorns other people will take the harvest. Many commentators either delete the line altogether or try to repoint it to make more sense out of it. G. R. Driver had taken the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬtowardsвАЭ) as the noun вАП„Р÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬstrong manвАЭ) and the noun вАП„¶÷і„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsinnim, вАЬthornsвАЭ) connected to Aramaic вАП„¶÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tsinnah, вАЬbasketвАЭ); he read it as вАЬa strong man snatches it from the basketsвАЭ (G. R. Driver, вАЬon Job 5:5, вАЭ TZ 12 [1956]: 485-86). E. Dhorme (Job, 60) changed the word slightly to вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷∞„§÷Љ÷ї„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (matspunim, вАЬhiding placesвАЭ), instead of вАП„Ю÷і„¶÷Љ÷і„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mitsinnim, вАЬout of the thornsвАЭ), to get the translation вАЬand unto hiding places he carries it.вАЭ This fits the use of the verb вАП„Ь÷Є„І÷Ј„ЧвАО (laqakh, вАЬto takeвАЭ) with the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬtowardsвАЭ) meaning вАЬcarry toвАЭ someplace. There seems to be no easy solution to the difficulty of the line.
n1800518: tn The word вАП„¶÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsammim) has been traditionally rendered вАЬrobbers.вАЭ But it has been connected by some of the ancient versions to the word for вАЬthirst,вАЭ making a nice parallel with вАЬhungry.вАЭ This would likely be pointed вАП„¶÷∞„Ю÷µ„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tseme Њim).
n1800519: tn The verb has been given many different renderings, some more radical than others: вАЬpant,вАЭ вАЬengulf,вАЭ вАЬdraws,вАЭ вАЬgather,вАЭ вАЬswallowвАЭ (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 53). The idea of вАЬswallowingвАЭ wealth is found in Job 20:15 though with a different verb. The general sense of the line is clear, in spite of the difficulties of determining the exact meaning of the verb.
n1800520: tn The LXX has several variations for the line. It reads something like the following: вАЬfor what they have collected the just shall eat, but they shall not be delivered out of calamities; let their strength be utterly exhausted.вАЭ The LXX may have gotten the idea of the вАЬrighteousвАЭ as those who suffer from hunger. Instead of вАЬthornsвАЭ the LXX has the idea of вАЬtrouble.вАЭ The Targum to Job interprets it with вАЬshieldвАЭ and adds вАЬwarriorsвАЭ as the subject.
n1800521: sn The previous discussion shows how trouble rises, namely, from the rebelliousness of the fool. Here Eliphaz simply summarizes the points made with this general principleвАФtrouble does not come from outside man, nor does it come as a part of the natural order, but rather it comes from the evil nature of man.
n1800522: tn Heb вАЬman [is].вАЭ Because вАЬmanвАЭ is used in a generic sense for humanity here, the generic вАЬpeopleвАЭ has been used in the translation.
n1800523: tn There is a slight difficulty here in that vv. 6 and 7 seem to be saying the opposite thing. Many commentators, therefore, emend the Niphal вАП„Щ„Х÷Љ„Ь÷Љ÷Є„УвАО (yullad, вАЬis bornвАЭ) to an active participle вАП„Щ„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„УвАО (yoled, вАЬbegetsвАЭ) to place the source of trouble in man himself. But the LXX seems to retain the passive idea: вАЬman is born to trouble.вАЭ The contrast between the two verses does not seem too difficult, for it still could imply that troubleвАЩs source is within the man.
n1800524: tn For the Hebrew вАП„С÷∞„†÷µ„Щ÷Њ„®÷ґ„©„Б÷ґ„£вАО (vene reshef, вАЬsons of the flameвАЭ) the present translation has the rendering вАЬsparks.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 62) thinks it refers to some kind of bird, but renders it вАЬsons of the lightningвАЭ because the eagle was associated with lightning in ancient interpretations. Sparks, he argues, do not soar high above the earth. Other suggestions include Resheph, the Phoenician god of lightning (Pope), the fire of passion (Buttenwieser), angels (Peake), or demons (Targum Job). None of these are convincing; the idea of sparks flying upward fits the translation well and makes clear sense in the passage.
n1800525: tn The simple translation of the last two words is вАЬfly highвАЭ or вАЬsoar aloftвАЭ which would suit the idea of an eagle. But, as H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 53) concludes, the argument to identify the expression preceding this with eagles is far-fetched.
n1800526: tn The LXX has the name of a bird here: вАЬthe vultureвАЩs young seek the high places.вАЭ The Targum to Job has вАЬsons of demonsвАЭ or вАЬthe sparks which shoot from coals of fire.вАЭ
n1800527: sn Eliphaz affirms that if he were in JobвАЩs place he would take refuge in God, but Job has to acknowledge that he has offended God and accept this suffering as his chastisement. Job eventually will submit to God in the end, but not in the way that Eliphaz advises here, for Job does not agree that the sufferings are judgments from God.
n1800528: tn The word вАП„Р„Х÷Љ„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њulam) is a strong adversative вАЬbut.вАЭ This forms the contrast with what has been said previously and so marks a new section.
n1800529: tn The independent personal pronoun here adds emphasis to the subject of the verb, again strengthening the contrast with what Job is doing (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 22, ¬І106).
n1800530: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse express not so much what Eliphaz does as what he would do if he were in JobвАЩs place (even though in 13:3 we have the affirmation). The use fits the category of the imperfect used in conditional clauses (see GKC 319 ¬І107.x).
n1800531: tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (darash, вАЬto seekвАЭ) followed by the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬtowardsвАЭ) has the meaning of addressing oneself to (God). See 8:19 and 40:10.
n1800532: tn The Hebrew employs вАП„Р÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њel) in the first line and вАП„Р÷±„Ь÷є„Ф÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њelohim) in the second for вАЬGod,вАЭ but the LXX uses ќЇѕНѕБќєќњѕВ (kurios, вАЬLordвАЭ) in both places in this verse. However, in the second colon it also has вАЬLord of all.вАЭ This is replaced in the Greek version of Aquila by ѕАќ±ќљѕДќњќЇѕБќђѕДѕЙѕБ (pantokrat≈Нr, traditionally translated вАЬAlmightyвАЭ). On the basis of this information, H. M. Orlinsky suggests that the second name for God in the verse should be вАЬShaddaiвАЭ (JQR 25 [1934/35]: 271).
n1800533: tn The Hebrew simply has вАЬmy word,вАЭ but in this expression that uses вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim) with the meaning of вАЬlay beforeвАЭ or вАЬexpound a causeвАЭ in a legal sense, вАЬcaseвАЭ or вАЬcauseвАЭ would be a better translation.
n1800534: tn Heb вАЬwho does.вАЭ It is common for such doxologies to begin with participles; they follow the pattern of the psalms in this style. Because of the length of the sentence in Hebrew and the conventions of English style, a new sentence was started here in the translation.
n1800535: tn The Hebrew has вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷µ„Щ„Я „Ч÷µ„І÷ґ„®вАО (ve Њen kheqer), literally, вАЬand no investigation.вАЭ The use of the conjunction on the expression follows a form of the circumstantial clause construction, and so the entire expression describes the great works as вАЬunsearchable.вАЭ
n1800536: tn The preposition in вАП„Ґ÷Ј„У÷Њ„Р÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО ( њad  Њen, вАЬuntil there was noвАЭ) is stereotypical; it conveys the sense of having no number (see Job 9:10; Ps 40:13).
n1800537: sn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 54) notes that the verse fits EliphazвАЩs approach very well, for he has good understanding of the truth, but has difficulty in making the correct conclusions from it.
n1800538:
tn Heb вАЬwho gives.вАЭ The participle continues the doxology here. But the article is necessary because of the distance between this verse and the reference to God.
sn He gives rain. The use of the verb вАЬgivesвАЭ underscores the idea that rain is a gift from God. This would be more keenly felt in the Middle East where water is scarce.
n1800539: tn In both halves of the verse the literal rendering would be вАЬupon the face of the earthвАЭ and вАЬupon the face of the fields.вАЭ
n1800540: tn The second participle is simply coordinated to the first and therefore does not need the definite article repeated (see GKC 404 ¬І126.b).
n1800541: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„¶„Х÷є„™вАО (khutsot) basically means вАЬoutside,вАЭ or what is outside. It could refer to streets if what is meant is outside the house, but it refers to fields here (parallel to the more general word) because it is outside the village. See Ps 144:13 for the use of the expression for вАЬcountryside.вАЭ The LXX gives a much wider interpretation: вАЬwhat is under heaven.вАЭ
n1800542: tn Heb вАЬsetting.вАЭ The infinitive construct clause is here taken as explaining the nature of God, and so parallel to the preceding descriptions. If read simply as a purpose clause after the previous verse, it would suggest that the purpose of watering the earth was to raise the humble (cf. NASB, вАЬAnd sends water on the fields, // So that He sets on high those who are lowlyвАЭ). A. B. Davidson (Job, 39) makes a case for this interpretation, saying that GodвАЩs gifts in nature have the wider purpose of blessing man, but he prefers to see the line as another benevolence, parallel to v. 10, and so suggests a translation вАЬsetting upвАЭ rather than вАЬto set up.вАЭ
n1800543: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷∞„§÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (shefalim) refers to вАЬthose who are down.вАЭ This refers to the lowly and despised of the earth. They are the opposite of the вАЬproudвАЭ (see Ps 138:6). Here there is a deliberate contrast between вАЬlowlyвАЭ and вАЬon high.вАЭ
n1800544: tn The meaning of the word is вАЬto be dark, dirtyвАЭ; therefore, it refers to the ash-sprinkled head of the mourner (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 54). The custom was to darken oneвАЩs face in sorrow (see Job 2:12; Pss 35:14; 38:7).
n1800545: tn The perfect verb may be translated вАЬbe set on high; be raised up.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 64) notes that the perfect is parallel to the infinitive of the first colon, and so he renders it in the same way as the infinitive, comparing the construction to that of 28:25.
n1800546: tn The Hiphil form вАП„Ю÷µ„§÷µ„®вАО (mefer) is the participle from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (parar, вАЬto annul; to frustrate; to breakвАЭ). It continues the doxological descriptions of God, but because of the numerous verses in this section, it may be clearer to start a new sentence with this form (rather than translating it вАЬwhoвА¶вАЭ).
n1800547: tn The word is related to the verb вАЬto think; to plan; to devise,вАЭ and so can mean вАЬthoughts; plans; imagination.вАЭ Here it refers to the plan of the crafty that must be frustrated (see also Isa 44:25 for the contrast).
n1800548: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷≤„®„Х÷Љ„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њarumim) means вАЬcraftyвАЭ or вАЬshrewd.вАЭ It describes the shrewdness of some to achieve their ends (see Gen 3:1, where the serpent is more cunning than all the creatures, that is, he knows where the dangers are and will attempt to bring down the innocent). In the next verse it describes the clever plans of the wiseвАФthose who are wise in their own sight.
n1800549: tn The consecutive clause showing result or purpose is simply introduced with the vav and the imperfect/jussive (see GKC 504-5 ¬І166.a).
n1800550: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„©„Б÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tushiyyah) is a technical word from wisdom literature. It has either the idea of the faculty of foresight, or of prudence in general (see 12:6; 26:3). It can be parallel in the texts to вАЬwisdom,вАЭ вАЬcounsel,вАЭ вАЬhelp,вАЭ or вАЬstrength.вАЭ Here it refers to what has been planned ahead of time.
n1800551: tn The participles continue the description of God. Here he captures or ensnares the wise in their wickedly clever plans. See also Ps 7:16, where the wicked are caught in the pit they have dugвАФthey are only wise in their own eyes.
n1800552: sn This is the only quotation from the book of Job in the NT (although Rom 11:35 seems to reflect 41:11, and Phil 1:19 is similar to 13:6). Paul cites it in 1 Cor 3:19.
n1800553: tn The etymology of вАП„†÷і„§÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (niftalim) suggests a meaning of вАЬtwistedвАЭ (see Prov 8:8) in the sense of tortuous. See Gen 30:8; Ps 18:26 [27].
n1800554: tn The Niphal of вАП„Ю÷Є„Ф÷Ј„®вАО (mahar) means вАЬto be hasty; to be irresponsible.вАЭ The meaning in the line may be understood in this sense: The counsel of the wily is hastened, that is, precipitated before it is ripe, i.e., frustrated (A. B. Davidson, Job, 39).
n1800555: sn God so confuses the crafty that they are unable to fulfill their plansвАФit is as if they encounter darkness in broad daylight. This is like the Syrians in 2 Kgs 6:18-23.
n1800556: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„©„БвАО (mashash) expresses the idea of groping about in the darkness. This is part of the fulfillment of Deut 28:29, which says, вАЬand you shall grope at noonday as the blind grope in darkness.вАЭ This image is also in Isa 59:10.
n1800557: sn The verse provides a picture of the frustration and bewilderment in the crafty who cannot accomplish their ends because God thwarts them.
n1800558: tn The verb, the Hiphil preterite of вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО (yasha њ, вАЬand he savesвАЭ) indicates that by frustrating the plans of the wicked God saves the poor. So the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive shows the result in the sequence of the verses.
n1800559: tn The juxtaposition of вАЬfrom the sword from their mouthвАЭ poses translation difficulties. Some mss do not have the preposition on вАЬtheir mouth,вАЭ but render the expression as a construct: вАЬfrom the sword of their mouth.вАЭ This would mean their tongue, and by metonymy, what they say. The expression вАЬfrom their mouthвАЭ corresponds well with вАЬfrom the handвАЭ in the next colon. And as E. Dhorme (Job, 67) notes, what is missing is a parallel in the first part with вАЬthe poorвАЭ in the second. So he follows Cappel in repointing вАЬfrom the swordвАЭ as a Hophal participle, вАП„Ю÷є„Ч÷≥„®÷Є„СвАО (mokhorav), meaning вАЬthe ruined.вАЭ If a change is required, this has the benefit of only changing the pointing. The difficulty with this is that the word вАЬdesolate, ruinedвАЭ is not used for people, but only to cities, lands, or mountains. The sense of the verse can be supported from the present pointing: вАЬfrom the sword [which comes] from their mouthвАЭ; the second phrase could also be in apposition, meaning, вАЬfrom the sword, i.e., from their mouth.вАЭ
n1800560: tn If the word вАЬpoorвАЭ is to do double duty, i.e., serving as the object of the verb вАЬsavesвАЭ in the first colon as well as the second, then the conjunction should be explanatory.
n1800561: tn Other translations render this вАЬinjusticeвАЭ (NIV, NRSV, CEV) or вАЬunrighteousnessвАЭ (NASB).
n1800562: tn The verse summarizes the result of GodвАЩs intervention in human affairs, according to EliphazвАЩ idea that even-handed justice prevails. Ps 107:42 parallels v. 16b.
n1800563: tn The particle вАЬthereforeвАЭ links this section to the preceding; it points this out as the logical consequence of the previous discussion, and more generally, as the essence of JobвАЩs suffering.
n1800564: tn The word вАП„Р÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„®÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њashre, вАЬblessedвАЭ) is often rendered вАЬhappy.вАЭ But вАЬhappyвАЭ relates to what happens. вАЬBlessedвАЭ is a reference to the heavenly bliss of the one who is right with God.
n1800565: tn The construction is an implied relative clause. The literal rendering would simply be вАЬthe man God corrects him.вАЭ The suffix on the verb is a resumptive pronoun, completing the use of the relative clause. The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh) is a legal term; it always has some sense of a charge, dispute, or conflict. Its usages show that it may describe a strife breaking out, a charge or quarrel in progress, or the settling of a dispute (Isa 1:18). The derived noun can mean вАЬreproach; recrimination; chargeвАЭ (13:6; 23:4). Here the emphasis is on the consequence of the charge brought, namely, the correction.
n1800566: tn The noun вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Ј„®вАО (musar) is parallel to the idea of the first colon. It means вАЬdiscipline, correctionвАЭ (from вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„®вАО, yasar). Prov 3:11 says almost the same thing as this line.
n1800567: sn The name Shaddai occurs 31 times in the book. This is its first occurrence. It is often rendered вАЬAlmightyвАЭ because of the LXX and some of the early fathers. The etymology and meaning of the word otherwise remains uncertain, in spite of attempts to connect it to вАЬmountainsвАЭ or вАЬbreasts.вАЭ
n1800568: sn Verses 18-23 give the reasons why someone should accept the chastening of GodвАФthe hand that wounds is the same hand that heals. But, of course, the lines do not apply to Job because his suffering is not due to divine chastening.
n1800569: tn The addition of the independent pronoun here makes the subject emphatic, as if to say, вАЬFor it is he who makesвА¶.вАЭ
n1800570: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse describe the characteristic activities of God; the classification as habitual imperfect fits the idea and is to be rendered with the English present tense.
n1800571: tn The verb is the Hiphil imperfect of вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„ЬвАО (natsal, вАЬdeliverвАЭ). These verbs might have been treated as habitual imperfects if it were not for the use of the numerical imagesвАФвАЬsix calamitiesвА¶in seven.вАЭ So the nuance is specific future instead.
n1800572: tn The use of a numerical ladder as we have hereвАФвАЬsix // sevenвАЭ is frequent in wisdom literature to show completeness. See Prov 6:16; Amos 1:3, Mic 5:5. A number that seems to be sufficient for the point is increased by one, as if to say there is always one more. By using this Eliphaz simply means вАЬin all troublesвАЭ (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 56).
n1800573: sn Targum Job here sees an allusion to the famine of Egypt and the war with Amalek.
n1800574: tn Heb вАЬfrom the hand of the sword.вАЭ This is idiomatic for вАЬthe power of the sword.вАЭ The expression is also metonymical, meaning from the effect of the sword, which is death.
n1800575: tn The Hebrew verb essentially means вАЬyou will be hidden.вАЭ In the Niphal the verb means вАЬto be hidden, to be in a hiding place,вАЭ and protected (Ps 31:20).
n1800576:
tn Heb вАЬfrom the lash [i.e., whip] of the tongue.вАЭ Sir 26:9 and 51:2 show usages of these kinds of expressions: вАЬthe lash of the tongueвАЭ or вАЬthe blow of the tongue.вАЭ The expression indicates that a malicious gossip is more painful than a blow.
sn The Targum saw here a reference to Balaam and the devastation brought on by the Midianites.
n1800577: tn The word here is вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„УвАО (shod); it means вАЬdestruction,вАЭ but some commentators conjecture alternate readings: вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (sho Њah, вАЬdesolationвАЭ); or вАП„©„Б÷µ„УвАО (shed, вАЬdemonвАЭ). One argument for maintaining вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„УвАО (shod) is that it fits the assonance within the verse вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„УвАОвА¶вАП„Ь÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАОвА¶вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„ШвАО (shotвА¶lashonвА¶shod).
n1800578:
tc The repetition of вАЬdestructionвАЭ and вАЬfamineвАЭ here has prompted some scholars to delete the whole verse. Others try to emend the text. The LXX renders them as вАЬthe unrighteous and the lawless.вАЭ But there is no difficulty in having the repetition of the words as found in the MT.
tn The word for вАЬfamineвАЭ is an Aramaic word found again in 30:3. The book of Job has a number of Aramaisms that are used to form an alternative parallel expression (see notes on вАЬwitnessвАЭ in 16:19).
n1800579: tn The verb is a negated jussive. According to GKC it is used here to express the conviction that something cannot or should not happen (GKC 322 ¬І109.e). The examples in GKC are generally not compelling, faltering in the Psalms by not accounting for changes in speaking voices, and especially concerning the idea that something cannot happen. However, the notion that something should (not) happen is the sort of deontic modality typical of the jussive generally, here sounding like advice (GKC 321 ¬І109.b).
n1800580: tn Heb вАЬyour covenant is with the stones of the field.вАЭ The line has been variously interpreted and translated. It is omitted in the LXX. It seems to mean there is a deep sympathy between man and nature. Some think it means that the boundaries will not be violated by enemies; Rashi thought it represented some species of beings, like genii of the field, and so read вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њadone, вАЬlordsвАЭ) for вАП„Р÷Ј„С÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њavne, вАЬstonesвАЭ). Ball takes the word as вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (bene, вАЬsonsвАЭ), as in вАЬsons of the field,вАЭ to get the idea that the reference is to the beasts. E. Dhorme (Job, 71) rejects these ideas as too contrived; he says to have a pact with the stones of the field simply means the stones will not come and spoil the ground, making it less fertile.
n1800582: tn This is the only occurrence of the Hophal of the verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷µ„ЭвАО (shalem, вАЬto make or have peaceвАЭ with someone). Cf. Isa 11:6-9 and Ps 91:13. The verb form is the perfect; here it is the perfect consecutive following a noun clause (see GKC 494 ¬І159.g).
n1800583: sn Verses 19-23 described the immunity from evil and trouble that Job would enjoyвАФif he were restored to peace with God. Now, v. 24 describes the safety and peace of the homestead and his possessions if he were right with God.
n1800584: tn The verb is again the perfect, but in sequence to the previous structure so that it is rendered as a future. This would be the case if Job were right with God.
n1800586: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom) means вАЬpeace; safety; security; wholeness.вАЭ The same use appears in 1 Sam 25:6; 2 Sam 20:9.
n1800587: tn The verb is вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad, вАЬto visitвАЭ). The idea here is вАЬto gather together; to look over; to investigate,вАЭ or possibly even вАЬto numberвАЭ as it is used in the book of Numbers. The verb is the perfect with the vav consecutive; it may be subordinated to the imperfect verb that follows to form a temporal clause.
n1800588: tn The verb is usually rendered вАЬto sin,вАЭ but in this context the more specific primary meaning of вАЬto miss the markвАЭ or вАЬto fail to find something.вАЭ Neither JobвАЩs tent nor his possessions will be lost.
n1800590: tn The word means вАЬyour shootsвАЭ and is parallel to вАЬyour seedвАЭ in the first colon. It refers here (as in Isa 34:1 and 42:5) to the produce of the earth. Some commentators suggest that Eliphaz seems to have forgotten or was insensitive to JobвАЩs loss of his children; H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 57) says his conventional theology is untouched by human feeling.
n1800591: tn The word translated вАЬin a full ageвАЭ has been given an array of meanings: вАЬhealth; integrityвАЭ; вАЬlike a new blade of cornвАЭ; вАЬin your strength [or vigor].вАЭ The numerical value of the letters in the word вАП„С÷∞„Ы÷ґ„Ь÷Є„ЧвАО (vekhelakh, вАЬin old ageвАЭ) was 2, 20, 30, and 8, or 60. This led some of the commentators to say that at 60 one would enter the ripe old age (E. Dhorme, Job, 73).
n1800592: tn To make a better parallelism, some commentators have replaced the imperative with another finite verb, вАЬwe have found it.вАЭ
n1800593: tn The preposition with the suffix (referred to as the ethical dative) strengthens the imperative. An emphatic personal pronoun also precedes the imperative. The resulting force would be something like вАЬand you had better apply it for your own good!вАЭ
n1800594: sn With this the speech by Eliphaz comes to a close. His two mistakes with it are: (1) that the tone was too cold and (2) the argument did not fit JobвАЩs case (see further, A. B. Davidson, Job, 42).
n180062: tn The conjunction вАП„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (lu, вАЬif, if onlyвАЭ) introduces the wishвАФan unrealizable wishвАФwith the Niphal imperfect.
n180063: tn Job pairs вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„°÷і„ЩвАО (ka њsi, вАЬmy griefвАЭ) and вАП„Ф÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (hayyati, вАЬmy misfortuneвАЭ). The first word, used in Job 4:2, refers to JobвАЩs whole demeanor that he shows his friendsвАФthe impatient and vexed expression of his grief. The second word expresses his misfortune, the cause of his grief. Job wants these placed together in the balances so that his friends could see the misfortune is greater than the grief. The word for вАЬmisfortuneвАЭ is a Kethib-Qere reading. The two words have essentially the same meaning; they derive from the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Х÷Ј„ФвАО (havah, вАЬto fallвАЭ) and so mean a misfortune.
n180064: tn The Qal infinitive absolute is here used to intensify the Niphal imperfect (see GKC 344-45 ¬І113.w). The infinitive absolute intensifies the wish as well as the idea of weighing.
n180065: tn The third person plural verb is used here; it expresses an indefinite subject and is treated as a passive (see GKC 460 ¬І144.g).
n180066: tn The adverb normally means вАЬtogether,вАЭ but it can also mean вАЬsimilarly, too.вАЭ In this verse it may not mean that the two things are to be weighed together, but that the whole calamity should be put on the scales (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 43).
n180067: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 76) notes that вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Њ„Ґ÷Ј„™÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (ki  њattah) has no more force than вАЬbutвАЭ; and that the construction is the same as in 17:4; 20:19-21; 23:14-15. The initial clause is causative, and the second half of the verse gives the consequence (вАЬbecauseвАЭвА¶вАЬthat is whyвАЭ). Others take 3a as the apodosis of v. 2, and translate it вАЬfor now it would be heavierвА¶вАЭ (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 43).
n180068: sn The point of the comparison with the sand of the sea is that the sand is immeasurable. So the grief of Job cannot be measured.
n180069: tn The verb вАП„Ь÷Є„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (la њu) is traced by E. Dhorme (Job, 76) to a root вАП„Ь÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (la њah), cognate to an Arabic root meaning вАЬto chatter.вАЭ He shows how modern Hebrew has a meaning for the word вАЬto stammer out.вАЭ But that does not really fit JobвАЩs outbursts. The idea in the context is rather that of speaking wildly, rashly, or charged with grief. This would trace the word to a hollow or geminate word and link it to Arabic вАЬtalk wildlyвАЭ (see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 158). In the older works the verb was taken from a geminate root meaning вАЬto suckвАЭ or вАЬto swallowвАЭ (cf. KJV), but that yields a very difficult sense to the line.
n1800610: sn Job uses an implied comparison here to describe his misfortuneвАФit is as if God had shot poisoned arrows into him (see E. Dhorme, Job, 76-77 for a treatment of poisoned arrows in the ancient world).
n1800611: sn Job here clearly states that his problems have come from the Almighty, which is what Eliphaz said. But whereas Eliphaz said Job provoked the trouble by his sin, Job is perplexed because he does not think he did.
n1800612: tn Most commentators take вАЬmy spiritвАЭ as the subject of the participle вАЬdrinks.вАЭ The NEB does not; it follows the older versions to say that the poison вАЬdrinks up (or вАЬsoaks inвАЭ) the spirit.вАЭ The image of the poisoned arrow represents the calamity or misfortune from God, which is taken in by JobвАЩs spirit and enervates him.
n1800613: tn The LXX translators knew that a liquid should be used with the verb вАЬdrink,вАЭ but they took the line to be вАЬwhose violence drinks up my blood.вАЭ For the rest of the verse they came up with, вАЬwhenever I am going to speak they pierce me.вАЭ
n1800614: tn The word translated вАЬsudden terrorsвАЭ is found only here and in Ps 88:16 [17]. G. R. Driver notes that the idea of suddenness is present in the root, and so renders this word as вАЬsudden assaultsвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 73).
n1800615: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh) means вАЬto set in battle array.вАЭ The suffix on the verb is dative (see GKC 369 ¬І117.x). Many suggestions have been made for changing this word. These seem unnecessary since the MT pointing yields a good meaning: but for the references to these suggestions, see D. J. A. Clines, Job (WBC), 158. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 59), nonetheless, follows the suggestion of Driver that connects it to a root meaning вАЬwear me down.вАЭ This change of meaning requires no change in the Hebrew text. The image is of a beleaguering army; the host is made up of all the terrors from God. The reference is to the terrifying and perplexing thoughts that assail Job (A. B. Davidson, Job, 44).
n1800616: tn There have been suggestions to identify this animal as something other than a wild donkey, but the traditional interpretation has been confirmed (see P. Humbert, вАЬEn marge du dictionnaire h√©bra√ѓque,вАЭ ZAW 62 [1950]: 199-207).
n1800617: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„ІвАО (nahaq, вАЬbrayвАЭ) occurs in Arabic and Aramaic and only in Job 30:7 in Hebrew, where it refers to unfortunate people in the wilderness who utter cries like the hungry wild donkey.
n1800618: sn In this brief section Job indicates that it would be wiser to seek the reason for the crying than to complain of the cry. The wild donkey will bray when it finds no food (see Jer 14:6).
n1800619: tn The construction forms a double question (вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАОвА¶вАП„Ф÷≤вАО, haвА¶ Њim) but not to express mutually exclusive questions in this instance. Instead, it is used to repeat the same question in different words (see GKC 475 ¬І150.h).
n1800620: tn Most translations have вАЬlowвАЭ (ASV, ESV, Holman, KJV, NASB); a few have вАЬbellowвАЭ ( CEB, NIV, NLV). The verb is rare but cognate languages suggest a loud sound (e.g. Syriac вАЬto screamвАЭ Ugaritic вАЬto roar,вАЭ see HALOT 199). The rhetorical question expects a вАЬnoвАЭ answer and context suggests that the (unexpected) sound would convey discontent or complaint.
n1800621: tn Rather than grass or hay, this is mixed grain fodder prepared for domesticated animals (cf. also Akkadian ballu; CAD B 63-64).
n1800622: tn Heb вАЬa tasteless thingвАЭ; the word вАЬfoodвАЭ is supplied from the context.
n1800623: tn The point is in giving an example of something tasteless although the specifics are uncertain. Several meanings have been proposed for the word вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷Є÷Љ„Ю„Х÷Љ„™вАО (khallamut), which occurs only here. The root of the word may be connected to вАЬdream,вАЭ вАЬhealthy,вАЭ вАЬeggвАЭ (via Aramaic cognate), or вАЬsoft cheeseвАЭ (via Arabic cognate). It has also been connected with various plants: the marsh mallow (althaea), bugloss, milkweed, and purslane. The term вАП„®÷і„Щ„®вАО (rir, вАЬspittle, mucus, slimeвАЭ) occurs only here and in 1 Sam 21:13, where it means saliva, a meaning in agreement with Aramaic and Arabic cognates. The phrase tends to be taken as the gelatinous juice of plants or the white of an egg, both of which would parallel the idea of being tasteless or insipid in the A line. Dhorme says the phrase refers to вАЬthe glair which surrounds the yolk of an egg,вАЭ drawing support from the Targum and Saadia (E. Dhorme, Job 79). He also offers an explanation for how the LXX produced the reading вАЬin empty wordsвАЭ as an example of interpretation more than translation. вАЬ[The LXX] renders вАП„С„®„Щ„® „Ч„Ь„Ю„Х„™вАО by бЉРќљ бњ•ќЃќЉќ±ѕГќєќљ ќЇќµќљќњбњЦѕВ, which has caused some critics to believe there was a reading вАП„У„С„®вАО [davar, вАЬwordвАЭ] instead of вАП„®„Щ„®вАО. It seems more likely that [the LXX] interprets вАП„®„Щ„® „Ч„Ь„Ю„Х„™вАО by connecting the second word with вАП„Ч„Ь„ЭвАО вАШdreamвАЩ (cf. inf.), i.e., the saliva of dreams, what one says while sleeping, empty words, baseless dreamsвАЭ (E. Dhorme, Job 78).
n1800624: tn The traditional rendering of вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi) is вАЬmy soul.вАЭ But since вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) means the whole person, body and soul, it is best to translate it with its suffix simply as an emphatic pronoun.
n1800625: tn For the explanation of the perfect verb with its completed action in the past and its remaining effects, see GKC 311 ¬І106.g.
n1800626: tn The phrase вАЬsuch thingsвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text but has been supplied.
n1800627: tn The second colon of the verse is difficult. The word вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Х÷µ„ЩвАО (deve) means вАЬsickness ofвАЭ and yields a meaning вАЬlike the sickness of my food.вАЭ This could take the derived sense of вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (davah) and mean вАЬimpureвАЭ or вАЬcorruptвАЭ food. The LXX has вАЬfor I loathe my food as the smell of a lionвАЭ and so some commentators emend вАЬtheyвАЭ (which has no clear antecedent) to mean вАЬI loathe it [like the sickness of my food].вАЭ Others have more freely emended the text to вАЬmy palate loathes my foodвАЭ (McNeile) or вАЬmy bowels resound with sufferingвАЭ (I. Eitan, вАЬAn unknown meaning of RAHAMI√ЭM,вАЭ JBL 53 [1934]: 271). Pope has вАЬthey are putrid as my flesh [= my meat].вАЭ D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 159) prefers the suggestion in BHS, вАЬit [my soul] loathes them as my food.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 80) repoints the second word of the colon to get вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„С÷є„У÷і„ЩвАО (kevodi, вАЬmy gloryвАЭ): вАЬmy heart [glory] loathes/is sickened by my bread.вАЭ
n1800628: tn The Hebrew expresses the desire (desiderative clause) with вАЬwho will give?вАЭ (see GKC 477 ¬І151.d).
n1800629: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬgoвАЭ) has the sense of вАЬto be realized; to come to pass; to be fulfilled.вАЭ The optative вАЬWho will give [that] my request be realized?вАЭ is вАЬO that my request would be realized.вАЭ
n1800630:
tn The text has вАП„™÷і„І÷∞„Х÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (tiqvati, вАЬhopeвАЭ). There is no reason to change the text to вАЬmy desireвАЭ (as Driver and others do) if the word is interpreted metonymicallyвАФit means вАЬwhat I hope for.вАЭ What Job hopes for and asks for is death.
sn See further W. Riggans, вАЬJob 6:8-10: Short Comments,вАЭ ExpTim 99 (1987): 45-46.
n1800631: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (ya Њal) in the Hiphil means вАЬto be willing, to consent, to decide.вАЭ It is here the jussive followed by the dependent verb with a (вАП„ХвАО) vav: вАЬthat God would be willing and would crush meвАЭ means вАЬto crush me.вАЭ Gesenius, however, says that the conjunction introduces coordination rather than subordination; he says the principal idea is introduced in the second verb, the first verb containing the definition of the manner of the action (see GKC 386 ¬І120.d).
n1800632: tn The verb is used for loosening shoe straps in Isa 58:6, and of setting prisoners free in Pss 105:20 and 146:7. Job thinks that GodвАЩs hand has been restrained for some reason, and so desires that God be free to destroy him.
n1800633: tn The final verb is an imperfect (or jussive) following the jussive (of вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО, natar); it thus expresses the result (вАЬand thenвАЭ or вАЬso thatвАЭ) or the purpose (вАЬin order thatвАЭ). Job longs for death, but it must come from God.
n1800634: tn Heb вАЬand cut me off.вАЭ The LXX reads this verse as вАЬLet the Lord begin and wound me, but let him not utterly destroy me.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 81) says the LXX is a paraphrase based on a pun with вАЬfree hand.вАЭ Targum Job has, вАЬGod has begun to make me poor; may he free his hand and make me rich,вАЭ apparently basing the reading on a metaphorical interpretation.
n1800635: tn Heb вАЬand it will/may be yet my comfort.вАЭ The comfort or consolation that he seeks, that he wishes for, is death. The next colon in the verse simply intensifies this thought, for he affirms if that should happen he would rejoice, in spite of what death involves. The LXX, apparently confusing letters (reading вАП„Ґ÷і„Щ„®вАО [ њir, вАЬcityвАЭ] instead of вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО [ њod, вАЬyetвАЭ], which then led to the mistake in the next colon, вАП„Ч÷µ„Щ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО [khelah, вАЬits wallвАЭ] for вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО [khilah, вАЬsufferingвАЭ]), has вАЬLet the grave be my city, upon the walls of which I have leaped.вАЭ
n1800636: tn In the apodosis of conditional clauses (which must be supplied from the context preceding), the cohortative expresses the consequence (see GKC 320 ¬І108.d).
n1800637: tn The Piel verb вАП„°÷і„Ь÷Љ÷µ„УвАО (silled) is a hapax legomenon. BDB 698 s.v. вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„УвАО gives the meaning вАЬto spring [i.e., jump] for joy,вАЭ which would certainly fit the passage. Others have emended the text, but unnecessarily. The LXX вАЬI jumpedвАЭ and Targum JobвАЩs вАЬexultвАЭ support the sense in the dictionaries, although the jumping is for joy and not over a wall (as the LXX has). D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 159) follows Driver in thinking this is untenable, choosing a meaning вАЬrecoiled in painвАЭ for the line.
n1800638: tn The word вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (khilah) also occurs only here, but is connected to the verb вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„ЬвАО / вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (khil / khul, вАЬto writhe in painвАЭ). E. Dhorme says that by extension the meaning denotes the cause of this trembling or writhingвАФterrifying pain. The final clause, вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„ЬвАО (lo Њ yakhmol, вАЬit has no pityвАЭ), serves as a kind of epithet, modifying вАЬpainвАЭ in general. If that pain has no pity or compassion, it is a ruthless pain (E. Dhorme, Job, 82).
n1800639: tn The вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki, вАЬforвАЭ) functions here to explain вАЬmy comfortвАЭ in the first colon; the second colon simply strengthens the first.
n1800640: sn The вАЬwordsвАЭ are the divine decrees of GodвАЩs providence, the decisions that he makes in his dealings with people. Job cannot conceal theseвАФhe knows what they are. What Job seems to mean by this clause in this verse is that there is nothing that would hinder his joy of dying for he has not denied or disobeyed GodвАЩs plan.
n1800641: tn Several commentators delete the colon as having no meaning in the verse, and because (in their view) it is probably the addition of an interpolator who wants to make Job sound more pious. But Job is at least consoling himself that he is innocent, and at the most anticipating a worth-while afterlife (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 60).
n1800642: sn Now, in vv. 11-13, Job proceeds to describe his hopeless condition. In so doing, he is continuing his defense of his despair and lament. The section begins with these rhetorical questions in which Job affirms that he does not have the strength to wait for the blessings that Eliphaz is talking about.
n1800643: tn The word translated вАЬmy endвАЭ is вАП„І÷і„¶÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (qitsi). It refers to the termination of his life. In Ps 39:5 it is parallel to вАЬthe measure of my days.вАЭ In a sense, Job is asking what future he has. To him, the вАЬendвАЭ of his affliction can only be death.
n1800644: sn The questions imply negative answers. Job is saying that it would take great strength to hold up under these afflictions, but he is only flesh and bone. The sufferings have almost completely overwhelmed him. To endure all of this to the end he would need a strength he does not have.
n1800645: tn For the use of the particle вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) in this kind of interrogative clause, see GKC 475 ¬І150.g, note.
n1800646: tn The word means something like вАЬrecovery,вАЭ or the powers of recovery; it was used in Job 5:12. In 11:6 it applies to a condition of the mind, such as mental resource. Job is thinking not so much of relief or rescue from his troubles, but of strength to bear them.
n1800647: tn In this context вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed) could be taken as вАЬloyaltyвАЭ (вАЬloyalty should be shown by his friendвАЭ).
n1800648: tn The Hebrew of this verse is extremely difficult, and while there are many suggestions, none of them has gained a consensus. The first colon simply has вАЬto the despairing // from his friend // kindness.вАЭ Several commentators prefer to change the first word вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Є„°вАО (lammas, вАЬto the one in despairвАЭ) to some sort of verb; several adopt the reading вАЬthe one who withholds/he withholds mercy from his friend forsakesвА¶.вАЭ The point of the first half of the verse seems to be that one should expect kindness (or loyalty) from a friend in times of suffering.
n1800649: tn The relationship of the second colon to the first is difficult. The line just reads literally вАЬand the fear of the Almighty he forsakes.вАЭ The вАП„ХвАО (vav) could be interpreted in several different ways: вАЬelse he will forsakeвА¶,вАЭ вАЬalthough he forsakesвА¶,вАЭ вАЬeven the one who forsakesвА¶,вАЭ or вАЬeven if he forsakesвА¶вАЭвАФthe reading adopted here. If the first colon receives the reading вАЬHis friend has scorned compassion,вАЭ then this clause would be simply coordinated with вАЬand forsakes the fear of the Almighty.вАЭ The sense of the verse seems to say that kindness/loyalty should be shown to the despairing, even to the one who is forsaking the fear of the Lord, meaning, saying outrageous things, like Job has been doing.
n1800650: sn Here the brothers are all his relatives as well as these intimate friends of Job. In contrast to what a friend should do (show kindness/loyalty), these friends have provided no support whatsoever.
n1800651: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Т÷∞„У„Х÷ЉвАО (bagedu, вАЬdealt treacherously) has been translated вАЬdealt deceitfully,вАЭ but it is a very strong word. It means вАЬto act treacherously [or deceitfully].вАЭ The deception is the treachery, because the deception is not innocentвАФit is in the place of a great need. The imagery will compare it to the brook that may or may not have water. If one finds no water when one expected it and needed it, there is deception and treachery. The LXX softens it considerably: вАЬhave not regarded me.вАЭ
n1800652: tn The Hebrew term used here is вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (nakhal); this word differs from words for rivers or streams in that it describes a brook with an intermittent flow of water. A brook where the waters are not flowing is called a deceitful brook (Jer 15:18; Mic 1:14); one where the waters flow is called faithful (Isa 33:16).
n1800653: tn Heb вАЬand as a stream bed of brooks/torrents.вАЭ The word вАП„Р÷Є„§÷і„Щ„ІвАО ( Њafiq) is the river bed or stream bed where the water flows. What is more disconcerting than finding a well-known torrent whose bed is dry when one expects it to be gushing with water (E. Dhorme, Job, 86)?
n1800654: tn The verb is rather simpleвАФвАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„С÷є„®„Х÷ЉвАО (ya њavoru). But some translate it вАЬpass awayвАЭ or вАЬflow away,вАЭ and others вАЬoverflow.вАЭ In the rainy season they are deep and flowing, or вАЬoverflowвАЭ their banks. This is a natural sense to the verb, and since the next verse focuses on this, some follow this interpretation (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 15). But this idea does not parallel the first part of v. 15. So it makes better sense to render it вАЬflow awayвАЭ and see the reference to the summer dry spells when one wants the water but is disappointed.
n1800655: tn The article on the participle joins this statement to the preceding noun; it can have the sense of вАЬtheyвАЭ or вАЬwhich.вАЭ The parallel sense then can be continued with a finite verb (see GKC 404 ¬І126.b).
n1800656: tn The participle вАП„Ф÷Ј„І÷Љ÷є„У÷∞„®„Щ„ЭвАО (haqqoderim), often rendered вАЬwhich are black,вАЭ would better be translated вАЬdark,вАЭ for it refers to the turbid waters filled with melting ice or melting snow, or to the frozen surface of the water, but not waters that are muddied. The versions failed to note that this referred to the waters introduced in v. 15.
n1800657: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷ґ„ЭвАО (yit њallem) has been translated вАЬis hidвАЭ or вАЬhides itself.вАЭ But this does not work easily in the sentence with the preposition вАЬupon them.вАЭ Torczyner suggested вАЬpile upвАЭ from an Aramaic root вАП„Ґ÷≤„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО ( њalam), and E. Dhorme (Job, 87) defends it without changing the text, contending that the form we have was chosen for alliterative value with the prepositional phrase before it.
n1800658: tn The LXX paraphrases the whole verse: вАЬThey who used to reverence me now come against me like snow or congealed ice.вАЭ
n1800659: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷∞„Ц÷є„®÷∞„С„Х÷ЉвАО (yezorevu, вАЬburnt, scorchedвАЭ) occurs only here. A good number of interpretations take the root as a by-form of вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО (tsarav) which means in the Niphal вАЬto be burntвАЭ (Ezek 21:3). The expression then would mean вАЬin the time they are burnt,вАЭ a reference to the scorching heat of the summer (вАЬwhen the great heat comesвАЭ) and the rivers dry up. Qimchi connected it to the Arabic вАЬcanal,вАЭ and this has led to the suggestion by E. Dhorme (Job, 88) that the root вАП„Ц÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО (zarav) would mean вАЬto flow.вАЭ In the Piel it would be вАЬto cause to flow,вАЭ and in the passive вАЬto be made to flow,вАЭ or вАЬmelt.вАЭ This is attractive, but it does require the understanding (or supplying) of вАЬice/snowвАЭ as the subject. G. R. Driver took the same meaning but translated it вАЬwhen they (the streams) pour down in torrents, they (straightway) die downвАЭ (ZAW 65 [1953]: 216-17). Both interpretations capture the sense of the brooks drying up.
n1800660: tn The verb вАП„†÷і„У÷∞„Ґ÷≤„Ы„Х÷ЉвАО (nid њakhu) literally means вАЬthey are extinguishedвАЭ or вАЬthey vanishвАЭ (cf. 18:5-6; 21:17). The LXX, perhaps confusing the word with the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ) has вАЬand it is not known what it was.вАЭ
n1800661: tn This is the usual rendering of the Hebrew вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њorkhot, вАЬway, pathвАЭ). It would mean that the course of the wadi would wind down and be lost in the sand. Many commentators either repoint the text to вАП„Р÷є„®÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њorekhot) when in construct (as in Isa 21:13), or simply redefine the existing word to mean вАЬcaravansвАЭ as in the next verse, and translate something like вАЬcaravans deviate from their route.вАЭ D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 160-61) allows that вАЬcaravansвАЭ will be introduced in the next verse, but urges retention of the usual sense here. The two verses together will yield the same idea in either caseвАФthe river dries up and caravans looking for the water deviate from their course looking for it.
n1800662: tn The verb literally means вАЬto go up,вАЭ but here no real ascent is intended for the wasteland. It means that they go inland looking for the water. The streams wind out into the desert and dry up in the sand and the heat. A. B. Davidson (Job, 47) notes the difficulty with the interpretation of this verse as a reference to caravans is that Ibn Ezra says that it is not usual for caravans to leave their path and wander inland in search of water.
n1800663: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (tohu) was used in Genesis for вАЬwaste,вАЭ meaning without shape or structure. Here the term refers to the trackless, unending wilderness (cf. 12:24).
n1800664: sn If the term вАЬpathsвАЭ (referring to the brook) is the subject, then this verb would mean it dies in the desert; if caravaneers are intended, then when they find no water they perish. The point in the argument would be the same in either case. Job is saying that his friends are like this water, and he like the caravaneer was looking for refreshment, but found only that the brook had dried up.
n1800665: sn Tema is the area of the oasis SE of the head of the Gulf of Aqaba; Sheba is in South Arabia. In Job 1:15 the Sabeans were raiders; here they are traveling merchants.
n1800666: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„С÷Ј„ШвАО (navat) means вАЬto gaze intentlyвАЭ; the looking is more intentional, more of a close scrutiny. It forms a fine parallel to the idea of вАЬhopeвАЭ in the second part. The NIV translates the second verb вАП„І÷і„Х÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (qivvu) as вАЬlook in hope.вАЭ In the previous verbs the imperfect form was used, expressing what generally happens (so the English present tense was used). Here the verb usage changes to the perfect form. It seems that Job is narrating a typical incident nowвАФthey looked, but were disappointed.
n1800667: tn The words вАЬfor these streamsвАЭ are supplied from context to complete the thought and make the connection with the preceding context.
n1800668: tn In Ps 68:24 this word has the meaning of вАЬprocessionsвАЭ; here that procession is of traveling merchants forming convoys or caravans.
n1800669: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„©„БвАО (bosh) basically means вАЬto be ashamedвАЭ; however, it has a wider range of meaning such as вАЬdisappointedвАЭ or вАЬdistressed.вАЭ The feeling of shame or distress is because of their confidence that they knew what they were doing. The verb is strengthened here with the parallel вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (khafar, вАЬto be confounded, disappointedвАЭ).
n1800670: tn The perfect verb has the nuance of past perfect here, for their confidence preceded their disappointment. Note the contrast, using these verbs, in Ps 22:6: вАЬthey trusted in you and they were not put to shame [i.e., disappointed].вАЭ
n1800671: tn The LXX misread the prepositional phrase as the noun вАЬtheir citiesвАЭ; it gives the line as вАЬThey too that trust in cities and riches shall come to shame.вАЭ
n1800672: tn There is a textual problem in this line, an issue of Kethib-Qere. Some read the form with the Qere as the preposition with a suffix referring to вАЬthe river,вАЭ with the idea вАЬyou are like it.вАЭ Others would read the form with the Kethib as the negative вАЬnot,вАЭ meaning вАЬfor now you are nothing.вАЭ The LXX and the Syriac read the word as вАЬto me.вАЭ RSV follows this and changes вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki, вАЬforвАЭ) to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken, вАЬthusвАЭ). However, such an emendation is unnecessary since вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) itself can be legitimately employed as an emphatic particle. In that case the translation would be, вАЬIndeed, now you areвАЭ in the sense of вАЬAt this time you certainly are behaving like those streams.вАЭ The simplest reading is вАЬfor now you have become [like] it.вАЭ The meaning seems clear enough in the context that the friends, like the river, proved to be of no use. But D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 161) points out that the difficulty with this is that all references so far to the rivers have been in the plural.
n1800673: tn The perfect of вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah) could be translated as either вАЬareвАЭ or вАЬhave beenвАЭ rather than вАЬhave becomeвАЭ (cf. Jo√Љon 2:373 ¬І113.p with regard to stative verbs). вАЬLike itвАЭ refers to the intermittent stream which promises water but does not deliver. The LXX has a paraphrase: вАЬBut you also have come to me without pity.вАЭ
n1800674: tn The word вАП„Ч÷≤„™÷Ј„™вАО (khatat) is a hapax legomenon. The word вАП„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (khat) means вАЬterrorвАЭ in 41:25. The construct form вАП„Ч÷і„™÷Љ÷Ј„™вАО (khittat) is found in Gen 35:5; and вАП„Ч÷і„™÷Љ÷і„Щ„™вАО (khittit) is found in Ezek 26:17; 32:23). The Akkadian cognate means вАЬterror.вАЭ It probably means that in JobвАЩs suffering they recognized some dreaded thing from God and were afraid to speak any sympathy toward him.
n1800675: tn The Hebrew вАП„Ф÷≤„Ы÷і„ЩвАО (hakhi) literally says вАЬIs it becauseвА¶.вАЭ
n1800676: sn For the next two verses Job lashes out in sarcasm against his friends. If he had asked for charity, for their wealth, he might have expected their cold response. But all he wanted was sympathy and understanding (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 63).
n1800677: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО (koakh) basically means вАЬstrength, force,вАЭ but like the synonym вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО (khayil), it can also mean вАЬwealth, fortune.вАЭ E. Dhorme notes that to the Semitic mind, riches bring power (Job, 90).
n1800678: tn Or вАЬbribes.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷і„Ч÷≤„У„Х÷ЉвАО (shikhadu) means вАЬgive a вАП„©„Б÷є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (shokhad, вАЬbribeвАЭ).вАЭ The significance is simply вАЬmake a giftвАЭ (especially in the sense of corrupting an official [Ezek 16:33]). For the spelling of the form in view of the guttural, see GKC 169 ¬І64.a.
n1800679: tn The verse now gives the ultimate reason why Job might have urged his friends to make a giftвАФif it were possible. The LXX, avoiding the direct speech in the preceding verse and this, does make this verse the purpose statementвАФвАЬto deliver from enemiesвА¶.вАЭ
n1800680: tn Heb вАЬhand,вАЭ as in the second half of the verse.
n1800681: tn The вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њaritsim) are tyrants, the people who inspire fear (Job 15:20; 27:13); the root verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО ( њarats) means вАЬto terrifyвАЭ (Job 13:25).
n1800682: tn The verb now is the imperfect; since it is parallel to the imperative in the first half of the verse it is imperfect of instruction, much like English uses the future for instruction. The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (padah) means вАЬto ransom, redeem,вАЭ often in contexts where payment is made.
n1800683: tn The verb вАЬteachвАЭ or вАЬinstructвАЭ is the Hiphil вАП„Ф„Х÷є„®„Х÷Љ„†÷і„ЩвАО (horuni), from the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (yarah); the basic idea of вАЬpoint, directвАЭ lies behind this meaning. The verb is cognate to the noun вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah, вАЬinstruction, teaching, lawвАЭ).
n1800684: tn The independent personal pronoun makes the subject of the verb emphatic: вАЬand I will be silent.вАЭ
n1800685: tn The verb is вАП„Ф÷Є„С÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (havinu, вАЬto cause someone to understandвАЭ); with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) following, it has the sense of вАЬexplain to me.вАЭ
n1800686: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah) has the sense of вАЬwandering, getting lost, being mistaken.вАЭ
n1800687: tn The word вАП„†÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„®÷∞„¶„Х÷ЉвАО (nimretsu, вАЬ[they] painful areвАЭ) may be connected to вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (marats, вАЬto be illвАЭ). This would give the idea of вАЬhow distressing,вАЭ or вАЬpainfulвАЭ in this stem. G. R. Driver (JTS 29 [1927/28]: 390-96) connected it to an Akkadian cognate вАЬto be illвАЭ and rendered it вАЬbitter.вАЭ It has also been linked with вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„°вАО (maras), meaning вАЬto be hard, strong,вАЭ giving the idea of вАЬhow persuasiveвАЭ (see N. S. Doniach and W. E. Barnes, вАЬJob vi 25. вИЪвАП„Ю„®„•вАО,вАЭ JTS [1929/30]: 291-92). There seems more support for the meaning вАЬto be illвАЭ (cf. Mal 2:10). Others follow Targum Job вАЬhow pleasant [to my palate are your words]вАЭ; E. Dhorme (Job, 92) follows this without changing the text but noting that the word has an interchange of letter with вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„•вАО (malats) for вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (marats).
n1800688: tn The вАП„Х÷ЉвАО (vav) here introduces the antithesis (GKC 484-85 ¬І154.a).
n1800689: tn The infinitive вАП„Ф„Х÷є„Ы÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (hokheakh, вАЬreproof,вАЭ from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО [yakhakh, вАЬproveвАЭ]) becomes the subject of the verb from the same root, вАП„Щ„Х÷є„Ы÷і„Щ„Ф÷ЈвАО (yokhiakh), and so serves as a noun (see GKC 340 ¬І113.b). This verb means вАЬto dispute, quarrel, argue, contendвАЭ (see BDB 406-7 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО). Job is saying, вАЬWhat does reproof from you prove?вАЭ
n1800690: tn The LXX again paraphrases this line: вАЬBut as it seems, the words of a true man are vain, because I do not ask strength of you.вАЭ But the rest of the versions are equally divided on the verse.
n1800691: tn This, in the context, is probably the meaning, although the Hebrew simply has the line after the first half of the verse read: вАЬand as/to wind the words of a despairing man.вАЭ The line could be translated вАЬand the words of a despairing man, [which are] as wind.вАЭ But this translation follows the same approach as RSV, NIV, and NAB, which take the idiom of the verb (вАЬthink, imagineвАЭ) with the preposition on вАЬwindвАЭ to mean вАЬreckon as windвАЭвАФвАЬand treat the words of a despairing man as wind.вАЭ
n1800692: tn The word вАЬlotsвАЭ is not in the text; the verb is simply вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (tappilu, вАЬyou castвАЭ). But the word вАЬlotsвАЭ is also omitted in 1 Sam 14:42. Some commentators follow the LXX and repoint the word and divide the object of the preposition to read вАЬand fall upon the blameless one.вАЭ Fohrer deletes the verse. Peake transfers it to come after v. 23. Even though it does not follow quite as well here, it nonetheless makes sense as a strong invective against their lack of sympathy, and the lack of connection could be the result of emotional speech. He is saying they are the kind of people who would cast lots over the child of a debtor, who, after the death of the father, would be sold to slavery.
n1800693: tn The verb вАП„™÷і„Ы÷∞„®„Х÷ЉвАО (tikhru) is from вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (karah), which is found in 41:6 with вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal), to mean вАЬto speculateвАЭ on an object. The form is usually taken to mean вАЬto barter for,вАЭ which would be an expression showing great callousness to a friend (NIV). NEB has вАЬhurl yourselves,вАЭ perhaps following the LXX вАЬrush against.вАЭ but G. R. Driver thinks that meaning is very precarious. As for the translation, вАЬto speculate about [or вАЬoverвАЭ] a friendвАЭ could be understood to mean вАЬengage in speculation concerning,вАЭ so the translation вАЬauction offвАЭ has been used instead.
n1800694: tn The second verb, the imperative вАЬturn,вАЭ is subordinated to the first imperative even though there is no vav present (see GKC 385-87 ¬І120.a, g).
n1800695: tn The line has вАЬand now, be pleased, turn to me [i.e., face me].вАЭ The LXX reverses the idea, вАЬAnd now, having looked upon your countenances, I will not lie.вАЭ The expression вАЬturn to meвАЭ means essentially to turn the eyes toward someone to look at him.
n1800696: tn The construction uses вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) as in a negative oath to mark the strong negative. He is underscoring his sincerity here. See M. R. Lehmann, вАЬBiblical Oaths,вАЭ ZAW 81 (1969): 74-92.
n1800697: tn The Hebrew verb вАП„©„Б÷ї„С„Х÷ЉвАО (shuvu) would literally be вАЬreturn.вАЭ It has here the sense of вАЬto begin again; to adopt another course,вАЭ that is, proceed on another supposition other than my guilt (A. B. Davidson, Job, 49). The LXX takes the word from вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (yashav, вАЬsit, dwellвАЭ) reading вАЬsit down now.вАЭ
