n1800698: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њavlah) is sometimes translated вАЬiniquity.вАЭ The word can mean вАЬperversion, wickedness, injusticeвАЭ (cf. 16:11). But here he means in regard to words. Unjust or wicked words would be words that are false and destroy.
n1800699: tn The verb here is also вАП„©„Б÷ї„С„Х÷ЉвАО (shuvu), although there is a Kethib-Qere reading. See R. Gordis, вАЬSome Unrecognized Meanings of the Root Shub,вАЭ JBL 52 (1933): 153-62.
n18006100: tn The text has simply вАЬyet my right is in it.вАЭ A. B. Davidson (Job, 49, 50) thinks this means that in his plea against God, Job has right on his side. It may mean this; it simply says вАЬmy righteousness is yet in it.вАЭ If the вАЬin itвАЭ does not refer to JobвАЩs cause, then it would simply mean вАЬis present.вАЭ It would have very little difference either way.
n18006101:
tn The word вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њavlah) is repeated from the last verse. Here the focus is clearly on wickedness or injustice spoken.
sn These words make a fitting transition to ch. 7, which forms a renewed cry of despair from Job. Job still feels himself innocent, but in the hands of cruel fate which is out to destroy him.
n18006102: tn Heb вАЬmy palate.вАЭ Here вАЬpalateвАЭ is used not so much for the organ of speech (by metonymy) as of discernment. In other words, what he says indicates what he thinks.
n18006103: tn The final word, вАП„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (havvot) is usually understood as вАЬcalamities.вАЭ He would be asking if he could not discern his misfortune. But some argue that the word has to be understood in the parallelism to вАЬwickednessвАЭ of words (D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 162). Gordis connects it to Mic 7:3 and Ps 5:10 [9] where the meaning вАЬdeceit, falsehoodвАЭ is found. The LXX has вАЬand does not my throat meditate understanding?вАЭ
n180071: tn The word вАП„¶÷Є„С÷Є„РвАО (tsava Њ) is actually вАЬarmyвАЭ; it can be used for the hard service of military service as well as other toil. As a military term it would include the fixed period of duty (the time) and the hard work (toil). Job here is considering the lot of all humans, not just himself.
n180072: tn The вАП„©„В÷Є„Ы÷і„Щ„®вАО (sakhir) is a hired man, either a man who works for wages, or a mercenary soldier (Jer 46:21). The latter sense may be what is intended here in view of the parallelism, although the next verse seems much broader.
n180073: tn This term вАП„Ґ÷ґ„С÷ґ„УвАО ( њeved) is the servant or the slave. He is compelled to work through the day in the heat, but he longs for evening when he can rest from the slavery.
n180074: tn The expression вАП„Щ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Р÷Ј„£÷Њ„¶÷µ„ЬвАО (yish Њaf tsel, вАЬlonging for the evening shadowвАЭ) could also be taken as a relative clause (without the relative pronoun): вАЬas a servant [who] longs for the evening shadowвАЭ (see GKC 487 ¬І155.g). In either case, the expressions in v. 2 emphasize the point of the comparison, which will be summed up in v. 3.
n180075: tn The two verbs in this verse stress the eager expectation and waiting. The first, вАП„©„Б÷Є„Р÷Ј„£вАО (sha Њaf), means вАЬto long for; to desireвАЭ; and the second, вАП„І÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (qavah), has the idea of вАЬto hope for; to look for; to wait.вАЭ The words would give the sense that the servant or hired man had the longing on his mind all day.
n180076: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (po њal) means вАЬwork.вАЭ But here the word should be taken as a metonymy, meaning the pay for the work that he has done (compare Jer 22:13).
n180077: tn вАЬThusвАЭ indicates a summary of vv. 1 and 2: like the soldier, the mercenary, and the slave, Job has labored through life and looks forward to death.
n180078: tn The form is the Hophal perfect of вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (nakhal): вАЬI have been made to inherit,вАЭ or more simply, вАЬI have inherited.вАЭ The form occurs only here. The LXX must have confused the letters or sounds, a вАП„ХвАО (vav) for the вАП„ЯвАО (nun), for it reads вАЬI have endured.вАЭ As a passive the form technically has two accusatives (see GKC 388 ¬І121.c). JobвАЩs point is that his sufferings have been laid on him by another, and so he has inherited them.
n180079: tn The word is вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„РвАО (shav Њ, вАЬvanity, deception, nothingness, futilityвАЭ). His whole lifeвАФmarked here in months to show its brevityвАФhas been futile. E. Dhorme (Job, 98) suggests the meaning вАЬdisillusionment,вАЭ explaining that it marks the deceptive nature of mortal life. The word describes life as hollow, insubstantial.
n1800710: tn вАЬSorrowвАЭ is вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО ( њamal), used in 3:10. It denotes anxious toil, labor, troublesome effort. It may be that the verse expresses the idea that the nights are when the pains of his disease are felt the most. The months are completely wasted; the nights are agonizing.
n1800711: tn The verb is literally вАЬthey have appointedвАЭ; the form with no expressed subject is to be interpreted as a passive (GKC 460 ¬І144.g). It is therefore not necessary to repoint the verb to make it passive. The word means вАЬto number; to count,вАЭ and so вАЬto determine; to allocate.вАЭ
n1800712: tn This is the main clause, and not part of the previous conditional clause; it is introduced by the conjunction вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО (вАЩim) (see GKC 336 ¬І112.gg).
n1800713: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (madad) normally means вАЬto measure,вАЭ and here in the Piel it has been given the sense of вАЬto extend.вАЭ But this is not well attested and not widely accepted. There are many conjectural emendations. Of the most plausible one might mention the view of Gray, who changes вАП„Ю÷і„У÷Љ÷Ј„УвАО (middad, Piel of вАП„Ю÷Є„У÷Љ÷Ј„УвАО) to вАП„Ю÷і„У÷Љ÷µ„ЩвАО (midde, comprising the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО [min] plus the noun вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„ЩвАО [day], meaning вАЬas often asвАЭ): вАЬas often as evening comes.вАЭ Dhorme, following the LXX to some extent, adds the word вАЬdayвАЭ after вАЬwhen/ifвАЭ and replaces вАП„Ю÷і„У÷Љ÷Ј„УвАО (middad) with вАП„Ю÷Є„™÷Ј„ЩвАО (matay, вАЬwhenвАЭ) to read вАЬIf I lie down, I say, вАШWhen comes the morning?вАЩ If I rise up, I say, вАШHow long till evening?вАЩвАЭ The LXX, however, may be based more on a recollection of Deut 28:67. One can make just as strong a case for the reading adopted here, that the night seems to drag on (so also NIV).
n1800714: tn The Hebrew term вАП„†÷∞„У÷ї„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (nedudim, вАЬtossingвАЭ) refers to the restless tossing and turning of the sick man at night on his bed. The word is a hapax legomenon derived from the verb вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (nadad, вАЬto flee; to wander; to be restlessвАЭ). The plural form here sums up the several parts of the actions (GKC 460 ¬І144.f). E. Dhorme (Job, 99) argues that because it applies to both his waking hours and his sleepless nights, it may have more of the sense of wanderings of the mind. There is no doubt truth to the fact that the mind wanders in all this suffering, but there is no need to go beyond the contextually clear idea of the restlessness of the night.
n1800716: tn The implied comparison is vivid: the dirty scabs cover his entire body like a garmentвАФso he is clothed with them.
n1800717: sn The word for вАЬwormsвАЭ (вАП„®÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО, rimmah, a collective noun), is usually connected with rotten food (Exod 16:24), or the grave (Isa 14:11). JobвАЩs disease is a malignant ulcer of some kind that causes the rotting of the flesh. One may recall that both Antiochus Epiphanes (2 Macc 9:9) and Herod Agrippa (Acts 12:23) were devoured by such worms in their diseases.
n1800718: tn The text has вАЬclods of dust.вАЭ The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (gish, вАЬdirty scabsвАЭ) is a hapax legomenon from вАП„Т÷Љ„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО (gush, вАЬclodвАЭ). Driver suggests the word has a medical sense, like вАЬpustulesвАЭ (G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 73) or вАЬscabsвАЭ (JB, NEB, NAB, NIV). Driver thinks вАЬclods of dustвАЭ is wrong; he repoints вАЬdustвАЭ to make a new verb вАЬto cover,вАЭ cognate to Arabic, and reads вАЬmy flesh is clothed with worms, and scab covers my skin.вАЭ This refers to the dirty scabs that crusted over the sores all over his body. The LXX links this with the second half of the verse: вАЬAnd my body has been covered with loathsome worms, and I waste away, scraping off clods of dirt from my eruption.вАЭ
n1800719: tn The meaning of вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (raga њ) is also debated here. D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 163) does not think the word can mean вАЬcrackedвАЭ because scabs show evidence of the sores healing. But E. Dhorme (Job, 100) argues that the usage of the word shows the idea of вАЬsplitting, separating, making a break,вАЭ or the like. Here then it would mean вАЬmy skin splitsвАЭ and as a result festers. This need not be a reference to the scabs, but to new places. Or it could mean that the scabbing never heals, but is always splitting open.
n1800720: sn The first five verses described the painfulness of his malady, his life; now, in vv. 6-10 he will focus on the brevity of his life, and its extinction with death. He introduces the subject with вАЬmy days,вАЭ a metonymy for his whole life and everything done on those days. He does not mean individual daysвАФthey drag on endlessly.
n1800721: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (qalal) means вАЬto be lightвАЭ (40:4), and then by extension вАЬto be swift; to be rapidвАЭ (Jer 4:13; Hab 1:8).
n1800722: sn The shuttle is the part which runs through the meshes of the web. In Judg 16:14 this word refers to the loom (see BDB 71 s.v. вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„ТвАО), but here it must be the shuttle. Hezekiah uses the imagery of the weaver, the loom, and the shuttle for the brevity of life (see Isa 38:12). The LXX used, вАЬMy life is lighter than a word.вАЭ
n1800723: tn The text includes a wonderful wordplay on this word. The noun is вАП„™÷Љ÷і„І÷∞„Х÷Є„ФвАО (tiqvah, вАЬhopeвАЭ). But it can also have the meaning of one of its cognate nouns, вАП„І÷Ј„ХвАО (qav, вАЬthread, cord,вАЭ as in Josh 2:18, 21). He is saying that his life is coming to an end for lack of thread/for lack of hope (see further E. Dhorme, Job, 101).
n1800724: sn Job is probably turning here to God, as is clear from v. 11 on. The NIV supplies the word вАЬGodвАЭ for clarification. It was God who breathed breath into manвАЩs nostrils (Gen 2:7), and so God is called to remember that man is but a breath.
n1800725: tn The word вАЬthatвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n1800726: tn The verb with the infinitive serves as a verbal hendiadys: вАЬreturn to seeвАЭ means вАЬsee again.вАЭ
n1800727: sn The meaning of the verse is that God will relent, but it will be too late. God now sees him with a hostile eye; when he looks for him, or looks upon him in friendliness, it will be too late.
n1800728: tn This verse is omitted in the LXX and so by several commentators. But the verb вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„®вАО (shur, вАЬturn, returnвАЭ) is so characteristic of Job (10 times) that the verse seems appropriate here.
n1800729: tn The comparison is implied; вАЬasвАЭ is therefore supplied in the translation.
n1800730: tn The two verbs вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ФвАО (kalah) and вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh) mean вАЬto come to an endвАЭ and вАЬto goвАЭ respectively. The picture is of the cloud that breaks up, comes to an end, is dispersed so that it is no longer a cloud; it then fades away or vanishes. This line forms a good simile for the situation of a man who comes to his end and disappears.
n1800731: tn The noun вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol) can mean вАЬthe grave,вАЭ вАЬdeath,вАЭ or вАЬSheolвАЭвАФthe realm of departed spirits. In Job this is a land from which there is no return (10:21 and here). It is a place of darkness and gloom (10:21-22), a place where the dead lie hidden (14:13), a place appointed for all no matter what their standing on earth might have been (30:23). In each case the precise meaning has to be determined. Here the grave makes the most sense, for Job is simply talking about death.
n1800732: sn It is not correct to try to draw theological implications from this statement or the preceding verse (Rashi said Job was denying the resurrection). Job is simply stating that when people die they are goneвАФthey do not return to this present life on earth. Most commentators and theologians believe that theological knowledge was very limited at such an early stage, so they would not think it possible for Job to have bodily resurrection in view. (See notes on ch. 14 and 19:25-27.)
n1800733: tn M. Dahood suggests the meaning is the same as вАЬhis abodeвАЭ (вАЬHebrew-Ugaritic Lexicography V,вАЭ Bib 48 [1967]: 421-38).
n1800734: tn The verb means вАЬto recognizeвАЭ by seeing. вАЬHis place,вАЭ the place where he was living, is the subject of the verb. This personification is intended simply to say that the place where he lived will not have him any more. The line is very similar to Ps 103:16bвАФwhen the wind blows the flower away, its place knows it no more.
n1800735: tn вАЬAlso IвАЭ has been rendered frequently as вАЬtherefore,вАЭ introducing a conclusion. BDB 168-69 s.v. вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЭвАО lists Ps 52:7 [5] as a parallel, but it also could be explained as an adversative.
n1800736: sn вАЬMouthвАЭ here is metonymical for what he saysвАФhe will not withhold his complaints. Peake notes that in this section Job comes very close to doing what Satan said he would do. If he does not curse God to his face, he certainly does cast off restraints to his lament. But here Job excuses himself in advance of the lament.
n1800737: tn The verb is not limited to mental musing; it is used for pouring out a complaint or a lament (see S. Mowinckel, вАЬThe Verb siah and the Nouns siah, siha,вАЭ ST 15 [1961]: 1-10).
n1800738: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (tannin) could be translated вАЬwhaleвАЭ as well as the more mythological вАЬdragonвАЭ or вАЬmonster of the deepвАЭ (see E. Dhorme, Job, 105). To the Hebrews this was part of GodвАЩs creation in Gen 1; in the pagan world it was a force to be reckoned with, and so the reference would be polemical. The sea is a symbol of the tumultuous elements of creation; in the sea were creatures that symbolized the powerful forces of chaosвАФLeviathan, Tannin, and Rahab. They required special attention.
n1800739: tn The imperfect verb here receives the classification of obligatory imperfect. Job wonders if he is such a threat to God that God must do this.
n1800740: tn The word вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Є„®вАО (mishmar) means вАЬguard; barrier.вАЭ M. Dahood suggested вАЬmuzzleвАЭ based on Ugaritic, but that has proven to be untenable (вАЬMismar, вАШMuzzle,вАЩ in Job 7:12, вАЭ JBL 80 [1961]: 270-71).
n1800741: tn The particle вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) could also be translated вАЬwhen,вАЭ but вАЬifвАЭ might work better to introduce the conditional clause and to parallel the earlier reasoning of Job in v. 4 (using вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО,  Њim). See GKC 336-37 ¬І112.hh.
n1800742: tn The verb literally means вАЬsay,вАЭ but here the connotation must be вАЬthinkвАЭ or вАЬsay to oneselfвАЭвАФвАЬwhen I think my bedвА¶.вАЭ
n1800743: sn Sleep is the recourse of the troubled and unhappy. Here вАЬbedвАЭ is metonymical for sleep. Job expects sleep to give him the comfort that his friends have not.
n1800744: tn The verb means вАЬto lift up; to take awayвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО, nasa Њ). When followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) with the complement of the verb, the idea is вАЬto bear a part; to take a share,вАЭ or вАЬto share in the burdenвАЭ (cf. Num 11:7). The idea then would be that the sleep would ease the complaint. It would not end the illness, but the complaining for a while.
n1800745:
tn The Piel of вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„™вАО (khatat) occurs only here and in Jer 51:56 (where it is doubtful). The meaning is clearly вАЬstartle, scare.вАЭ The perfect verb with the вАП„ХвАО (vav) is fitting in the apodosis of the conditional sentence.
sn Here Job is boldly saying that it is God who is behind the horrible dreams that he is having at night.
n1800746: tn The Piel of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (ba њat, вАЬterrifyвАЭ) is one of the characteristic words in the book of Job; it occurs in 3:5; 9:34; 13:11, 21; 15:24; 18:11; and 33:7.
n1800747: tn The prepositions вАП„СвАО (bet) and вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) interchange here; they express the instrument of causality. See N. Sarna, вАЬThe Interchange of the Prepositions bet and min in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ JBL 78 (1959): 310-16. Emphasis on the instruments of terror in this verse is highlighted by the use of chiasm in which the prepositional phrases comprise the central elements (ab//bвАЩaвАЩ). Verse 18 contains another example.
n1800748: tn The word вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) is often translated вАЬsoul.вАЭ But since Hebrew thought does not make such a distinction between body and soul, it is usually better to translate it with вАЬperson.вАЭ When a suffix is added to the word, then that pronoun would serve as the better translation, as here with вАЬmy soulвАЭ = вАЬIвАЭ (meaning with every fiber of my being).
n1800749: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (bakhar, вАЬchooseвАЭ) followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) can have the sense of вАЬprefer.вАЭ
n1800750: tn The meaning of the term вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷≤„†÷Є„ІвАО (makhanaq, вАЬstranglingвАЭ), a hapax legomenon, is clear enough; the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ІвАО (khanaq) in the Piel means вАЬto strangleвАЭ (Nah 2:13), and in the Niphal вАЬto strangle oneselfвАЭ (2 Sam 17:23). This word has tempted some commentators to take вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) in a very restricted sense of вАЬthroat.вАЭ
n1800751: tn The conjunction вАЬandвАЭ is supplied in the translation. вАЬDeathвАЭ could also be taken in apposition to вАЬstrangling,вАЭ providing the outcome of the strangling.
n1800752: tn This is one of the few words recognizable in the LXX: вАЬYou will separate life from my spirit, and yet keep my bones from death.вАЭ
n1800753: tn The comparative вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) after the verb вАЬchooseвАЭ will here have the idea of preferring something before another (see GKC 429-30 ¬І133.b).
n1800754: tn The word вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„¶÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„™÷Є„ЩвАО (me њatsmotay) means вАЬmore than my bonesвАЭ (= life or being). The line is poetic; вАЬbonesвАЭ is often used in scripture metonymically for the whole living person, so there is no need here for conjectural emendation. Nevertheless, there have been several suggestions made. The simplest and most appealing for those who desire a change is the repointing to вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„¶÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷є„™÷Є„ЩвАО (me њatsevotay, вАЬmy sufferings,вАЭ adopted by NAB, JB, Moffatt, Driver-Gray, E. Dhorme, H. H. Rowley, and others). Driver obtains this idea by positing a new word based on Arabic without changing the letters; it means вАЬgreatвАЭвАФbut he has to supply the word вАЬsufferings.вАЭ
n1800755: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 107-8) thinks the idea of loathing or despising is problematic since there is no immediate object. He notes that the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„Р÷Ј„°вАО (ma Њas, вАЬloatheвАЭ) is parallel to вАП„Ю÷Є„°÷Ј„°вАО (masas, вАЬmeltвАЭ) in the sense of вАЬflow, dripвАЭ (Job 42:6). This would give the idea вАЬI am fading awayвАЭ or вАЬI grow weaker,вАЭ or as Dhorme chooses, вАЬI am pining away.вАЭ
n1800756: tn There is no object for the verb in the text. But the most likely object would be вАЬmy lifeвАЭ from the last verse, especially since in this verse Job will talk about not living forever. Some have thought the object should be вАЬdeath,вАЭ meaning that Job despised death more than the pains. But that is a forced meaning; besides, as H. H. Rowley points out, the word here means to despise something, to reject it. Job wanted death.
n1800757: tn Heb вАЬcease from me.вАЭ This construction means essentially вАЬleave me in peace.вАЭ
n1800758: tn This word вАП„Ф÷ґ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (hevel) is difficult to translate. It means вАЬbreath; puff of air; vaporвАЭ and then figuratively, вАЬvanity.вАЭ Job is saying that his life is but a breathвАФit is brief and fleeting. Cf. Ps 144:4 for a similar idea.
n1800759: tn The verse is a rhetorical question; it is intended to mean that man is too little for God to be making so much over him in all this.
n1800760: tn The Piel verb is a factitive meaning вАЬto magnify.вАЭ The English word вАЬmagnifyвАЭ might not be the best translation here, for God, according to Job, is focusing inordinately on him. It means to magnify in thought, appreciate, think highly of. God, Job argues, is making too much of mankind by devoting so much bad attention on them.
n1800761: tn The expression вАЬset your heart onвАЭ means вАЬconcentrate your mind onвАЭ or вАЬpay attention to.вАЭ
n1800762: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) is a very common one in the Bible; while it is frequently translated вАЬvisit,вАЭ the вАЬvisitвАЭ is never comparable to a social call. When God вАЬvisitsвАЭ people it always means a divine intervention for blessing or cursingвАФbut the visit always changes the destiny of the one visited. Here Job is amazed that God Almighty would be so involved in the life of mere human beings.
n1800763: tn Now the verb вАЬto testвАЭ is introduced and gives further explanation to the purpose of the вАЬvisitвАЭ in the parallel line (see the same parallelism in Ps 17:3). The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЯвАО (bakhan) has to do with passing things through the fire or the crucible to purify the metal (see Job 23:10; Zech 13:9); metaphorically it means вАЬto examine carefullyвАЭ and вАЬto purify by testing.вАЭ
n1800764: sn The amazing thing is the regularity of the testing. Job is at first amazed that God would visit him, but even more is he amazed that God is testing him every moment. The employment of a chiasm with the two temporal adverbial phrases as the central elements emphasizes the regularity.
n1800765: tn Heb вАЬaccording to what [= how long] will you not look away from me.вАЭ
n1800766: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (sha њah, вАЬto lookвАЭ) with the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) means вАЬto look away from; to avert oneвАЩs gaze.вАЭ Job wonders if God would not look away from him even briefly, for the constant vigilance is killing him.
n1800767: tn The Hiphil of вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (rafah) means вАЬto leave someone alone.вАЭ
n1800768: tn The simple perfect verb can be used in a conditional sentence without a conditional particle present (see GKC 494 ¬І159.h).
n1800769: sn Job is not here saying that he has sinned; rather, he is posing the hypothetical conditionвАФif he had sinned, what would that do to God? In other words, he has not really injured God.
n1800770: sn In the Bible God is often described as watching over people to protect them from danger (see Deut 32:10; Ps 31:23). However, here it is a hostile sense, for God may detect sin and bring it to judgment.
n1800771: tn This word is a hapax legomenon from the verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Є„ҐвАО (paga њ, вАЬmeet, encounterвАЭ); it would describe what is hit or struck (as nouns of this pattern can indicate the place of the action)вАФthe target.
n1800772: tn In the prepositional phrase вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЩвАО ( њalay) the results of a scribal change are found (these changes were called tiqqune sopherim, вАЬcorrections of the scribesвАЭ made to avoid using improper language about God). The prepositional phrase would have been вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО ( њalekha, вАЬto you,вАЭ as in the LXX). But it offended the Jews to think of Job being burdensome to God. JobвАЩs sin could have repercussions on him, but not on God.
n1800773: tn The LXX has, вАЬfor now I will depart to the earth.вАЭ
n1800774: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shakhar) in the Piel has been translated вАЬto seek early in the morningвАЭ because of the possible link with the word вАЬdawn.вАЭ But the verb more properly means вАЬto seek diligentlyвАЭ (by implication).
n180081: sn This speech of Bildad ignores JobвАЩs attack on his friends and focuses rather on JobвАЩs comments about GodвАЩs justice. Bildad cannot even imagine saying that God is unjust. The only conclusion open to him is that JobвАЩs family brought this on themselves, and so the only recourse is for Job to humble himself and make supplication to God. To make his point, Bildad will appeal to the wisdom of the ancients, for his theology is traditional. The speech has three parts: vv. 2-7 form his affirmation of the justice of God; vv. 8-19 are his appeal to the wisdom of the ancients, and vv. 20-22 are his summation. See N. C. Habel, вАЬAppeal to Ancient Tradition as a Literary Form,вАЭ ZAW 88 (1976): 253-72; W. A. Irwin, вАЬThe First Speech of Bildad,вАЭ ZAW 51 (1953): 205-16.
n180082: sn вАЬThese thingsвАЭ refers to all of JobвАЩs speech, the general drift of which seems to Bildad to question the justice of God.
n180083: tn The second colon of the verse simply says вАЬand a strong wind the words of your mouth.вАЭ The simplest way to treat this is to make it an independent nominal sentence: вАЬthe words of your mouth are a strong wind.вАЭ Some have made it parallel to the first by apposition, understanding вАЬhow longвАЭ to do double duty. The line beginning with the вАП„ХвАО (vav) can also be subordinated as a circumstantial clause, as here.
n180084: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„®вАО (kabbir, вАЬgreatвАЭ) implies both abundance and greatness. Here the word modifies вАЬwindвАЭ; the point of the analogy is that JobвАЩs words are full of sound but without solid content.
n180085: tn See, however, G. R. DriverвАЩs translation, вАЬthe breath of one who is mighty are the words of your mouthвАЭ (вАЬHebrew Studies,вАЭ JRAS 1948: 170).
n180086: tn The Piel verb вАП„Щ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷µ„™вАО (ye њavvet) means вАЬto bend; to cause to swerve from the norm; to deviate; to pervert.вАЭ The LXX renders the first colon as вАЬwill the Lord be unjust when he judges?вАЭ
n180087: tn The first word is вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„™вАО (mishpat, вАЬjusticeвАЭ). It can mean an act of judgment, place of judgment, or what is just, that is, the outcome of the decision. It basically describes an umpireвАЩs decision. The parallel word is вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq, вАЬrighteousness,вАЭ or вАЬwhat is rightвАЭ). The basic idea here is that which conforms to the standard, what is right. See S. H. Scholnick, вАЬThe Meaning of Mishpat in the Book of Job,вАЭ JBL 101 (1982): 521-29.
n180088: tn Some commentators think that the second verb should be changed in order to avoid the repetition of the same word and to reflect the different words in the versions. The suggestion is to read вАП„Щ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (ye њavveh) instead; this would mean вАЬto cause someone to deviate,вАЭ for the root means вАЬto bend.вАЭ The change is completely unwarranted; the LXX probably chose different words for stylistic reasons (see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 198). The repetition in the Hebrew text is a common type; it strengthens the enormity of the charge Job seems to be making.
n180089: tn The AV and RV take the protasis down to the middle of v. 6. The LXX changes the вАЬifвАЭ at the beginning of v. 5 to вАЬthenвАЭ and makes that verse the apodosis. If the apodosis comes in the second half of v. 4, then v. 4 would be a complete sentence (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 71; A. B. Davidson, Job, 60). The particle вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) has the sense of вАЬsinceвАЭ in this section.
n1800810: tn The verb is a Piel preterite with a vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. The вАП„ХвАО (vav) need not be translated if the second half of the verse is the apodosis of the firstвАФsince they sinnedвА¶he did this. The verb вАП„©„Б÷і„Ь÷Љ÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shilleakh) means вАЬto expel; to thrust outвАЭ normally; here the sense of вАЬdeliver upвАЭ or вАЬdeliver overвАЭ fits the sentence well. The verse is saying that sin carries its own punishment, and so God merely delivered the young people over to it.
n1800811: tn Heb вАЬinto the hand of their rebellion.вАЭ The word вАЬhandвАЭ often signifies вАЬpower.вАЭ The rebellious acts have the power to destroy, and so that is what happenedвАФaccording to Bildad. BildadвАЩs point is that Job should learn from what happened to his family.
n1800812: tn вАЬButвАЭ is supplied to show the contrast between this verse and the preceding line.
n1800813: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷і„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shikhar) means вАЬto seek; to seek earnestlyвАЭ (see 7:21). With the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel) the verb may carry the nuance of вАЬto address; to have recourse toвАЭ (see E. Dhorme, Job, 114). The LXX connected it etymologically to вАЬearlyвАЭ and read, вАЬBe early in prayer to the Lord Almighty.вАЭ
n1800814: tn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„™÷∞„Ч÷Ј„†÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО (titkhannan) means вАЬto make supplication; to seek favor; to seek graceвАЭ (from вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО, khanan). Bildad is saying that there is only one way for Job to escape the same fate as his childrenвАФhe must implore GodвАЩs mercy. JobвАЩs speech had spoken about GodвАЩs seeking him and not finding him, but Bildad is speaking of the importance of JobвАЩs seeking God.
n1800815: tn A verb form needs to be supplied here. Bildad is not saying to Job, вАЬIf you are pure [as you say you are].вАЭ Bildad is convinced that Job is a sinner. Therefore, вАЬIf you become pureвАЭ makes more sense here.
n1800817: tn Many commentators delete this colon as a moralizing gloss on v. 5, but the phrase makes good sense and simply serves as another condition. Besides, the expression is in the LXX.
n1800818: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷і„Щ„®вАО (ya њir, вАЬrouse, stir upвАЭ) is a strong anthropomorphism. The LXX has вАЬhe will answer your prayerвАЭ (which is probably only the LXXвАЩs effort to avoid the anthropomorphism [D. J. A. Clines, Job (WBC), 198]). A reading of вАЬwatch over youвАЭ has been adopted because of parallel texts (see H. L. Ginsberg, вАЬTwo North Canaanite Letters from Ugarit,вАЭ BASOR 72 [1938]: 18-19; and H. N. Richardson, вАЬA Ugaritic Letter of a King to His Mother,вАЭ JBL 66 [1947]: 321-24). Others suggest вАЬhis light will shine on youвАЭ or вАЬhe will bestow health on you.вАЭ But the idea of вАЬawakeвАЭ is common enough in the Bible to be retained here.
n1800819: tn The Piel of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (shalam) means вАЬto make good; to repay; to restore something to its wholeness; to reestablish.вАЭ The best understanding here would be вАЬrestore [Job] to his place.вАЭ Some take the verb in the sense of вАЬreward [Job himself] with a righteous habitation.вАЭ
n1800820: tn The construct вАП„†÷∞„Х÷Ј„™вАО (nevat) is feminine; only the masculine occurs in Hebrew. But the meaning вАЬabode of your righteousnessвАЭ is clear enough. The righteousness of Job is pictured as inhabiting an estate, or it pictures the place where Job lives as a righteous man. A translation вАЬrightful habitationвАЭ would mean вАЬthe habitation that you deserveвАЭвАФif you are righteous.
n1800821: tn The reference to вАЬyour beginningвАЭ is a reference to JobвАЩs former estate of wealth and peace. The reference to вАЬlatter endвАЭ is a reference to conditions still in the future. What Job had before will seem so small in comparison to what lies ahead.
n1800822: tn The verb has the idea of вАЬto growвАЭ; here it must mean вАЬto flourish; to grow considerablyвАЭ or the like. The statement is not so much a prophecy; rather Bildad is saying that вАЬif Job had recourse to God, thenвА¶.вАЭ This will be fulfilled, of course, at the end of the book.
n1800823: sn Bildad is not calling for Job to trace through the learning of antiquity, but of the most recent former generation. Hebrews were fond of recalling what the вАЬfathersвАЭ had taught, for each generation recalled what their fathers had taught.
n1800824: tn The verb вАП„Ы„Х÷є„†÷µ„ЯвАО (khonen, from вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО, kun) normally would indicate вАЬprepare yourselfвАЭ or вАЬfixвАЭ oneвАЩs heart on something, i.e., give attention to it. The verb with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition after it does mean вАЬto think onвАЭ or вАЬto meditateвАЭ (Isa 51:13). But some commentators wish to change the вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) to a вАП„СвАО (bet) in the verb to get вАЬto considerвАЭ (from вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО, bin). However, M. Dahood shows a connection between вАП„Ы„†„ЯвАО (knn) and вАП„©„Б„Р„ЬвАО (sh Њl) in Ugaritic (вАЬHebrew-Ugaritic Lexicography,вАЭ Bib 46 [1965]: 329).
n1800825: tn The Hebrew has вАЬthe search of their fathers,вАЭ but the word is probably intended to mean what that observation or search yielded (so вАЬsearchвАЭ is a metonymy of cause).
n1800827: tn The Hebrew has вАЬwe are of yesterday,вАЭ the adverb functioning as a predicate. BildadвАЩs point is that they have not had time to acquire great knowledge because they are recent.
n1800828: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 116) observes that the shadow is the symbol of ephemeral things (14:2; 17:7; Ps 144:4). The shadow passes away quickly (116).
n1800829: tn The sentence begins emphatically: вАЬIs it not they.вАЭ
n1800830: tn The вАЬandвАЭ is not present in the line. The second clause seems to be in apposition to the first, explaining it more thoroughly: вАЬIs it not they [who] will instruct you, [who] will speak to you.вАЭ
n1800831: tn The noun may have been left indeterminate for the sake of emphasis (GKC 401-2 ¬І125.c), meaning вАЬimportant words.вАЭ
n1800833: sn H. H. Rowley observes the use of the words for plants that grow in Egypt and suspects that Bildad either knew Egypt or knew that much wisdom came from Egypt. The first word refers to papyrus, which grows to a height of six feet (so the verb means вАЬto grow tall; to grow highвАЭ). The second word refers to the reed grass that grows on the banks of the river (see Gen 41:2, 18).
n1800834: tn The two verbs, вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ga Њah) and вАП„©„В÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (sagah), have almost the same meanings of вАЬflourish, grow, become tall.вАЭ
n1800835: tn The word has been traditionally translated вАЬgreennessвАЭ (so KJV, ASV), but some modern commentators argue for вАЬin flower.вАЭ The word is found only in Song 6:11 (where it may be translated вАЬblossomsвАЭ). From the same root is вАП„Р÷Є„С÷і„Щ„СвАО ( Њaviv, вАЬfresh young ears of barleyвАЭ). Here the word refers to the plant that is still in its early stages of flowering. It should not be translated to suggest the plant is flowering (so NRSV), but translating as if the plant is green (so NASB) is also problematic.
n1800836: sn The idea is that as the plant begins to flower, but before it is to be cut down, there is no sign of withering or decay in it. But if the water is withdrawn, it will wither sooner than any other herb. The point Bildad will make of this is that when people rebel against God and his grace is withheld, they perish more swiftly than the water reed.
n1800837: tn The imperfect verb here is the modal use of potential, вАЬcan wither awayвАЭ if the water is not there.
n1800839: tn The LXX interprets the line: вАЬdoes not any herb wither before it has received moisture?вАЭ
n1800840: tn The word вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њorkhot) means вАЬwaysвАЭ or вАЬpathsвАЭ in the sense of tracks of destiny or fate. The word вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh, вАЬway, road, pathвАЭ) is used in a similar way (Isa 40:27; Ps 37:5). However, many commentators emend the text to read вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™вАО ( Њakharit, вАЬendвАЭ) in harmony with the LXX. But Prov 1:19 (if not emended as well) confirms the primary meaning here without changing the text (see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 199).
n1800841: tn The word вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷µ„£вАО (khanef) is often translated вАЬhypocrite.вАЭ But the root verb means вАЬto be profane,вАЭ and this would be done by idolatry or bloodshed. It describes an irreligious person, a godless person. In Dan 11:32 the word seems to mean вАЬmake someone pagan.вАЭ The word in this verse is parallel to вАЬthose who forget God.вАЭ
n1800842: tn The relative pronoun introduces the verse as a relative clause, working with the вАЬgodless personвАЭ of the preceding verse. The relative pronoun is joined to the resumptive pronoun in the translation: вАЬwho + his trustвАЭ = вАЬwhose trust.вАЭ
n1800843: tn The noun вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„°÷ґ„ЬвАО (kesel) in this half of the verse must correspond to вАЬhis securityвАЭ in the second half. The meaning must be вАЬhis trustвАЭ (see 4:6). The two words will again be parallel in 31:24.
n1800844: tn The word вАП„Щ÷Є„І„Х÷є„ШвАО (yaqot) is not known anywhere else; here it looks like it should be a noun to parallel вАЬspiderвАЩs houseвАЭ in the next colon. But scholars have tried to identify it as a verb, perhaps an imperfect of вАП„І„Х÷є„ШвАО (qot, BDB 876 s.v.), or related to an Arabic qatta, вАЬto cut.вАЭ Some versions have вАЬbreak in sunderвАЭ (ASV, RV); others вАЬcut offвАЭ (RSV). Apart from verbs, some commentators follow Sa`adiaвАЩs Arabic translation вАЬsun cords,вАЭ meaning вАЬgossamer.вАЭ Accordingly, there are emendations like вАЬthreads,вАЭ вАЬthreads of summer,вАЭ вАЬspider threads,вАЭ and the like. D. J. A. Clines agrees with those who conclude that emendations based on Sa`adiaвАЩs translation lack a sound philological basis. E. Dhorme вАЬsomewhat timidlyвАЭ suggests вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„ШвАО (yalqut), the shepherdвАЩs bag or scrip (1 Sam 17:40). He suggests that an empty bag would be a symbol of something unstable and futile. It seems impossible to determine exactly what the word meant. One can only conclude that it means something like вАЬfragileвАЭ or вАЬfutile.вАЭ The LXX is of no help: вАЬfor his house shall be without inhabitants.вАЭ
n1800845: sn The second half of the verse is very clear. What the godless person relies on for security is as fragile as a spiderвАЩs webвАФhe may as well have nothing. The people of the Middle East view the spiderвАЩs web as the frailest of all вАЬhouses.вАЭ
n1800846: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО ( њamad, вАЬto standвАЭ) is almost synonymous with the parallel вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum, вАЬto rise; to standвАЭ). The distinction is that the former means вАЬto remain standingвАЭ (so it is translated here вАЬhold upвАЭ), and the latter вАЬrise, stand up.вАЭ
n1800847: sn The idea is that he grabs hold of the house, not to hold it up, but to hold himself up or support himself. But it cannot support him. This idea applies to both the spiderвАЩs web and the false security of the pagan.
n1800848: tn The figure now changes to a plant that is flourishing and spreading and then suddenly cut off. The word вАП„®÷Є„Ш÷Ј„СвАО (ratav) means вАЬto be moist; to be watered.вАЭ The word occurs in Arabic, Aramaic, and Akkadian, but only twice in the Bible: here as the adjective and in 24:8 as the verb.
n1800849: tn The Hebrew is вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne, вАЬbeforeвАЭ). Does this mean вАЬin the presence of the sun,вАЭ i.e., under a sweltering sun, or вАЬbeforeвАЭ the sun rises? It seems more natural to take вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne) as вАЬin the presence ofвАЭ or вАЬunder.вАЭ
n1800851: tc Some have emended this phrase to obtain вАЬover the roofs.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬout of his corruption.вАЭ H. M. Orlinsky has shown that this reading arose from an internal LXX change, saprias having replaced prasias, вАЬgardenвАЭ (JQR 26 [1935/36]: 134-35).
n1800852: tn Cheyne reads вАЬspringвАЭ or вАЬwellвАЭ rather than вАЬheap.вАЭ However, this does not fit the parallelism very well, and so he emends the second half as well. Nevertheless the Hebrew text needs no emending here.
n1800853: tn The idea is that the plant grows, looking for a place to grow among the stones. Some trees grow so tightly around the rocks and stones that they are impossible to uproot. The rocky ground where it grows forms вАЬa house of stones.вАЭ The LXX supports an emendation from вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷±„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО (yekhezeh, вАЬit looksвАЭ) to вАП„Щ÷і„Ч÷∞„Щ÷ґ„ФвАО (yikhyeh, вАЬit livesвАЭ). Others have tried to emend the text in a variety of ways: вАЬpushesвАЭ (Budde), вАЬcleaveвАЭ (Gordis), вАЬwas oppositeвАЭ (Driver), or вАЬrun againstвАЭ (NEB, probably based on G. R. Driver). If one were to make a change, the reading with the LXX would be the easiest to defend, but there is no substantial reason to do that. The meaning is about the same without such a change.
n1800854: sn The idea seems to be that the stones around which the roots of the tree wrap themselves suggest strength and security for the tree, but uprooting comes to it nevertheless (v. 18). The point is that the wicked may appear to be living in security and flourishing, yet can be quickly destroyed (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 74).
n1800855:
tc Ball reads вАП„Р÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬGodвАЭ) instead of вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬifвАЭ): вАЬGod destroys itвАЭвАФbut there is no reason for this. The idea would be implied in the context. A. B. Davidson rightly points out that who destroys it is not important, but the fact that it is destroyed.
tn The Hebrew has вАЬif one destroys itвАЭ; the indefinite subject allows for a passive interpretation. The verb means вАЬswallowвАЭ in the Qal, but in the Piel it means вАЬto engulf; to destroy; to ruinвАЭ (2:3; 10:8). It could here be rendered вАЬremoved from its placeвАЭ (the place where it is rooted); since the picture is that of complete destruction, вАЬuprootedвАЭ would be a good rendering.
n1800856:
tn Heb вАЬitвАЭ; the referent (вАЬhis placeвАЭ in the preceding line) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
sn The place where the plant once grew will deny ever knowing it. Such is the completeness of the uprooting that there is not a trace left.
n1800857: tn Here вАЬsayingвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n1800858: tn This line is difficult. If the MT stands as it is, the expression must be ironic. It would be saying that the joy (all the security and prosperity) of its way (its life) is short-livedвАФthat is the way its joy goes. Most commentators are not satisfied with this. Dhorme, for one, changes вАП„Ю÷∞„©„В„Х÷є„©„ВвАО (mesos, вАЬjoyвАЭ) to вАП„Ю÷∞„°„Х÷є„°вАО (mesos, вАЬrottingвАЭ), and gets вАЬbehold him lie rotting on the path.вАЭ The sibilants can interchange this way. But Dhorme thinks the MT was written the way it was because the word was thought to be вАЬjoy,вАЭ when it should have been the other way. The word вАЬwayвАЭ then becomes an accusative of place. The suggestion is rather compelling and would certainly fit the context. The difficulty is that a root вАП„°„Х÷Љ„°вАО (sus, вАЬto rotвАЭ) has to be proposed. E. Dhorme does this by drawing on Arabic sas, вАЬto be eaten by moths or worms,вАЭ thus вАЬworm-eaten; decaying; rotting.вАЭ Cf. NIV вАЬits life withers awayвАЭ; also NAB вАЬthere he lies rotting beside the road.вАЭ
n1800860: sn As with the tree, so with the godless manвАФhis place will soon be taken by another.
n1800861: sn This is the description that the book gave to Job at the outset, a description that he deserved according to GodвАЩs revelation. The theme вАЬGod will not reject the blameless manвАЭ becomes JobвАЩs main point (see 9:20-21; 10:3).
n1800862: sn The idiom вАЬto grasp the handвАЭ of someone means to support or help the person.
n1800863: tn The word вАП„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО ( њad, вАЬuntilвАЭ) would give the reading вАЬuntil he fills your mouth with laughter,вАЭ subordinating the verse to the preceding with some difficulty in interpretation. It would be saying that God will not reject the blameless man until he filled Job with joy. Almost all commentators and modern versions change the pointing to вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО ( њod, вАЬyetвАЭ), forming a hope for the future blessing of joy for Job.
n1800864: sn вАЬLaughterвАЭ (and likewise вАЬgladnessвАЭ) will here be metonymies of effect or adjunct, being put in place of the reason for the joyвАФrestoration.
n1800865: sn These verses show several points of similarity with the style of the book of Psalms. вАЬThose who hate youвАЭ and the вАЬevil-doersвАЭ are fairly common words to describe the ungodly in the Psalms. вАЬThose who hate youвАЭ are enemies of the righteous man because of the parallelism in the verse. By this line Bildad is showing Job that he and his friends are not among those who are his enemies, and that Job himself is really among the righteous. It is an appealing way to end the discourse. See further G. W. Anderson, вАЬEnemies and Evil-doers in the Book of Psalms,вАЭ BJRL 48 (1965/66): 18-29.
n1800866: tn вАЬShameвАЭ is compared to a garment that can be worn. The вАЬshameвАЭ envisioned here is much more than embarrassment or disgraceвАФit is utter destruction. For parallels in the Psalms, see Pss 35:26; 132:18; 109:29.
n180091: sn This speech of Job in response to Bildad falls into two large sections, chs. 9 and 10. In ch. 9 he argues that GodвАЩs power and majesty prevent him from establishing his integrity in his complaint to God. And in ch. 10 Job tries to discover in GodвАЩs plan the secret of his afflictions. The speech seems to continue what Job was saying to Eliphaz more than it addresses Bildad. See K. Fullerton, вАЬOn Job 9 and 10,вАЭ JBL 53 (1934): 321-49.
n180092: tn The adverb вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷∞„†÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њomnam, вАЬin truthвАЭ) is characteristic of the book of Job (12:2; 19:4; 34:12; 36:4). The friends make commonplace statements, general truths, and Job responds with вАЬtruly I know this is so.вАЭ Job knows as much about these themes as his friends do.
n180093: sn The interrogative is used to express what is an impossibility.
n180094: tn The attempt to define вАП„Р÷±„†„Х÷є„©„БвАО ( Њenosh) as вАЬweakвАЭ or вАЬmortalвАЭ man is not compelling. Such interpretations are based on etymological links without the clear support of usage (an issue discussed by J. Barr, Comparative Philology and the Text of the Old Testament). This seems to be a poetic word for вАЬhumanвАЭ (the only nonpoetic use is in 2 Chr 14:10).
n180095: tn The preposition is вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬwith, before, in the presence ofвАЭ). This is more specific than вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) in 4:17.
n180096: sn The point of JobвАЩs rhetorical question is that man cannot be justified as against God, because God is too powerful and too cleverвАФhe controls the universe. He is discussing now the question that Eliphaz raised in 4:17. Peake observes that Job is raising the question of whether something is right because God says it is right, or that God declares it right because it is right.
n180097: tn Some commentators take God to be the subject of this verb, but it is more likely that it refers to the mortal who tries to challenge God in a controversy. The verb is used of Job in 13:3.
n180098: tn The verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) is a common one; it has the idea of вАЬcontention; dispute; legal dispute or controversy; go to law.вАЭ With the preposition вАП„Ґ÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) the idea must be вАЬto contend withвАЭ or вАЬto dispute with.вАЭ The preposition reflects the prepositional phrase вАЬwith GodвАЭ in v. 2, supporting the view that man is the subject.
n180099: tn This use of the imperfect as potential imperfect assumes that the human is the subject, that in a dispute with God he could not answer one of GodвАЩs questions (for which see the conclusion of the book when God questions Job). On the other hand, if the interpretation were that God does not answer the demands of mortals, then a simple progressive imperfect would be required. In support of this is the frustration of Job that God does not answer him.
n1800910:
tn The genitive phrase translated вАЬin heartвАЭ would be a genitive of specification, specifying that the wisdom of God is in his intelligent decisions.
sn The heart is the seat of intelligence and understanding, the faculty of decision making.
n1800911: sn The words вАП„Р÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„•вАО ( Њammits) and вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО (koakh) are synonyms, the first meaning вАЬsturdy; mighty; robust,вАЭ and the second вАЬstrength.вАЭ It too can be interpreted as a genitive of specificationвАФGod is mighty with respect to his power. But that comes close to expressing a superlative idea (like вАЬsong of songsвАЭ or вАЬanger of his wrathвАЭ).
n1800912: tn The first half of the verse simply has вАЬwise of heart and mighty of strength.вАЭ The entire line is a casus pendens that will refer to the suffix on вАП„Р÷µ„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( Њelayv) in the second colon. So the question is вАЬWho has resisted the one who is wise of heart and mighty of strength?вАЭ Again, the rhetorical question is affirming that no one has done this.
n1800913: tn The verb is the Hiphil of the verb вАП„І÷Є„©„Б÷Є„ФвАО (qashah, вАЬto be hardвАЭ). It frequently is found with the word for вАЬneck,вАЭ describing people as вАЬstiff-necked,вАЭ i.e., stubborn, unbending. So the idea of resisting God fits well. The fact that this word occurs in Exodus with the idea of hardening the heart against God may indicate that there is an allusion to Pharaoh here.
n1800914: tn The use of вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷µ„ЭвАО (shalem) in the Qal is rare. It has been translated вАЬremain safeвАЭ by E. Dhorme, вАЬsurvivedвАЭ by the NEB, вАЬremained unscathedвАЭ by the NAB and NIV, or вАЬsucceededвАЭ by KJV, G. R. Driver.
n1800915: tn The verb is plural: вАЬthey do not know it.вАЭ This suggests that the mountains would not know it. Some follow the Syriac with a singular verb, i.e., God does not know it, meaning, it is so trifling to God that he can do it without thinking. But the better interpretation may be вАЬsuddenly.вАЭ This would be interpreted from the MT as it stands; it would imply вАЬbefore they know anything,вАЭ thus вАЬsuddenlyвАЭ (Gray, Dhorme, Buttenwieser, et. al.). D. W. Thomas connects the meaning to another verb based on Arabic and translates it, вАЬ so that they are no longer stillвАЭ (вАЬAdditional Notes on the Root yada њ in Hebrew,вАЭ JTS 15 [1964]: 54-57). J. A. Emerton works with a possible root вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) meaning вАЬbe stillвАЭ (вАЬA Consideration of Some Alleged Meanings of yada` in Hebrew,вАЭ JSS 15 [1970]: 145-80).
n1800916: sn This line beginning with the relative pronoun can either be read as a parallel description of God, or it can be subordinated by the relative pronoun to the first (вАЬthey do not know who overturned themвАЭ).
n1800917: sn Shakes the earth out of its place probably refers to earthquakes, although some commentators protest against this in view of the idea of the pillars. In the ancient world the poetical view of the earth is that it was a structure on pillars, with water around it and under it. In an earthquake the pillars were shaken, and the earth moved.
n1800918: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷і„™÷∞„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Є„•вАО (hitpallats) is found only here, but the root seems clearly to mean вАЬto be tossed; to be thrown about,вАЭ and so in the Hitpael вАЬquiver; shake; tremble.вАЭ One of the three nouns from this root is вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Є„¶„Х÷Љ„™вАО (pallatsut), the вАЬshudderвАЭ that comes with terror (see Job 21:6; Isa 21:4; Ezek 7:18; Ps 55:6).
n1800919: tn The form could also be subordinated: вАЬthat it shine notвАЭ (see further GKC 323 ¬І109.g).
n1800920:
tn The verb вАП„Ц÷Є„®÷Ј„ЧвАО (zarakh) means вАЬrise.вАЭ This is the ordinary word for the sunrise. But here it probably has the idea of вАЬshine; glisten,вАЭ which is also attested in Hebrew and Aramaic.
sn There are various views on the meaning of this line in this verse. Some think it refers to some mysterious darkness like the judgment in Egypt (Exod 10:21-23), or to clouds building (3:5), often in accompaniment of earthquakes (see Joel 2:10; 3:15-16; Isa 13:10-13). It could also refer to an eclipse. All this assumes that the phenomenon here is limited to the morning or the day, but it could simply be saying that God controls light and darkness.
n1800921: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„ЭвАО (khatam) with вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (be њad) before its complement, means вАЬto seal; to wall up; to enclose.вАЭ This is a poetic way of saying that God prevents the stars from showing their light.
n1800923: tn The reference is probably to the waves of the sea. This is the reading preserved in NIV and NAB, as well as by J. Crenshaw, вАЬWed≈НrƒУk  њal-bƒБm≈Пt√™  ЊƒБreбє£,вАЭ CBQ 34 (1972): 39-53. But many see here a reference to Canaanite mythology. The marginal note in the RSV has вАЬthe back of the sea dragon.вАЭ The view would also see in вАЬseaвАЭ the Ugaritic god Yammu.
n1800924: sn The Hebrew has вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„БвАО ( њash), although in 38:32 it is вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Щ÷і„©„БвАО ( њayish). This has been suggested to be Aldebaran, a star in the constellation Taurus, but there have been many other suggestions put forward by the commentaries.
n1800925: sn There is more certainty for the understanding of this word as Orion, even though there is some overlap of the usage of the words in the Bible. In classical literature we have the same stereotypical reference to these three (see E. Dhorme, Job, 131).
n1800926: sn The identification of this as the Pleiades is accepted by most (the Vulgate has вАЬHyadesвАЭ). In classical Greek mythology, the seven Pleiades were seven sisters of the Hyades who were pursued by Orion until they were changed into stars by Zeus. The Greek myth is probably derived from an older Semitic myth.
n1800927: tn Heb вАЬand the chambers of the south.вАЭ
n1800928:
tn Only slight differences exist between this verse and 5:9 which employs the simple вАП„ХвАО (vav) conjunction before вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО ( Њen) in the first colon and omits the вАП„ХвАО (vav) conjunction before вАП„†÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р„Х÷є„™вАО (nifla Њot, вАЬwonderful thingsвАЭ) in the second colon.
sn There is probably great irony in JobвАЩs using this same verse as in 5:9. But JobвАЩs meaning here is different than that of Eliphaz.
n1800929: tn The NIV has вАЬwhenвАЭ to form a temporal clause here. For the use of вАЬif,вАЭ see GKC 497 ¬І159.w.
n1800930: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse are consistent with the clauses. In the conditional clauses a progressive imperfect is used, but in the following clauses the verbs are potential imperfects.
n1800931: tn The pronoun вАЬhimвАЭ is supplied here; it is not in MT, but the Syriac and Vulgate have it (probably for translation purposes as well).
n1800932: sn Like the mountains, Job knows that God has passed by and caused him to shake and tremble, but he cannot understand or perceive the reasons.
n1800933: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 133) surveys the usages and concludes that the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„£вАО (khataf) normally describes the wicked actions of a man, especially by treachery or trickery against another. But a verb вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„£вАО (khataf) is found nowhere else; a noun вАЬrobberвАЭ is found in Prov 23:28. Dhorme sees no reason to emend the text, because he concludes that the two verbs are synonymous. Job is saying that if God acts like a plunderer, there is no one who can challenge what he does.
n1800934: tn The verb is the Hiphil imperfect (potential again) from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv). In this stem it can mean вАЬturn back, refute, repelвАЭ (BDB 999 s.v. Hiph.5).
n1800935: sn The meaning of the line is that GodвАЩs anger will continue until it has accomplished its purpose (23:13-14).
n1800936: sn вАЬRahabвАЭ is not to be confused with the harlot of the same name from Jericho. вАЬRahabвАЭ is identified with Tiamat of the Babylonian creation epic, or Leviathan of the Canaanite myths. It is also used in parallelism to the sea (26:12), or the Red Sea (Ps 74:13), and so comes to symbolize Egypt (Isa 30:7). In the Babylonian Creation Epic there is reference to the helpers of Tiamat. In the Bible the reference is only to the raging sea, which the Lord controlled at creation.
n1800937: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh) means вАЬto be prostrateвАЭ or вАЬto crouch.вАЭ Here the enemies are prostrate under the feet of GodвАФthey are crushed.
n1800938: tn The construction вАП„Р÷Ј„£ „Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Њ„Р÷Є„†÷є„Ы÷і„ЩвАО ( Њaf ki  Њanokhi) is an expression that means either вАЬhow much moreвАЭ or вАЬhow much less.вАЭ Here it has to mean вАЬhow much less,вАЭ for if powerful forces like Rahab are crushed beneath GodвАЩs feet, how could Job contend with him?
n1800939: tn The imperfect verb here is to be taken with the nuance of a potential imperfect. The idea of вАЬanswer himвАЭ has a legal context, i.e., answering God in a court of law. If God is relentless in his anger toward greater powers, then Job realizes it is futile for him.
n1800940: sn In a legal controversy with God it would be essential to choose the correct words very carefully (humanly speaking), but the calmness and presence of mind to do that would be shattered by the overwhelming terror of GodвАЩs presence.
n1800941: tn The verb is supplied in this line.
n1800942: tn The preposition вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬwithвАЭ) carries the idea of вАЬin contest withвАЭ in a number of passages (compare vv. 2, 3; 16:21).
n1800943: tn The LXX goes a different way after changing the first person to the third: вАЬOh then that he would hearken to me, or judge my cause.вАЭ
n1800944: tn The line begins with вАП„Р÷≤„©„Б÷ґ„®вАО ( Њasher, вАЬwhichвАЭ), which is omitted in the LXX and the Syriac. The particle вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) can introduce a concessive clause (GKC 498 ¬І160.a) or a conditional clause (GKC 495 ¬І159.n). The idea here seems to be вАЬeven if I wereвА¶I could notвА¶.вАЭ
n1800945: tn The verb is вАП„¶÷Є„У÷Ј„І÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (tsadaqti, вАЬI am right [or вАЬrighteousвАЭ]вАЭ). The term here must be forensic, meaning вАЬin the rightвАЭ or вАЬinnocentвАЭ (see 11:2; 13:18; 33:12; 40:8). Job is claiming to be in the right, but still has difficulty speaking to God.
n1800946: tn The form is the Qal imperfect of the verb вАЬanswer.вАЭ As the text stands, Job is saying that he cannot answer or could not answer (contend with) God if given a chance. Some commentators think a Niphal fits better here: вАЬI am not answered,вАЭ meaning God does not reply to him. This has the LXX, the Syriac, and Theodotion in support of it. The advantage would be to avoid the repetition of the same word from v. 14. But others rightly reject this, because all Job is saying here is that he would be too overwhelmed by God to answer him in court. The LXX change to a passive is understandable in that it would be seeking a different idea in this verse and without vocalization might have assumed a passive voice here.
n1800947: tn The verb вАП„Р÷ґ„™÷∞„Ч÷Ј„†÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО ( Њetkhannan) is the Hitpael of вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan), meaning вАЬseek favor,вАЭ make supplication,вАЭ or вАЬplead for mercy.вАЭ The nuance would again be a modal nuance; if potential, then the translation would be вАЬI could [only] plead for mercy.вАЭ
n1800948: tn The word вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷є„§÷∞„Ш÷і„ЩвАО (meshofeti) appears to be simply вАЬmy judge.вАЭ But most modern interpretations take the Poel participle to mean вАЬmy adversary in a court of law.вАЭ Others argue that the form is at least functioning as a noun and means вАЬjudgeвАЭ (see 8:5). This would fit better with the idea of appealing for mercy from God. The dilemma of Job, of course, is that the Lord would be both his adversary in the case and his judge.
n1800949: sn The idea of вАЬanswerвАЭ in this line is that of responding to the summons, i.e., appearing in court. This preterite and the perfect before it have the nuance of hypothetical perfects since they are in conditional clauses (GKC 330 ¬І111.x). D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 219) translates literally, вАЬIf I should call and he should answer.вАЭ
n1800950: tn The Hiphil imperfect in the apodosis of this conditional sentence expresses what would (not) happen if God answered the summons.
n1800951: tn The relative pronoun indicates that this next section is modifying God, the Judge. Job does not believe that God would respond or listen to him, because this is the one who is crushing him.
n1800952: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„§÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (yeshufeni) is the same verb that is used in Gen 3:15 for the wounding of the serpent. The Targum to Job, the LXX, and the Vulgate all translate it вАЬto crush; to pound,вАЭ or вАЬto bruise.вАЭ The difficulty for many exegetes is that this is to be done вАЬwith a tempest.вАЭ The Syriac and Targum Job see a different vocalization and read вАЬwith a hair.вАЭ The text as it stands is understandable and so no change is needed. The fact that the word вАЬtempestвАЭ is written with a different sibilant in other places in Job is not greatly significant in this consideration.
n1800953: tn вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (khinnam) is adverbial, meaning вАЬgratuitously, without a cause, for no reason, undeservedly.вАЭ See its use in 2:4.
n1800954: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО (natan) essentially means вАЬto give,вАЭ but followed by the infinitive (without the вАП„ЬвАО [lamed] here) it means вАЬto permit; to allow.вАЭ
n1800955: tn The Hiphil of the verb means вАЬto bring backвАЭ; with the object вАЬmy breath,вАЭ it means вАЬget my breathвАЭ or simply вАЬbreathe.вАЭ The infinitive is here functioning as the object of the verb (see GKC 350 ¬І114.m).
n1800956: sn The meaning of the word is вАЬto satiate; to fill,вАЭ as in вАЬdrink to the full, be satisfied.вАЭ Job is satiatedвАФin the negative senseвАФwith bitterness. There is no room for more.
n1800957: tn The MT has only вАЬif of strength.вАЭ
n1800958: tn вАЬMost certainlyвАЭ translates the particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh).
n1800959:
tn The question could be taken as вАЬwho will summon me?вАЭ (see Jer 49:19 and 50:44). This does not make immediate sense. Some have simply changed the suffix to вАЬwho will summon him.вАЭ If the MT is retained, then supplying something like вАЬhe will sayвАЭ could make the last clause fit the whole passage. Another option is to take it as вАЬWho will reveal it to me?вАЭвАФi.e., Job could be questioning his friendsвАЩ qualifications for being GodвАЩs emissaries to bring GodвАЩs charges against him (cf. KJV, NKJV; and see 10:2 where Job uses the same verb in the Hiphil to request that God reveal what his sin has been that has led to his suffering).
sn Job is saying that whether it is a trial of strength or an appeal to justice, he is unable to go against God.
n1800960: tn The idea is the same as that expressed in v. 15, although here the imperfect verb is used and not the perfect. Once again with the concessive clause (вАЬalthough I am rightвАЭ) Job knows that in a legal dispute he would be confused and would end up arguing against himself.
n1800961: tn Some commentators wish to change this to вАЬhis mouth,вАЭ meaning GodвАЩs response to JobвАЩs complaints. But the MT is far more expressive, and вАЬmy mouthвАЭ fits the context in which Job is saying that even though he is innocent, if he spoke in a court setting in the presence of God he would be overwhelmed, confused, and no doubt condemn himself.
n1800962: tn The verb has the declarative sense in the Hiphil, вАЬto declare guilty [or wicked]вАЭ or вАЬto condemn.вАЭ
n1800963: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„І÷Ј„©„БвАО ( њaqash) means вАЬto be twisted; to be tortuous.вАЭ The Piel has a meaning вАЬto bend; to twistвАЭ (Mic 3:9) and вАЬto pervertвАЭ (Isa 59:8). The form here is classified as a Hiphil, with the softening of the vowel i (see GKC 147 ¬І53.n). It would then also be a declarative use of the Hiphil.
n1800964: tn Dhorme, in an effort to avoid tautology, makes this a question: вАЬAm I blameless?вАЭ The next clause then has Job answering that he does not know. But through the last section Job has been proclaiming his innocence. The other way of interpreting these verses is to follow NIV and make all of them hypothetical (вАЬIf I were blameless, he would pronounce me guiltyвАЭ) and then come to this verse with Job saying, вАЬI am blameless.вАЭ The second clause of this verse does not fit either view very well. In vv. 20, 21, and 22 Job employs the same term for вАЬblamelessвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО, tam) as in the prologue (1:1). God used it to describe Job in 1:8 and 2:3. Bildad used it in 8:20. These are the final occurrences in the book.
n1800965:
tn The meaning of the expression вАЬI do not know myselfвАЭ seems to be, вАЬI do not care.вАЭ NIV translates it, вАЬI have no concern for my life.вАЭ
sn Job believes he is blameless and not deserving of all this suffering; he will hold fast to that claim, even if the future is uncertain, especially if that future involved a confrontation with God.
n1800966:
tc The LXX omits the phrase вАЬIt is all one.вАЭ Modern scholars either omit it or transpose it for clarity.
sn The expression вАЬit is oneвАЭ means that GodвАЩs dealings with people are indiscriminating. The number вАЬoneвАЭ could also be taken to mean вАЬthe sameвАЭвАФвАЬit is all the same.вАЭ The implication is that it does not matter if Job is good or evil, if he lives or dies. This is the conclusion of the preceding section.
n1800967: tn The relationships of these clauses is in some question. Some think that the poet has inverted the first two, and so they should read, вАЬThat is why I have said: вАШIt is all one.вАЩвАЭ Others would take the third clause to be what was said.
n1800968:
tc The LXX contains a paraphrase: вАЬfor the worthless die, but the righteous are laughed to scorn.вАЭ
sn The point of these verses is to showвАФrather boldlyвАФthat God does not distinguish between the innocent and the guilty.
n1800969: sn This bold anthropomorphism means that by his treatment of the despair of the innocent, God is in essence mocking them.
n1800970: tn The term вАП„Ю÷Ј„°÷Љ÷Ј„™вАО (massat), a hapax legomenon, was translated вАЬtrialвАЭ in the older versions, but it is not from вАП„†÷Є„°÷Є„ФвАО (nasah, вАЬto tempt; to test; to tryвАЭ); instead it is from вАП„Ю÷Є„°÷Ј„°вАО (masas, вАЬto flowвАЭ). It is used in the Niphal to speak of the heart вАЬmeltingвАЭ in suffering. So the idea behind this image is that of despair. This is the view that most interpreters adopt; it requires no change of the text whatsoever.
n1800971: sn Job uses this word to refute Eliphaz; cf. 4:7.
n1800972: tn Some would render this вАЬearth,вАЭ meaning the whole earth, and having the verse be a general principle for all mankind. But Job may have in mind the more specific issue of individual land.
n1800973: sn The details of the verse are not easy to explain, but the meaning of the whole verse seems to be about the miscarriage of justice in the courts and the failure of God to do anything about it.
n1800974: tn The subject of the verb is God. The reasoning goes this way: It is the duty of judges to make sure that justice prevails and that restitution and restoration are carried through, but when the wicked gain control of the land of other people and the judges are ineffective to stop it, then God must be veiling their eyes.
n1800975: sn That these words are strong, if not wild, is undeniable. But Job is only taking the implications of his friendsвАЩ speeches to their logical conclusionвАФif God dispenses justice in the world, and there is no justice, then God is behind it all. The LXX omitted these words, perhaps out of reverence for God.
n1800976: tn This seems to be a broken-off sentence (anacoluthon), and so is rather striking. The scribes transposed the words вАП„Р÷µ„§„Х÷є„РвАО ( Њefo Њ) and вАП„Ф„Х÷Љ„РвАО (hu Њ) to make the smoother reading: вАЬIf it is not he, who then is it?вАЭ
n1800977: tn The text has вАЬand my daysвАЭ following the thoughts in the previous section.
n1800978: sn Job returns to the thought of the brevity of his life (7:6). But now the figure is the swift runner instead of the weaverвАЩs shuttle.
n1800980: tn The word вАП„Р÷µ„С÷ґ„ФвАО ( Њeveh) means вАЬreed, papyrus,вАЭ but it is a different word than was in 8:11. What is in view here is a light boat made from bundles of papyrus that glides swiftly along the Nile (cf. Isa 18:2 where papyrus vessels and swiftness are associated).
n1800981: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ш„Х÷Љ„©„ВвАО (yatus) is also a hapax legomenon; the Aramaic cognate means вАЬto soar; to hover in flight.вАЭ The sentence here requires the idea of swooping down while in flight.
n1800983: tn The construction here uses the infinitive construct with a pronominal suffixвАФвАЬif my sayingвАЭ is this, or вАЬif I say.вАЭ For the conditional clause using вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) with a noun clause, see GKC 496 ¬І159.u.
n1800984: tn The verbal form is a cohortative of resolve: вАЬI will forgetвАЭ or вАЬI am determined to forget.вАЭ The same will be used in the second colon of the verse.
n1800985: tn Heb вАЬI will abandon my face,вАЭ i.e., change my expression. The construction here is unusual; G. R. Driver connected it to an Arabic word  њadaba, вАЬmade agreeableвАЭ (IV), and so interpreted this line to mean вАЬmake my countenance pleasantвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 76). M. Dahood found a Ugaritic root meaning вАЬmake, arrangeвАЭ (вАЬThe Root  њzb II in Job,вАЭ JBL 78 [1959]: 303-9), and said, вАЬI will arrange my face.вАЭ But see H. G. Williamson, вАЬA Reconsideration of  њazab II in Ugaritic,вАЭ ZAW 87 (1985): 74-85; Williamson shows it is probably not a legitimate cognate. D. J. A. Clines (Job [WBC], 219) observes that with all these suggestions there are too many homonyms for the root. The MT construction is still plausible.
n1800986: tn In the Hiphil of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ТвАО (balag) corresponds to Arabic balija which means вАЬto shineвАЭ and вАЬto be merry.вАЭ The shining face would signify cheerfulness and smiling. It could be translated вАЬand brighten [my face].вАЭ
n1800987: tn The word was used in Job 3:25; it has the idea of вАЬdread, fear, tremble at.вАЭ The point here is that even if Job changes his appearance, he still dreads the sufferings, because he knows that God is treating him as a criminal.
n1800988: sn See Job 7:15; see also the translation by G. Perles, вАЬI tremble in every nerveвАЭ (вАЬThe Fourteenth Edition of Gesenius-BuhlвАЩs Dictionary,вАЭ JQR 18 [1905/06]: 383-90).
n1800989: tn The conjunction вАЬforвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n1800990: sn A. B. Davidson (Job, 73) appropriately notes that JobвАЩs afflictions were the proof of his guilt in the estimation of God. If God held him innocent, he would remove the afflictions.
n1800991: tn The clause simply has вАЬI am guilty.вАЭ It is the same type of construction found in v. 24. It is also the opposite of that in v. 20. GKC 317 ¬І107.n lists this as an example of the use of the imperfect to express an obligation or necessity according to the judgment of others; it would therefore mean вАЬif I am to be guilty.вАЭ
n1800992: tn The demonstrative pronoun is included to bring particular emphasis to the question, as if to say, вАЬWhy in the worldвА¶вАЭ (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 24, ¬І118).
n1800993: tn The verb means вАЬtire oneselfвАЭ; see 3:17.
n1800994: tn Here вАП„Ф÷ґ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (hevel, вАЬbreath, vapor, vanityвАЭ) is used as an adverb (adverbial accusative).
n1800995: tn The Syriac and Targum Job read with the Qere вАЬwith water of [вАП„С÷∞„Ю÷µ„ЩвАО, veme] snow.вАЭ The Kethib simply has вАЬin [вАП„С÷∞„Ю„Х÷євАО, vemo] snow.вАЭ In Ps 51:9 and Isa 1:18 snow forms a simile for purification. Some protest that snow water is not necessarily clean, but if fresh melting snow is meant, then the runoff would be very clear. The image would work well here. Nevertheless, others have followed the later Hebrew meaning for вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„ТвАО (sheleg)вАФвАЬsoapвАЭ (so NIV, NRSV, NLT). Even though that makes a nice parallelism, it is uncertain whether that meaning was in use at the time this text was written.
n1800996: tn The word вАП„С÷Љ÷є„®вАО (bor, вАЬlye, potashвАЭ) does not refer to purity (Syriac, KJV, ASV), but refers to the ingredient used to make the hands pure or clean. It has the same meaning as вАП„С÷Љ÷є„®÷і„Щ„™вАО (borit), the alkali or soda made from the ashes of certain plants.
n1800997: tn The pointing in the MT gives the meaning вАЬpitвАЭ or вАЬditch.вАЭ A number of expositors change the pointing to вАП„©„Б÷ї„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (shukhot) to obtain the equivalent of вАП„©„В÷ї„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (sukhot) / вАП„°÷ї„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (sukhot): вАЬfilthвАЭ (Isa 5:25). This would make the contrast vividвАФJob has just washed with pure water and soap, and now God plunges him into filth. M. H. Pope argues convincingly that the word вАЬpitвАЭ in the MT includes the idea of вАЬfilth,вАЭ making the emendation unnecessary (вАЬThe Word sahat in Job 9:31, вАЭ JBL 83 [1964]: 269-78).
n1800998: tn The personal pronoun that would be expected as the subject of a noun clause is sometimes omitted (see GKC 360 ¬І116.s). Here it has been supplied.
n1800999: tn The consecutive clause is here attached without the use of the вАП„ХвАО (vav), but only by simple juxtaposition (see GKC 504-5 ¬І166.a).
n18009100: tn The sense of the verb вАЬcomeвАЭ with вАЬtogether in judgmentвАЭ means вАЬto confront one another in court.вАЭ See Ps 143:2.
n18009101:
tn The participle вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ы÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (mokhiakh) is the вАЬarbiterвАЭ or вАЬmediator.вАЭ The word comes from the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh, вАЬdecide, judgeвАЭ), which is concerned with legal and nonlegal disputes. The verbal forms can be used to describe the beginning of a dispute, the disputation in progress, or the settling of it (here, and in Isa 1:18).
sn The old translation of вАЬdaysmanвАЭ came from a Latin expression describing the fixing of a day for arbitration.
n18009102: tn The relative pronoun is understood in this clause.
n18009103: tn The jussive in conditional sentences retains its voluntative sense: let something be so, and this must happen as a consequence (see GKC 323 ¬І109.i).
n18009104: sn The idiom of вАЬlay his hand on the two of usвАЭ may come from a custom of a judge putting his hands on the two in order to show that he is taking them both under his jurisdiction. The expression can also be used for protection (see Ps 139:5). Job, however, has a problem in that the other party is God, who himself will be arbiter in judgment.
n18009105: tn The verse probably continues the description from the last verse, and so a relative pronoun may be supplied here as well.
n18009106: tn According to some, the reference of this suffix would be to God. The arbiter would remove the rod of God from Job. But others take it as a separate sentence with God removing his rod.
n18009107: sn The вАЬrodвАЭ is a symbol of the power of God to decree whatever judgments and afflictions fall upon people.
n18009108: tn вАЬHis terrorвАЭ is metonymical; it refers to the awesome majesty of God that overwhelms Job and causes him to be afraid.
n18009109: tn There is no conjunction with this cohortative, but the implication from the context is that if GodвАЩs rod were withdrawn, if the terror were removed, then Job would speak up without fear.
n18009110: tn The last half of the verse is rather cryptic: вАЬbut not so I with me.вАЭ NIV renders it вАЬbut as it now stands with me, I cannot.вАЭ This is very smooth and interpretive. Others transpose the two halves of the verse to read, вАЬSince it is not so, I with myself // will commune and not fear him.вАЭ Job would be saying that since he cannot contend with God on equal terms, and since there is no arbiter, he will come on his own terms. English versions have handled this differently: вАЬfor I know I am not what I am thought to beвАЭ (NEB); вАЬsince this is not the case with meвАЭ (NAB); вАЬI do not see myself like that at allвАЭ (JB).
n180101: tn The Hebrew has вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷і„ЩвАО (nafshi), usually rendered вАЬmy soul.вАЭ
n180102: tn The verb is pointed like a Qal form but is originally a Niphal from вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ШвАО (qut). Some wish to connect the word to Akkadian cognates for a meaning вАЬI am in anguish,вАЭ but the meaning вАЬI am wearyвАЭ fits the passage well.
n180103: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО ( њazav) means вАЬto abandon.вАЭ It may have an extended meaning of вАЬto let goвАЭ or вАЬto let slip.вАЭ But the expression вАЬabandon to myselfвАЭ means to abandon all restraint and give free course to the complaint.
n180104: tn The negated jussive is the Hiphil jussive of вАП„®÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО (rasha њ); its meaning then would be literally вАЬdo not declare me guilty.вАЭ The negated jussive stresses the immediacy of the request.
n180105: tn The Hiphil imperative of вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) would more literally be вАЬcause me to know.вАЭ It is a plea for God to help him understand the afflictions.
n180106: tn The verb is вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv), meaning вАЬto dispute; to contend; to strive; to quarrelвАЭвАФoften in the legal sense. The precise words chosen in this verse show that the setting is legal. The imperfect verb here is progressive, expressing what is currently going on.
n180107: tn Or вАЬDoes it give you pleasure?вАЭ The expression could also mean, вАЬIs it profitable for you?вАЭ or вАЬIs it fitting for you?вАЭ
n180108: tn The construction uses вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) with the imperfect verbвАФвАЬthat you oppress.вАЭ Technically, this clause serves as the subject, and вАЬgoodвАЭ is the predicate adjective. In such cases one often uses an English infinitive to capture the point: вАЬIs it good for you to oppress?вАЭ The LXX changes the meaning considerably: вАЬIs it good for you if I am unrighteous, for you have disowned the work of your hands.вАЭ
n1801010: tn Now, in the second half of the verse, there is a change in the structure. The conjunction on the preposition followed by the perfect verb represents a circumstantial clause.
n1801011: tn The Hiphil of the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„§÷Ј„ҐвАО (yafa њ) means вАЬshine.вАЭ In this context the expression вАЬyou shine uponвАЭ would mean вАЬhave a glowing expression,вАЭ be radiant, or smile.
n1801012: tn Here вАЬfleshвАЭ is the sign of humanity. The expression вАЬeyes of fleshвАЭ means essentially вАЬhuman eyes,вАЭ i.e., the outlook and vision of humans.
n1801013: sn The verb translated вАЬseeвАЭ could also include the figurative category of perceive as well. The answer to JobвАЩs question is found in 1 Sam 16:7: вАЬThe Lord sees not as a man sees; man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart.вАЭ
n1801014: sn In this verse Job asks whether or not God is liable to making mistakes or errors of judgment. He wonders if God has no more insight than his friends have. Of course, the questions are rhetorical, for he knows otherwise. But his point is that God seems to be making a big mistake here.
n1801015:
tn The Hebrew has repeated here вАЬlike the days of,вАЭ but some scholars think that this was an accidental replacement of what should be here, namely, вАЬlike the years of.вАЭ D. J. A. Clines notes that such repetition is not uncommon in Job, but suggests that the change should be made for English style even if the text is not emended (Job [WBC], 221). This has been followed in the present translation.
sn The question Job asks concerns the mode of life and not just the length of it (see Job 7:1). Humans spend their days and years watching each other and defending themselves. But there is also the implication that if God is so limited like humans he may not uncover JobвАЩs sins before he dies.
n1801016: tn The clause seems to go naturally with v. 4: do you have eyes of fleshвА¶that you have to investigate? For that reason some like Duhm would delete v. 5. But v. 5 adds to the premise: are you also like a human running out of time that you must try to find out my sin?
n1801017: tn The imperfect verbs in this verse are best given modal nuances. Does God have such limitations that he must make such an investigation? H. H. Rowley observes that Job implies that God has not yet found the iniquity, or extracted a confession from him (Job [NCBC], 84).
n1801018: tn Heb вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО ( њal da њtekha, вАЬupon your knowledgeвАЭ). The use of the preposition means basically вАЬin addition to your knowledge,вАЭ or вАЬin spite of your knowledge,вАЭ i.e., вАЬnotwithstandingвАЭ or вАЬalthoughвАЭ (see GKC 383 ¬І119.aa, n. 2).
n1801019:
tn Heb вАЬand there is no deliverer.вАЭ
sn The fact is that humans are the work of GodвАЩs hands. They are helpless in the hand of God. But it is also unworthy of God to afflict his people.
n1801020: tn The root вАП„Ґ÷Є„¶÷Ј„СвАО ( њatsav) is linked by some to an Arabic word meaning вАЬto cut out, hew.вАЭ The derived word вАП„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Ј„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њatsabbim) means вАЬidols.вАЭ Whatever the precise meaning, the idea is that God formed or gave shape to mankind in creation.
n1801021: tn The verb in this part is a preterite with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. However, here it has merely an external connection with the preceding perfects, so that in reality it presents an antithesis (see GKC 327 ¬І111.e).
