n1801022: tn Heb вАЬtogether round about and you destroy me.вАЭ The second half of this verse is very difficult. Most commentators follow the LXX and connect the first two words with the second colon as the MT accents indicate (NJPS, вАЬthen destroyed every part of meвАЭ), rather than with the first colon (вАЬand made me complete,вАЭ J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 185). Instead of вАЬtogetherвАЭ some read вАЬafter.вАЭ Others see in вАП„°÷Є„С÷і„Щ„СвАО (saviv) not so much an adjectival use but a verbal or adverbial use: вАЬyou turn and destroyвАЭ or вАЬyou destroy utterly (all around).вАЭ This makes more sense than вАЬturn.вАЭ In addition, the verb form in the line is the preterite with vav consecutive; this may be another example of the transposition of the copula (see 4:6). For yet another option (вАЬYou have engulfed me about altogetherвАЭ), see R. Fuller, вАЬExodus 21:22: The Miscarriage Interpretation and the Personhood of the Fetus,вАЭ JETS 37 (1994): 178.
n1801023: tn The preposition вАЬlikeвАЭ creates a small tension here. So some ignore the preposition and read вАЬclayвАЭ as an adverbial accusative of the material (GKC 371 ¬І117.hh but cf. 379 ¬І119.i with reference to beth essentiae: вАЬas it were, by clayвАЭ). The NIV gets around the problem with a different meaning for the verb: вАЬyou molded me like clay.вАЭ Some suggest the meaning was вАЬas [with] clayвАЭ (in the same manner that we have вАЬas [in] the day of MidianвАЭ [Isa 9:4]).
n1801024: tn The text has a conjunction: вАЬand to dustвА¶.вАЭ
n1801025: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (natakh) means вАЬto flow,вАЭ and in the Hiphil, вАЬto cause to flow.вАЭ
n1801026: tn This verb вАП„І÷Є„§÷Є„РвАО (qafa Њ) means вАЬto coagulate.вАЭ In the Hiphil it means вАЬto stiffen; to congeal.вАЭ
n1801027:
tn The verbs in v. 10 are prefixed conjugations; since the reference is to the womb, these would need to be classified as preterites.
sn These verses figuratively describe the formation of the embryo in the womb.
n1801028: tn The skin and flesh form the exterior of the body and so the image of вАЬclothingвАЭ is appropriate. Once again the verb is the prefixed conjugation, expressing what God did.
n1801029: tn This verb is found only here (related nouns are common) and in the parallel passage of Ps 139:13. The word вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (sakhakh), here a Poel prefixed conjugation (preterite), means вАЬto knit together.вАЭ The implied comparison is that the bones and sinews form the tapestry of the person (compare other images of weaving the life).
n1801031: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 150) suggests that the relation between these two words is like a hendiadys. In other words, вАЬlife,вАЭ which he says is made prominent by the shift of the copula, specifies the nature of the grace. He renders it вАЬthe favor of life.вАЭ D. J. A. Clines at least acknowledges that the expression вАЬyou showed loyal love with meвАЭ is primary. There are many other attempts to improve the translation of this unusual combination.
n1801032: tn The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„І÷ї„У÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (pequddah), originally translated вАЬvisitation,вАЭ actually refers to any divine intervention for blessing on the life. Here it would include the care and overseeing of the life of Job. вАЬProvidenceвАЭ may be too general for the translation, but it is not far from the meaning of this line. The LXX has вАЬyour oversight.вАЭ
n1801033: sn вАЬThese thingsвАЭ refers to the affliction that God had brought on Job. They were concealed by God from the beginning.
n1801034: sn The meaning of the line is that this was GodвАЩs purpose all along. вАЬThese thingsвАЭ and вАЬthisвАЭ refer to the details that will now be given in the next few verses.
n1801035: sn The contradiction between how God had provided for and cared for JobвАЩs life and how he was now dealing with him could only be resolved by Job with the supposition that God had planned this severe treatment from the first as part of his plan.
n1801036: sn The verbs вАЬguiltyвАЭ and вАЬinnocentвАЭ are actually the verbs вАЬI am wicked,вАЭ and вАЬI am righteous.вАЭ
n1801037: tn The exclamation occurs only here and in Mic 7:1.
n1801038: sn The action of lifting up the head is a symbol of pride and honor and self-respect (Judg 8:28)вАФlike вАЬhold your head high.вАЭ In 11:15 the one who is at peace with God lifts his head (face).
n1801039: tn The expression вАП„©„В÷∞„С÷Ј„Ґ „І÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЯвАО (seva њ qalon) may be translated вАЬfull of shame.вАЭ The expression literally means вАЬsated of ignominyвАЭ (or contempt [вАП„І÷Ј„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО, qalal]).
n1801040: tn The last clause is difficult to fit into the verse. It translates easily enough: вАЬand see my affliction.вАЭ Many commentators follow the suggestion of Geiger to read вАП„®÷∞„Х÷ґ„ФвАО (reveh, вАЬwatered withвАЭ) instead of вАП„®÷∞„Р÷µ„ФвАО (re Њeh, вАЬseeвАЭ). This could then be interpreted adjectivally and parallel to the preceding line: вАЬsteeped/saturated with affliction.вАЭ This would also delete the final yod as dittography (E. Dhorme, Job, 152). But D. J. A. Clines notes more recent interpretations that suggest the form in the text is an orthographic variant of raweh meaning вАЬsatiated.вАЭ This makes any emendation unnecessary (and in fact that idea of вАЬsteepedвАЭ was not helpful any way because it indicated imbibing rather than soaking). The NIV renders it вАЬand drowned in my afflictionвАЭ although footnoting the other possibility from the MT, вАЬaware of my afflictionвАЭ (assuming the form could be adjectival). The LXX omits the last line.
n1801041: tn The MT has the third person of the verb, вАЬand he lifts himself up.вАЭ One might assume that the subject is вАЬmy headвАЭвАФbut that is rather far removed from the verb. It appears that Job is talking about himself in some way. Some commentators simply emend the text to make it first person. This has the support of Targum Job, which would be expected since it would be interpreting the passage in its context (see D. M. Stec, вАЬThe Targum Rendering of WYGвАЩH in Job X 16, вАЭ VT 34 [1984]: 367-8). Pope and Gordis make the word adjectival, modifying the subject: вАЬproudly you hunt me,вАЭ but support is lacking. E. Dhorme thinks the line should be parallel to the two preceding it, and so suggests вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Љ÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yagea њ, вАЬexhaustedвАЭ) for вАП„Щ÷і„Т÷∞„Р÷ґ„ФвАО (yig Њeh, вАЬlift upвАЭ). The contextual argument is that Job has said that he cannot raise his head, but if he were to do so, God would hunt him down. God could be taken as the subject of the verb if the text is using enallage (shifting of grammatical persons within a discourse) for dramatic effect. Perhaps the initial third person was intended with respect within a legal context of witnesses and a complaint, but was switched to second person for direct accusation.
n1801042: sn There is some ambiguity here: Job could be the lion being hunted by God, or God could be hunting Job like a lion hunts its prey. The point of the line is clear in either case.
n1801043: tn The text uses two verbs without a coordinating conjunction: вАЬthen you return, you display your power.вАЭ This should be explained as a verbal hendiadys, the first verb serving adverbially in the clause (see further GKC 386-87 ¬І120.g).
n1801044: tn The form is the Hitpael of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„РвАО (pala Њ, вАЬto be wonderful; to be surpassing; to be extraordinaryвАЭ). Here in this stem it has the sense of вАЬmake oneself admirable, surpassingвАЭ or вАЬrender oneself powerful, glorious.вАЭ The text is ironic; the word that described GodвАЩs marvelous creation of Job is here used to describe GodвАЩs awesome destruction of Job.
n1801045: tn The text has вАЬyou renew/increase your witnesses.вАЭ This would probably mean JobвАЩs sufferings, which were witness to his sins. But some suggested a different word here, one that is cognate to Arabic  Њadiya, вАЬto be an enemy; to be hostileвАЭ: thus вАЬyou renew your hostility against me.вАЭ Less convincing are suggestions that the word is cognate to Ugaritic вАЬtroopsвАЭ (see W. G. E. Watson, вАЬThe Metaphor in 10, 17, вАЭ Bib 63 [1982]: 255-57).
n1801046: tn The Hebrew simply says вАЬchanges and a host are with me.вАЭ The вАЬchanges and a hostвАЭ is taken as a hendiadys, meaning relieving troops (relief troops of the army). The two words appear together again in 14:14, showing that emendation is to be avoided. The imagery depicts blow after blow from GodвАФalways fresh attacks.
n1801047: tn The two imperfect verbs in this section are used to stress regrets for something which did not happen (see GKC 317 ¬І107.n).
n1801048: sn This means вАЬIf only I had never come into existence.вАЭ
n1801049: tn Heb вАЬare not my days few; cease/let it ceaseвА¶.вАЭ The versions have вАЬthe days of my lifeвАЭ (reading вАП„Щ÷∞„Ю÷µ„Щ „Ч÷ґ„Ь÷∞„У÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО [yeme kheldi] instead of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ „Х÷Ј„Ч÷≤„У÷Є„ЬвАО [yamay vakhadal]). Many commentators and the RSV, NAB, and NRSV accept this reading. The Kethib is an imperfect or jussive, вАЬlet it cease/ it will cease.вАЭ The Qere is more intelligible for some interpretersвАФвАЬceaseвАЭ (as in 7:16). For a discussion of the readings, see D. W. Thomas, вАЬSome Observations on the Hebrew Root вАП„Ф„У„ЬвАО,вАЭ VTSup 4 [1957]: 14). But the text is not impossible as it stands.
n1801050: tn Taking the form as the imperative with the вАП„ХвАО (vav), the sentence follows the direct address to God (as in v. 18 as well as 7:16). This requires less changes. See the preceding note regarding the plausibility of the jussive. The point of the verse is clear in either readingвАФhis life is short, and he wants the suffering to stop.
n1801051: tn In the different suggestions for the line, the вАП„ЩвАО (yod) of this word is believed to belong to the preceding word making вАЬmy life.вАЭ That would here leave an imperative rather than an imperfect. But if the Qere is read, then it would be an imperative anyway, and there would be no reason for the change.
n1801052: tn Heb вАЬput from me,вАЭ an expression found nowhere else. The Qere has a вАП„ХвАО (vav) and not a вАП„ЩвАО (yod), forming an imperative rather than an imperfect. H. H. Rowley suggests that there is an ellipsis here, вАЬhandвАЭ needing to be supplied. Job wanted God to take his hand away from him. That is plausible, but difficult.
n1801053: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ТвАО (balag) in the Hiphil means вАЬto have cheer [or joy]вАЭ (see 9:27; Ps 39:14 HT [39:13 ET]). The cohortative following the imperatives shows the purpose or resultвАФвАЬin order that.вАЭ
n1801054: sn The verbs are simple, вАЬI goвАЭ and вАЬI return,вАЭ but Job clearly means before he dies. A translation of вАЬdepartвАЭ comes closer to communicating this. The second verb may be given a potential imperfect translation to capture the point. The NIV offered more of an interpretive paraphrase: вАЬbefore I go to the place of no return.вАЭ
n1801056: tn The word вАП„°÷µ„У÷ґ„®вАО (seder, вАЬorderвАЭ) occurs only here in the Bible. G. R. Driver found a new meaning in Arabic sadira, вАЬdazzled by the glareвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 76-77); this would mean вАЬwithout a ray of light.вАЭ This is accepted by those who see chaos out of place in this line. But the word вАЬorderвАЭ is well attested in later Hebrew (see J. Carmignac, вАЬPr√©cisions aport√©es au vocabulaire dвАЩh√©breu biblique par La guerre des fils de lumi√®re contre les fils de t√©n√®bres,вАЭ VT 5 [1955]: 345-65).
n1801057: tn The Hebrew word literally means вАЬit shinesвАЭ; the feminine verb implies a subject like вАЬthe lightвАЭ (but see GKC 459 ¬І144.c).
n1801058: tn The verse multiplies images for the darkness in death. Several commentators omit вАЬas darkness, deep darknessвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷є „Р÷є„§÷ґ„Ь „¶÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО, kemo  Њofel tsalmavet) as glosses on the rare word вАП„Ґ÷µ„Щ„§÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО ( њefatah, вАЬdarknessвАЭ) drawn from v. 21 (see also RSV). The verse literally reads: вАЬ[to the] land of darkness, like the deep darkness of the shadow of death, without any order, and the light is like the darkness.вАЭ
n180111: sn Zophar begins with a strong rebuke of Job with a wish that God would speak (2-6); he then reflects for a few verses on the unsearchable wisdom of God (7-12); and finally, he advises Job that the way to restoration is repentance (13-20).
n180112:
tc The LXX, Targum Job, Symmachus, and Vulgate all assume that the vocalization of вАП„®÷є„СвАО (rov, вАЬabundanceвАЭ) should be вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО (rav, вАЬgreatвАЭ): вАЬgreat of words.вАЭ This would then mean вАЬone who is abundant of words,вАЭ meaning, вАЬa man of many words,вАЭ and make a closer parallel to the second half. But the MT makes good sense as it stands.
tn There is no article or demonstrative with the word; it has been added here simply to make a smoother connection between the chapters.
n180113: tn The Niphal verb вАП„Щ÷µ„Ґ÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО (ye њaneh, вАЬhe answeredвАЭ) would normally require a personal subject, but вАЬabundanceвАЭ functions as the subject in this sentence. The nuance of the imperfect is obligatory.
n180114: tn The word is supplied here also for clarification.
n180115: tn The bound construction вАЬman of lipsвАЭ means вАЬa boasterвАЭ or вАЬproud talkerвАЭ (attributive genitive; and see GKC 417 ¬І128.t). Zophar is saying that Job pours out this stream of words, but he is still not right.
n180116: tn The word is literally вАЬbe right, righteous.вАЭ The idea of being right has appeared before for this word (cf. 9:15). The point here is that just because Job talks a lot does not mean he is right or will be shown to be right through it all.
n180117: tn This Hiphil verb comes from the stative root вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (kharash, вАЬto be silent/deafвАЭ). As typical of stative roots in the Hiphil it means to act in the character of the state described by the root (its basic meaning expressed in the Qal stem). Most translations (e.g. KJV, NRSV, NASB, NIV, ESV) treat the verb as if it were a dynamic root and translate causatively, e.g. вАЬwill your boasts put men to silence?вАЭ HALOT classifies most Hiphils of this root correctly with meanings like вАЬto keep silent, to fall silent, pretend to be deaf,вАЭ but makes the mistake of including a causative вАЬreduce to silenceвАЭ for this verse (HALOT 358 s.v. II вАП„Ч„®„©„БвАО). Like the two preceding lines, the first noun of this line (вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„УвАО, bad, вАЬloose talk, boastingвАЭ) is the object of the verb, while the interrogative continues to be implied from 11:2a. Recognizing this, the subject of the verb is вАЬpeopleвАЭ and the verb behaves normally and uniformly in all of its Hiphil occurrences.
n180118: tn The word means вАЬchatter, pratings, boastingsвАЭ (see Isa 16:6; Jer 48:30).
n180119: tn The form вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Ь÷і„ЭвАО (makhlim, вАЬhumiliating, mockingвАЭ) is the Hiphil participle. The verb вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (kalam) has the meaning вАЬcover with shame, insultвАЭ (Job 20:3).
n1801110: tn The construction shows the participle to be in the circumstantial clause: вАЬwill you mockвАФand [with] no one rebuking.вАЭ
n1801111: tn The word translated вАЬteachingвАЭ is related etymologically to the Hebrew word вАЬreceive,вАЭ but that does not restrict the teaching to what is received.
n1801112: tn The wish formula вАП„Ю÷і„Щ÷Њ„Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (mi yitten, вАЬwho will giveвАЭ; see GKC 477 ¬І151.b) is followed here by an infinitive (Exod 16:3; 2 Sam 19:1).
n1801113: sn Job had expressed his eagerness to challenge God; Zophar here wishes that God would take up that challenge.
n1801114: tn The text seems to be saying вАЬthat it [wisdom] is double in understanding.вАЭ The point is that it is different than Job conceived itвАФit far exceeded all perception. But some commentators have thought this still too difficult, and so have replaced the word вАП„Ы÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (khiflayim, вАЬtwo sidesвАЭ) with вАП„Ы÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khifla Њim, вАЬlike wonders,вАЭ or, more simply, вАЬwondersвАЭ without the preposition). But it is still a little strange to talk about GodвАЩs wisdom being like wonders. Others have had more radical changes in the text; J. J. Slotki has вАЬfor sound wisdom is his. And know that double [punishment] shall God exact of youвАЭ (вАЬJob 11:6, вАЭ VT 35 [1985]: 229-30).
n1801115: tn The verb is the imperative with a вАП„ХвАО (vav). Following the jussive, this clause would be subordinated to the preceding (see GKC 325 ¬І110.i).
n1801116: tn Heb вАЬGod causes to be forgotten for you part of your iniquity.вАЭ The meaning is that God was exacting less punishment from Job than Job deserved, for Job could not remember all his sins. This statement is fitting for Zophar, who is the cruelest of JobвАЩs friends (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 88). Others in an attempt to improve the text make too many unwarranted changes. Some would read вАП„Щ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Р÷Є„Ь÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yish Њalekha, вАЬhe asks of youвАЭ) instead of вАП„Щ÷Ј„©„В÷Љ÷ґ„Ф „Ь÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yasseh lekha, вАЬhe causes to be forgotten for youвАЭ). This would mean that God demands an account of JobвАЩs sin. But, as D. J. A. Clines says, this change is weak and needless (Job [WBC], 254-55).
n1801117: tn The verb is вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (matsa Њ, вАЬto find; to discoverвАЭ). Here it should be given the nuance of potential imperfect. In the rhetorical question it is affirming that Job cannot find out the essence of God.
n1801118: tn The word means вАЬsearch; investigation,вАЭ but it here means what is discovered in the search (so a metonymy of cause for the effect).
n1801119: tn The same verb is now found in the second half of the verse, with a slightly different senseвАФвАЬattain, reach.вАЭ A. R. Ceresko notes this as an example of antanaclasis (repetition of a word with a slightly different senseвАФвАЬfind/attainвАЭ). See вАЬThe Function of Antanaclasis in Hebrew Poetry,вАЭ CBQ 44 (1982): 560-61.
n1801120: tn The abstract вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„™вАО (takhlit) from вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah, вАЬto be complete; to be perfectвАЭ) may mean the end or limit of something, perhaps to perfection. So the NIV has вАЬcan you probe the limits of the Almighty?вАЭ The LXX has: вАЬhave you come to the end of that which the Almighty has made?вАЭ
n1801121: tn The Hebrew says вАЬheights of heaven, what can you do?вАЭ A. B. Davidson suggested this was an exclamation and should be left that way. But most commentators will repoint вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„С÷∞„Ф÷µ„Щ „©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (govhe shamayim, вАЬheights of heavenвАЭ) to вАП„Т÷Љ÷∞„С÷є„Ф÷Є„Ф „Ю÷і„©„Б÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (gevohah mishamayim, вАЬhigher than the heavensвАЭ) to match the parallel expression. The LXX may have rearranged the text: вАЬheaven is high.вАЭ
n1801122: tn Or вАЬdeeper than hell.вАЭ The word вАЬSheolвАЭ always poses problems for translation. Here because it is the opposite of heaven in this merism, вАЬhellвАЭ would be a legitimate translation. It refers to the realm of the deadвАФthe grave and beyond. The language is excessive, but the point is that GodвАЩs wisdom is immeasurableвАФand Job is powerless before it.
n1801123: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ь÷є„£вАО (yakhalof) is literally вАЬpasses by/throughвАЭ (NIV вАЬcomes alongвАЭ in the sense of вАЬif it should so happenвАЭ). Many accept the emendation to вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„™÷Љ÷є„£вАО (yakhtof, вАЬhe seizes,вАЭ cf. Gordis, Driver), but there is not much support for these.
n1801124: tn The verb is the Hiphil of вАП„°÷Є„Т÷Ј„®вАО (sagar, вАЬto close; to shutвАЭ) and so here in this context it probably means something like вАЬto shut in; to confine.вАЭ But this is a difficult meaning, and the sentence is cryptic. E. Dhorme (Job, 162) thinks this word and the next have to be antithetical, and so he suggests from a meaning вАЬto keep confinedвАЭ the idea of keeping a matter secret; and with the next verb, вАЬto convene an assembly,вАЭ he offers вАЬto divulge it.вАЭ
n1801125: tn The pronoun вАЬyouвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text but has been supplied in the translation.
n1801126: tn The denominative Hiphil of вАП„І÷Є„Ф÷Є„ЬвАО (qahal, вАЬan assemblyвАЭ) has the idea of вАЬto convene an assembly.вАЭ In this context there would be the legal sense of convening a court, i.e., calling Job to account (D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 255). See E. Ullendorff, вАЬThe Meaning of QHLT,вАЭ VT 12 (1962): 215; he defines the verb also as вАЬargue, rebuke.вАЭ
n1801127: tn The verb means вАЬturn him back.вАЭ Zophar uses JobвАЩs own words (see 9:12).
n1801128: tn The pronoun is emphatic. Zophar implies that God indeed knows JobвАЩs sin even if Job does not.
n1801129: tn The expression is literally вАЬmen of emptinessвАЭ (see Ps 26:4). These are false men, for вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„РвАО (shav Њ) can mean вАЬvain, empty, or false, deceitful.вАЭ
n1801130: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 162) reads the prepositional phrase вАЬto himвАЭ rather than the negative; he translates the line as вАЬhe sees iniquity and observes it closely.вАЭ
n1801131: tn Some commentators do not take this last clause as a question, but simply as a statement, namely, that when God sees evil he does not need to ponder or consider itвАФhe knows it instantly. In that case it would be a circumstantial clause: вАЬwithout considering it.вАЭ D. J. A. Clines lists quite an array of other interpretations for the line (Job [WBC], 255); for example, вАЬand he is himself unobservedвАЭ; taking the word вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ) as an emphatic; taking the negative as a noun, вАЬconsidering them as nothingвАЭ; and others that change the verb to вАЬthey do not understand it.вАЭ But none of these are compelling; they offer no major improvement.
n1801132: tn As A. B. Davidson (Job, 84) says, the one thing will happen when the other happensвАФwhich is never. The word вАЬemptyвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„С„Х÷Љ„СвАО, navuv) means вАЬhollow; witless,вАЭ and вАЬbecome wiseвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„С÷µ„СвАО, yillavev) is вАЬwill get heartвАЭ (not to вАЬlack heartвАЭ as Driver suggested). Many commentators do not like the last line of the verse, and so offer even more emendations. E. F. Sutcliffe wanted to change вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„РвАО (pere Њ, вАЬdonkeyвАЭ) to вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„УвАО (pered, вАЬstallionвАЭ), rendering вАЬa witless wight may get wit when a mule is born a stallionвАЭ (вАЬNotes on Job, textual and exegetical,вАЭ Bib 30 [1949]: 70-71). Others approached the verse by changing the verb from вАП„Щ÷і„Х÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷µ„УвАО (yivvaled, вАЬis bornвАЭ) to вАП„Щ÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷µ„УвАО (yillamed, вАЬis taughtвАЭ), resulting in вАЬa hollow man may get understanding, and a wild donkeyвАЩs colt may be taught [= tamed]вАЭ (cf. NAB).
n1801133: tn The pronoun is emphatic, designed to put Job in a different class than the hollow menвАФat least to raise the possibility of his being in a different class.
n1801134: tn The Hebrew uses the perfect of вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬestablishвАЭ) with the object вАЬyour heart.вАЭ The verb can be translated вАЬprepare, fix, make firmвАЭ your heart. To fix the heart is to make it faithful and constant, the heart being the seat of the will and emotions. The use of the perfect here does not refer to the past, but should be given a future perfect senseвАФif you shall have fixed your heart, i.e., prove faithful. Job would have to make his heart secure, so that he was no longer driven about by differing views.
n1801135: tn This half-verse is part of the protasis and not, as in the RSV, the apodosis to the first half. The series of вАЬifвАЭ clauses will continue through these verses until v. 15.
n1801136: sn This is the posture of prayer (see Isa 1:15). The expression means вАЬspread out your palms,вАЭ probably meaning that the one praying would fall to his knees, put his forehead to the ground, and spread out his hands in front of him on the ground.
n1801137: tn Verse 14 should be taken as a parenthesis and not a continuation of the protasis, because it does not fit with v. 13 in that way (D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 256).
n1801138: tn Many commentators follow the Vulgate and read the line вАЬif you put away the sin that is in your hand.вАЭ They do this because the imperative comes between the protasis (v. 13) and the apodosis (v. 15) and does not appear to be clearly part of the protasis. The idea is close to the MT, but the MT is much more forcefulвАФif you find sin in your hand, get rid of it.
n1801139: tn The absolute certainty of the statement is communicated with the addition of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki). See GKC 498 ¬І159.ee.
n1801140: tn For this use of the preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) see GKC 382 ¬І119.w.
n1801141: tn The word вАЬlift upвАЭ is chosen to recall JobвАЩs statement that he could not lift up his head (10:15); the words вАЬwithout spotвАЭ recall his words вАЬfilled with shame.вАЭ The sentence here says that he will lift up his face in innocence and show no signs of GodвАЩs anger on him.
n1801142: tn The form вАП„Ю÷ї„¶÷Є„ІвАО (mutsaq) is a Hophal participle from вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ІвАО (yatsaq, вАЬto pourвАЭ). The idea is that of metal being melted down and then poured to make a statue, and so hard, firm, solid. The LXX reads the verse, вАЬfor thus your face shall shine again, like pure water, and you shall divest yourself of uncleanness, and shall not fear.вАЭ
n1801143: tn For a second time (see v. 13) Zophar employs the emphatic personal pronoun. Could he be providing a gentle reminder that Job might have forgotten the sin that has brought this trouble? After all, there will come a time when Job will not remember this time of trial.
n1801144: sn It is interesting to note in the book that the resolution of JobвАЩs trouble did not come in the way that Zophar prescribed it.
n1801145: tn The perfect verb forms an abbreviated relative clause (without the pronoun) modifying вАЬwater.вАЭ
n1801146: tn Some translations add the pronoun to make it specifically related to Job (вАЬyour lifeвАЭ), but this is not necessary. The word used here has the nuance of lasting life.
n1801147: tn Heb вАЬand more than the noonday life will arise.вАЭ The present translation is an interpretation in the context. The connotation of вАЬariseвАЭ in comparison with the noonday, and in contrast with the darkness, supports the interpretation.
n1801148: tn The form in the MT is the 3fsg imperfect verb, вАЬ[though] it be dark.вАЭ Most commentators revocalize the word to make it a noun (вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷ї„§÷Є„ФвАО, te њufah), giving the meaning вАЬthe darkness [of your life] will be like the morning.вАЭ The contrast is with Job 10:22; here the darkness will shine like the morning.
n1801149: tn The Hebrew verb means вАЬto dig,вАЭ but this does not provide a good meaning for the verse. A. B. Davidson offers an interpretation of вАЬsearch,вАЭ suggesting that before retiring at night Job would search and find everything in order. Some offer a better solution, namely, redefining the word on the basis of Arabic hafara, вАЬto protectвАЭ and repointing it to вАП„Х÷∞„Ч÷ї„§÷Ј„®÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (vekhufarta, вАЬyou will be protectedвАЭ). Other attempts to make sense of the line have involved the same process, but they are less convincing (for some of the more plausible proposals, see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 257).
n1801150: tn The clause that reads вАЬand there is no one making you afraid,вАЭ is functioning circumstantially here (see 5:4; 10:7).
n1801151: tn Heb вАЬthey will stroke your face,вАЭ a picture drawn from the domestic scene of a child stroking the face of the parent. The verb is a Piel, meaning вАЬstroke, make soft.вАЭ It is used in the Bible of seeking favor from God (supplication), but it may on the human level also mean seeking to sway people by flattery. See further D. R. Ap-Thomas, вАЬNotes on Some Terms Relating to Prayer,вАЭ VT 6 (1956): 225-41.
n1801152: tn The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah) means вАЬto fail, cease, fade away.вАЭ The fading of the eyes, i.e., loss of sight, loss of lifeвАЩs vitality, indicates imminent death.
n1801153: tn Heb вАЬa place of escapeвАЭ (with this noun pattern). There is no place to escape to because they all perish.
n1801154: tn The word is to be interpreted as a metonymy; it represents what is hoped for.
n1801155: tn Heb вАЬthe breathing out of the soulвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV вАЬthe giving up of the ghost.вАЭ The line is simply saying that the brightest hope that the wicked have is death.
n180121: sn This long speech of Job falls into three parts: in 12:2-25 Job expresses his resentment at his friendsвАЩ attitude of superiority and acknowledges the wisdom of God; then, in 13:1-28 Job expresses his determination to reason with God, expresses his scorn for his friendsвАЩ advice, and demands to know what his sins are; and finally, in 14:1-22 Job laments the brevity of life and the finality of death.
n180122: tn The expression вАЬyou are the peopleвАЭ is a way of saying that the friends hold the popular opinionвАФthey represent it. The line is sarcastic. Commentators do not think the parallelism is served well by this, and so offer changes for вАЬpeople.вАЭ Some have suggested вАЬyou are completeвАЭ (based on Arabic), вАЬyou are the strong oneвАЭ (based on Ugaritic), etc. J. A. Davies tried to solve the difficulty by making the second clause in the verse a paratactic relative clause: вАЬyou are the people with whom wisdom will dieвАЭ (вАЬNote on Job 12:2, вАЭ VT 25 [1975]: 670-71).
n180123: sn The sarcasm of Job admits their claim to wisdom, as if no one has it besides them. But the rest of his speech will show that they do not have a monopoly on it.
n180124: tn The word is literally вАЬheart,вАЭ meaning a mind or understanding.
n180125: tn Because this line is repeated in 13:2, many commentators delete it from this verse (as does the LXX). The Syriac translates вАП„†÷є„§÷µ„ЬвАО (nofel) as вАЬlittle,вАЭ and the Vulgate вАЬinferior.вАЭ Job is saying that he does not fall behind them in understanding.
n180126: tn Heb вАЬWith whom are not such things as these?вАЭ The point is that everyone knows the things that these friends have been sayingвАФthey are commonplace.
n180127: tn Some are troubled by the disharmony with вАЬI amвАЭ and вАЬto his friend.вАЭ Even though the difficulty is not insurmountable, some have emended the text. Some simply changed the verb to вАЬhe is,вАЭ which was not very compelling. C. D. Isbell argued that вАП„Р÷ґ„Ф÷∞„Щ÷ґ„ФвАО ( Њehyeh, вАЬI amвАЭ) is an orthographic variant of вАП„Щ÷і„Ф÷∞„Щ÷ґ„ФвАО (yihyeh, вАЬhe willвАЭ)вАФвАЬa person who does not know these things would be a laughingstockвАЭ (JANESCU 37 [1978]: 227-36). G. R. Driver suggests the meaning of the MT is something like вАЬ(One that is) a mockery to his friend I am to be.вАЭ
n180128: tn The word simply means вАЬlaughter,вАЭ but it can also mean the object of laughter (see Jer 20:7). The LXX jumps from one вАЬlaughterвАЭ to the next, eliminating everything in between, presumably due to haplography.
n180129: tn Heb вАЬhis friend.вАЭ A number of English versions (e.g., NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT) take this collectively, вАЬto my friends.вАЭ
n1801210: tn Heb вАЬone calling to God and he answered him.вАЭ H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 92) contends that because Job has been saying that God is not answering him, these words must be part of the derisive words of his friends.
n1801211: tn The two words, вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„І „™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsadiq tamim), could be understood as a hendiadys (= вАЬblamelessly justвАЭ) following W. G. E. Watson (Classical Hebrew Poetry, 327).
n1801212: tn The first word, вАП„Ь÷Ј„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„УвАО (lapid), could be rendered вАЬa torch of scorn,вАЭ but this gives no satisfying meaning. The вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) is often taken as an otiose letter, and the noun вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„УвАО (pid) is вАЬmisfortune, calamityвАЭ (cf. Job 30:24; 31:29).
n1801213: tn The noun вАП„Ґ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„™вАО ( њashtut, preferably вАП„Ґ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„™÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО,  њashtot) is an abstract noun from вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„™вАО ( њashat, вАЬto thinkвАЭ). The word вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Р÷≤„†÷Є„ЯвАО (sha Њanan) means вАЬeasy in mind, carefree,вАЭ and вАЬhappy.вАЭ
n1801214: tn The form has traditionally been taken to mean вАЬis readyвАЭ from the verb вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬis fixed, sureвАЭ). But many commentators look for a word parallel to вАЬcalamity.вАЭ So the suggestion has been put forward that вАП„†÷Є„Ы„Х÷є„ЯвАО (nakhon) be taken as a noun from вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (nakhah, вАЬstrike, smiteвАЭ): вАЬa blowвАЭ (Schultens, Dhorme, Gordis), вАЬthrustвАЭ or вАЬkickвАЭ (HALOT 698 s.v. I вАП„†÷Є„Ы„Х÷є„ЯвАО).
n1801215: tn The verse gives the other side of the coin now, the fact that the wicked prosper.
n1801216: tn The plural is used to suggest the supreme degree of arrogant confidence (E. Dhorme, Job, 171).
n1801217: sn The line is perhaps best understood as describing one who thinks he is invested with the power of God.
n1801218: sn As J. E. Hartley (Job [NICOT], 216) observes, in this section Job argues that respected tradition вАЬmust not be accepted uncritically.вАЭ
n1801219: tn The singular verb is used here with the plural collective subject (see GKC 464 ¬І145.k).
n1801220: tn The word in the MT means вАЬto complain,вАЭ not simply вАЬto speak,вАЭ and one would expect animals as the object here in parallel to the last verse. So several commentators have replaced the word with words for animals or reptilesвАФtotally different words (cf. NAB, вАЬreptilesвАЭ). The RSV and NRSV have here the word вАЬplantsвАЭ (see 30:4, 7; and Gen 21:15).
n1801221: tn A. B. Davidson (Job, 90) offers a solution by taking вАЬearthвАЭ to mean all the lower forms of life that teem in the earth (a metonymy of subject).
n1801222: tn This line could also be translated вАЬby all these,вАЭ meaning вАЬwho is not instructed by nature?вАЭ (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 93). But D. J. A. Clines points out that the verses have presented the animals as having knowledge and communicating it, so the former reading would be best (Job [WBC], 279).
n1801223: tc Some commentators have trouble with the name вАЬYahwehвАЭ in this verse, which is not the pattern in the poetic section of Job. Three mss of Kennicott and two of de Rossi have вАЬGod.вАЭ If this is so the reminiscence of Isaiah 41:20 led the copyist to introduce the tetragrammaton. But one could argue equally that the few mss with вАЬGodвАЭ were the copyistsвАЩ attempt to correct the text in accord with usage elsewhere.
n1801224: sn The expression вАЬhas done thisвАЭ probably refers to everything that has been discussed, namely, the way that God in his wisdom rules over the world, but specifically it refers to the infliction of suffering in the world.
n1801225: tn The construction with the relative clause includes a resumptive pronoun referring to God: вАЬwho in his handвАЭ = вАЬin his hand.вАЭ
n1801226: tn The two words вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) and вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) are synonymous in general. They could be translated вАЬsoulвАЭ and вАЬspirit,вАЭ but вАЬsoulвАЭ is not precise for вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh), and so вАЬlifeвАЭ is to be preferred. Since that is the case for the first half of the verse, вАЬbreathвАЭ will be preferable in the second part.
n1801227: tn Human life is made of вАЬfleshвАЭ and вАЬspirit.вАЭ So here the line reads вАЬand the spirit of all flesh of man.вАЭ If the text had simply said вАЬall flesh,вАЭ that would have applied to all flesh in which there is the breath of life (see Gen 6:17; 7:15). But to limit this to human beings requires the qualification with вАЬman.вАЭ
n1801228: tn The вАП„ХвАО (vav) introduces the comparison here (see 5:7; 11:12); see GKC 499 ¬І161.a.
n1801230:
tn The final preposition with its suffix is to be understood as a pleonastic dativus ethicus and not translated (see GKC 439 ¬І135.i).
sn In the rest of the chapter Job turns his attention away from creation to the wisdom of ancient men. In Job 13:1 when Job looks back to this part, he refers to both the eye and the ear. In vv. 13-25 Job refers to many catastrophes which he could not have seen, but must have heard about.
n1801231: tn The statement in the Hebrew Bible simply has вАЬamong the agedвАФwisdom.вАЭ Since this seems to be more the idea of the friends than of Job, scholars have variously tried to rearrange it. Some have proposed that Job is citing his friends: вАЬWith the old men, you say, is wisdomвАЭ (Budde, Gray, Hitzig). Others have simply made it a question (Weiser). But others take вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ) from the previous verse and make it the negative here, to say, вАЬwisdom is notвА¶.вАЭ But Job will draw on the wisdom of the aged, only with discernment, for ultimately all wisdom is with God.
n1801232: tn Heb вАЬhimвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1801233: sn A. B. Davidson (Job, 91) says, вАЬThese attributes of GodвАЩs [sic] confound and bring to nought everything bearing the same name among men.вАЭ
n1801234: tn The use of вАП„Ф÷µ„ЯвАО (hen, equivalent to вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО, hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) introduces a hypothetical condition.
n1801235: tn The verse employs antithetical ideas: вАЬtear downвАЭ and вАЬbuild up,вАЭ вАЬimprisonвАЭ and вАЬescape.вАЭ The Niphal verbs in the sentences are potential imperfects. All of this is to say that humans cannot reverse the will of God.
n1801236: tc The LXX has a clarification: вАЬhe will dry the earth.вАЭ
n1801237: sn The verse is focusing on the two extremes of drought and flood. Both are described as being under the power of God.
n1801238: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (hafakh) means вАЬto overthrow; to destroy; to overwhelm.вАЭ It was used in Job 9:5 for вАЬoverturningвАЭ mountains. The word is used in Genesis for the destruction of Sodom.
n1801239: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„©„Б÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tushiyyah) is here rendered вАЬprudence.вАЭ Some object that GodвАЩs power is intended here, and so a word for power and not wisdom should be included. But v. 13 mentioned wisdom. The point is that it is GodвАЩs efficient wisdom that leads to success. One could interpret this as a metonymy of cause, the intended meaning being victory or success.
n1801240: tn The Hebrew text uses a wordplay here: вАП„©„Б÷є„Т÷µ„ТвАО (shogeg) is вАЬthe one going astray,вАЭ i.e., the one who is unable to guard and guide his life. The second word is вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„Т÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (mashgeh), from a different but historically related root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah), which here in the Hiphil means вАЬthe one who misleads, causes to go astray.вАЭ These two words are designed to include everybodyвАФall are under the wisdom of God.
n1801241: tn The personal pronoun normally present as the subject of the participle is frequently omitted (see GKC 381 ¬І119.s).
n1801242: tn GKC 361-62 ¬І116.x notes that almost as a rule a participle beginning a sentence is continued with a finite verb with or without a вАП„ХвАО (vav). Here the participle (вАЬleadsвАЭ) is followed by an imperfect (вАЬmakes foolsвАЭ) after a вАП„ХвАО (vav).
n1801243: tn The word вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„ЬвАО (sholal), from the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (shalal, вАЬto plunder; to stripвАЭ), is an adjective expressing the state (and is in the singular, as if to say, вАЬin the state of one nakedвАЭ [GKC 375 ¬І118.o]). The word is found in military contexts (see Mic 1:8). It refers to the carrying away of people in nakedness and shame by enemies who plunder (see also Isa 8:1-4). They will go away as slaves and captives, deprived of their outer garments. Some (cf. NAB) suggest вАЬbarefoot,вАЭ based on the LXX of Mic 1:8, but the meaning of that is uncertain. G. R. Driver wanted to derive the word from an Arabic root вАЬto be mad; to be giddy,вАЭ forming a better parallel.
n1801244: sn The judges, like the counselors, are nobles in the cities. God may reverse their lot, either by captivity or by shame, and they cannot resist his power.
n1801245: tn Some translate this вАЬmakes madвАЭ as in Isa 44:25, but this gives the wrong connotation today; more likely God shows them to be fools.
n1801246: tn The verb may be classified as a gnomic perfect, or possibly a potential perfectвАФвАЬhe can loosen.вАЭ The Piel means вАЬto untie; to unbindвАЭ (Job 30:11; 38:31; 39:5).
n1801247: tc There is a potential textual difficulty here. The MT has вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Ј„®вАО (musar, вАЬdisciplineвАЭ), which might have replaced вАП„Ю„Х÷є„°÷µ„®вАО (moser, вАЬbond, chainвАЭ) from вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„®вАО ( Њasar, вАЬto bindвАЭ). Or вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Ј„®вАО might be an unusual form of вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„®вАО (an option noted in HALOT 557 s.v. *вАП„Ю„Х÷є„°÷µ„®вАО). The line is saying that if the kings are bound, God can set them free, and in the second half, if they are free, he can bind them. Others take the view that this word вАЬbondвАЭ refers to the power kings have over others, meaning that God can reduce kings to slavery.
n1801248: tn Some commentators want to change вАП„Р÷µ„Ц„Х÷є„®вАО ( Њezor, вАЬgirdleвАЭ) to вАП„Р÷µ„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО ( Њesur, вАЬbondвАЭ) because binding the loins with a girdle was an expression for strength. But H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 96) notes that binding the kingвАЩs loins this way would mean that he would serve and do menial tasks. Such a reference would certainly indicate troubled times.
n1801249: tn Except for вАЬpriests,вАЭ the phraseology is identical to v. 17a.
n1801250: tn The verb has to be defined by its context: it can mean вАЬfalsifyвАЭ (Exod 23:8), вАЬmake tortuousвАЭ (Prov 19:3), or вАЬplungeвАЭ into misfortune (Prov 21:12). God overthrows those who seem to be solid.
n1801251: tn The original meaning of вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„™÷Є„ЯвАО ( Њetan) is вАЬperpetual.вАЭ It is usually an epithet for a torrent that is always flowing. It carries the connotations of permanence and stability; here applied to people in society, it refers to one whose power and influence does not change. These are the pillars of society.
n1801252: tn The Hebrew вАП„†÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷Є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ne Њemanim) is the Niphal participle; it is often translated вАЬthe faithfulвАЭ in the Bible. The Rabbis rather fancifully took the word from вАП„†÷∞„Р÷ї„ЭвАО (ne Њum, вАЬoracle, utteranceвАЭ) and so rendered it вАЬthose who are eloquent, fluent in words.вАЭ But that would make this the only place in the Bible where this form came from that root or any other root besides вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman, вАЬconfirm, supportвАЭ). But to say that God takes away the speech of the truthful or the faithful would be very difficult. It has to refer to reliable men, because it is parallel to the elders or old men. The NIV has вАЬtrusted advisers,вАЭ which fits well with kings and judges and priests.
n1801253: tn Heb вАЬhe removes the lip of the trusted ones.вАЭ
n1801255: tn The expression in Hebrew uses вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (meziakh, вАЬbeltвАЭ) and the Piel verb вАП„®÷і„§÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (rippah, вАЬto loosenвАЭ) so that вАЬto loosen the belt of the mightyвАЭ would indicate вАЬto disarm/incapacitate the mighty.вАЭ Others have opted to change the text: P. Jo√Љon emends to read вАЬforeheadвАЭвАФвАЬhe humbles the brow of the mighty.вАЭ
n1801256: tn The word вАП„Р÷Є„§÷Ј„ІвАО ( Њafaq, вАЬto be strongвАЭ) is well-attested, and the form вАП„Р÷Є„§÷і„Щ„ІвАО ( Њafiq) is a normal adjective formation. So a translation like вАЬmightyвАЭ (KJV, NIV) or вАЬpowerfulвАЭ is acceptable, and further emendations are unnecessary.
n1801257: tn The Hebrew word is traditionally rendered вАЬshadow of deathвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); see comments at Job 3:3.
n1801258: tn The word вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„В÷∞„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„РвАО (masgi Њ, вАЬmakes greatвАЭ) is a common Aramaic word, but only occurs in Hebrew here and in Job 8:11 and 36:24. Some mss have a change, reading the form from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah, вАЬleading astrayвАЭ). The LXX omits the line entirely.
n1801259: tn The difficulty with the verb вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (nakhah) is that it means вАЬto lead; to guide,вАЭ but not вАЬto lead awayвАЭ or вАЬto disperse,вАЭ unless this passage provides the context for such a meaning. Moreover, it never has a negative connotation. Some vocalize it вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Ј„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ч÷ґ„ЭвАО (vayyannikhem), from вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh), the causative meaning of вАЬrest,вАЭ or вАЬabandonвАЭ (Driver, Gray, Gordis). But even there it would mean вАЬleave in peace.вАЭ Blommerde suggests the second part is antithetical parallelism, and so should be positive. So Ball proposed вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„Ч÷ґ„ЭвАО (vayyimkhem) from вАП„Ю÷Є„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (makhah): вАЬand he cuts them off.вАЭ
n1801260: sn The rise and fall of nations, which does not seem to be governed by any moral principle, is for Job another example of GodвАЩs arbitrary power.
n1801261: tn Heb вАЬthe heads of the people of the earth.вАЭ
n1801263: tn The text has вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„™÷є„Ф„Х÷Љ „Ь÷є„Р÷Њ„У÷Є„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (betohu lo Њ darekh): вАЬin wasteвАФno way,вАЭ or вАЬin a wasteland [where there is] no way,вАЭ thus, вАЬtracklessвАЭ (see the discussion of negative attributes using вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО [lo Њ] in GKC 482 ¬І152.u).
n1801264: tn The word is an adverbial accusative.
n1801265: tn The verb is the same that was in v. 24, вАЬHe makes them [the leaders still] wanderвАЭ (the Hiphil of вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО, ta њah). But in this passage some commentators emend the text to a Niphal of the verb and put it in the plural, to get the reading вАЬthey reel to and fro.вАЭ But even if the verse closes the chapter and there is no further need for a word of divine causation, the Hiphil sense works well hereвАФcausing people to wander like a drunken man would be the same as making them stagger.
n180131: sn Chapter 13 records JobвАЩs charges against his friends for the way they used their knowledge (1-5), his warning that God would find out their insincerity (6-12), and his pleading of his case to God in which he begs for God to remove his hand from him and that he would not terrify him with his majesty and that he would reveal the sins that caused such great suffering (13-28).
n180132: tn Hebrew has вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„ЬвАО (kol, вАЬallвАЭ); there is no reason to add anything to the text to gain a meaning вАЬall this.вАЭ
n180133: tn Heb вАЬLike your knowledgeвАЭ; in other words Job is saying that his knowledge is like their knowledge.
n180134: tn The pronoun makes the subject emphatic and stresses the contrast: вАЬI knowвАФI also.вАЭ
n180135: tn The verb вАЬfallвАЭ is used here as it was in Job 4:13 to express becoming lower than someone, i.e., inferior.
n180136: tn The verb is simply the Piel imperfect вАП„Р÷≤„У÷Ј„С÷Љ÷µ„®вАО ( Њadabber, вАЬI speakвАЭ). It should be classified as a desiderative imperfect, saying, вАЬI desire to speak.вАЭ This is reinforced with the verb вАЬto wish, desireвАЭ in the second half of the verse.
n180137: tn The Hebrew title for God here is вАП„Р÷ґ„Ь÷Њ„©„Б÷Ј„У÷Љ÷Ј„ЩвАО ( Њel shadday, вАЬEl ShaddaiвАЭ).
n180138: tn The infinitive absolute functions here as the direct object of the verb вАЬdesireвАЭ (see GKC 340 ¬І113.b).
n180139: tn The infinitive вАП„Ф„Х÷є„Ы÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (hokheakh) is from the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh), which means вАЬto argue, plead, debate.вАЭ It has the legal sense here of arguing a case (cf. 5:17).
n1801310: tn The вАП„Ш÷є„§÷∞„Ь÷µ„Щ÷Њ„©„Б÷Є„І÷ґ„®вАО (tofele shaqer) are вАЬplasterers of liesвАЭ (Ps 119:69). The verb means вАЬto coat, smear, plaster.вАЭ The idea is that of imputing something that is not true. Job is saying that his friends are inventors of lies. The LXX was influenced by the next line and came up with вАЬfalse physicians.вАЭ
n1801311: tn The literal rendering of the construct would be вАЬhealers of worthlessness.вАЭ Ewald and Dillmann translated it вАЬpatchersвАЭ based on a meaning in Arabic and Ethiopic; this would give the idea вАЬbotchers.вАЭ But it makes equally good sense to take вАЬhealersвАЭ as the meaning, for JobвАЩs friends came to minister comfort and restoration to himвАФbut they failed. See P. Humbert, вАЬMaladie et medicine dans lвАЩAT,вАЭ RHPR 44 (1964): 1-29.
n1801312: tn The construction is the imperfect verb in the wish formula preceded by the infinitive that intensifies it. The Hiphil is not directly causative here, but internallyвАФвАЬkeep silent.вАЭ
n1801313: tn Heb вАЬand it would be for you for wisdom,вАЭ or вАЬthat it would become your wisdom.вАЭ Job is rather sarcastic here, indicating if they shut up they would prove themselves to be wise (see Prov 17:28).
n1801314: sn Job first will argue with his friends. His case that he will plead with God begins in v. 13. The same root вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh, вАЬargue, pleadвАЭ) is used here as in v. 3b (see note). Synonymous parallelism between the two halves of this verse supports this translation.
n1801315: tn The Hebrew word вАП„®÷і„С„Х÷є„™вАО (rivot, вАЬdisputes, contentionsвАЭ) continues the imagery of presenting a legal case. The term is used of legal disputations and litigation. See, also, v. 19a.
n1801316: tn Heb вАЬspeak iniquity.вАЭ The form functions adverbially. The noun вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њavlah) means вАЬperversion; injustice; iniquity; falsehood.вАЭ Here it is parallel to вАП„®÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (remiyyah, вАЬfraud; deceit; treacheryвАЭ).
n1801317: tn The expression вАЬfor GodвАЭ means вАЬin favor of GodвАЭ or вАЬon GodвАЩs behalf.вАЭ Job is amazed that they will say false things on GodвАЩs behalf.
n1801318: sn The idiom used here is вАЬWill you lift up his face?вАЭ Here Job is being very sarcastic, for this expression usually means that a judge is taking a bribe. Job is accusing them of taking GodвАЩs side.
n1801319: tn The same root is used here (вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО, riv, вАЬdispute, contentionвАЭ) as in v. 6b (see note).
n1801320: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (khafar) means вАЬto search out, investigate, examine.вАЭ In the conditional clause the imperfect verb expresses the hypothetical case.
n1801321: tn Both the infinitive and the imperfect of вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (talal, вАЬdeceive, mockвАЭ) retain the вАП„ФвАО (he) (GKC 148 ¬І53.q). But for the alternate form, see F. C. Fensham, вАЬThe Stem HTL in Hebrew,вАЭ VT 9 (1959): 310-11. The infinitive is used here in an adverbial sense after the preposition.
n1801322:
tn The verbal idea is intensified with the infinitive absolute. This is the same verb used in v. 3; here it would have the sense of вАЬrebuke, convict.вАЭ
sn PeakeвАЩs observation is worth noting, namely, that as Job attacks the unrighteousness of God boldly he nonetheless has confidence in GodвАЩs righteousness that would not allow liars to defend him.
n1801323: sn The use of the word вАЬin secretвАЭ or вАЬsecretlyвАЭ suggests that what they do is a guilty action (31:27a).
n1801324: sn The word translated вАЬhis majestyвАЭ or вАЬhis splendorвАЭ (вАП„©„В÷∞„Р÷µ„™„Х÷євАО, se Њeto) forms a play on the word вАЬshow partialityвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷і„©„В÷Љ÷Є„Р„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО, tissa Њun) in the last verse. They are both from the verb вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Ј„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto lift upвАЭ).
n1801325: tn On this verb in the Piel, see 7:14.
n1801326: tn Heb вАЬHis dreadвАЭ; the suffix is a subjective genitive.
n1801327: tn The word is вАП„Ц÷і„Ы÷∞„®÷є„†÷µ„Щ„Ы÷ґ„ЭвАО (zikhronekhem, вАЬyour remembrancesвАЭ). The word вАП„Ц÷і„Ы÷Љ÷Є„®÷є„ЯвАО (zikkaron) not only can mean the act of remembering, but also what is rememberedвАФwhat provokes memory or is worth being remembered. In the plural it can mean all the memorabilia, and in this verse all the sayings and teachings. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 99) suggests that in JobвАЩs speech it could mean вАЬall your memorized sayings.вАЭ
n1801328: tn The parallelism of вАЬdustвАЭ and вАЬashesвАЭ is fairly frequent in scripture. But вАЬproverbs of ashesвАЭ is difficult. The genitive is certainly describing the proverbs; it could be classified as a genitive of apposition, proverbs that are/have become ashes. Ashes represent something that at one time may have been useful, but now has been reduced to what is worthless.
n1801329: tn There is a division of opinion on the source of this word. Some take it from вАЬanswer,вАЭ related to Arabic, Aramaic, and Syriac words for вАЬanswer,вАЭ and so translate it вАЬresponsesвАЭ (JB). Others take it from a word for вАЬback,вАЭ with a derived meaning of the вАЬbossвАЭ of the shield, and translate it вАЬbulwarkвАЭ or вАЬdefensesвАЭ (NEB, RSV, NIV). The idea of вАЬanswersвАЭ may fit the parallelism better, but вАЬdefensesвАЭ can be taken figuratively to refer to verbal defenses.
n1801330: sn Any defense made with clay would crumble on impact.
n1801331: tn The Hebrew has a pregnant construction: вАЬbe silent from me,вАЭ meaning вАЬstand away from me in silence,вАЭ or вАЬrefrain from talking with me.вАЭ See GKC 384 ¬І119.ff. The LXX omits вАЬfrom me,вАЭ as do several commentators.
n1801332: tn The verb is the Piel cohortative; following the imperative of the first colon this verb would show purpose or result. The inclusion of the independent personal pronoun makes the focus emphaticвАФвАЬso that I (in my turn) may speak.вАЭ
n1801333: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО ( њavar, вАЬpass overвАЭ) is used with the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬuponвАЭ) to express the advent of misfortune, namely, something coming against him.
n1801334: tn The interrogative pronoun вАП„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mah) is used in indirect questions, here introducing a clause [with the verb understood] as the objectвАФвАЬwhatever it beвАЭ (see GKC 443-44 ¬І137.c).
n1801335: tc Most editors reject вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО ( њal mah) as dittography from the last verse.
n1801336: tn Heb вАЬwhy do I take my flesh in my teeth?вАЭ This expression occurs nowhere else. It seems to be drawn from animal imagery in which the wild beast seizes the prey and carries it off to a place of security. The idea would then be that Job may be destroying himself. An animal that fights with its flesh (prey) in its mouth risks losing it. Other commentators do not think this is satisfactory, but they are unable to suggest anything better.
n1801337: tn There is a textual difficulty here that factors into the interpretation of the verse. The Kethib is вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ, вАЬnotвАЭ), but the Qere is вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo, вАЬto himвАЭ). The RSV takes the former: вАЬBehold, he will slay me, I have no hope.вАЭ The NIV takes it as вАЬthough he slay me, yet will I hope in him.вАЭ Job is looking ahead to death, which is not an evil thing to him. The point of the verse is that he is willing to challenge God at the risk of his life; and if God slays him, he is still confident that he will be vindicatedвАФas he says later in this chapter. Other suggestions are not compelling. E. Dhorme (Job, 187) makes a slight change of вАП„Р÷≤„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њayakhel, вАЬI will hopeвАЭ) to вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њakhil, вАЬI will [not] trembleвАЭ). A. B. Davidson (Job, 98) retains the MT, but interprets the verb more in line with its use in the book: вАЬI will not waitвАЭ (cf. NLT).
n1801338: tn On вАП„Р÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њakh, вАЬsurelyвАЭ) see GKC 483 ¬І153 on intensive clauses.
n1801339: tn The verb once again is вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (yakhah, in the Hiphil, вАЬargue a case, plead, defend, contestвАЭ). But because the word usually means вАЬaccuseвАЭ rather than вАЬdefend,вАЭ I. L. Seeligmann proposed changing вАЬmy waysвАЭ to вАЬhis waysвАЭ (вАЬZur Terminologie f√Љr das Gerichtsverfahren im Wortschatz des biblischen Hebr√§isch,вАЭ VTSup 16 [1967]: 251-78). But the word can be interpreted appropriately in the context without emendation.
n1801340: sn The fact that Job will dare to come before God and make his case is evidenceвАФto Job at leastвАФthat he is innocent.
n1801341: tn The infinitive absolute intensifies the imperative, which serves here with the force of an immediate call to attention. In accordance with GKC 342 ¬І113.n, the construction could be translated, вАЬKeep listeningвАЭ (so ESV).
n1801342: tn The verb has to be supplied in this line, for the MT has вАЬand my explanation in your ears.вАЭ In the verse, both вАЬwordвАЭ and вАЬexplanationвАЭ are Aramaisms (the latter appearing in Dan 5:12 for the explanation of riddles).
n1801343: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh) functions almost as an imperative here, calling attention to what follows: вАЬlookвАЭ (archaic: behold).
n1801344: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh) means вАЬto set in order, set in array [as a battle], prepareвАЭ in the sense here of arrange and organize a lawsuit.
n1801345: tn The pronoun is added because this is what the verse means.
n1801346: tn The word вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat) usually means вАЬjudgment; decision.вАЭ Here it means вАЬlawsuitвАЭ (and so a metonymy of effect gave rise to this usage; see Num 27:5; 2 Sam 15:4).
n1801347: tn The pronoun is emphatic before the verb: вАЬI know that it is I who am right.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto be right; to be righteous.вАЭ Some have translated it вАЬvindicated,вАЭ looking at the outcome of the suit.
n1801348: tn The interrogative is joined with the emphatic pronoun, stressing вАЬwho is he [who] will contend,вАЭ or more emphatically, вАЬwho in the world will contend.вАЭ Job is confident that no one can bring charges against him. He is certain of success.
n1801349: sn Job is confident that he will be vindicated. But if someone were to show up and have proof of sin against him, he would be silent and die (literally вАЬkeep silent and expireвАЭ).
n1801350: tn The line reads вАЬdo not do two things.вАЭ
n1801351: tn вАЬGodвАЭ is supplied to the verse, for the address is now to him. Job wishes to enter into dispute with God, but he first appeals that God not take advantage of him with his awesome power.
n1801352: tn The imperative вАП„Ф÷Ј„®÷∞„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (harkhaq, вАЬremoveвАЭ; GKC 98 ¬І29.q), from вАП„®÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (rakhaq, вАЬfar, be farвАЭ) means вАЬtake away [far away]; to remove.вАЭ
n1801353: sn This is a common, but bold, anthropomorphism. The fact that the word used is вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„£вАО (kaf, properly вАЬpalmвАЭ) rather than вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО (yad, вАЬhand,вАЭ with the sense of power) may stress JobвАЩs feeling of being trapped or confined (see also Ps 139:5, 7).
n1801355: tn The imperatives in the verse function like the future tense in view of their use for instruction or advice. The chiastic arrangement of the verb forms is interesting: imperative + imperfect, imperfect + imperative. The imperative is used for God, but the imperfect is used when Job is the subject. Job is calling for the court to conveneвАФhe will be either the defendant or the prosecutor.
n1801356: tn The pronoun вАЬmyвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied here in the translation.
n1801357: sn Job uses three words for sin here: вАЬiniquities,вАЭ which means going astray, erring; вАЬsins,вАЭ which means missing the mark or the way; and вАЬtransgressions,вАЭ which are open rebellions. They all emphasize different kinds of sins and different degrees of willfulness. Job is demanding that any sins be brought up. Both Job and his friends agree that great afflictions would have to indicate great offensesвАФhe wants to know what they are.
n1801358: sn The anthropomorphism of вАЬhide the faceвАЭ indicates a withdrawal of favor and an outpouring of wrath (see Pss 27:9; 30:7 [8]; Isa 54:8). Sometimes God вАЬhides his faceвАЭ to make himself invisible or aloof (see 34:29). In either case, if God covers his face it is because he considers Job an enemyвАФat least this is what Job thinks.
n1801359: tn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„®„Х÷є„•вАО (ta њarots, вАЬyou tormentвАЭ) is from вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО ( њarats), which usually means вАЬfear; dread,вАЭ but can also mean вАЬto make afraid; to terrifyвАЭ (Isa 2:19, 21). The imperfect is here taken as a desiderative imperfect: вАЬwhy do you want to,вАЭ but it could also be a simple future: вАЬwill you torment.вАЭ
n1801360: tn The word вАП„†÷і„У÷Љ÷Є„£вАО (niddaf) is вАЬdrivenвАЭ from the root вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„£вАО (nadaf, вАЬdriveвАЭ). The words вАЬby the windвАЭ or the interpretation вАЬwindblownвАЭ has to be added for the clarification. Job is comparing himself to this leaf (so an implied comparison, called hypocatastasis)вАФso light and insubstantial that it is amazing that God should come after him. Guillaume suggests that the word is not from this root, but from a second root вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„£вАО (nadaf), cognate to Arabic nadifa, вАЬto dry upвАЭ (A. Guillaume, вАЬA Note on Isaiah 19:7, вАЭ JTS 14 [1963]: 382-83). But as D. J. A. Clines notes (Job [WBC], 283), a dried leaf is a driven leafвАФa point Guillaume allows as he says there is ambiguity in the term.
n1801361: tn The word вАП„І÷Ј„©„БвАО (qash) means вАЬchaff; stubble,вАЭ or a wisp of straw. It is found in Job 41:20-21 for that which is so worthless and insignificant that it is hardly worth mentioning. If dried up or withered, it too will be blown away in the wind.
n1801362: tn The meaning is that of writing down a formal charge against someone (cf. Job 31:15).
n1801363: sn Job acknowledges sins in his youth, but they are trifling compared to the suffering he now endures. Job thinks it unjust of God to persecute him now for thoseвАФif that is what is happening.
n1801364: tn The word occurs here and in Job 33:11. It could be taken as вАЬstocks,вАЭ in which the feet were held fast; or it could be вАЬshackles,вАЭ which allowed the prisoner to move about. The parallelism favors the latter, if the two lines are meant to be referring to the same thing.
n1801365: tn The word means вАЬways; roads; paths,вАЭ but it is used here in the sense of the вАЬwayвАЭ in which one goes about his activities.
n1801366: tn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„™÷∞„Ч÷Ј„І÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (titkhaqqeh) is a Hitpael from the root вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (khaqah, parallel to вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО, khaqaq). The word means вАЬto engraveвАЭ or вАЬto carve out.вАЭ This Hitpael would mean вАЬto imprint something on oneselfвАЭ (E. Dhorme [Job, 192] says on oneвАЩs mind, and so derives the meaning вАЬexamine.вАЭ). The object of this is the expression вАЬon the roots of my feet,вАЭ which would refer to where the feet hit the ground. Since the passage has more to do with GodвАЩs restricting JobвАЩs movement, the translation вАЬyou set a boundary to the soles of my feetвАЭ would be better than DhormeвАЩs view. The image of inscribing or putting marks on the feet is not found elsewhere. It may be, as Pope suggests, a reference to marking the slaves to make tracking them easier. The LXX has вАЬyou have penetrated to my heels.вАЭ
n1801367: tn Heb вАЬand he.вАЭ Some of the commentators move the verse and put it after Job 14:2, 3 or 6.
n1801368: tn The word вАП„®÷Є„І÷Є„СвАО (raqav) is used elsewhere in the Bible of dry rot in a house, or rotting bones in a grave. It is used in parallelism with вАЬmothвАЭ both here and in Hos 5:12. The LXX has вАЬlike a wineskin.вАЭ This would be from вАП„®÷є„І÷ґ„СвАО (roqev, вАЬwineskinвАЭ). This word does not occur in the Hebrew Bible, but is attested in Sir 43:20 and in Aramaic. The change is not necessary.
n180141: tn The first of the threefold apposition for вАП„Р÷Є„У÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њadam, вАЬmanвАЭ) is вАЬborn of a woman.вАЭ The genitive (вАЬwomanвАЭ) after a passive participle denotes the agent of the action (see GKC 359 ¬І116.l).
n180142: tn The second description is simply вАЬ[is] short of days.вАЭ The meaning here is that his life is short (вАЬdaysвАЭ being put as the understatement for вАЬyearsвАЭ).
n180143: tn The third expression is вАЬconsumed/full/satedвАФwith/ofвАФtrouble/restlessness.вАЭ The latter word, вАП„®÷є„Т÷ґ„ЦвАО (rogez), occurred in Job 3:17; see also the idea in 10:15.
n180144: tn Heb вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (yatsa Њ, вАЬcomes forthвАЭ). The perfect verb expresses characteristic action and so is translated by the present tense (see GKC 329 ¬І111.s).
n180145: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ЬвАО (vayyimmal) is from the root вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (malal, вАЬto languish; to witherвАЭ) and not from a different root вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (malal, вАЬto cut offвАЭ).
n180146: tn The verb is вАЬand he does not stand.вАЭ Here the verb means вАЬto stay fixed; to abide.вАЭ The shadow does not stay fixed, but continues to advance toward darkness.
n180147: tn Heb вАЬopen the eye on,вАЭ an idiom meaning to prepare to judge someone.
n180148: tn The verse opens with вАП„Р÷Ј„£÷Њ„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО ( Њaf  њal zeh), meaning вАЬeven on such a one!вАЭ It is an exclamation of surprise.
n180149: tn The text clearly has вАЬmeвАЭ as the accusative, but many wish to emend it to say вАЬhimвАЭ (вАП„Р÷є„™„Х÷євАО,  Њoto). But D. J. A. Clines rightly rejects this in view of the way Job is written, often moving back and forth between his own tragedy and othersвАЩ tragedies (Job [WBC], 283).
n1801410: tn The expression is вАП„Ю÷і„Щ÷Њ„Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (mi yitten, вАЬwho will giveвАЭ; see GKC 477 ¬І151.b). Some commentators (H. H. Rowley and A. B. Davidson) wish to take this as the optative formula: вАЬO that a clean might come out of an unclean!вАЭ But that does not fit the verse very well, and still requires the addition of a verb. The exclamation here simply implies something impossibleвАФman is unable to attain purity.
n1801411: sn The point being made is that the entire human race is contaminated by sin, and therefore cannot produce something pure. In this context, since man is born of woman, it is saying that the woman and the man who is brought forth from her are impure. See Ps 51:5; Isa 6:5; Gen 6:5.
n1801413: tn The passive participle is from вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (kharats), which means вАЬdetermined.вАЭ The word literally means вАЬcutвАЭ (Lev 22:22, вАЬmutilatedвАЭ). E. Dhorme, (Job, 197) takes it to mean вАЬengravedвАЭ as on stone; from a custom of inscribing decrees on tablets of stone he derives the meaning here of вАЬdecreed.вАЭ This, he argues, is parallel to the way вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО (khaqaq, вАЬengraveвАЭ) is used. The word вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq) is an вАЬordinanceвАЭ or вАЬstatuteвАЭ; the idea is connected to the verb вАЬto engrave.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬif his life should be but one day on the earth, and his months are numbered by him, you have appointed him for a time and he shall by no means exceed it.вАЭ
n1801414: tn Heb вАЬ[is] with you.вАЭ This clearly means under GodвАЩs control.
n1801415:
tn The word вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq) has the meanings of вАЬdecree, decision, and limitвАЭ (cf. Job 28:26; 38:10).
sn Job is saying that God foreordains the number of the days of man. He foreknows the number of the months. He fixes the limit of human life which cannot be passed.
n1801416: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„У÷Ј„ЬвАО (khadal) means вАЬto desist; to cease.вАЭ The verb would mean here вАЬand let him desist,вАЭ which some take to mean вАЬand let him rest.вАЭ But since this is rather difficult in the line, commentators have suggested other meanings. Several emend the text slightly to make it an imperative rather than an imperfect; this is then translated вАЬand desist.вАЭ The expression вАЬfrom himвАЭ must be added. Another suggestion that is far-fetched is that of P. J. Calderone (вАЬCHDL-II in poetic texts,вАЭ CBQ 23 [1961]: 451-60) and D. W. Thomas (VTSup 4 [1957]: 8-16), having a new meaning of вАЬbe fat.вАЭ
n1801417: tn There are two roots вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Ј„ФвАО (ratsah). The first is the common word, meaning вАЬto delight in; to have pleasure in.вАЭ The second, most likely used here, means вАЬto pay; to acquit a debtвАЭ (cf. Lev 26:34, 41, 43). Here with the mention of the simile with the hired man, the completing of the job is in view.
n1801418: tn The genitive after the construct is one of advantageвАФit is hope for the tree.
n1801419: sn The figure now changes to a tree for the discussion of the finality of death. At least the tree will sprout again when it is cut down. Why, Job wonders, should what has been granted to the tree not also be granted to humans?
n1801420: tn The Hiphil of вАП„Ц÷Є„І÷Ј„ЯвАО (zaqan, вАЬto be oldвАЭ) is here an internal causative, вАЬto grow old.вАЭ
n1801421: tn The Hiphil is here classified as an inchoative Hiphil (see GKC 145 ¬І53.e), for the tree only begins to die. In other words, it appears to be dead, but actually is not completely dead.
n1801422:
tn The LXX translates вАЬdustвАЭ [soil] with вАЬrock,вАЭ probably in light of the earlier illustration of the tree growing in the rocks.
sn Job is thinking here of a tree that dies or decays because of a drought rather than being uprooted, because the next verse will tell how it can revive with water.
n1801423: tn The personification adds to the comparison with peopleвАФthe tree is credited with the sense of smell to detect the water.
n1801424: tn The sense of вАЬflourishвАЭ for this verb is found in Ps 92:12, 13 [13, 14 HT], and Prov 14:11. It makes an appropriate parallel with вАЬbring forth boughsвАЭ in the second half.
n1801426: tn There are two words for вАЬmanвАЭ in this verse. The first (вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО, gever) can indicate a вАЬstrongвАЭ or вАЬmature manвАЭ or вАЬmighty man,вАЭ the hero; and the second (вАП„Р÷Є„У÷Є„ЭвАО,  Њadam) simply designates the person as mortal.
n1801427: tn The word вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„©„БвАО (khalash) in Aramaic and Syriac means вАЬto be weakвАЭ (interestingly, the Syriac OT translated вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„©„БвАО [khalash] with вАЬfade awayвАЭ here). The derived noun вАЬthe weakвАЭ would be in direct contrast to вАЬthe mighty man.вАЭ In the transitive sense the verb means вАЬto weaken; to defeatвАЭ (Exod 17:13); here it may have the sense of вАЬbe lifeless, unconscious, inanimateвАЭ (cf. E. Dhorme, Job, 199). Many commentators emend the text to вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ь÷є„£вАО (yakhalof, вАЬpasses on; passes awayвАЭ). A. Guillaume tries to argue that the form is a variant of the other, the letters вАП„©„БвАО (shin) and вАП„§вАО (pe) being interchangeable (вАЬThe Use of halas in Exod 17:13, Isa 14:12, and Job 14:10, вАЭ JTS 14 [1963]: 91-92). G. R. Driver connected it to Arabic halasa, вАЬcarry off suddenlyвАЭ (вАЬThe Resurrection of Marine and Terrestrial Creatures,вАЭ JSS 7 [1962]: 12-22). But the basic idea of вАЬbe weak, powerlessвАЭ is satisfactory in the text. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 105) says, вАЬWhere words are so carefully chosen, it is gratuitous to substitute less expressive words as some editors do.вАЭ
n1801428: tn This break to a question adds a startling touch to the whole verse. The obvious meaning is that he is gone. The LXX weakens it: вАЬand is no more.вАЭ
n1801429: tn The comparative clause may be signaled simply by the context, especially when facts of a moral nature are compared with the physical world (see GKC 499 ¬І161.a).
n1801430: tn The Hebrew word вАП„Щ÷Є„ЭвАО (yam) can mean вАЬseaвАЭ or вАЬlake.вАЭ
n1801431: tc The Hebrew construction is вАЬuntil not,вАЭ which is unusual if not impossible; it is found in only one other type of context. In its six other occurrences (Num 21:35; Deut 3:3; Josh 8:22; 10:33; 11:8; 2 Kgs 10:11) the context refers to the absence of survivors. Aquila, Symmachus, Theodotion, Syriac, and Vulgate all have вАЬtill the heavens wear out.вАЭ Most would emend the text just slightly from вАП„Ґ÷Ј„У÷Њ„С÷Љ÷і„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО ( њad bilti, вАЬare no moreвАЭ) to вАП„Ґ÷Ј„У „С÷Љ÷∞„Ь„Х÷є„™вАО ( њad belot, вАЬuntil the wearing out of,вАЭ see Ps 102:26 [27]; Isa 51:6). Gray rejects emendation here, finding the unusual form of the MT in its favor. Orlinsky (p. 57) finds a cognate Arabic word meaning вАЬwill not awakeвАЭ and translates it вАЬso long as the heavens are not rent asunderвАЭ (H. M. Orlinsky, вАЬThe Hebrew and Greek Texts of Job 14:12, вАЭ JQR 28 [1937/38]: 57-68). He then deletes the last line of the verse as a later gloss.
n1801432: tn The verb is plural because the subject, вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish), is viewed as a collective: вАЬmankind.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto wake up; to awakeвАЭ; another root, вАП„І„Х÷Љ„•вАО (quts, вАЬto split openвАЭ) cognate to Arabic qada and Akkadian kasu, was put forward by H. M. Orlinsky (вАЬThe Hebrew and Greek Texts of Job 14:12, вАЭ JQR 28 [1937-38]: 57-68) and G. R. Driver (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 72-93).
n1801433:
tn The optative mood is introduced here again with вАП„Ю÷і„Щ „Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (mi yitten), literally, вАЬwho will give?вАЭ
sn After arguing that man will die without hope, Job expresses his desire that there be a resurrection, and what that would mean. The ancients all knew that death did not bring existence to an end; rather, they passed into another place, but they continued to exist. Job thinks that death would at least give him some respite from the wrath of God, but this wrath would eventually be appeased, and then God would remember the one he had hidden in Sheol just as he remembered Noah. Once that happened, it would be possible that Job might live again.
n1801434: sn Sheol in the Bible refers to the place where the dead go. But it can have different categories of meaning: death in general, the grave, or the realm of the departed spirits [hell]. A. Heidel shows that in the Bible when hell is in view the righteous are not thereвАФit is the realm of the departed spirits of the wicked. When the righteous go to Sheol, the meaning is usually the grave or death. See chapter 3 in A. Heidel, The Gilgamesh Epic and the Old Testament Parallels.
n1801435: tn The construction used here is the preposition followed by the infinitive construct followed by the subjective genitive, forming an adverbial clause of time.
n1801436: tn This is the same word used in v. 5 for вАЬlimit.вАЭ
n1801437: tn The verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar) means more than simply вАЬto remember.вАЭ In many cases, including this one, it means вАЬto act on what is remembered,вАЭ i.e., deliver or rescue (see Gen 8:1, вАЬand God remembered NoahвАЭ). In this sense, a prayer вАЬremember meвАЭ is a prayer for God to act upon his covenant promises.
n1801438: tc The LXX removes the interrogative and makes the statement affirmative, i.e., that man will live again. This reading is taken by D. H. Gard (вАЬThe Concept of the Future Life according to the Greek Translator of the Book of Job,вАЭ JBL 73 [1954]: 137-38). D. J. A. Clines follows this, putting both of the expressions in the wish clause: вАЬif a man dies and could live againвА¶вАЭ (Job [WBC], 332). If that is the way it is translated, then the verbs in the second half of the verse and in the next verse would all be part of the apodosis, and should be translated вАЬwould.вАЭ The interpretation would not greatly differ; it would be saying that if there was life after death, Job would long for his releaseвАФhis death. If the traditional view is taken and the question was raised whether there was life after death (the implication of the question being that there is), then Job would still be longing for his death. The point the line is making is that if there is life after death, that would be all the more reason for Job to eagerly expect, to hope for, his death.
n1801440: tn The verb вАП„Р÷≤„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њayakhel) may be rendered вАЬI will/would waitвАЭ or вАЬI will/would hope.вАЭ The word describes eager expectation and longing hope.
n1801441: tn The construction is the same as that found in the last verse: a temporal preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО ( њad) followed by the infinitive construct followed by the subjective genitive вАЬrelease/relief.вАЭ Due, in part, to the same verb (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО, khalaf) having the meaning вАЬsprout againвАЭ in v. 7, some take вАЬrenewalвАЭ as the meaning here (J. E. Hartley, Alden, NIV, ESV).
n1801442: sn The idea would be that God would sometime in the future call Job into his fellowship again when he longed for the work of his hands (cf. Job 10:3).
n1801443: tn The independent personal pronoun is emphatic, as if to say, вАЬand I on my part will answer.вАЭ
n1801444: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО (kasaf) originally meant вАЬto turn pale.вАЭ It expresses the sentiment that causes pallor of face, and so is used for desire ardently, covet. The object of the desire is always introduced with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition (see E. Dhorme, Job, 202).
n1801445: tn Heb вАЬlong for the work of your hands.вАЭ
n1801446: sn The hope for life after death is supported now by a description of the severity with which God deals with people in this life.
n1801447: tn If v. 16a continues the previous series, the translation here would be вАЬthenвАЭ (as in RSV). Others take it as a new beginning to express GodвАЩs present watch over Job, and interpret the second half of the verse as a question, or emend it to say God does not pass over his sins.
n1801448: sn Cf. Ps 130:3-4, which says, вАЬIf you should mark iniquity O Lord, Lord, who could stand? But with you there is forgiveness, in order that you might be feared.вАЭ
n1801449: tn The second colon of the verse can be contrasted with the first, the first being the present reality and the second the hope looked for in the future. This seems to fit the context well without making any changes at all.
n1801450: tn The passive participle вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷ї„ЭвАО (khatum), from вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„ЭвАО (khatam, вАЬsealвАЭ), which is used frequently in the Bible, means вАЬsealed up.вАЭ The image of sealing sins in a bag is another of the many poetic ways of expressing the removal of sin from the individual (see 1 Sam 25:29). Since the term most frequently describes sealed documents, the idea here may be more that of sealing in a bag the record of JobвАЩs sins (see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 334).
n1801451: tn The idea has been presented that the background of putting tally stones in a bag is intended (see A. L. Oppenheim, вАЬOn an Operational Device in Mesopotamian Bureaucracy,вАЭ JNES 18 [1959]: 121-28).
n1801452: tn This verb was used in Job 13:4 for вАЬplasterers of lies.вАЭ The idea is probably that God coats or paints over the sins so that they are forgotten (see Isa 1:18). A. B. Davidson (Job, 105) suggests that the sins are preserved until full punishment is exacted. But the verse still seems to be continuing the thought of how the sins would be forgotten in the next life.
n1801453: tn The indication that this is a simile is to be obtained from the conjunction beginning 19c (see GKC 499 ¬І161.a).
n1801454: tn The word вАП„Щ÷і„С÷Љ„Х÷є„ЬвАО (yibbol) usually refers to a flower fading and so seems strange here. The LXX and the Syriac translate вАЬand will fallвАЭ; most commentators accept this and repoint the preceding word to get вАЬand will surely fall.вАЭ Duhm retains the MT and applies the image of the flower to the falling mountain. The verb is used of the earth in Isa 24:4, and so NIV, RSV, and NJPS all have the idea of вАЬcrumble away.вАЭ
n1801455: tn Heb вАЬthe overflowings of itвАЭ; the word вАП„°÷∞„§÷і„Щ„Ч÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (sefikheha) in the text is changed by just about everyone. The idea of вАЬits overflowingsвАЭ or more properly вАЬits aftergrowthsвАЭ (Lev 25:5; 2 Kgs 19:29; etc.) does not fit here at all. Budde suggested reading вАП„°÷∞„Ч÷і„§÷Є„ФвАО (sekhifah), which is cognate to Arabic sahifeh, вАЬtorrential rain, rainstormвАЭвАФthat which sweeps away the soil. The word вАП„°÷Є„Ч÷Ј„£вАО (sakhaf) in Hebrew might have a wider usage than the effects of rain.
n1801457: sn The meaning for Job is that death shatters all of manвАЩs hopes for the continuation of life.
n1801458: tn D. W. Thomas took вАП„†÷µ„¶÷Ј„ЧвАО (netsakh) here to have a superlative meaning: вАЬYou prevail utterly against himвАЭ (вАЬUse of netsach as a superlative in Hebrew,вАЭ JSS 1 [1956]: 107). Death would be GodвАЩs complete victory over him.
n1801459: tn The subject of the participle is most likely God in this context. Some take it to be man, saying вАЬhis face changes.вАЭ Others emend the text to read an imperfect verb, but this is not necessary.
n1801460: tn The clause may be interpreted as a conditional clause, with the second clause beginning with the conjunction serving as the apodosis.
n1801461: tn There is no expressed subject for the verb вАЬthey honor,вАЭ and so it may be taken as a passive.
n1801462: sn Death is separation from the living, from the land of the living. And ignorance of what goes on in this life, good or bad, is part of death. See also Eccl 9:5-6, which makes a similar point.
n1801463: tn The verb is вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin, вАЬto perceive; to discernвАЭ). The parallelism between вАЬknowвАЭ and вАЬperceiveвАЭ stresses the point that in death a man does not realize what is happening here in the present life.
n1801464: tn The prepositional phrases using вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО ( њalayv, вАЬfor him[self]вАЭ) express the object of the suffering.
