It is for himself that the dead man вАЬgrieves.вАЭ So this has to be joined with вАП„Р÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њakh), yielding вАЬonly for himself.вАЭ Then, вАЬfleshвАЭ and вАЬsoul/personвАЭ form the parallelism for the subjects of the verbs.
n1801465: sn In this verse Job is expressing the common view of life beyond death, namely, that in Sheol there is no contact with the living, only separation, but in Sheol there is a conscious awareness of the dreary existence.
n180151: sn In the first round of speeches, Eliphaz had emphasized the moral perfection of God, Bildad his unwavering justice, and Zophar his omniscience. Since this did not bring the expected response from Job, the friends see him as a menace to true religion, and so they intensify their approach. Eliphaz, as dignified as ever, rebukes Job for his arrogance and warns about the judgment the wicked bring on themselves. The speech of Eliphaz falls into three parts: the rebuke of Job for his irreverence (2-6); the analysis of JobвАЩs presumption about wisdom (7-16), and his warning about the fate of the wicked (17-35).
n180152: tn The Hebrew is вАП„У÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™÷Њ„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (da њat ruakh). This means knowledge without any content, vain knowledge.
n180153: tn The image is rather graphic. It is saying that he puffs himself up with the wind and then brings out of his mouth blasts of this wind.
n180154: tn The word for вАЬeast wind,вАЭ вАП„І÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (qadim), is parallel to вАЬspirit/windвАЭ also in Hos 12:2. The east wind is maleficent, but here in the parallelism it is so much hot air.
n180155:
tn The infinitive absolute in this place is functioning either as an explanatory adverb or as a finite verb.
sn Eliphaz draws on JobвАЩs claim with this word (cf. Job 13:3), but will declare it hollow.
n180156: tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЯвАО (sakhan) means вАЬto be useful, profitable.вАЭ It is found 5 times in the book with this meaning. The Hiphil of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (ya њal) has the same connotation. E. Lipi≈Дski offers a new meaning on a second root, вАЬincur dangerвАЭ or вАЬrun risksвАЭ with words, but this does not fit the parallelism (FO 21 [1980]: 65-82).
n180157: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (parar) in the Hiphil means вАЬto annul; to frustrate; to destroy; to break,вАЭ and this fits the line quite well. The NEB reflects G. R. DriverвАЩs suggestion of an Arabic cognate meaning вАЬto expel; to banishвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 77).
n180159: tn The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (gara њ) means вАЬto diminish,вАЭ regard as insignificant, occasionally with the sense of вАЬpull downвАЭ (Deut 4:2; 13:1). It is here that Eliphaz is portraying Job as a menace to the religion of society because he dissuades people from seeking God.
n1801510: tn The word вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (sikhah) is вАЬcomplaint; cry; meditation.вАЭ Job would be influencing people to challenge God and not to meditate before or pray to him.
n1801511: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷µ„£вАО ( Њallef) has the meaning of вАЬto teach; to instruct,вАЭ but it is unlikely that the idea of revealing is intended. If the verb is understood metonymically, then вАЬto inspire; to promptвАЭ will be sufficient. Dahood and others find another root, and render the verb вАЬto increase,вАЭ reversing subject and object: вАЬyour mouth increases your iniquity.вАЭ
n1801513: tn The word means вАЬshrewd; crafty; cunningвАЭ (see Gen 3:1). Job uses clever speech that is misleading and destructive.
n1801514: tn The Hiphil of this root means вАЬdeclare wicked, guiltyвАЭ (a declarative Hiphil), and so вАЬcondemns.вАЭ
n1801515: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( њanah) with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition following it means вАЬto testify against.вАЭ For Eliphaz, it is enough to listen to Job to condemn him.
n1801516: tn The meaning of вАП„°„Х÷є„УвАО (sod) is вАЬconfidence.вАЭ In the context the implication is вАЬsecret counselвАЭ of the Lord God (see Jer 23:18). It is a question of confidence on the part of God, that only wisdom can know (see Prov 8:30, 31). Job seemed to them to claim to have access to the mind of God.
n1801517: tn In v. 4 the word meant вАЬlimitвАЭ; here it has a slightly different sense, namely, вАЬto reserve for oneself.вАЭ
n1801518: tn The last clause simply has вАЬand it is not with us.вАЭ It means that one possesses something through knowledge. Note the parallelism of вАЬknowвАЭ and вАЬwith meвАЭ in Ps 50:11.
n1801519: tn The participle вАП„©„В÷Є„СвАО (sav), from вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„СвАО (siv, вАЬto have white hairвАЭ; 1 Sam 12:2), only occurs elsewhere in the Bible in the Aramaic sections of Ezra. The word вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (yashish, вАЬagedвАЭ) occurred in 12:12.
n1801521: tn The line reads: вАЬ[men] greater than your father [in] days.вАЭ The expression вАЬin daysвАЭ underscores their ageвАФthey were older than JobвАЩs father, and therefore wiser.
n1801522: sn The words of comfort and consolation that they have been offering to Job are here said to be from God, but Job will call them miserable comforters (16:2).
n1801523: tn The formula вАЬis it too little for youвАЭ or вАЬis it too slight a matter for youвАЭ is also found in Isa 7:13 (see GKC 430 ¬І133.c).
n1801524: tn The word вАЬspokenвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation.
n1801525: tn The interrogative вАП„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mah) here has the sense of вАЬwhy?вАЭ (see Job 7:21).
n1801526: tn The verb simply means вАЬto take.вАЭ The RSV has вАЬcarry you away.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 212-13) goes further, saying that it implies being unhinged by passion, to be carried away by the passions beyond good sense (pp. 212-13). Pope and Tur-Sinai suggest that the suffix on the verb is datival, and translate it, вАЬWhat has taken from you your mind?вАЭ But the parallelism shows that вАЬyour heartвАЭ and вАЬyour eyesвАЭ are subjects.
n1801527: tn Here is another word that occurs only here, and in the absence of a completely convincing suggestion, probably should be left as it is. The verb is вАП„®÷Є„Ц÷Ј„ЭвАО (razam, вАЬwink, flashвАЭ). Targum Job and the Syriac equate it with a verb found in Aramaic and postbiblical Hebrew with the same letters but metathesizedвАФвАП„®÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЦвАО (ramaz). It would mean вАЬto make a signвАЭ or вАЬto wink.вАЭ Budde, following the LXX probably, has вАЬWhy are your eyes lofty?вАЭ Others follow an Arabic root meaning вАЬbecome weak.вАЭ
n1801528: tn The Hebrew is вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (rukhekha, вАЬyour spiritвАЭ or вАЬyour breathвАЭ). But the fact that this is turned вАЬagainst God,вАЭ means that it must be given a derived meaning, or a meaning that is metonymical. It is used in the Bible in the sense of angerвАФwhat the spirit vents (see Judg 8:3; Prov 16:32; and Job 4:9 with вАЬblastвАЭ).
n1801529: tn The verb is a Hiphil perfect of yasaвАЩ, вАЬto go out, proceed, issue forth.вАЭ
n1801531: tn Eliphaz here reiterates the point made in Job 4:18.
n1801532: sn The question here is whether the reference is to material вАЬheavensвАЭ (as in Exod 24:10 and Job 25:5), or to heavenly beings. The latter seems preferable in this context.
n1801533: tn The two descriptions here used are вАЬabominable,вАЭ meaning вАЬdisgustingвАЭ (a Niphal participle with the value of a Latin participle [see GKC 356-57 ¬І116.e]), and вАЬcorruptвАЭ (a Niphal participle which occurs only in Pss 14:3 and 53:4), always in a moral sense. On the significance of the first description, see P. Humbert, вАЬLe substantif to ЊƒУbƒБ et le verbe t Њb dans lвАЩAncien Testament,вАЭ ZAW 72 [1960]: 217-37). On the second word, G. R. Driver suggests from Arabic, вАЬdebauched with luxury, corruptвАЭ (вАЬSome Hebrew Words,вАЭ JTS 29 [1927/28]: 390-96).
n1801534: sn Man commits evil with the same ease and facility as he drinks in waterвАФfreely and in large quantities.
n1801535: tn The demonstrative pronoun is used here as a nominative, to introduce an independent relative clause (see GKC 447 ¬І138.h).
n1801536: tn Here the vav (вАП„ХвАО) apodosis follows with the cohortative (see GKC 458 ¬І143.d).
n1801537: tn The word вАЬtraditionвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation.
n1801538: tn Heb вАЬtheir fathers.вАЭ Some commentators change one letter and follow the reading of the LXX: вАЬand their fathers have not hidden.вАЭ Pope tries to get the same reading by classifying the вАП„ЭвАО (mem) as an enclitic mem. The MT on first glance would read вАЬand did not hide from their fathers.вАЭ Some take the clause вАЬand they did not hideвАЭ as adverbial and belonging to the first part of the verse: вАЬwhat wise men declare, hiding nothing, according to the tradition of their fathers.вАЭ
n1801539: sn Eliphaz probably thinks that Edom was the proverbial home of wisdom, and so the reference here would be to his own people. If, as many interpret, the biblical writer is using these accounts to put Yahwistic ideas into the discussion, then the reference would be to Canaan at the time of the fathers. At any rate, the tradition of wisdom to Eliphaz has not been polluted by foreigners, but has retained its pure and moral nature from antiquity.
n1801540: tn Heb вАЬall the days of the wicked, he suffers.вАЭ The word вАЬallвАЭ is an adverbial accusative of time, stating along with its genitives (вАЬof the days of a wicked manвАЭ) how long the individual suffers. When the subject is composed of a noun in construct followed by a genitive, the predicate sometimes agrees with the genitive (see GKC 467 ¬І146.a).
n1801541: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО (mitkholel) is a Hitpolel participle from вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (khil, вАЬto trembleвАЭ). It carries the idea of вАЬtorment oneself,вАЭ or вАЬbe tormented.вАЭ Some have changed the letter вАП„ЧвАО (khet) for a letter вАП„ФвАО (he), and obtained the meaning вАЬshows himself mad.вАЭ Theodotion has вАЬis mad.вАЭ Syriac (вАЬbehave arrogantly,вАЭ apparently confusing Hebrew вАП„Ч„Х„ЬвАО with вАП„Ч„Ь„ЬвАО; Heidi M. Szpek, Translation Technique in the Peshitta to Job [SBLDS], 277), Symmachus, and Vulgate have вАЬboasts himself.вАЭ But the reading of the MT is preferable.
n1801542: tn It is necessary, with Rashi, to understand the relative pronoun before the verb вАЬthey are stored up/reserved.вАЭ
n1801543: tn This has been translated with the idea of вАЬoppressorвАЭ in Job 6:23; 27:13.
n1801544: tn The word вАЬfillвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation.
n1801545: tn The word вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„У÷µ„УвАО (shoded) means вАЬa robber; a plundererвАЭ (see Job 12:6). With the verb вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ ) the sentence means that the robber pounces on or comes against him (see GKC 373 ¬І118.f). H. H. Rowley observes that the text does not say that he is under attack, but that the sound of fears is in his ears, i.e., that he is terrified by thoughts of this.
n1801546:
tn This is the meaning of the Hiphil imperfect negated: вАЬhe does not believeвАЭ or вАЬhe has no confidence.вАЭ It is followed by the infinitive construct functioning as the direct objectвАФhe does not expect to return (to escape) from darkness.
sn The meaning of this line is somewhat in question. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 111) thinks it could mean that he is afraid he will not wake up from the night, or he dreads misfortune, thinking it will be final for him.
n1801547: sn In the context of these arguments, вАЬdarknessвАЭ probably refers to calamity, and so the wicked can expect a calamity that is final.
n1801548: tn Heb вАЬhe is watched [or waited for] by the sword.вАЭ G. R. Driver reads it, вАЬhe is marked down for the swordвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 78). Ewald suggested вАЬlaid up for the sword.вАЭ Ball has вАЬlooks for the sword.вАЭ The MT has a passive participle from вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (tsafah, вАЬto observe, watchвАЭ) which can be retained in the text; the meaning of the form can then be understood as the result of the inspection (E. Dhorme, Job, 217).
n1801549: tn The MT has вАЬhe wanders about for foodвАФwhere is it?вАЭ The LXX has вАЬhe has been appointed for food for vultures,вАЭ reading вАП„Р÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО ( Њayyah, вАЬvultureвАЭ) for вАП„Р÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО ( Њayyeh, вАЬwhere is it?вАЭ). This would carry on the thought of the passageвАФhe sees himself destined for the sword and food for vultures. Many commentators follow this reading while making a number of smaller changes in вАП„†÷є„У÷µ„УвАО (noded, вАЬwanderingвАЭ) such as вАП„†÷і„™÷Љ÷Ј„ЯвАО (nittan, вАЬis givenвАЭ), вАП„†„Х÷є„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (no њad, вАЬis appointedвАЭ), вАП„†„Х÷є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (noda њ, вАЬis knownвАЭ), or something similar. The last involves no major change in consonants. While the MT вАЬwanderingвАЭ may not be as elegant as some of the other suggestions, it is not impossible. But there is no reading of this verse that does not involve some change. The LXX has вАЬand he has been appointed for food for vultures.вАЭ
n1801550: tn This line is fraught with difficulties (perceived or real), which prompt numerous suggestions. The reading of the MT is вАЬhe knows that a day of darkness is fixed in his hand,вАЭ i.e., is certain. Many commentators move вАЬday of darknessвАЭ to the next verse, following the LXX. Then, suggestions have been offered for вАП„†÷Є„Ы„Х÷є„ЯвАО (nakhon, вАЬreadyвАЭ), such as вАП„†÷µ„Ы÷ґ„®вАО (nekher, вАЬdisasterвАЭ); and for вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Щ÷Є„У„Х÷євАО (beyado, вАЬin his handвАЭ) a number of ideasвАФвАП„Ь÷∞„Р÷µ„Щ„УвАО (le Њed, вАЬcalamityвАЭ) or вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„У„Х÷євАО (pido, вАЬhis disasterвАЭ). Wright takes this last view and renders it вАЬhe knows that misfortune is imminent,вАЭ leaving the вАЬday of darknessвАЭ to the next verse.
n1801551: tn If вАЬday and darknessвАЭ are added to this line, then this verse is made into a tri-colonвАФthe main reason for transferring it away from the last verse. But the newly proposed reading follows the LXX structure precisely, as if that were the approved construction. The Hebrew of MT has вАЬdistress and anguish terrify him.вАЭ
n1801552: tn This last colon is deleted by some, moved to v. 26 by others, and the NEB puts it in brackets. The last word (translated here as вАЬlaunch an attackвАЭ) occurs only here. HALOT 472 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„У„Х÷є„®вАО links it to an Arabic root kadara, вАЬto rush down,вАЭ as with a bird of prey. J. Reider defines it as вАЬperturbationвАЭ from the same root (вАЬEtymological Studies in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ VT 2 [1952]: 127).
n1801553: sn The symbol of the outstretched hand is the picture of attempting to strike someone, or shaking a fist at someone; it is a symbol of a challenge or threat (see Isa 5:25; 9:21; 10:4).
n1801554: tn The Hitpael of вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО (gavar) means вАЬto act with mightвАЭ or вАЬto behave like a hero.вАЭ The idea is that the wicked boldly vaunts himself before the Lord.
n1801555: tn Heb вАЬhe runs against [or upon] him with the neck.вАЭ The RSV takes this to mean вАЬwith a stiff neck.вАЭ Several commentators, influenced by the LXXвАЩs вАЬinsolently,вАЭ have attempted to harmonize with some idiom for neck (вАЬoutstretched neck,вАЭ for example). Others have made more extensive changes. Pope and Anderson follow Tur-Sinai in accepting вАЬwith full battle armor.вАЭ But the main idea seems to be that of a headlong assault on God.
n1801556: tn Heb вАЬwith the thickness of the bosses of his shield.вАЭ The bosses are the convex sides of the bucklers, turned against the foe. This is a defiant attack on God.
n1801557: sn This verse tells us that he is not in any condition to fight, because he is bloated and fat from luxurious living.
n1801558: tn D. W. Thomas defends a meaning вАЬcoverвАЭ for the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„В÷Є„ФвАО ( њasah). See вАЬTranslating Hebrew  њasah,вАЭ BT 17 [1966]: 190-93.
n1801559: tn The term вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (pimah), a hapax legomenon, is explained by the Arabic fa Њima, вАЬto be fat.вАЭ Pope renders this вАЬblubber.вАЭ Cf. KJV вАЬand maketh collops of fat on his flanks.вАЭ
n1801560: sn K&D 11:266 rightly explains that these are not cities that he, the wicked, has destroyed, but that were destroyed by a judgment on wickedness. Accordingly, Eliphaz is saying that the wicked man is willing to risk such a curse in his confidence in his prosperity (see further H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 113).
n1801561: tn The verbal idea serves here to modify вАЬhousesвАЭ as a relative clause; so a relative pronoun is added.
n1801562: tn The Hebrew has simply вАЬthey are made ready for heaps.вАЭ The LXX translates it, вАЬwhat they have prepared, let others carry away.вАЭ This would involve a complete change of the last word.
n1801563: tn This word вАП„Ю÷і„†÷∞„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО (minlam) also is a hapax legomenon, although almost always interpreted to mean вАЬpossessionвАЭ (with Arabic manal) and repointed as вАП„Ю÷∞„†÷є„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО (menolam). M. Dahood further changes вАЬearthвАЭ to the netherworld, and interprets it to mean вАЬhis possessions will not go down to the netherworldвАЭ (вАЬValue of Ugaritic for Textual Criticism,вАЭ Bib 40 [1959]: 164-66). Others suggest it means вАЬear of grain,вАЭ either from the common word for вАЬears of grainвАЭ or a hapax legomenon in Deut 23:26 HT (23:25 ET).
n1801564: tn Some editions and commentators delete the first line of this verse, arguing that it is simply a paraphrase of v. 22a, and that it interrupts the comparison with a tree that falls (although that comparison only starts next).
n1801565: tn This last line in the verse is the difficult one. The MT has вАЬhe shall depart by the breath of his mouth.вАЭ If this reading stands, then it must be understood that it is the breath of GodвАЩs mouth that is intended. In place of вАЬhis mouthвАЭ the LXX has вАЬflowerвАЭ (reading вАП„§÷Љ÷і„®„Ф„Х÷євАО [pirho, properly, вАЬhis fruitвАЭ] instead of вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ХвАО piv), and вАЬfallвАЭ instead of вАЬdepart.вАЭ Modern commentators and a number of English versions (e.g., RSV, NRSV, TEV) alter вАП„Щ÷Є„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО (yasur, вАЬdepartвАЭ) to something like вАП„Щ÷∞„°÷є„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (yeso њar, from вАП„°÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО [sa њar, вАЬto drive awayвАЭ]), or the like, to get вАЬwill be swept away.вАЭ The result is a reading: вАЬand his blossom will be swept away by the wind.вАЭ The LXX may have read the Hebrew exactly, but harmonized it with v. 33 (see H. Heater, A Septuagint Translation Technique in the Book of Job [CBQMS]: 61-62).
n1801566: tn The word, although difficult in its form, is вАЬvanity,вАЭ i.e., that which is worthless. E. Dhorme (Job, 224) thinks that the form вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„РвАО (shav Њ) conceals the word вАП„©„Б÷і„Щ„Р„Х÷євАО (shi Њo, вАЬhis statureвАЭ). But Dhorme reworks most of the verse. He changes вАП„†÷і„™÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (nit њah, вАЬdeceivedвАЭ) to вАП„†÷µ„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (neda њ, вАЬwe knowвАЭ) to arrive at вАЬwe know that it is vanity.вАЭ The last two words of the verse are then moved to the next. The LXX has вАЬlet him not think that he shall endure, for his end shall be vanity.вАЭ
n1801567: tn This word is found in Job 20:18 with the sense of вАЬtrading.вАЭ It can mean the exchange of goods or the profit from them. Some commentators change вАП„™÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„®÷Є„™„Х÷євАО (temurato, вАЬhis rewardвАЭ) because they wish to put it with the next verse as the LXX seems to have done (although the LXX does not represent this). Suggestions include вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Ю÷є„®÷Є„™„Х÷євАО (timorato, вАЬhis palm treeвАЭ) and вАП„Ц÷∞„Ю÷є„®÷Є„™„Х÷євАО (zemorato, вАЬhis vine shootвАЭ). A number of writers simply delete all of v. 31. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 115) suggests the best reading (assuming one were going to make changes) would be, вАЬLet him not trust in his stature, deceiving himself, for it is vanity,вАЭ putting вАЬhis palm treeвАЭ with the next verse.
n1801569: tn Those who put the last colon of v. 31 with v. 32 also have to change the verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷µ„РвАО (timmale Њ, вАЬwill be fulfilledвАЭ). E. Dhorme (Job, 225) says, вАЬa mere glance at the use of yimmalвА¶abundantly proves that the original text had timmal (G, Syr., Vulg), which became timmale Њ through the accidental transposition of the  Њalep of besi ЊoвА¶in verse 31вА¶.вАЭ This, of course, is possible, if all the other changes up to now are granted. But the meaning of a word elsewhere in no way assures it should be the word here. The LXX has вАЬhis harvest shall perish before the time,вАЭ which could translate any number of words that might have been in the underlying Hebrew text. A commercial metaphor is not out of place here, since parallelism does not demand that the same metaphor appear in both lines.
n1801570: tn Now, in the second half of the verse, the metaphor of a tree with branches begins.
n1801571: tn The verb means вАЬto treat violentlyвАЭ or вАЬto wrong.вАЭ It indicates that the vine did not nourish the grapes well enough for them to grow, and so they dry up and drop off.
n1801572: sn The point is that like the tree the wicked man shows signs of life but produces nothing valuable. The olive tree will have blossoms in the years that it produces no olives, and so eventually drops the blossoms.
n1801573: tn The LXX renders this line: вАЬfor death is the witness of an ungodly man. вАЬDeathвАЭ represents вАЬbarren/sterile,вАЭ and вАЬwitnessвАЭ represents вАЬassembly.вАЭ
n1801574: sn This may refer to the fire that struck Job (cf. 1:16).
n1801575: tn Heb вАЬthe tents of bribery.вАЭ The word вАЬbriberyвАЭ can mean a вАЬgift,вАЭ but most often in the sense of a bribe in court. It indicates that the wealth and the possessions that the wicked man has gained may have been gained unjustly.
n1801576: tn Infinitives absolute are used in this verse in the place of finite verbs. They lend a greater vividness to the description, stressing the basic meaning of the words.
n1801577: tn At the start of the speech Eliphaz said JobвАЩs belly was filled with the wind; now it is there that he prepares deception. This inclusio frames the speech.
n180161: sn In the next two chapters we have JobвАЩs second reply to Eliphaz. Job now feels abandoned by God and by his friends, and so complains that this all intensifies his sufferings. But he still holds to his innocence as he continues his appeal to God as his witness. There are four sections to this speech: in vv. 2-5 he dismisses the consolation his friends offered; in vv. 6-17 he laments that he is abandoned by God and man; in 16:8-17:9 he makes his appeal to God in heaven as a witness; and finally, in 10-16 he anticipates death.
n180162: tn The expression uses the Piel participle in construct: вАП„Ю÷∞„†÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ю÷µ„Щ „Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО (menahame  њamal, вАЬcomforters of troubleвАЭ), i.e., comforters who increase trouble instead of relieving it. D. W. Thomas translates this вАЬbreathers out of troubleвАЭ (вАЬA Note on the Hebrew Root naham,вАЭ ExpTim 44 [1932/33]: 192).
n180163: tn Disjunctive questions are introduced with the sign of the interrogative; the second part is introduced with вАП„Р„Х÷євАО ( Њo, see GKC 475 ¬І150.g).
n180164: tn In v. 3 the second person singular is employed rather than the plural as in vv. 2 and 4. The singular might be an indication that the words of v. 3 were directed at Eliphaz specifically.
n180166: tn The Hiphil of вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (marats) does not occur anywhere else. The word means вАЬto compel; to forceвАЭ (see 6:25).
n180167: tn The LXX seems to have gone a different way: вАЬWhat, is there any reason in vain words, or what will hinder you from answering?вАЭ
n180168: tn For the use of the cohortative in the apodosis of conditional sentences, see GKC 322 ¬І109.f.
n180169: tn The conjunction вАП„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (lu) is used to introduce the optative, a condition that is incapable of fulfillment (see GKC 494-95 ¬І159.l).
n1801610: tn This verb вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷∞„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„®÷Є„ФвАО ( Њakhbirah) is usually connected to вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО (khavar, вАЬto bindвАЭ). There are several suggestions for this word. J. J. Finkelstein proposed a second root, a homonym, meaning вАЬto make a sound,вАЭ and so here вАЬto harangueвАЭ (вАЬHebrew habar and Semitic HBR,вАЭ JBL 75 [1956]: 328-31; see also O. Loretz, вАЬHBR in Job 16:4, вАЭ CBQ 23 [1961]: 293-94, who renders it вАЬI could make noisy speechesвАЭ). Other suggestions have been for new meanings based on cognate studies, such as вАЬto make beautifulвАЭ (i.e., make polished speeches).
n1801611: sn The action is a sign of mockery (see Ps 22:7 [8]; Isa 37:22; Matt 27:39).
n1801612: tn вАЬButвАЭ has been added in the translation to strengthen the contrast.
n1801613: tn The Piel of вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„•вАО ( Њamats) means вАЬto strengthen, fortify.вАЭ
n1801615: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„©„В÷є„Ъ÷∞вАО (yakhsokh) means вАЬto restrain; to withhold.вАЭ There is no object, so many make it first person subject, вАЬI will not restrain.вАЭ The LXX and the Syriac have a different personвАФвАЬI would not restrain.вАЭ G. R. Driver, arguing that the verb is intransitive here, made it вАЬthe solace of my lips would not [added] be withheldвАЭ (see JTS 34 [1933]: 380). D. J. A. Clines says that what is definitive is the use of the verb in the next line, where it clearly means вАЬsoothed, assuaged.вАЭ
n1801616: tn вАЬButвАЭ is supplied in the translation to strengthen the contrast.
n1801617: tn The Niphal вАП„Щ÷µ„Ч÷Є„©„В÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (yekhasekh) means вАЬto be soothed; to be assuaged.вАЭ
n1801618: tn Some argue that вАП„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mah) in the text is the Arabic ma, the simple negative. This would then mean вАЬit does not depart far from me.вАЭ The interrogative used rhetorically amounts to the same thing, however, so the suggestion is not necessary.
n1801619: tn In poetic discourse there is often an abrupt change from one person to another. See GKC 462 ¬І144.p. Some take the subject of this verb to be God, others the pain (вАЬsurely now it has worn me outвАЭ).
n1801620: tn The verb is вАП„І÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ШвАО (qamat) which is used only here and in 22:16; it means вАЬto seize; to grasp.вАЭ By GodвАЩs seizing him, Job means his afflictions.
n1801622: tn The verb is used in Ps 109:24 to mean вАЬto be leanвАЭ; and so вАЬleannessвАЭ is accepted here for the noun by most. Otherwise the word is вАЬlie, deceit.вАЭ Accordingly, some take it here as вАЬmy slandererвАЭ or вАЬmy liarвАЭ (gives evidence against me).
n1801623: tn The referent of these pronouns in v. 9 (вАЬhis angerвА¶he has gnashedвА¶his teethвА¶his eyesвАЭ) is best taken as God.
n1801624: sn The figure used now is that of a wild beast. GodвАЩs affliction of Job is compared to the attack of such an animal. Cf. Amos 1:11.
n1801625: tn The verb вАП„©„В÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЭвАО (satam) is translated вАЬhateвАЭ in the RSV, but this is not accepted by very many. Many emend it to вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю„ШвАО (shamat), reading вАЬand he dropped meвАЭ (from his mouth). But that suggests escape. D. J. A. Clines notes that usage shows it reflects ongoing hatred represented by an action such as persecution or attack (Job [WBC], 370).
n1801626: tn The verb is used of sharpening a sword in Ps 7:12; here it means вАЬto look intentlyвАЭ as an animal looks for prey. The verse describes GodвАЩs relentless pursuit of Job.
n1801627: tn вАЬPeopleвАЭ is supplied; the Hebrew verb is third plural. The colon reads, вАЬthey have opened against me with [the preposition is instrumental] their mouth.вАЭ The gestures here follow the animal imagery; they reflect destructive opposition and attack (see Ps 22:13 among others).
n1801628: tn This is an вАЬinsultвАЭ or a вАЬreproach.вАЭ
n1801629: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Є„Р„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (yitmalla Њun) is taken from вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷µ„РвАО (male Њ), вАЬto be full,вАЭ and in this stem, вАЬto pile up; to press together.вАЭ The term has a military connotation, such as вАЬto mobilizeвАЭ (see D. W. Thomas, вАЬml Њw in Jeremiah 4:5 : a military term,вАЭ JJS 3 [1952]: 47-52). Job sees himself surrounded by enemies who persecute him and mock him.
n1801630:
tn The word вАП„Ґ÷≤„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( њavil) means вАЬchild,вАЭ and this cannot be right here. If it is read as вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„ЬвАО ( њavval) as in Job 27:7 it would be the unrighteous.
sn Job does not refer here to his friends, but more likely to the wicked men who set about to destroy him and his possessions, or to the rabble in ch. 30.
n1801631: tn The word вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Ш÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (yirteni) does not derive from the root вАП„®÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (ratah) as would fit the pointing in the MT, but from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„ШвАО (yarat), cognate to Arabic warrata, вАЬto throw; to hurl.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 236) thinks that since the normal form would have been вАП„Щ÷і„Щ„®÷∞„Ш÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (yireteni), it is probable that one of the yods (вАП„ЩвАО) would have affected the earlier word вАП„Ґ÷≤„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( њavil)вАФbut that seems out of place.
n1801632: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (parar) means вАЬto shake.вАЭ In the Hiphil it means вАЬto break; to shatterвАЭ (5:12; 15:4). The Pilpel means вАЬto break in pieces,вАЭ and in the Poel in Jer 23:29 вАЬto smash up.вАЭ So Job was living at ease, and God shattered his life.
n1801633: tn Here is another Pilpel, now from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„¶÷Ј„•вАО (patsats) with a similar meaning to the other verb. It means вАЬto dash into piecesвАЭ and even scatter the pieces. The LXX translates this line, вАЬhe took me by the hair of the head and plucked it out.вАЭ
n1801634: tn The meaning of вАЬhis archersвАЭ is supported for вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (rabbayv) in view of Jer 50:29. The LXX, Syriac, Vulgate, Targum Job, followed by several translations and commentators prefer вАЬarrows.вАЭ They see this as a more appropriate figure without raising the question of who the archers might be (see 6:4). The point is an unnecessary distinction, for the figure is an illustration of the affliction that God has brought on him.
n1801635: tn Heb вАЬand he does not pity,вАЭ but the clause is functioning adverbially in the line.
n1801636: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (palakh) in the Piel means вАЬto pierceвАЭ (see Prov 7:23). A fuller comparison should be made with Lam 3:12-13.
n1801637: tn This word вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷µ„®÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (mererati, вАЬmy gallвАЭ) is found only here. It is close to the form in Job 13:26, вАЬbitter things.вАЭ In Job 20:14 it may mean вАЬpoison.вАЭ The thought is also found in Lam 2:11.
n1801638: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (parats) means вАЬto make a breachвАЭ in a wall (Isa 5:5; Ps 80:13). It is used figuratively in the birth and naming of Peres in Gen 38:29. Here the image is now of a military attack that breaks through a wall. The text uses the cognate accusative, and then with the addition of вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„§÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО ( њal pene, вАЬin additionвАЭ) it repeats the cognate noun. A smooth translation that reflects the three words is difficult. E. Dhorme (Job, 237) has вАЬhe batters me down, breach upon breach.вАЭ
n1801640: sn The language is hyperbolic; Job is saying that the sackcloth he has put on in his lamentable state is now stuck to his skin as if he had stitched it into the skin. It is now a habitual garment that he never takes off.
n1801641: tn The Poel вАП„Ґ÷є„Ь÷Ј„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО ( њolalti) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њalal, вАЬto enterвАЭ) has here the meaning of вАЬto thrust in.вАЭ The activity is the opposite of вАЬraising high the horn,вАЭ a picture of dignity and victory.
n1801642: tn There is no English term that captures exactly what вАЬhornвАЭ is meant to do. Drawn from the animal world, the image was meant to convey strength and pride and victory. Some modern commentators have made other proposals for the line. Svi Rin suggested from Ugaritic that the verb be translated вАЬlowerвАЭ or вАЬdipвАЭ (вАЬUgariticвАФOld Testament Affinities,вАЭ BZ 7 [1963]: 22-33).
n1801643: tn An intensive form, a Qetaltal form of the root вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (khamar, вАЬredвАЭ) is used here. This word has as probable derivatives вАП„Ч÷є„Ю÷ґ„®вАО (khomer, вАЬ[red] clayвАЭ) and вАП„Ч÷≤„Ю„Х÷є„®вАО (khamor, вАЬ[red] assвАЭ) and the like. Because of the weeping, his whole complexion has been reddened (the LXX reads вАЬmy bellyвАЭ).
n1801644: sn A. B. Davidson (Job, 122) notes that spontaneous and repeated weeping is one of the symptoms of elephantiasis.
n1801645: sn See Job 3:5. Just as joy brings light and life to the eyes, sorrow and suffering bring darkness. The вАЬeyelidsвАЭ here would be synecdoche, reflecting the whole facial expression as sad and sullen.
n1801646: tn For the use of the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal) to introduce concessive clauses, see GKC 499 ¬І160.c.
n1801647: sn Job knows that he will die, and that his death, signified here by blood on the ground, will cry out for vindication.
n1801648: tn The word is simply вАЬa place,вАЭ but in the context it surely means a hidden place, a secret place that would never be discovered (see 18:21).
n1801649: sn The witness in heaven must be God, to whom the cries and prayers come. JobвАЩs dilemma is serious, but common to the human experience: the hostility of God toward him is baffling, but he is conscious of his innocence and can call on God to be his witness.
n1801650: tn The parallelism now uses the Aramaic word вАЬmy advocateвАЭвАФthe one who testifies on my behalf. The word again appears in Gen 31:47 for LabanвАЩs naming of the вАЬheap of witnessвАЭ in AramaicвАФвАЬSahadutha.вАЭ
n1801651: tn The first two words of this verse are problematic: вАП„Ю÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„¶÷Ј„Щ „®÷µ„Ґ÷Є„ЩвАО (melitsay re њay, вАЬmy scorners are my friendsвАЭ). The word вАП„Ю÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (melits), from or related to the word for вАЬscornerвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО, lits) in wisdom literature especially, can also mean вАЬmediatorвАЭ (Job 33:23), вАЬinterpreterвАЭ (Gen 42:23). This gives the idea that вАЬscornвАЭ has to do with the way words are used. It may be that the word here should have the singular suffix and be taken as вАЬmy spokesman.вАЭ This may not be from the same root as вАЬscornвАЭ (see N. H. Richardson, вАЬSome Notes on lis and Its Derivatives,вАЭ VT 5 [1955]: 434-36). This is the view of the NIV, NJPS, JB, NAB, as well as a number of commentators. The idea of вАЬmy friends are scornersвАЭ is out of place in this section, unless taken as a parenthesis. Other suggestions are not convincing. The LXX has вАЬMay my prayer come to the Lord, and before him may my eye shed tears.вАЭ Some have tried to change the Hebrew to fit this. The word вАЬmy friendsвАЭ also calls for some attention. Instead of a plural noun suffix, most would see it as a singular, a slight vocalic change. But others think it is not the word вАЬfriend.вАЭ D. J. A. Clines accepts the view that it is not вАЬfriendsвАЭ but вАЬthoughtsвАЭ (вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО, rea њ). E. Dhorme takes it as вАЬclamor,вАЭ from вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (rua њ) and so interprets вАЬmy claimant word has reached God.вАЭ J. B. Curtis tries вАЬMy intercessor is my shepherd,вАЭ from вАП„®÷є„Ґ÷і„ЩвАО (ro њi). See вАЬOn JobвАЩs Witness in Heaven,вАЭ JBL 102 [1983]: 549-62.
n1801652: tn The Hebrew verb means вАЬto drip; to stream; to flowвАЭ; the expression is cryptic, but understandable: вАЬmy eye flows [with tears as I cry out] to God.вАЭ But many suggestions have been made for this line too. Driver suggested in connection with cognate words that it be given the meaning вАЬsleeplessвАЭ (JTS 34 [1933]: 375-85), but this would also require additional words for a smooth reading. See also E. A. Speiser, вАЬThe Semantic Range of dalapu,вАЭ JCS 5 (1951): 64-66, for the Akkadian connection. But for the retention of вАЬdripping eyesвАЭ based on the Talmudic use, see J. C. Greenfield, вАЬLexicographical Notes I,вАЭ HUCA 29 (1958): 203-28.
n1801653: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 240) alters this slightly to read вАЬWould thatвАЭ or вАЬAh! if only.вАЭ
n1801654: tn This is the simple translation of the expression вАЬson of manвАЭ in Job. But some commentators wish to change the word вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ben, вАЬsonвАЭ) to вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„Щ„ЯвАО (ben, вАЬbetweenвАЭ). It would then be вАЬ[as] between a man and [for] his friend.вАЭ Even though a few mss have this reading, it is to be rejected. But see J. Barr, вАЬSome Notes on вАШbenвАЩ in Classical Hebrew,вАЭ JSS 23 (1978): 1-22.
n1801655: tn The verb is supplied from the parallel clause.
n1801656: tn The expression is вАЬyears of number,вАЭ meaning that they can be counted, and so вАЬthe years are few.вАЭ The verb simply means вАЬcomesвАЭ or вАЬlie ahead.вАЭ
n1801657: tn The verbal expression вАЬI will not returnвАЭ serves here to modify the journey that he will take. It is вАЬthe road [of] I will not return.вАЭ
n180171: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО (khaval, вАЬto act badlyвАЭ) in the Piel means вАЬto ruin.вАЭ The Pual translation with вАЬmy spiritвАЭ as the subject means вАЬbrokenвАЭ in the sense of finished (not in the sense of humbled as in Ps 51).
n180172: tn The verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (za њaq, equivalent of Aramaic вАП„У÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ІвАО [da њaq]) means вАЬto be extinguished.вАЭ It only occurs here in the Hebrew.
n180173: tn The plural вАЬgravesвАЭ could be simply an intensification, a plural of extension (see GKC 397 ¬І124.c), or a reference to the graveyard. Coverdale had: вАЬI am harde at deathes dore.вАЭ The Hebrew expression simply reads вАЬgraves for me.вАЭ It probably means that graves await him.
n180174: tn The noun is the abstract noun, вАЬmockery.вАЭ It indicates that he is the object of derision. But many commentators either change the word to вАЬmockersвАЭ (Tur-Sinai, NEB), or argue that the form in the text is a form of the participle (Gordis).
n180175: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 243) interprets the preposition to mean вАЬaimed at me.вАЭ
n180176: tn The meaning of вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷є„™÷Є„ЭвАО (hammerotam) is unclear, and the versions offer no help. If the MT is correct, it would probably be connected to вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (marah, вАЬto be rebelliousвАЭ) and the derived form something like вАЬhostility; provocation.вАЭ But some commentators suggest it should be related to вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷є„®„Х÷є„™вАО (marorot, вАЬbitter thingsвАЭ). Others have changed both the noun and the verb to obtain something like вАЬMy eye is weary of their contentiousnessвАЭ (Holscher), or вАЬmine eyes are wearied by your stream of peevish complaintsвАЭ (G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 78). There is no alternative suggestion that is compelling.
n180177:
tc The MT has two imperatives: вАЬSet (down), pledge me, with you.вАЭ Most commentators think that the second imperative, вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷∞„С÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО ( њareveni, вАЬpledge security for), should be repointed as a noun, вАП„Ґ÷µ„®÷∞„С÷є„†÷і„ЩвАО ( њerevoni, вАЬmy pledge of securityвАЭ) and take it to say, вАЬSet my pledge beside you.вАЭ A. B. Davidson (Job, 126) suggests that the first verb means вАЬgive a pledge,вАЭ and so the two similar verbs would be emphatic: вАЬGive a pledge, be my surety.вАЭ However, the verb вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim, вАЬsetвАЭ) does not work with other verbs in this manner in any other contexts.
sn Job shows his desperation in lacking anyone to act as a guarantor on his behalf by asking God to accept himself as his own guarantor, a somewhat self-contradictory notion.
n180178: sn The idiom is вАЬto strike the hand.вАЭ Here the wording is a little different, вАЬWho is he that will strike himself into my hand?вАЭ
n180179: tn This half-verse gives the reason for the next half-verse.
n1801710: sn The pronoun their refers to JobвАЩs friends. They have not pledged security for him because God has hidden or sealed off their understanding.
n1801711: tn The object вАЬthemвАЭ is supplied. This is the simplest reading of the line, taking the verb as an active Polel. Some suggest that the subject is вАЬtheir handвАЭ and the verb is to be translated вАЬis not raised.вАЭ This would carry through the thought of the last verse, but it is not necessary to the point.
n1801712: tn Heb вАЬfor a portion.вАЭ This verse is rather obscure. The words are not that difficult, but the sense of them in this context is. Some take the idea to mean вАЬhe denounces his friends for a portion,вАЭ and others have a totally different idea of вАЬhe invites his friends to share with him.вАЭ The former fits the context better, indicating that JobвАЩs friends speak out against him for some personal gain. The second half of the verse then promises that his children will suffer loss for this attempt at gain. The line is surely proverbial. A number of other interpretations can be found in the commentaries.
n1801713: tn The verb is the third person, and so God is likely the subject. The LXX has вАЬyou have made me.вАЭ So most commentators clarify the verb in some such way. However, without an expressed subject it can also be taken as a passive.
n1801714: tn The word вАЬbywordвАЭ is related to the word translated вАЬproverbвАЭ in the Bible (вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Є„ЬвАО, mashal). JobвАЩs case is so well known that he is synonymous with afflictions and with abuse by people.
n1801715: tn The word вАП„™÷є„§÷ґ„™вАО (tofet) is a hapax legomenon. The expression is вАЬand a spitting in/to the face I have become,вАЭ i.e., вАЬI have become one in whose face people spit.вАЭ Various suggestions have been made, including a link to Tophet, but they are weak. The verse as it exists in the MT is fine, and fits the context well.
n1801716: tn See the usage of this verb in Gen 27:1 and Deut 34:7. Usually it is age that causes the failing eyesight, but here it is the grief.
n1801717: tn The word вАП„Щ÷∞„¶÷ї„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yetsurim), here with a suffix, occurs only here in the Bible. The word is related to вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (yatsar, вАЬto form, fashionвАЭ). And so Targum Job has вАЬmy forms,вАЭ and the Vulgate вАЬmy members.вАЭ The Syriac uses вАЬthoughtsвАЭ to reflect вАП„Щ÷µ„¶÷ґ„®вАО (yetser). Some have followed this to interpret, вАЬall my thoughts have dissolved into shadows.вАЭ But the parallel with вАЬeyeвАЭ would suggest вАЬform.вАЭ The plural вАЬmy forms, all of themвАЭ would refer to the whole body.
n1801718: tn This verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (shamam, вАЬappalledвАЭ) is the one found in Isa 52:14, translated there вАЬastonished.вАЭ
n1801719: tn The verb means вАЬto rouse oneself to excitement.вАЭ It naturally means вАЬto be agitated; to be stirred up.вАЭ
n1801720: tn The last two words are the imperfect verb вАП„Щ÷є„°÷і„Щ„£вАО (yosif) which means вАЬhe adds,вАЭ and the abstract noun вАЬenergy, strength.вАЭ This noun is not found elsewhere; its Piel verb occurs in Job 4:4 and 16:5. вАЬhe increases strength.вАЭ
n1801721: tn The form says вАЬall of them.вАЭ Several editors would change it to вАЬall of you,вАЭ but the lack of concord is not surprising; the vocative elsewhere uses the third person (see Mic 1:2; see also GKC 441 ¬І135.r).
n1801722: tn The first verb, the jussive, means вАЬto returnвАЭ; the second verb, the imperative, means вАЬto come.вАЭ The two could be taken as a hendiadys, the first verb becoming adverbial: вАЬto come again.вАЭ
n1801723: tn Instead of the exact correspondence between coordinate verbs, other combinations occurвАФhere we have a jussive and an imperative (see GKC 386 ¬І120.e).
n1801724: tn This term usually means вАЬplans; devicesвАЭ in a bad sense, although it can be used of GodвАЩs plans (see e.g., Zech 8:15).
n1801725: tn Although not in the Hebrew text, вАЬevenвАЭ is supplied in the translation, because this line is in apposition to the preceding.
n1801726: tn This word has been linked to the root вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (yarash, вАЬto inheritвАЭ) yielding a meaning вАЬthe possessions of my heart.вАЭ But it is actually to be connected to вАП„Р÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО ( Њarash, вАЬto desireвАЭ) cognate to the Akkadian eresu, вАЬdesire.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬlimbs,вАЭ which may come from an Aramaic word for вАЬropes.вАЭ An emendation based on the LXX would be risky.
n1801727: tn The verse simply has the plural, вАЬthey change.вАЭ But since this verse seems to be a description of his friends, a clarification of the referent in the translation is helpful.
n1801728: tn The same verb вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim, вАЬsetвАЭ) is used this way in Isa 5:20: вАЬвА¶who change darkness into light.вАЭ
n1801729: tn The rest of the verse makes better sense if it is interpreted as what his friends say.
n1801730: tn This expression is open to alternative translations: (1) It could mean that they say in the face of darkness, вАЬLight is near.вАЭ (2) It could also mean вАЬThe light is near the darknessвАЭ or вАЬThe light is nearer than the darkness.вАЭ
n1801731: tn The clause begins with вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) which here has more of the sense of вАЬsince.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 253) takes a rather rare use of the word to get вАЬCan I hope againвАЭ (see also GKC 475 ¬І150.f for the caveat).
n1801732: tn This is understood because the conditional clauses seem to run to the apodosis in v. 15.
n1801733: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (shakhat) may be the word вАЬcorruptionвАЭ from a root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (shakhat, вАЬto destroyвАЭ) or a word вАЬpitвАЭ from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shuakh, вАЬto sink downвАЭ). The same problem surfaces in Ps 16:10, where it is parallel to вАЬSheol.вАЭ E. F. Sutcliffe, The Old Testament and the Future Life, 76ff., defends the meaning вАЬcorruption.вАЭ But many commentators here take it to mean вАЬthe graveвАЭ in harmony with вАЬSheol.вАЭ But in this verse вАЬwormsвАЭ would suggest вАЬcorruptionвАЭ is better.
n1801734: tn The adverb вАП„Р÷µ„§„Х÷євАО ( Њefo, вАЬthenвАЭ) plays an enclitic role here (see Job 4:7).
n1801735: tn The repetition of вАЬmy hopeвАЭ in the verse has thrown the versions off, and their translations have led commentators also to change the second one to something like вАЬgoodness,вАЭ on the assumption that a word cannot be repeated in the same verse. The word actually carries two different senses here. The first would be the basic meaning вАЬhope,вАЭ but the second a metonymy of cause, namely, what hope produces, what will be seen.
n1801736: sn It is natural to assume that this verse continues the interrogative clause of the preceding verse.
n1801737: tn The plural form of the verb probably refers to the two words, or the two senses of the word in the preceding verse. Hope and what it produces will perish with Job.
n1801738: tn The Hebrew word вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (baddim) describes the вАЬbarsвАЭ or вАЬboltsвАЭ of Sheol, referring (by synecdoche) to the вАЬgates of Sheol.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬwith me to Sheol,вАЭ and many adopt that as вАЬby my side.вАЭ
n1801739: tn The conjunction вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) confirms the interrogative interpretation.
n1801740: tn The translation follows the LXX and the Syriac versions with the change of vocalization in the MT. The MT has the noun вАЬrest,вАЭ yielding, вАЬwill our rest be together in the dust?вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nakhat) in Aramaic means вАЬto go down; to descend.вАЭ If that is the preferred readingвАФand it almost is universally accepted hereвАФthen it would be spelled вАП„†÷µ„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nekhat). In either case the point of the verse is clearly describing death and going to the grave.
n180181: sn Bildad attacks Job with less subtlety than Eliphaz. He describes the miserable existence of the wicked, indicating that it is the proof of sin. His speech falls into two main parts: why is Job so contemptuous toward his friends (Job 18:2-4), and the fate of the wicked (18:5-21). On this chapter see N. M. Sarna, вАЬThe Mythological Background of Job 18,вАЭ JBL 82 (1963): 315-18; and W. A. Irwin, вАЬJobвАЩs Redeemer,вАЭ JBL 81 (1962): 217-29.
n180182: tn The verb is plural, and so most commentators make it singular. But it seems from the context that Bildad is addressing all of them, and not just Job.
n180183: tn The construction is вАП„І÷і„†÷∞„¶÷µ„Щ „Ь÷∞„Ю÷і„Ь÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (qintse lemillin), which is often taken to be вАЬend of words,вАЭ as if the word was from вАП„І÷µ„•вАО (qets, вАЬendвАЭ). But a plural of вАЬendвАЭ is not found in the OT. Some will link the word to Arabic qanasa, вАЬto hunt; to give chase,вАЭ to get an interpretation of вАЬsnares for words.вАЭ But E. Dhorme (Job, 257) objects that this does not fit the speech of Bildad (as well as it might JobвАЩs). He finds a cognate qinsu, вАЬfetters, shackles,вАЭ and reads вАЬhow long will you put shackles on words.вАЭ But G. R. Driver had pointed out that this cognate does not exist (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 72-93). So it would be preferable to take the reading вАЬendsвАЭ and explain the вАП„ЯвАО (nun) as from a Aramaizing by-form. This is supported by 11QtgJob that uses вАП„°„Х÷є„£вАО (sof, вАЬendвАЭ). On the construction, GKC 421 ¬І130.a explains this as a use of the construct in rapid narrative to connect the words; in such cases a preposition is on the following noun.
n180184: tn The imperfect verb, again plural, would be here taken in the nuance of instruction, or a modal nuance of obligation. So Bildad is telling his listeners to be intelligent. This would be rather cutting in the discourse.
n180186: tn The verb вАП„†÷і„Ш÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (nitminu) has been explained from different roots. Some take it from вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷µ„РвАО (tame Њ, вАЬto be uncleanвАЭ), and translate it вАЬWhy should we be unclean in your eyes?вАЭ Most would connect it to вАП„Ш÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (tamam, вАЬto stop upвАЭ), meaning вАЬto be stupidвАЭ in the Niphal. Another suggestion is to follow the LXX and read from вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (damam, вАЬto be reduced to silenceвАЭ). Others take it from вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (damah) with a meaning вАЬto be like.вАЭ But what is missing is the term of comparisonвАФlike what? Various suggestions have been made, but all are simply conjectures.
n180187: tn The construction uses the participle and then third person suffixes: вАЬO tearer of himself in his anger.вАЭ But it is clearly referring to Job, and so the direct second person pronouns should be used to make that clear. The LXX is an approximation or paraphrase here: вАЬAnger has possessed you, for what if you should dieвАФwould under heaven be desolate, or shall the mountains be overthrown from their foundations?вАЭ
n180188: tn There is a good deal of study on this word in this passage, and in Job in general. M. Dahood suggested a root вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО ( њazav) meaning вАЬto arrange; to rearrangeвАЭ (вАЬThe Root  њzb II in Job,вАЭ JBL 78 [1959]: 303-9). But this is refuted by H. G. M. Williamson, вАЬA Reconsideration of  њzb II in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ ZAW 97 (1985): 74-85.
n180189: sn Bildad is asking if Job thinks the whole moral order of the world should be interrupted for his sake, that he may escape the punishment for wickedness.
n1801810: tn Hebrew вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЭвАО (gam, вАЬalso; moreoverвАЭ), in view of what has just been said.
n1801811: sn The lamp or the light can have a number of uses in the Bible. Here it is probably an implied metaphor for prosperity and happiness, for the good life itself.
n1801812: tn The expression is literally вАЬthe flame of his fire,вАЭ but the pronominal suffix qualifies the entire bound construction. The two words together intensify the idea of the flame.
n1801813:
tn The LXX interprets a little more precisely: вАЬhis lamp shall be put out with him.вАЭ
sn This thesis of Bildad will be questioned by Job in 21:17вАФhow often is the lamp of the wicked snuffed out?
n1801814: tn Heb вАЬthe steps of his vigor,вАЭ the genitive being the attribute.
n1801815: tn The verb вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (tsarar) means вАЬto be cramped; to be straitened; to be hemmed in.вАЭ The trouble has hemmed him in, so that he cannot walk with the full, vigorous steps he had before. The LXX has вАЬLet the meanest of men spoil his goods.вАЭ
n1801816: tn The LXX has вАЬcauses him to stumble,вАЭ which many commentators accept, but this involves the transposition of the three letters. The verb is вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (shalakh, вАЬthrowвАЭ) not вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ЬвАО (kashal, вАЬstumbleвАЭ).
n1801818: tn The word вАП„©„В÷∞„С÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (sevakhah) is used in scripture for the lattice window (2 Kgs 1:2). The Arabic cognate means вАЬto be intertwined.вАЭ So the term could describe a net, matting, grating, or lattice. Here it would be the netting stretched over a pit.
n1801819: tn This word вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„ЧвАО (pakh) specifically refers to the snare of the fowlerвАФthus a bird trap. But its plural seems to refer to nets in general (see Job 22:10).
n1801820: tn This word does not occur elsewhere. But another word from the same root means вАЬplait of hair,вАЭ and so this term has something to do with a net like a trellis or lattice.
n1801821: tn Heb вАЬhis rope.вАЭ The suffix must be a genitive expressing that the trap was for him, to trap him, and so an objective genitive.
n1801822: tn Heb вАЬhis trap.вАЭ The pronominal suffix is objective genitive here as well.
n1801823: sn Bildad is referring here to all the things that afflict a person and cause terror. It would then be a metonymy of effect, the cause being the afflictions.
n1801824: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ„Х÷Љ„•вАО (puts) in the Hiphil has the meaning вАЬto pursueвАЭ and вАЬto scatter.вАЭ It is followed by the expression вАЬat his feet.вАЭ So the idea is easily derived: they chase him at his feet. But some commentators have other proposals. The most far-fetched is that of Ehrlich and Driver (ZAW 24 [1953]: 259-60) which has вАЬand compel him to urinate on his feet,вАЭ one of many similar readings the NEB accepted from Driver.
n1801825: tn The jussive is occasionally used without its normal sense and only as an imperfect (see GKC 323 ¬І109.k).
n1801826: tn There are a number of suggestions for вАП„Р÷є„†„Х÷євАО ( Њono). Some take it as вАЬvigorвАЭ: thus вАЬhis strength is hungry.вАЭ Others take it as вАЬiniquityвАЭ: thus вАЬhis iniquity/trouble is hungry.вАЭ
n1801827: tn The expression means that misfortune is right there to destroy him whenever there is the opportunity.
n1801828: tn The expression вАЬthe limbs of his skinвАЭ makes no sense, unless a poetic meaning of вАЬpartsвАЭ (or perhaps вАЬlayersвАЭ) is taken. The parallelism has вАЬhis skinвАЭ in the first colon, and вАЬhis limbsвАЭ in the second. One plausible suggestion is to take вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„У÷Љ÷µ„ЩвАО (badde, вАЬlimbs ofвАЭ) in the first part to be вАП„С÷Љ÷і„У÷∞„Х÷Є„ЩвАО (bidvay, вАЬby a diseaseвАЭ; Dhorme, Wright, RSV). The verb has to be made passive, however. The versions have different things: The LXX has вАЬlet the branches of his feet be eatenвАЭ; the Syriac has вАЬhis cities will be swallowed up by forceвАЭ; the Vulgate reads вАЬlet it devour the beauty of his skinвАЭ; and Targum Job has вАЬit will devour the linen garments that cover his skin.вАЭ
n1801829: tn The вАЬfirstborn of deathвАЭ is the strongest child of death (Gen 49:3), or the deadliest death (like the вАЬfirstborn of the poor, the poorestвАЭ). The phrase means the most terrible death (A. B. Davidson, Job, 134).
n1801830: tn Heb вАЬfrom his tent, his security.вАЭ The apposition serves to modify the tent as his security.
n1801831: tn The verb is the Hiphil of вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (tsa њad, вАЬto lead awayвАЭ). The problem is that the form is either a third feminine (Rashi thought it was referring to JobвАЩs wife) or the second person. There is a good deal of debate over the possibility of the prefix t- being a variant for the third masculine form. The evidence in Ugaritic and Akkadian is mixed, stronger for the plural than the singular. Gesenius has some samples where the third feminine form might also be used for the passive if there is no expressed subject (see GKC 459 ¬І144.b), but the evidence is not strong. The simplest choices are to change the prefix to a вАП„ЩвАО (yod), or argue that the вАП„™вАО (tav) can be masculine, or follow Gesenius.
n1801832: sn This is a reference to death, the king of all terrors. Other identifications are made in the commentaries: Mot, the Ugaritic god of death; Nergal of the Babylonians; Molech of the Canaanites, the one to whom people sent emissaries.
n1801833: tn This line is difficult as well. The verb, again a third feminine form, says вАЬit dwells in his tent.вАЭ But the next part (вАП„Ю÷і„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ „Ь„Х÷євАО, mibbeli lo) means something like вАЬthings of what are not his.вАЭ The best that can be made of the MT is вАЬThere shall live in his tent they that are not hisвАЭ (referring to persons and animals; see J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 279). G. R. Driver and G. B. Gray (Job [ICC], 2:161) refer вАЬthat which is naught of hisвАЭ to weeds and wild animals. M. Dahood suggested a reading вАП„Ю÷Ј„С÷Љ÷ґ„ЬвАО (mabbel) and a connection to Akkadian nablu, вАЬfireвАЭ (cf. Ugaritic nbl). The interchange of m and n is not a problem, and the parallelism with the next line makes good sense (вАЬSome Northwest Semitic words in Job,вАЭ Bib 38 [1957]: 312ff.). Others suggest an emendation to get вАЬnight-hagвАЭ or vampire. This suggestion, as well as DriverвАЩs вАЬmixed herbs,вАЭ are linked to the idea of exorcism. But if a change is to be made, DahoodвАЩs is the most compelling.
n1801834: tn Heb вАЬoutside.вАЭ Cf. ESV, вАЬin the street,вАЭ referring to absence from his communityвАЩs memory.
n1801835: tn The verbs in this verse are plural; without the expressed subject they should be taken in the passive sense.
n1801836: tn The two words вАП„†÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (nin, вАЬoffspringвАЭ) and вАП„†÷ґ„Ы÷ґ„УвАО (nekhed, вАЬposterityвАЭ) are always together and form an alliteration. This is hard to capture in English, but some have tried: Moffatt had вАЬson and scion,вАЭ and Tur-Sinai had вАЬbreed or brood.вАЭ But the words are best simply translated as вАЬlineage and posterityвАЭ or as in the NIV вАЬoffspring or descendants.вАЭ
n1801837: tn Heb вАЬin his sojournings.вАЭ The verb вАП„Т÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®вАО (gur) means вАЬto reside; to sojournвАЭ temporarily, without land rights. Even this word has been selected to stress the temporary nature of his stay on earth.
n1801838: tn The word вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њakharonim) means вАЬthose [men] coming after.вАЭ And the next word, вАП„І÷Ј„У÷∞„Ю÷є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (qadmonim), means вАЬthose [men] coming before.вАЭ Some commentators have tried to see here references to people who lived before and people who lived after, but that does not explain their being appalled at the fate of the wicked. So the normal way this is taken is in connection to the geography, notably the seasвАФвАЬthe hinder seaвАЭ refers to the Mediterranean, the West, and вАЬthe front seaвАЭ refers to the Dead Sea (Zech 14:8), namely, the East. The versions understood this as temporal: вАЬthe last groaned for him, and wonder seized the firstвАЭ (LXX).
n1801840: tn The expression has вАЬthey seize horror.вАЭ The RSV renders this вАЬhorror seizes them.вАЭ The same idiom is found in Job 21:6: вАЬlaid hold on shuddering.вАЭ The idiom would solve the grammatical problem and not change the meaning greatly, but it would change the parallelism.
n1801841: tn The word вАЬsayingвАЭ is supplied in the translation to mark and introduce the following as a quotation of these people who are seized with horror. The alternative is to take v. 21 as BildadвАЩs own summary statement (cf. G. R. Driver and G. B. Gray, Job [ICC], 2:162; J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 280).
n1801842: tn The term is in the plural, вАЬthe tabernaclesвАЭ; it should be taken as a plural of local extension (see GKC 397 ¬І124.b).
n1801843: tn The word вАЬplaceвАЭ is in construct; the clause following it replaces the genitive: вАЬthis is the place ofвАФhe has not known God.вАЭ
n180191: sn Job is completely stunned by BildadвАЩs speech, and feels totally deserted by God and his friends. Yet from his despair a new hope emerges with a stronger faith. Even though he knows he will die in his innocence, he knows that God will vindicate him and that he will be conscious of the vindication. There are four parts to this reply: JobвАЩs impatience with the speeches of his friends (2-6), GodвАЩs abandonment of Job and his attack (7-12), JobвАЩs forsaken state and appeal to his friends (13-22), and JobвАЩs confidence that he will be vindicated (23-29).
n180192: tn Heb вАЬtorment my soul,вАЭ with вАЬsoulвАЭ representing the self or individual. The MT has a verb from вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (yagah, вАЬto afflict; to tormentвАЭ). This is supported by the versions. But the LXX has вАЬto tireвАЭ which is apparently from вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (yaga њ). The form in the MT is unusual because it preserves the final (original) yod in the Hiphil (see GKC 214 ¬І75.gg). So this unusual form has been preserved, and is the correct reading. A modal nuance for the imperfect fits best here: вАЬHow long do you intend to do this?вАЭ
n180193: tn The MT has вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ы÷Є„РвАО (dakha Њ), вАЬto crushвАЭ in the Piel. The LXX, however, has a more general word which means вАЬto destroy.вАЭ
n180194: tn The LXX adds to the verse: вАЬonly know that the Lord has dealt with me thus.вАЭ
n180195: sn The number вАЬtenвАЭ is a general expression to convey that this has been done often (see Gen 31:7; Num 14:22).
n180196: tn The Hiphil of the verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (kalam) means вАЬoutrage; insult; shame.вАЭ The verbs in this verse are prefixed conjugations, and may be interpreted as preterites if the reference is to the past time. But since the action is still going on, progressive imperfects work well.
n180197: tn The second half of the verse uses two verbs, the one dependent on the other. It could be translated вАЬyou are not ashamed to attack meвАЭ (see GKC 385-86 ¬І120.c), or вАЬyou attack me shamelessly.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (hakhar) poses some difficulties for both the ancient versions and the modern commentators. The verb seems to be cognate to Arabic hakara, вАЬto oppress; to ill-treat.вАЭ This would mean that there has been a transformation of вАП„ЧвАО (khet) to вАП„ФвАО (he). Three Hebrew mss actually have the вАП„ЧвАО (khet). This has been widely accepted; other suggestions are irrelevant.
n180198: tn Job has held to his innocence, so the only way that he could say вАЬI have erredвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷і„Щ„™÷і„ЩвАО, shagiti) is in a hypothetical clause like this.
n180199: tn There is a long addition in the LXX: вАЬin having spoken words which it is not right to speak, and my words err, and are unreasonable.вАЭ
n1801910: tn The word вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Т÷Є„ФвАО (meshugah) is a hapax legomenon. It is derived from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (shug, вАЬto wander; to errвАЭ) with root paralleling вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Ј„ТвАО (shagag) and вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah). What Job is saying is that even if it were true that he had erred, it did not injure themвАФit was solely his concern.
n1801911: tn The introductory particles repeat вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷∞„†÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њamnam, вАЬindeedвАЭ) but now with вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬifвАЭ). It could be interpreted to mean вАЬis it not true,вАЭ or as here in another conditional clause.
n1801912: tn The verb is the Hiphil of вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„ЬвАО (gadal); it can mean вАЬto make greatвАЭ or as an internal causative вАЬto make oneself greatвАЭ or вАЬto assume a lofty attitude, to be insolent.вАЭ There is no reason to assume another root here with the meaning of вАЬquarrelвАЭ (as Gordis does).
n1801913: sn JobвАЩs friends have been using his shame, his humiliation in all his sufferings, as proof against him in their case.
n1801914: tn The imperative is used here to introduce a solemn affirmation. This verse proves that Job was in no way acknowledging sin in v. 4. Here Job is declaring that God has wronged him, and in so doing, perverted justice.
n1801915: tn The Piel of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷Ј„™вАО ( њavat) means вАЬto warp justiceвАЭ (see 8:3), or here, to do wrong to someone (see Ps 119:78). The statement is chosen to refute the question that Bildad asked in his first speech.
n1801916: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„І÷Ј„£вАО (naqaf) means вАЬto turn; to make a circle; to encircle.вАЭ It means that God has encircled or engulfed Job with his net.
n1801917: tn The word вАП„Ю÷∞„¶„Х÷Љ„У„Х÷євАО (metsudo) is usually connected with вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„УвАО (tsud, вАЬto huntвАЭ), and so is taken to mean вАЬa net.вАЭ Gordis and Habel, however, interpret it to mean вАЬsiegeworksвАЭ thrown up around a cityвАФbut that would require changing the вАП„УвАО (dalet) to a вАП„®вАО (resh) (cf. NLT, вАЬI am like a city under siegeвАЭ). The LXX, though, has вАЬbulwark.вАЭ Besides, the previous speech used several words for вАЬnet.вАЭ
n1801918: tn The particle is used here as in 9:11 (see GKC 497 ¬І159.w).
n1801919: tc The LXX has вАЬI laugh at reproach.вАЭ
n1801920: tn The same idea is expressed in Jer 20:8 and Hab 1:2. The cry is a cry for help, that he has been wronged, that there is no justice.
n1801921: tn The Niphal is simply вАЬI am not answered.вАЭ See Prov 21:13b.
n1801922: tn The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„®вАО (gadar) means вАЬto wall up; to fence up; to block.вАЭ God has blocked JobвАЩs way so that he cannot get through. See the note on 3:23. Cf. Lam 3:7.
n1801923: tn Some commentators take the word to be вАП„Ч÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (hasak), related to an Arabic word for вАЬthorn hedge.вАЭ
n1801924: sn The images here are fairly common in the Bible. God has stripped away JobвАЩs honorable reputation. The crown is the metaphor for the esteem and dignity he once had. See 29:14; Isa 61:3; see Ps 8:5 [6].
n1801925: tn The metaphors are changed now to a demolished building and an uprooted tree. The verb is вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„•вАО (natats, вАЬto demolishвАЭ). Since it is Job himself who is the object, the meaning cannot be вАЬdemolishвАЭ (as of a house so that an inhabitant has to leave), but more of the attack or the battering.
n1801926: tn The text has вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh, вАЬto leaveвАЭ). But in view of Job 14:20, вАЬperishвАЭ or вАЬdepartвАЭ would be a better meaning here.
n1801927: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ҐвАО (nasa њ) means вАЬto travelвАЭ generally, but specifically it means вАЬto pull up the tent pegs and move.вАЭ The Hiphil here means вАЬuproot.вАЭ It is used of a vine in Ps 80:9. The idea here does not contradict Job 14:7, for there the tree still had roots and so could grow.
n1801928: tn The NEB has вАЬmy tent rope,вАЭ but that seems too contrived here. It is absurd to pull up a tent-rope like a tree.
n1801929: tn Heb вАЬlike a tree.вАЭ The words вАЬone uprootsвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1801930: tn The verb is a nonpreterite vayyiqtol perhaps employed to indicate that the contents of v. 11 are a logical sequence to the actions described in v. 10.
n1801931: tn This second half of the verse is a little difficult. The Hebrew has вАЬand he reckons me for him like his adversaries.вАЭ Most would change the last word to a singular in harmony with the versions, вАЬas his adversary.вАЭ But some retain the MT pointing and try to explain it variously: Weiser suggests that the plural might have come from a cultic recitation of YahwehвАЩs deeds against his enemies; Fohrer thinks it refers to the primeval enemies; Gordis takes it as distributive, вАЬas one of his foes.вАЭ If the plural is retained, this last view makes the most sense.
n1801932: sn Now the metaphor changes again. Since God thinks of Job as an enemy, he attacks with his troops, builds the siege ramp, and camps around him to besiege him. All the power and all the forces are at GodвАЩs disposal in his attack of Job.
n1801933: tn Heb вАЬthey throw up their way against me.вАЭ The verb вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (salal) means вАЬto build a siege rampвАЭ or вАЬto throw up a rampвАЭ; here the object is вАЬtheir way.вАЭ The latter could be taken as an adverbial accusative, вАЬas their way.вАЭ But as the object it fits just as well. Some delete the middle clause; the LXX has вАЬTogether his troops fell upon me, they beset my ways with an ambush.вАЭ
n1801935: tn The LXX apparently took вАП„Р÷Ј„Ъ÷∞÷Њ„Ц÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њakh, вАЬeven, only,вАЭ and zaru, вАЬthey turn awayвАЭ) together as if it was the verb вАП„Р÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Ц÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њakhzaru, вАЬthey have become cruel,вАЭ as in 20:21). But the grammar in the line would be difficult with this. Moreover, the word is most likely from вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„®вАО (zur, вАЬto turn awayвАЭ). See L. A. Snijders, вАЬThe Meaning of zar in the Old Testament,вАЭ OTS 10 (1964): 1-154 (especially p. 9).
n1801936: tn The Pual participle is used for those вАЬknownвАЭ to him, or with whom he is вАЬfamiliar,вАЭ whereas вАП„І÷Є„®„Х÷є„СвАО (qarov, вАЬnearвАЭ) is used for a relative.
n1801937: tn Many commentators add the first part of v. 15 to this verse, because it is too loaded and this is too short. That gives the reading вАЬMy kinsmen and my familiar friends have disappeared, they have forgotten me (15) the guests I entertained.вАЭ There is not much support for this, nor is there much reason for it.
n1801938: tn The Hebrew вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„®÷µ„Щ „С÷µ„Щ„™÷і„ЩвАО (gare veti, вАЬthe guests of my houseвАЭ) refers to those who sojourned in JobвАЩs houseвАФnot residents, but guests.
n1801939: tn The form of the verb is a feminine plural, which would seem to lend support to the proposed change of the lines (see last note to v. 14). But the form may be feminine primarily because of the immediate reference. On the other side, the suffix of вАЬtheir eyesвАЭ is a masculine plural. So the evidence lies on both sides.
n1801940: tn This word вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (nokhri) is the person from another race, from a strange land, the foreigner. The previous word, вАП„Т÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (ger), is a more general word for someone who is staying in the land but is not a citizen, that is, a sojourner.
n1801941: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (qara Њ) followed by the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition means вАЬto summon.вАЭ Contrast Ps 123:2.
n1801942: tn Heb вАЬplead for graceвАЭ or вАЬplead for mercyвАЭ (ESV).
n1801943: tn The Hebrew appears to have вАЬmy breath is strange to my wife.вАЭ This would be the meaning if the verb was from вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„®вАО (zur, вАЬto turn aside; to be a strangerвАЭ). But it should be connected to вАП„Ц÷і„Щ„®вАО (zir), cognate to Assyrian zaru, вАЬto feel repugnance toward.вАЭ Here it is used in the intransitive sense, вАЬto be repulsive.вАЭ L. A. Snijders, following Driver, doubts the existence of this second root, and retains вАЬstrangeвАЭ (вАЬThe Meaning of zar in the Old Testament,вАЭ OTS 10 [1964]: 1-154).
n1801944: tn The normal meaning here would be based on the root вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan, вАЬto be graciousвАЭ). And so we have versions reading вАЬalthough I entreatedвАЭ or вАЬmy supplication.вАЭ But it seems more likely it is to be connected to another root meaning вАЬto be offensive; to be loathsome.вАЭ For the discussion of the connection to the Arabic, see E. Dhorme, Job, 278.
n1801945: tn The text has вАЬthe sons of my belly [= body].вАЭ This would normally mean вАЬmy sons.вАЭ But they are all dead. And there is no suggestion that Job had other sons. The word вАЬmy bellyвАЭ will have to be understood as вАЬmy womb,вАЭ i.e., the womb I came from. Instead of вАЬbrothers,вАЭ the sense could be вАЬsiblingsвАЭ (both brothers and sisters; G. R. Driver and G. B. Gray, Job [ICC], 2:168).
n1801946: sn The use of the verb вАЬriseвАЭ is probably fairly literal. When Job painfully tries to get up and walk, the little boys make fun of him.
n1801947: tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷і„С÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (dibber) followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) indicates speaking against someone, namely, scoffing or railing against someone (see Pss 50:20; 78:19). Some commentators find another root with the meaning вАЬto turn oneвАЩs back on; to turn aside from.вАЭ The argument is weak philologically because it requires a definition вАЬfromвАЭ for the preposition вАП„СвАО. See among others I. Eitan, вАЬStudies in Hebrew Roots,вАЭ JQR 14 (1923-24): 31-52, especially 38-41.
n1801948: tn Heb вАЬmen of my confidence,вАЭ or вАЬmen of my council,вАЭ i.e., intimate friends, confidants.
n1801949: tn The pronoun вАП„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО (zeh) functions here in the place of a nominative (see GKC 447 ¬І138.h).
n1801950: tn T. Penar translates this вАЬturn away from meвАЭ (вАЬJob 19, 19 in the Light of Ben Sira 6, 11, вАЭ Bib 48 [1967]: 293-95).
n1801951: tn The meaning would be вАЬI am nothing but skin and bonesвАЭ in current English idiom. Both lines of this verse need attention. The first half seems to say, вАЬMy skin and my flesh sticks to my bones.вАЭ Some think that this is too long, and that the bones can stick to the skin, or the flesh, but not both. Dhorme proposes вАЬin my skin my flesh has rotted awayвАЭ (вАП„®÷Є„І÷Ј„СвАО, raqav). This involves several changes in the line, however. He then changes the second line to read вАЬand I have gnawed my bone with my teethвАЭ (transferring вАЬboneвАЭ from the first half and omitting вАЬskinвАЭ). There are numerous other renderings of this; some of the more notable are: вАЬI escape, my bones in my teethвАЭ (Merx); вАЬmy teeth fall outвАЭ (Duhm); вАЬmy teeth fall from my gumsвАЭ (Pope); вАЬmy bones protrude in sharp pointsвАЭ (Kissane). A. B. Davidson retains вАЬthe skin of my teeth,вАЭ meaning вАЬgums.вАЭ This is about the last thing that Job has, or he would not be able to speak. For a detailed study of this verse, D. J. A. Clines devotes two full pages of textual notes (Job [WBC], 430-31). He concludes with вАЬMy bones hang from my skin and my flesh, I am left with only the skin of my teeth.вАЭ
n1801953: tn The word вАЬaliveвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1801954: sn Strahan comments, вАЬThe whole tragedy of the book is packed into these extraordinary words.вАЭ
n1801955: sn The idiom of eating the pieces of someone means вАЬslanderвАЭ in Aramaic (see Dan 3:8), Arabic and Akkadian.
n1801956: tn The optative is again expressed with the interrogative clause вАЬWho will give that they be written?вАЭ Job wishes that his words be preserved long after his death.
n1801957: tn While the sense of this line is clear, there is a small problem and a plausible solution. The last word is indeed вАП„°÷ґ„§÷ґ„®вАО (sefer, вАЬbookвАЭ), usually understood here to mean вАЬscroll.вАЭ But the verb that follows it in the verse is вАП„Щ÷ї„Ч÷Є„І„Х÷ЉвАО (yukhaqu), from вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО (khaqaq, вАЬto engrave; to carveвАЭ). While the meaning is clearly that Job wants his words to be retained, the idea of engraving in a book, although not impossible, is unusual. And so many have suggested that the Akkadian word siparru, вАЬcopper; brass,вАЭ is what is meant here (see Isa 30:8; Judg 5:14). The consonants are the same, and the vowel pattern is close to the original vowel pattern of this segholate noun. Writing on copper or bronze sheets has been attested from the 12th to the 2nd centuries, notably in the Copper Scroll from Qumran (3Q15), which would allow the translation вАЬscrollвАЭ in our text (for more bibliography see D. J. A. Clines, Job [WBC], 432). But H. S. Gehman notes that in Phoenician our word can mean вАЬinscriptionвАЭ (вАЬвАП„°÷µ„§÷ґ„®вАО, An Inscription, in the Book of Job,вАЭ JBL 63 [1944]: 303-7), making the proposed substitution unnecessary.
n1801958: sn There is some question concerning the use of the lead. It surely cannot be a second description of the tool, for a lead tool would be of no use in chiseling words into a rock. It was RashiвАЩs idea, followed by Dillmann and Duhm, that lead was run into the cut-out letters. The suggestion that they wrote on lead tablets does not seem to fit the verse (cf. NIV). See further A. Baker, вАЬThe Strange Case of JobвАЩs Chisel,вАЭ CBQ 31 (1969): 370-79.
n1801959: tn Or вАЬmy Vindicator.вАЭ The word is the active participle from вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (ga Њal, вАЬto redeem, protect, vindicateвАЭ). The word is well-known in the OT because of its identification as the kinsman-redeemer (see the book of Ruth). This is the near kinsman who will pay off oneвАЩs debts, defend the family, avenge a killing, marry the widow of the deceased. The word вАЬredeemerвАЭ evokes the wrong connotation for people familiar with the NT alone; a translation of вАЬVindicatorвАЭ would capture the idea more. The concept might include the description of the mediator already introduced in Job 16:19, but surely here Job is thinking of God as his vindicator. The interesting point to be stressed here is that Job has said clearly that he sees no vindication in this life, that he is going to die. But he knows he will be vindicated, and even though he will die, his vindicator lives. The dilemma remains though: his distress lay in GodвАЩs hiding his face from him, and his vindication lay only in beholding God in peace.
n1801960: tn The word вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО ( Њakharon, вАЬlastвАЭ) has triggered a number of interpretations. Here it is an adjectival form and not adverbial; it is an epithet of the vindicator. Some commentators, followed by the RSV, change the form to make it adverbial, and translate it вАЬat last.вАЭ T. H. Gaster translates it вАЬeven if he were the last person to existвАЭ (вАЬShort notes,вАЭ VT 4 [1954]: 78).
n1801961: tn The Hebrew has вАЬand he will rise/stand upon [the] dust.вАЭ The verb вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum) is properly вАЬto rise; to arise,вАЭ and certainly also can mean вАЬto stand.вАЭ Both English ideas are found in the verb. The concept here is that of God rising up to mete out justice. And so to avoid confusion with the idea of resurrection (which although implicit in these words which are pregnant with theological ideas yet to be revealed, is not explicitly stated or intended in this context) the translation вАЬstandвАЭ has been used. The Vulgate had вАЬI will rise,вАЭ which introduced the idea of JobвАЩs resurrection. The word вАЬdustвАЭ is used as in 41:33. The word вАЬdustвАЭ is associated with death and the grave, the very earthly particles. Job assumes that God will descend from heaven to bring justice to the world. The use of the word also hints that this will take place after Job has died and returned to dust. Again, the words of Job come to mean far more than he probably understood.
n1801962: tn This verse on the whole has some serious interpretation problems that have allowed commentators to go in several directions. The verbal clause is вАЬthey strike off this,вАЭ which is then to be taken as a passive in view of the fact that there is no expressed subject. Some have thought that Job was referring to this life, and that after his disease had done its worst he would see his vindication (see T. J. Meek, вАЬJob 19:25-27, вАЭ VT 6 [1956]: 100-103; E. F. Sutcliffe, вАЬFurther notes on Job, textual and exegetical,вАЭ Bib 31 [1950]: 377; and others). But Job has been clearвАФhe does not expect to live and see his vindication in this life. There are a host of other interpretations that differ greatly from the sense expressed in the MT. Duhm, for example, has вАЬand another shall arise as my witness.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 284-85) argues that the vindication comes after death; he emends the verb to get a translation: вАЬand that, behind my skin, I shall stand up.вАЭ He explains this to mean that it will be Job in person who will be present at the ultimate drama. But the interpretation is forced, and really unnecessary.
n1801963: tn The Hebrew phrase is вАЬand from my flesh.вАЭ This could mean вАЬwithout my flesh,вАЭ i.e., separated from my flesh, or вАЬfrom my flesh,вАЭ i.e., in or with my flesh. The former view is taken by those who think JobвАЩs vindication will come in this life, and who find the idea of a resurrection unlikely to be in JobвАЩs mind. The latter view is taken by those who interpret the preceding line as meaning death and the next verse underscoring that it will be his eye that will see. This would indicate that JobвАЩs faith rises to an unparalleled level at this point.
n1801964: tn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 140) says, вАЬThe text of this verse is so difficult, and any convincing reconstruction is so unlikely, that it seems best not to attempt it.вАЭ His words have gone unheeded, even by himself, and rightly so. There seem to be two general interpretations, the details of some words notwithstanding. An honest assessment of the evidence would have to provide both interpretations, albeit still arguing for one. Here Job says he will see God. This at the least means that he will witness his vindication, which it seems clear from the other complaints of Job will occur after his death (it is his blood that must be vindicated). But in what way, exactly, Job will see God is not clarified.
