In this verse the verb that is used is often used of prophetic visions, but in the next verse the plain word for seeingвАФwith his eyeвАФis used. The fulfillment will be more precise than Job may have understood. Rowley does conclude: вАЬThough there is no full grasping of a belief in a worthwhile Afterlife with God, this passage is a notable landmark in the program toward such a belief.вАЭ The difficulty is that Job expects to dieвАФhe would like to be vindicated in this life, but is resolved that he will die. (1) Some commentators think that vv. 25 and 26 follow the wish for vindication now; (2) others (traditionally) see it as in the next life. Some of the other interpretations that take a different line are less impressive, such as KissaneвАЩs, вАЬdid I but see GodвА¶were I to behold GodвАЭ; or L. WatermanвАЩs translation in the English present, making it a mystic vision in which Job already sees that God is his vindicator (вАЬNote on Job 19:23-27: JobвАЩs Triumph of Faith,вАЭ JBL 69 [1950]: 379-80).
n1801965: tn The emphasis is on вАЬIвАЭ and вАЬfor myself.вАЭ No other will be seeing this vindication, but Job himself will see it. Of that he is confident. Some take вАП„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (li, вАЬfor myselfвАЭ) to mean favorable to me, or on my side (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 143). But Job is expecting (not just wishing for) a face-to-face encounter in the vindication.
n1801966: tn Hitzig offered another interpretation that is somewhat forced. The вАЬotherвАЭ (вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО, zar) or вАЬstrangerвАЭ would refer to Job. He would see God, not as an enemy, but in peace.
n1801967: tn Heb вАЬkidneys,вАЭ a poetic expression for the seat of emotions.
n1801968: tn Heb вАЬfail/grow faint in my breast.вАЭ Job is saying that he has expended all his energy with his longing for vindication.
n1801969: tc The MT reads вАЬin me.вАЭ If that is retained, then the question would be in the first colon, and the reasoning of the second colon would be JobвАЩs. But over 100 mss have вАЬin him,вАЭ and so this reading is accepted by most editors. The verse is a little difficult, but it seems to form a warning by Job that GodвАЩs appearance which will vindicate Job will bring judgment on those who persecute him and charge him falsely.
n1801970: tn The word вАЬwrathвАЭ probably refers to divine wrath for the wicked. Many commentators change this word to read вАЬthey,вАЭ or more precisely, вАЬthese things.вАЭ
n1801971: tn The word is вАЬiniquities,вАЭ but here as elsewhere it should receive the classification of the punishment for iniquity (a category of meaning that developed from a metonymy of effect).
n1801972: tc The last word is problematic because of the textual variants in the Hebrew. In place of вАП„©„Б÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (shaddin, вАЬjudgmentвАЭ) some have proposed вАП„©„Б÷Ј„У÷Љ÷Ј„ЩвАО (shadday, вАЬAlmightyвАЭ) and read it вАЬthat you may know the AlmightyвАЭ (Ewald, Wright). Some have read it вАП„Щ÷µ„©„Б „У÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО (yesh dayyan, вАЬthere is a judge,вАЭ Gray, Fohrer). Others defend the traditional view, arguing that the вАП„©„БвАО (shin) is the abbreviated relative particle on the word вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din, вАЬjudgmentвАЭ).
n180201: sn Zophar breaks in with an impassioned argument about the brevity and prosperity of the life of the wicked. But every statement that he makes is completely irrelevant to the case at hand. The speech has four sections: after a short preface (2-3) he portrays the brevity of the triumph of the wicked (4-11), retribution for sin (12-22), and GodвАЩs swift judgment (23-29). See further B. H. Kelly, вАЬTruth in Contradiction, A Study of Job 20 and 21,вАЭ Int 15 (1961): 147-56.
n180202: tn The ordinary meaning of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ы÷µ„ЯвАО (lakhen) is вАЬtherefore,вАЭ coming after an argument. But at the beginning of a speech it is an allusion to what follows.
n180203: tn The verb is вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto returnвАЭ), but in the Hiphil, вАЬbring me back,вАЭ i.e., prompt me to make another speech. The text makes good sense as it is, and there is no reason to change the reading to make a closer parallel with the second halfвАФindeed, the second part explains the first.
n180204: tn The word is normally taken from the root вАЬto hasten,вАЭ and rendered вАЬbecause of my haste within me.вАЭ But K&D 11:374 proposed another root, and similarly, but closer to the text, E. Dhorme (Job, 289-90) found an Arabic word with the meaning вАЬfeeling, sensation.вАЭ He argues that from this idea developed the meanings in the cognates of вАЬthoughtsвАЭ as well. Similarly, Gordis translates it вАЬmy feeling pain.вАЭ See also Eccl 2:25.
n180205: tn There is no indication that this clause is to be subordinated to the next, other than the logical connection, and the use of the вАП„ХвАО (vav) in the second half.
n180207: tn The phrase actually has вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷Ј „Ю÷і„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„†÷Є„™÷і„ЩвАО (ruakh mibbinati, вАЬa spirit/wind/breath/impulse from my understandingвАЭ). Some translate it вАЬout of my understanding a spirit answers me.вАЭ The idea is not that difficult, and so the many proposals to rewrite the text can be rejected. The spirit of his understanding prompts the reply.
n180208: tn To take this verb as a simple Qal and read it вАЬanswers me,вАЭ does not provide a clear idea. The form can just as easily be taken as a Hiphil, with the sense вАЬcauses me to answer.вАЭ It is Zophar who will вАЬreturnвАЭ and who will вАЬanswer.вАЭ
n180209: tn The MT has вАЬDo you not know?вАЭ The question can be interpreted as a rhetorical question affirming that Job must know this. The question serves to express the conviction that the contents are well-known to the audience (see GKC 474 ¬І150.e).
n1802010: tn Heb вАЬfrom the putting of man on earth.вАЭ The infinitive is the object of the preposition, which is here temporal. If вАЬmanвАЭ is taken as the subjective genitive, then the verb would be given a passive translation. Here вАЬmanвАЭ is a generic, referring to вАЬmankindвАЭ or вАЬthe human race.вАЭ
n1802011: tn The expression in the text is вАЬquite near.вАЭ This indicates that it is easily attained, and that its end is near.
n1802012: tn For the discussion of вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷µ„£вАО (khanef, вАЬgodlessвАЭ) see Job 8:13.
n1802013: tn The phrase is вАЬuntil a moment,вАЭ meaning it is short-lived. But see J. Barr, вАЬHebrew  њad, especially at Job 1:18 and Neh 7:3, вАЭ JSS 27 (1982): 177-88.
n1802014: tn The word вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„РвАО (si Њ) has been connected with the verb вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto lift upвАЭ), and so interpreted here as вАЬpride.вАЭ The form is parallel to вАЬheadвАЭ in the next part, and so here it refers to his stature, the part that rises up and is crowned. But the verse does describe the pride of such a person, with his head in the heavens.
n1802015: tn There have been attempts to change the word here to вАЬlike a whirlwind,вАЭ or something similar. But many argue that there is no reason to remove a coarse expression from Zophar.
n1802016: tn Heb вАЬand they do not find him.вАЭ The verb has no expressed subject, and so here is equivalent to a passive. The clause itself is taken adverbially in the sentence.
n1802017: tn Heb вАЬthe eye that had seen him.вАЭ Here a part of the person (the eye, the instrument of vision) is put by metonymy for the entire person.
n1802018: tn The early versions confused the root of this verb, taking it from вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Ј„•вАО (ratsats, вАЬmistreatвАЭ) and not from вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Є„ФвАО (ratsah, вАЬbe please withвАЭ). So it was taken to mean, вАЬLet inferiors destroy his children.вАЭ But the verb is вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Є„ФвАО (ratsah). This has been taken to mean вАЬhis sons will seek the favor of the poor.вАЭ This would mean that they would be reduced to poverty and need help from even the poor. Some commentators see this as another root вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Є„ФвАО (ratsah) meaning вАЬto compensate; to restoreвАЭ wealth their father had gained by impoverishing others. This fits the parallelism well, but not the whole context that well.
n1802019: tn Some commentators are surprised to see вАЬhis handsвАЭ here, thinking the passage talks about his death. Budde changed it to вАЬhis children,вАЭ by altering one letter. R. Gordis argued that вАЬhandвАЭ can mean offspring, and so translated it that way without changing anything in the text (вАЬA note on YAD,вАЭ JBL 62 [1943]: 343).
n1802020: tn вАЬBonesвАЭ is often used metonymically for the whole person, the bones being the framework, meaning everything inside, as well as the body itself.
n1802021: sn This line means that he dies prematurelyвАФat the height of his youthful vigor.
n1802022: tn The conjunction вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) introduces clauses that are conditional or concessive. With the imperfect verb in the protasis it indicates what is possible in the present or future. See GKC 496 ¬І159.q).
n1802023: sn The wicked person holds on to evil as long as he can, savoring the taste or the pleasure of it.
n1802024: tn Heb вАЬin the middle of his palate.вАЭ
n1802025: tn The perfect verb in the apodosis might express the suddenness of the change (see S. R. Driver, Tenses in Hebrew, 204), or it might be a constative perfect looking at the action as a whole without reference to inception, progress, or completion (see IBHS 480-81 ¬І30.1d). The Niphal perfect simply means вАЬis turnedвАЭ or вАЬturnsвАЭ; вАЬsourвАЭ is supplied in the translation to clarify what is meant.
n1802026: tn The word is вАЬin his loinsвАЭ or вАЬwithin him.вАЭ Some translate more specifically вАЬbowels.вАЭ
n1802027: sn Some commentators suggest that the ancients believed that serpents secreted poison in the gall bladder, or that the poison came from the gall bladder of serpents. In any case, there is poison (from the root вАЬbitterвАЭ) in the system of the wicked person; it may simply be saying it is that type of poison.
n1802029: tn The choice of words is excellent. The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (yarash) means either вАЬto inheritвАЭ or вАЬto disinherit; to dispossess.вАЭ The context makes the figure clear that God is administering the emetic to make the wicked throw up the wealth (thus, вАЬGod will make him throw it outвА¶вАЭ), but since wealth is the subject there is a disinheritance meant here.
n1802030: tn The word is a homonym for the word for вАЬhead,вАЭ which has led to some confusion in the early versions.
n1802031: sn To take the possessions of another person is hereby compared to sucking poison from a serpentвАФit will kill eventually.
n1802033: tn Some have thought this verse is a gloss on v. 14 and should be deleted. But the word for вАЬviperвАЭ (вАП„Р÷ґ„§÷∞„Ґ÷ґ„ФвАО,  Њef њeh) is a rare word, occurring only here and in Isa 30:6; 59:5. It is unlikely that such a rare word would be used in a gloss. But the point is similar to v. 14вАФthe wealth that was greedily sucked in by the wicked proves to be their undoing. Either this is totally irrelevant to JobвАЩs case, a general discussion, or the man is raising questions about how Job got his wealth.
n1802034: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (pelaggot) simply means вАЬstreamsвАЭ or вАЬchannels.вАЭ Because the word is used elsewhere for вАЬstreams of oilвАЭ (cf. 29:6), which makes good parallelism here, some supply вАЬoilвАЭ (cf. NAB, NLT). But the second colon of the verse is probably in apposition to the first. The verb вАЬseeвАЭ followed by the preposition bet (which would mean вАЬto look on; to look overвАЭ) means вАЬto enjoy as a possession,вАЭ an activity of the victor.
n1802035: tn The construct nouns here have caused a certain amount of revision. It says вАЬrivers of, torrents of.вАЭ The first has been emended by Klostermann to вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷∞„Ф÷Є„®вАО (yitshar, вАЬoilвАЭ) and connected to the first colon. Older editors argued for a вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Є„®вАО (nahar) that meant вАЬoil,вАЭ but that was not convincing. On the other hand, there is support for having more than one construct together serving as apposition (see GKC 422 ¬І130.e). If the word вАЬstreamsвАЭ in the last colon is a construct, that would mean three of them, but that one need not be a construct. The reading would be вАЬHe will not see the streams, [that is] the rivers [which are] the torrents of honey and butter.вАЭ It is unusual, but workable.
n1802036: sn This word is often translated вАЬcurds.вАЭ It is curdled milk, possibly a type of butter.
n1802037: tn The idea is the fruit of his evil work. The word вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷Є„ҐвАО (yaga њ) occurs only here; it must mean ill-gotten gains. The verb is in 10:3.
n1802038: tn Heb вАЬand he does not swallow.вАЭ In the context this means вАЬconsumeвАЭ for his own pleasure and prosperity. The verbal clause is here taken adverbially.
n1802039: sn The expression is вАЬaccording to the wealth of his exchange.вАЭ This means he cannot enjoy whatever he gained in his business deals. Some mss have the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet), making the translation easier, but this is evidence of a scribal correction.
n1802040: tc The verb indicates that after he oppressed the poor he abandoned them to their fate. But there have been several attempts to improve on the text. Several have repointed the text to get a word parallel to вАЬhouse.вАЭ Ehrlich came up with вАП„Ґ÷є„Ц÷µ„СвАО ( њozev, вАЬmud hutвАЭ), Kissane had вАЬhovelвАЭ (similar to Neh 3:8). M. Dahood did the same (вАЬThe Root  њzb II in Job,вАЭ JBL 78 [1959]: 306-7). J. Reider came up with вАП„Ґ÷ґ„Ц÷ґ„СвАО ( њezev, the вАЬleavingsвАЭ), what the rich were to leave for the poor (вАЬContributions to the Scriptural text,вАЭ HUCA 24 [1952/53]: 103-6). But an additional root вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО ( њazav) is questionable. And while the text as it stands is general and not very striking, there is absolutely nothing wrong with it. Dhorme reverses the letters to gain вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷є„ЦвАО (be њoz, вАЬwith force [or violence]вАЭ).
n1802041: tn The last clause says, вАЬand he did not build it.вАЭ This can be understood in an adverbial sense, supplying the relative pronoun to the translation.
n1802042: tn Heb вАЬbelly,вАЭ which represents his cravings, his desires and appetites. The вАЬsatisfactionвАЭ is actually the word for вАЬquiet; peace; calmness; ease.вАЭ He was driven by greedy desires, or he felt and displayed an insatiable greed.
n1802043: tn The verb is the passive participle of the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО (khamad) which is one of the words for вАЬcovet; desire.вАЭ This person is controlled by his desires; there is no escape. He is a slave.
n1802044: tn The verb is difficult to translate in this line. It basically means вАЬto cause to escape; to rescue.вАЭ Some translate this verb as вАЬit is impossible to escapeвАЭ; this may work, but is uncertain. Others translate the verb in the sense of saving something else: N. Sarna says, вАЬOf his most cherished possessions he shall save nothingвАЭ (вАЬThe Interchange of the Preposition bet and min in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ JBL 78 [1959]: 315-16). The RSV has вАЬhe will save nothing in which he delightsвАЭ; NIV has вАЬhe cannot save himself by his treasure.вАЭ
n1802045: tn Heb вАЬfor his eating,вАЭ which is frequently rendered вАЬfor his gluttony.вАЭ It refers, of course, to all the desires he has to take things from other people.
n1802046: sn The point throughout is that insatiable greed and ruthless plundering to satisfy it will be recompensed with utter and complete loss.
n1802047: tn The word вАП„©„В÷Є„§÷Ј„ІвАО (safaq) occurs only here; it means вАЬsufficiency; wealth; abundance (see D. W. Thomas, вАЬThe Text of Jesaia 2:6 and the Word вАП„©„В„§„ІвАО,вАЭ ZAW 75 [1963]: 88-90).
n1802048: tn Heb вАЬthere is straightness for him.вАЭ The root вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (tsarar) means вАЬto be narrowed in straits, to be in a bind.вАЭ The word here would have the idea of pressure, stress, trouble. One could say he is in a bind.
n1802049: tn Heb вАЬevery hand of trouble comes to him.вАЭ The pointing of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷µ„ЬвАО ( њamel) indicates it would refer to one who brings trouble; LXX and Latin read an abstract noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Є„ЬвАО ( њamal, вАЬtroubleвАЭ) here.
n1802050: tn D. J. A. Clines observes that to do justice to the three jussives in the verse, one would have to translate вАЬMay it be, to fill his belly to the full, that God should sendвА¶and rainвАЭ (Job [WBC], 477). The jussive form of the verb at the beginning of the verse could also simply introduce a protasis of a conditional clause (see GKC 323 ¬І109.h, i). This would mean, вАЬif he [God] is about to fill his [the wickedвАЩs] belly to the full, he will sendвА¶.вАЭ The NIV reads вАЬwhen he has filled his belly.вАЭ These fit better, because the context is talking about the wicked in his evil pursuit being cut down.
n1802051: tn вАЬGodвАЭ is understood as the subject of the judgment.
n1802053: tn Heb вАЬrain down upon him, on his flesh.вАЭ Dhorme changes вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷µ„Щ„Ю„Х÷євАО ( њalemo, вАЬupon himвАЭ) to вАЬhis arrowsвАЭ; he translates the line as вАЬhe rains his arrows upon his flesh.вАЭ The word вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Ь÷∞„Ч„Х÷Љ„Ю„Х÷євАО (bilkhumo, вАЬhis fleshвАЭ) has been given a wide variety of translations: вАЬas his food,вАЭ вАЬon his flesh,вАЭ вАЬupon him, his anger,вАЭ or вАЬmissiles or weapons of war.вАЭ
n1802054: tn Heb вАЬa bronze bow pierces him.вАЭ The words вАЬan arrow fromвАЭ are implied and are supplied in the translation; cf. вАЬpulls it outвАЭ in the following verse.
n1802055: tn The MT has вАЬhe draws out [or as a passive, вАЬit is drawn out/forthвАЭ] and comes [or goes] out of his back.вАЭ For the first verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО (shalaf, вАЬpull, drawвАЭ), many commentators follow the LXX and use вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shelakh, вАЬa spearвАЭ). It then reads вАЬand a shaft comes out of his back, a sword flash comes out of his liver.вАЭ But the verse could also be a continuation of the preceding.
n1802057: tn Heb вАЬall darkness is hidden for his laid up things.вАЭ вАЬAll darknessвАЭ refers to the misfortunes and afflictions that await. The verb вАЬhiddenвАЭ means вАЬis destined for.вАЭ
n1802058: tn Heb вАЬnot blown upon,вАЭ i.e., not kindled by man. But G. R. Driver reads вАЬunquenchedвАЭ (вАЬHebrew notes on the вАШWisdom of Jesus Ben SirachвАЩ,вАЭ JBL 53 [1934]: 289).
n1802059: tn For the word вАП„Р÷і„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷євАО ( Њimro) some propose reading вАЬhis appointment,вАЭ and the others, вАЬhis word.вАЭ Driver shows that вАЬthe heritage of his appointmentвАЭ means вАЬhis appointed heritageвАЭ (see GKC 440 ¬І135.n).
n180211: sn In this chapter Job actually answers the ideas of all three of his friends. Here Job finds the flaw in their argumentвАФhe can point to wicked people who prosper. But whereas in the last speech, when he looked on his suffering from the perspective of his innocence, he found great faith and hope, in this chapter when he surveys the divine government of the world, he sinks to despair. The speech can be divided into five parts: he appeals for a hearing (2-6), he points out the prosperity of the wicked (7-16), he wonders exactly when the godless suffer (17-22), he shows how death levels everything (23-26), and he reveals how experience contradicts his friendsвАЩ argument (27-34).
n180212: tn The intensity of the appeal is again expressed by the imperative followed by the infinitive absolute for emphasis. See note on вАЬlisten carefullyвАЭ in 13:17.
n180213: tc The LXX negates the sentence, вАЬthat I may not have this consolation from you.вАЭ
n180214: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„†÷∞„Ч„Х÷Љ„Ю÷є„™÷µ„Щ„Ы÷ґ„ЭвАО (tankhumotekhem) is literally вАЬyour consolations,вАЭ the suffix being a subjective genitive. The friends had thought they were offering Job consolation (Job 15:11), but the consolation he wants from them is that they listen to him and respond accordingly.
n180215: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ) means вАЬto lift up; to raise up,вАЭ but in this context it means вАЬto endure; to tolerateвАЭ (see Job 7:21).
n180216: tn The conjunction and the independent personal pronoun draw emphatic attention to the subject of the verb: вАЬand I on my part will speak.вАЭ
n180217: tn The adverbial clauses are constructed of the preposition вАЬafterвАЭ and the Piel infinitive construct with the subjective genitive suffix: вАЬmy speaking,вАЭ or вАЬI speak.вАЭ
n180218: tn The verb is the imperfect of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ТвАО (la њag). The Hiphil has the same basic sense as the Qal, вАЬto mock; to deride.вАЭ The imperfect here would be modal, expressing permission. The verb is in the singular, suggesting that Job is addressing Zophar; however, most of the versions put it into the plural. Note the singular in 16:3 between the plural in 16:1 and 16:4.
n180219: tn The addition of the independent pronoun at the beginning of the sentence (вАЬIs it I / against a man / my complaintвАЭ) strengthens the pronominal suffix on вАЬcomplaintвАЭ (see GKC 438 ¬І135.f).
n1802110: sn The point seems to be that if his complaint were merely against men he might expect sympathy from other men, but no one dares offer him sympathy when his complaint is against God. So he will give free expression to his spirit (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 147).
n1802112: tn Heb вАЬwhy should my spirit/breath not be shortвАЭ (see Num 21:4; Judg 16:16).
n1802113: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„†„Х÷ЉвАО (penu) is from the verb вАЬto turn,вАЭ related to the word for вАЬface.вАЭ In calling for them to turn toward him, he is calling for them to look at him. But here it may be more in the sense of their attention rather than just a looking at him.
n1802114: tn The idiom is вАЬput a hand over a mouth,вАЭ the natural gesture for keeping silent and listening (cf. Job 29:9; 40:4; Mic 7:16).
n1802115: tn The verb is вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (zakhar, вАЬto rememberвАЭ). Here it has the sense of вАЬto keep in memory; to meditate; to think upon.вАЭ
n1802116: tn The main clause is introduced here by the conjunction, following the adverbial clause of time.
n1802117: tn Some commentators take вАЬshudderвАЭ to be the subject of the verb, вАЬa shudder seizes my body.вАЭ But the word is feminine (and see the usage, especially in Job 9:6 and 18:20). It is the subject in Isa 21:4; Ps 55:6; and Ezek 7:18.
n1802118: sn A. B. Davidson (Job, 154) clarifies that JobвАЩs question is of a universal scope. In the government of God, why do the wicked exist at all? The verb could be translated вАЬcontinue to live.вАЭ
n1802119: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„™÷Ј„ІвАО ( њataq) means вАЬto move; to proceed; to advance.вАЭ Here it is вАЬto advance in yearsвАЭ or вАЬto grow old.вАЭ This clause could serve as an independent clause, a separate sentence, but it more likely continues the question of the first colon and is parallel to the verb вАЬlive.вАЭ
n1802121: tn The text uses вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„Щ„Ф÷ґ„Э „Ґ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (lifnehem  њimmam, вАЬbefore them, with themвАЭ). Many editors think that these were alternative readings, and so omit one or the other. Dhorme moved вАП„Ґ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО ( њimmam) to the second half of the verse and emended it to read вАП„Ґ÷є„Ю÷∞„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њomedim, вАЬabideвАЭ). Kissane and Gordis changed only the vowels and came up with вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО ( њammam, вАЬtheir kinfolkвАЭ). But Gordis thinks the presence of both of them in the line is evidence of a conflated reading (p. 229).
n1802122: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeace, safetyвАЭ) is here a substantive after a plural subject (see GKC 452 ¬І141.c, n. 3).
n1802123: tn The form вАП„Ю÷і„§÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (mippakhad) is translated вАЬwithout fear,вАЭ literally вАЬfrom fearвАЭ; the preposition is similar to the alpha privative in Greek. The word вАЬfear, dreadвАЭ means nothing that causes fear or dreadвАФthey are peaceful, secure. See GKC 382 ¬І119.w.
n1802124: tn Heb вАЬno rod of God.вАЭ The words вАЬpunishment fromвАЭ have been supplied in the translation to make the metaphor understandable for the modern reader by stating the purpose of the rod.
n1802125: sn In 9:34 Job was complaining that there was no umpire to remove GodвАЩs rod from him, but here he observes no such rod is on the wicked.
n1802126: tn Heb вАЬhis bull,вАЭ but it is meant to signify the bulls of the wicked.
n1802127: tn The verb used here means вАЬto impregnate,вАЭ and not to be confused with the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО ( њavar, вАЬto pass overвАЭ).
n1802128: tn The use of the verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ga њar) in this place is interesting. It means вАЬto rebuke; to abhor; to loathe.вАЭ In the causative stem it means вАЬto occasion impurityвАЭ or вАЬto reject as loathsome.вАЭ The rabbinic interpretation is that it does not emit semen in vain, and so the meaning is it does not fail to breed (see E. Dhorme, Job, 311; R. Gordis, Job, 229).
n1802129: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shalakh) means вАЬto send forth,вАЭ but in the Piel вАЬto release; to allow to run free.вАЭ The picture of children frolicking in the fields and singing and dancing is symbolic of peaceful, prosperous times.
n1802130: tn The verb is simply вАЬthey take up [or lift up],вАЭ but the understood object is вАЬtheir voices,вАЭ and so it means вАЬthey sing.вАЭ
n1802131: tc The Kethib has вАЬthey wear outвАЭ but the Qere and the versions have вАП„Щ÷∞„Ы÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yekhallu, вАЬbring to an endвАЭ). The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah) means вАЬto finish; to complete,вАЭ and here with the object вАЬtheir days,вАЭ it means that they bring their life to a (successful) conclusion. Both readings are acceptable in the context, with very little difference in the overall meaning (which according to Gordis is proof the Qere does not always correct the Kethib).
n1802132: tc The MT has вАП„Щ÷µ„Ч÷Є„™÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yekhattu, вАЬthey are frightened [or broken]вАЭ), taking the verb from вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„™вАО (khatat, вАЬbe terrifiedвАЭ). But most would slightly repoint it to вАП„Щ÷µ„Ч÷Є„™„Х÷ЉвАО (yekhatu), an Aramaism, вАЬthey go down,вАЭ from вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nakhat, вАЬgo downвАЭ). See Job 17:16.
n1802133: tn The word вАП„®÷ґ„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (rega њ) has been interpreted as вАЬin a momentвАЭ or вАЬin peaceвАЭ (on the basis of Arabic raja њa, вАЬreturn to restвАЭ). Gordis thinks this is a case of talhinвАФ both meanings present in the mind of the writer.
n1802134: sn Contrast Ps 25:4, which affirms that walking in GodвАЩs ways means to obey GodвАЩs willвАФthe Torah.
n1802135: tn The interrogative clause is followed by ki, similar to Exod 5:2, вАЬWho is Yahweh, that I should obey him?вАЭ
n1802136: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (paga њ) means вАЬto encounter; to meet,вАЭ but also вАЬto meet with request; to intercede; to interpose.вАЭ The latter meaning is a derived meaning by usage.
n1802137: tn The verse is not present in the LXX. It may be that it was considered too blasphemous and therefore omitted.
n1802138:
tn Heb вАЬis not in their hand.вАЭ
sn The implication of this statement is that their well-being is from God, which is the problem Job is raising in the chapter. A number of commentators make it a question, interpreting it to mean that the wicked enjoy prosperity as if it is their right. Some emend the text to say вАЬhis handsвАЭвАФGordis reads it, вАЬIndeed, our prosperity is not in his hands.вАЭ
n1802139: sn Even though their life seems so good in contrast to his own plight, Job cannot and will not embrace their principlesвАФвАЬfar be from me their counsel.вАЭ
n1802140: tn The interrogative вАЬHow oftenвАЭ occurs only with the first colon; it is supplied for smoother reading in the next two.
n1802141: tn The pronominal suffix is objective; it re-enforces the object of the preposition, вАЬupon them.вАЭ The verb in the clause is вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ) followed by вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal), вАЬcome upon [or against],вАЭ may be interpreted as meaning attack or strike.
n1802142: tn вАП„Ч÷≤„С÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khavalim) can mean вАЬropesвАЭ or вАЬcords,вАЭ but that would not go with the verb вАЬapportionвАЭ in this line. The meaning of вАЬpangs (as in вАЬbirth-pangsвАЭ) seems to fit best here. The wider meaning would be вАЬphysical agony.вАЭ
n1802143: tn The phrase вАЬto themвАЭ is understood and thus is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1802144: tn To retain the sense that the wicked do not suffer as others, this verse must either be taken as a question or a continuation of the question in v. 17.
n1802145: tn The verb used actually means вАЬrob.вАЭ It is appropriate to the image of a whirlwind suddenly taking away the wisp of straw.
n1802146: tn These words are supplied. The verse records an idea that Job suspected they might have, namely, that if the wicked die well God will make their children pay for their sins (see Job 5:4; 20:10; as well as Exod 20:5).
n1802147: tn The text simply has вАП„Р„Х÷є„†„Х÷євАО ( Њono, вАЬhis iniquityвАЭ), but by usage, вАЬthe punishment for the iniquity.вАЭ
n1802149: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (shalam) in the Piel has the meaning of restoring things to normal, making whole, and so reward, repay (if for sins), or recompense in general.
n1802150: tn The text simply has вАЬlet him repay [to] him.вАЭ
n1802151: tn The imperfect verb after the jussive carries the meaning of a purpose clause, and so taken as a final imperfect: вАЬin order that he may be humbled.вАЭ
n1802152: tn The common verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) means вАЬto know.вАЭ Among homophonous roots DCH includes вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО II meaning вАЬbe quiet, at rest; be submissive, humbledвАЭ (cf. Prov 5:6; Isa 45:4; Jer 14:18; Hos 9:7).
n1802153: tc This word occurs only here. The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„УвАО (kid) was connected to Arabic kaid, вАЬfraud, trickery,вАЭ or вАЬwarfare.вАЭ The word is emended by the commentators to other ideas, such as вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„УвАО (pid, вАЬ[his] calamityвАЭ). Dahood and others alter it to вАЬcupвАЭ; Wright to вАЬweapons.вАЭ A. F. L. Beeston argues for a meaning вАЬcondemnationвАЭ for the MT form, and so makes no change in the text (Mus 67 [1954]: 315-16). If the connection to Arabic вАЬwarfareвАЭ is sustained, or if such explanations of the existing MT can be sustained, then the text need not be emended. In any case, the sense of the line is clear.
n1802154: tn Heb вАЬhis desire.вАЭ The meaning is that after he is gone he does not care about what happens to his household (вАЬhouseвАЭ meaning вАЬfamilyвАЭ here).
n1802155: tn Heb вАЬafter him,вАЭ but clearly the meaning is вАЬafter he is gone.вАЭ
n1802156: tc The rare word вАП„Ч÷ї„¶÷Љ÷Є„¶„Х÷ЉвАО (khutsatsu) is probably a cognate of hassa in Arabic, meaning вАЬto cut off.вАЭ There is also an Akkadian word вАЬto cut in twoвАЭ and вАЬto break.вАЭ These fit the context here rather well. The other Hebrew words that are connected to the root вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷Ј„•вАО (khatsats) do not offer any help.
n1802157: tn The imperfect verb in this question should be given the modal nuance of potential imperfect. The question is rhetoricalвАФit is affirming that no one can teach God.
n1802158: tn The clause begins with the disjunctive vav (вАП„ХвАО) and the pronoun, вАЬand he.вАЭ This is to be subordinated as a circumstantial clause. See GKC 456 ¬І142.d.
n1802159: tc The Hebrew has вАП„®÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ramim), a plural masculine participle of вАП„®„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (rum, вАЬto be high; to be exaltedвАЭ). This is probably a reference to the angels. But M. Dahood restores an older interpretation that it refers to вАЬthe Most HighвАЭ (вАЬSome Northwest Semitic words in Job,вАЭBib 38 [1957]: 316-17). He would take the word as a singular form with an enclitic mem (вАП„ЭвАО). He reads the verse, вАЬwill he judge the Most High?вАЭ
n1802160: tn The line has вАЬin the bone of his perfection.вАЭ The word вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„ЭвАО ( Њetsem), which means вАЬbone,вАЭ is used pronominally to express вАЬthe same, veryвАЭ; here it is вАЬin the very fullness of his strengthвАЭ (see GKC 449 ¬І139.g). The abstract вАП„™÷Љ÷є„ЭвАО (tom) is used here in the sense of physical perfection and strengths.
n1802161: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЯвАО ( њatan) has the precise meaning of вАЬpress olives.вАЭ But because here it says вАЬfull of milk,вАЭ the derived meaning for the noun has been made to mean вАЬbreastsвАЭ or вАЬpailsвАЭ (although in later Hebrew this word occursвАФbut with olives, not with milk). Dhorme takes it to refer to вАЬhis sides,вАЭ and repoints the word for вАЬmilkвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Є„СвАО, khalav) to get вАЬfatвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„СвАО, khelev)вАФвАЬhis sides are full of fat,вАЭ a rendering followed by NASB. However, this weakens the parallelism.
n1802162: tn This interpretation, adopted by several commentaries and modern translations (cf. NAB, NIV), is a general rendering to capture the sense of the line.
n1802163: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (shaqah) means вАЬto waterвАЭ and here вАЬto be watered thoroughly.вАЭ The picture in the line is that of health and vigor.
n1802164: tn The expression вАЬthis (v. 23)вА¶and thisвАЭ (v. 25) means вАЬoneвА¶the other.вАЭ
n1802165: tn The text literally has вАЬand this [man] dies in soul of bitterness.вАЭ Some simply reverse it and translate вАЬin the bitterness of soul.вАЭ The genitive вАЬbitternessвАЭ may be an attribute adjective, вАЬwith a bitter soul.вАЭ
n1802166: tn Heb вАЬeaten what is good.вАЭ It means he died without having enjoyed the good life.
n1802167: tn The word is вАЬyour thoughts.вАЭ The word for вАЬthoughtsвАЭ (from вАП„Ч÷Є„¶÷Ј„СвАО [khatsav, вАЬto think; to reckon; to planвАЭ]) has more to do with their intent than their general thoughts. He knows that when they talked about the fate of the wicked they really were talking about him.
n1802168: tn For the meaning of this word, and its root вАП„Ц÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (zamam), see Job 17:11. It usually means the вАЬplansвАЭ or вАЬschemesвАЭ that are concocted against someone.
n1802169: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 321) distinguishes the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„°вАО (khamas) from the noun for вАЬviolence.вАЭ He proposes a meaning of вАЬthink, imagineвАЭ: вАЬand the ideas you imagined about me.вАЭ
n1802170: sn The question implies the answer will be вАЬvanishedвАЭ or вАЬgone.вАЭ
n1802171: tn Heb вАЬAnd where is the tent, the dwellings of the wicked.вАЭ The word вАЬdwellings of the wickedвАЭ is in apposition to вАЬtent.вАЭ A relative pronoun must be supplied in the translation.
n1802172:
tc The LXX reads, вАЬAsk those who go by the way, and do not disown their signs.вАЭ
tn The idea is that the merchants who travel widely will talk about what they have seen and heard. These travelers give a different account of the wicked; they tell how he is spared. E. Dhorme (Job, 322) interprets вАЬsignsвАЭ concretely: вАЬTheir custom was to write their names and their thoughts somewhere at the main cross-roads. The main roads of Sinai are dotted with these scribblings made by such passers of a day.вАЭ
n1802173: tn The verb means вАЬto be led forth.вАЭ To be вАЬled forth in the day of troubleвАЭ means to be delivered.
n1802174: tn The expression вАЬand he has doneвАЭ is taken here to mean вАЬwhat he has done.вАЭ
n1802175: tn Heb вАЬWho declares his way to his face? // Who repays him for what he has done?вАЭ These rhetorical questions, which expect a negative answer (вАЬNo one!вАЭ) have been translated as indicative statements to bring out their force clearly.
n1802176: tn The verb says вАЬhe will watch.вАЭ The subject is unspecified, so the translation is passive.
n1802177: tn The Hebrew word refers to the tumulus, the burial mound that is erected on the spot where the person is buried.
n1802178: tn The clods are those that are used to make a mound over the body. And, for a burial in the valley, see Deut 34:6. The verse here sees him as participating in his funeral and enjoying it. Nothing seems to go wrong with the wicked.
n1802179: tn The word вАП„Ю÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (ma њal) is used for вАЬtreachery; deception; fraud.вАЭ Here Job is saying that their way of interpreting reality is dangerously unfaithful.
n180221: sn The third and final cycle of speeches now begins with EliphazвАЩ final speech. Eliphaz will here underscore the argument that manвАЩs ills are brought about by sin; he will then deduce from JobвАЩs sufferings the sins he must have committed and the sinful attitude he has about God. The speech has four parts: JobвАЩs suffering is proof of his sin (2-5), JobвАЩs sufferings demonstrate the kinds of sin Job committed (6-11), JobвАЩs attitude about God (12-20), and the final appeal and promise to Job (21-30).
n180222: tn Some do not take this to be parallel to the first colon, taking this line as a statement, but the parallel expressions here suggest the question is repeated.
n180223: tn The word вАП„Ч÷µ„§÷ґ„•вАО (khefets) in this passage has the nuance of вАЬspecial benefit; favor.вАЭ It does not just express the desire for something or the interest in it, but the profit one derives from it.
n180224: tn The verb вАП„™÷Ј„™÷Љ÷µ„ЭвАО (tattem) is the Hiphil imperfect of вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (tamam, вАЬbe complete, finishedвАЭ), following the Aramaic form of the geminate verb with a doubling of the first letter.
n180225: tn The word вАЬyour fearвАЭ or вАЬyour pietyвАЭ refers to JobвАЩs reverenceвАФit is his fear of God (thus a subjective genitive). When вАЬfearвАЭ is used of religion, it includes faith and adoration on the positive side, fear and obedience on the negative.
n180226: sn Of course the point is that God does not charge Job because he is righteous; the point is he must be unrighteous.
n180227: tn The adjective вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (rabbah) normally has the idea of вАЬgreatвАЭ in quantity (вАЬabundant,вАЭ ESV) rather than вАЬgreatвАЭ in quality.
n180228: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО (khaval) means вАЬto take pledges.вАЭ In this verse Eliphaz says that Job not only took as pledge things the poor need, like clothing, but he did it for no reason.
n180229: tn The вАЬnakedвАЭ here refers to people who are poorly clothed. Otherwise, a reading like the NIV would be necessary: вАЬyou stripped the clothesвА¶[leaving them] naked.вАЭ So either he made them naked by stripping their garments off, or they were already in rags.
n1802210: tn The term вАП„Ґ÷Є„Щ÷µ„£вАО ( њayef) can be translated вАЬweary,вАЭ вАЬfaint,вАЭ вАЬexhausted,вАЭ or вАЬtired.вАЭ Here it may refer to the fainting because of thirstвАФthat would make a good parallel to the second part.
n1802211: tn The idiom is вАЬa man of armвАЭ (= вАЬpowerfulвАЭ; see Ps 10:15). This is in comparison to the next line, вАЬman of faceвАЭ (= вАЬdignity; high rankвАЭ; see Isa 3:5).
n1802212: tn Heb вАЬand a man of arm, to whom [was] land.вАЭ The line is in contrast to the preceding one, and so the vav here introduces a concessive clause.
n1802213: tn The expression is unusual: вАЬthe one lifted up of face.вАЭ This is the вАЬhonored one,вАЭ the one to whom the dignity will be given.
n1802214: tn Many commentators simply delete the verse or move it elsewhere. Most take it as a general reference to Job, perhaps in apposition to the preceding verse.
n1802215: tn The вАЬarms of the orphansвАЭ are their helps or rights on which they depended for support.
n1802216: tn The verb in the text is Pual: вАП„Щ÷∞„У÷ї„Ы÷Љ÷Є„РвАО (yedukka Њ, вАЬwas [were] crushedвАЭ). GKC 388 ¬І121.b would explain вАЬarmsвАЭ as the complement of a passive imperfect. But if that is too difficult, then a change to Piel imperfect, second person, will solve the difficulty. In its favor is the parallelism, the use of the second person all throughout the section, and the reading in all the versions. The versions may have simply assumed the easier reading, however.
n1802217: tn Heb вАЬor dark you cannot see.вАЭ Some commentators and the RSV follow the LXX in reading вАП„Р„Х÷євАО ( Њo, вАЬorвАЭ) as вАП„Р„Х÷є„®вАО ( Њor, вАЬlightвАЭ) and translate it вАЬThe light has become darkвАЭ or вАЬYour light has become dark.вАЭ A. B. Davidson suggests the reading вАЬOr seest thou not the darkness.вАЭ This would mean Job does not understand the true meaning of the darkness and the calamities.
n1802218: tn The word вАП„©„Б÷і„§÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (shif њat) means вАЬmultitude of.вАЭ It is used of men, camels, horses, and here of waters in the heavens.
n1802219: tn This reading preserves the text as it is. The nouns вАЬhighвАЭ and вАЬheavensвАЭ would then be taken as adverbial accusatives of place (see GKC 373-74 ¬І118.g).
n1802220: tn The parallel passage in Isa 40:26-27, as well as the context here, shows that the imperative is to be retained here. The LXX has вАЬhe sees.вАЭ
n1802222: sn Eliphaz is giving to Job the thoughts and words of the pagans, for they say, вАЬHow does God know, and is there knowledge in the Most High?вАЭ (see Pss 73:11; 94:11).
n1802223: tn Heb вАЬand he does not see.вАЭ The implied object is вАЬus.вАЭ
n1802224: sn The word is вАЬcircle; domeвАЭ; here it is the dome that covers the earth, beyond which God sits enthroned. A. B. Davidson (Job, 165) suggests вАЬon the arch of heavenвАЭ that covers the earth.
n1802225: sn The idea suggested here is that God is not only far off, but he is unconcerned as he strolls around heavenвАФthis is what Eliphaz says Job means.
n1802226: tn The вАЬold pathвАЭ here is the way of defiance to God. The text in these two verses is no doubt making reference to the flood in Genesis, one of the perennial examples of divine judgment.
n1802227: tn The word вАЬmenвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied to clarify the relative pronoun вАЬwho.вАЭ
n1802228: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ШвАО (qamat) basically means вАЬto seize; to tie together to make a bundle.вАЭ So the Pual will mean вАЬto be bundled away; to be carried off.вАЭ
n1802229: tn The clause has вАЬand [it was] not the time.вАЭ It may be used adverbially here.
n1802230: tn The word is вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„®вАО (nahar, вАЬriverвАЭ or вАЬcurrentвАЭ); it is taken here in its broadest sense of the waters on the earth that formed the current of the flood (Gen 7:6, 10).
n1802231: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ІвАО (yatsaq) means вАЬto pour out; to shed; to spill; to flow.вАЭ The Hophal means вАЬto be poured outвАЭ (as in Lev 21:10 and Ps 45:3).
n1802232: tn This word is then to be taken as an adverbial accusative of place. Another way to look at this verse is what A. B. Davidson (Job, 165) proposes вАЬwhose foundation was poured away and became a flood.вАЭ This would mean that that on which they stood sank away.
n1802233: tn The form in the text is вАЬto them.вАЭ The LXX and the Syriac versions have вАЬto us.вАЭ
n1802234: tn The pronoun is added for this emphasis; it has вАЬbut heвАЭ before the verb.
n1802236: tc The LXX has вАЬfrom him,вАЭ and this is followed by several commentators. But the MT is to be retained, for Eliphaz is recalling the words of Job. Verses 17 and 18 are deleted by a number of commentators as a gloss because they have many similarities to 21:14-16. But Eliphaz is recalling what Job said, in order to say that the prosperity to which Job alluded was only the prelude to a disaster he denied (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 156).
n1802237: tn The line is talking about the rejoicing of the righteous when judgment falls on the wicked. An object (вАЬdestructionвАЭ) has to be supplied here to clarify this (see Pss 52:6 [8]; 69:32 [33]; 107:42).
n1802238: sn In Ps 2:4 it was God who mocked the wicked by judging them.
n1802239: tc The word translated вАЬour enemiesвАЭ is found only here. The word means вАЬhostility,вАЭ but used here as a collective for those who are hostileвАФвАЬenemies.вАЭ Some commentators follow the LXX and read вАЬpossessions,вАЭ explaining its meaning and derivation in different ways. Gordis simply takes the word in the text and affirms that this is the meaning. On the other hand, to get this, E. Dhorme (Job, 336) repoints вАП„І÷і„Щ„Ю÷Є„†„Х÷ЉвАО (qimanu) of the MT to вАП„Щ÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (yequmam), arguing that вАП„Щ÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (yequm) means вАЬwhat exists [or has substance]вАЭ (although that is used of animals). He translates: вАЬhave not their possessions been destroyed.вАЭ
n1802240: tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЯвАО (sakhan) meant вАЬto be useful; to be profitableвАЭ in v. 2. Now, in the Hiphil it means вАЬto be accustomed toвАЭ or вАЬto have experience with.вАЭ Joined by the preposition вАЬwithвАЭ it means вАЬto be reconciled with him.вАЭ W. B. Bishai cites Arabic and Ugaritic words to support a meaning вАЬacquiesceвАЭ (вАЬNotes on hskn in Job 22:21, вАЭ JNES 20 [1961]: 258-59).
n1802241: tn Heb вАЬhimвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1802242: tn The two imperatives in this verse imply a relationship of succession and not consequence.
n1802243: tn The Hebrew word here is вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah), its only occurrence in the book of Job.
n1802244: tc M. Dahood has вАЬwrite his wordsвАЭ (вАЬMetaphor in Job 22:22, вАЭ Bib 47 [1966]: 108-9).
n1802245: tc The MT has вАЬyou will be built upвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО, tibbaneh). But the LXX has вАЬhumble yourselfвАЭ (reading вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„†÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО [te њanneh] apparently). Many commentators read this; Dahood has вАЬyou will be healed.вАЭ
n1802246:
tc The form is the imperative. Eliphaz is telling Job to get rid of his gold as evidence of his repentance. Many commentators think that this is too improbable for Eliphaz to have said, and that Job has lost everything anyway, and so they make proposals for the text. Most would follow Theodotion and the Syriac to read вАП„Х÷∞„©„Б÷Є„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (veshatta, вАЬand you will esteemвА¶.вАЭ). This would mean that he is promising Job restoration of his wealth.
tn Heb вАЬplace.вАЭ
n1802247: tn The word for вАЬgoldвАЭ is the rare вАП„С÷Љ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„®вАО (betser), which may be derived from a cognate of Arabic basara, вАЬto see; to examine.вАЭ If this is the case, the word here would refer to refined gold. The word also forms a fine wordplay with вАП„С÷∞„¶„Х÷Љ„®вАО (vetsur, вАЬin the rockвАЭ).
n1802248: tn The Hebrew text simply has вАЬOphir,вАЭ a metonymy for the gold that comes from there.
n1802249: tn The form for вАЬgoldвАЭ here is plural, which could be a plural of extension. The LXX and Latin versions have вАЬThe Almighty will be your helper against your enemies.вАЭ
n1802250: tn E. Dhorme (Job, 339) connects this word with an Arabic root meaning вАЬto be elevated, steep.вАЭ From that he gets вАЬheaps of silver.вАЭ
n1802251: tc This is the same verb as in Ps 37:4. G. R. Driver suggests the word comes from another root that means вАЬabandon oneself to, depend onвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 84).
n1802252: tn The words вАЬto himвАЭ are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied.
n1802253: tn The word is вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„®вАО (gazar, вАЬto cutвАЭ), in the sense of deciding a matter.
n1802254: tn There is no expressed subject here, and so the verb is taken as a passive voice again.
n1802255: tn The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷µ„Х÷Є„ФвАО (gevah) means вАЬloftiness; pride.вАЭ Here it simply says вАЬup,вАЭ or вАЬpride.вАЭ The rest is paraphrased. Of the many suggestions, the following provide a sampling: вАЬIt is because of prideвАЭ (ESV), вАЬhe abases prideвАЭ (H. H. Rowley); вАЬ[he abases] the lofty and the proudвАЭ (Beer); вАЬ[he abases] the word of prideвАЭ [Duhm]; вАЬ[he abases] the haughtiness of prideвАЭ [Fohrer and others]; вАЬ[he abases] the one who speaks proudlyвАЭ [Weiser]; вАЬ[he abases] the one who boasts in prideвАЭ [Kissane]; and вАЬGod [abases] prideвАЭ [Budde, Gray].
n1802256: tn Or вАЬhumbleвАЭ; Heb вАЬthe lowly of eyes.вАЭ
n1802257: tc The Hebrew has вАП„Р÷і„Щ÷Њ„†÷Є„І÷і„ЩвАО ( Њi naqi), which could be taken as вАЬisland of the innocentвАЭ (so Ibn-Ezra), or вАЬhim that is not innocentвАЭ (so Rashi). But some have changed вАП„Р÷і„ЩвАО ( Њi) to вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬthe innocent manвАЭ). Others differ: A. Guillaume links вАП„Р÷і„ЩвАО ( Њi) to Arabic  њayya вАЬwhosoever,вАЭ and so leaves the text alone. M. Dahood secures the same idea from Ugaritic, but reads it вАП„Р÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њe).
n1802258: tc The MT has вАЬhe will escape [or be delivered].вАЭ Theodotion has the second person, вАЬyou will be delivered.вАЭ
n180231: sn Job answers Eliphaz, but not until he introduces new ideas for his own case with God. His speech unfolds in three parts: JobвАЩs longing to meet God (23:2-7), the inaccessibility and power of God (23:8-17), the indifference of God (24:1-25).
n180232: tc The MT reads here вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (meri, вАЬrebelliousвАЭ). The word is related to the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (marah, вАЬto revoltвАЭ). Many commentators follow the Vulgate, Targum Job, and the Syriac to read вАП„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (mar, вАЬbitterвАЭ). The LXX offers no help here.
n180233: tc The MT (followed by the Vulgate and Targum) has вАЬmy hand is heavy on my groaning.вАЭ This would mean вАЬmy stroke is heavier than my groaningвАЭ (an improbable view from Targum Job). A better suggestion is that the meaning would be that Job tries to suppress his groans but the hand with which he suppresses them is too heavy (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 159). Budde, E. Dhorme, J. E. Hartley, and F. I. Andersen all maintain the MT as the more difficult reading. F. I. Andersen (Job [TOTC], 208) indicates that the вАП ÷і „ЩвАО(yod) suffix could be an example of an unusual third masculine singular. Both the LXX and the Syriac versions have вАЬhis hand,вАЭ and many modern commentators follow this, along with the present translation. In this case the referent of вАЬhisвАЭ would be God, whose hand is heavy upon Job in spite of JobвАЩs groaning.
n180234: tn The preposition can take this meaning; it could be also translated simply вАЬupon.вАЭ R. Gordis (Job, 260) reads the preposition вАЬmore than,вАЭ saying that Job had been defiant (he takes that view) but GodвАЩs hand had been far worse.
n180235: tn The optative here is again expressed with the verbal clause, вАЬwho will give [that] I knewвА¶.вАЭ
n180236:
tn The form in Hebrew is вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷ґ„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„Р÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (ve Њemtsa Њehu), simply вАЬand I will find him.вАЭ But in the optative clause this verb is subordinated to the preceding verb: вАЬO that I knew where [and] I might find him.вАЭ It is not unusual to have the perfect verb followed by the imperfect in such coordinate clauses (see GKC 386 ¬І120.e). This could also be translated making the second verb a complementary infinitive: вАЬknew how to find him.вАЭ
sn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 159) quotes Strahan without reference: вАЬIt is the chief distinction between Job and his friends that he desires to meet God and they do not.вАЭ
n180237: tn This verb also depends on вАП„Ю÷і„Щ÷Њ„Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (mi yitten, вАЬwho will giveвАЭ) of the first part, forming an additional clause in the wish formula.
n180238: tn Or вАЬhis place of judgment.вАЭ The word is from вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬto prepare; to arrangeвАЭ) in the Polel and the Hiphil conjugations. The noun refers to a prepared place, a throne, a seat, or a sanctuary. A. B. Davidson (Job, 169) and others take the word to mean вАЬjudgment seatвАЭ or вАЬtribunalвАЭ in this context.
n180239: tn The word вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat) is normally вАЬjudgment; decision.вАЭ But in these contexts it refers to the legal case that Job will bring before God. With the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh, вАЬto set in order; to lay outвАЭ) the whole image of drawing up a lawsuit is complete.
n1802310: tn Heb вАЬthe words he would answer me.вАЭ
n1802311: tn The verb is now вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) and not вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh, вАЬcontendвАЭ); вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) means вАЬto quarrel; to dispute; to contend,вАЭ often in a legal context. Here it is still part of JobвАЩs questioning about this hypothetical meetingвАФwould God contend with all his power?
n1802312: tn The verbal clause вАП„Щ÷Є„©„В÷і„Э „С÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (yasim bi) has been translated вАЬhe would pay [attention] to me.вАЭ Job is saying that God will not need all his powerвАФhe will only have to pay attention to JobвАЩs complaint. Job does not need the display of powerвАФhe just wants a hearing.
n1802313: tn The adverb вАЬthereвАЭ has the sense of вАЬthenвАЭвАФthere in the future.
n1802314: tn The form of the verb is the Niphal вАП„†„Х÷є„Ы÷Є„ЧвАО (nokhakh, вАЬargue, present a caseвАЭ). E. Dhorme (Job, 346) is troubled by this verbal form and so changes it and other things in the line to say, вАЬhe would observe the upright man who argues with him.вАЭ The Niphal is used for вАЬengaging discussion,вАЭ вАЬarguing a case,вАЭ and вАЬsettling a dispute.вАЭ
n1802315: sn The text has вАЬthe left hand,вАЭ the Semitic idiom for directions. One faces the rising sun, and so left is north, right is south.
n1802316: tc The form вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„©„В÷є„™„Х÷євАО (ba њasoto) would be the temporal clause using the infinitive construct with a pronoun (subject genitive). This would be вАЬwhen he works.вАЭ Several follow the Syriac with вАЬI seek him.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬ[when] he turns.вАЭ R. Gordis (Job, 261) notes that there is no need to emend the text; he shows a link to the Arabic cognate ghasa, вАЬto cover.вАЭ To him this is a perfect parallel to вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„Ш÷є„£вАО (ya њtof, вАЬcovers himselfвАЭ).
n1802317: tn The verb is the apocopated form of the imperfect. The object is supplied.
n1802318: tn The MT has вАЬhe turns,вАЭ but the Syriac and Vulgate have вАЬI turn.вАЭ
n1802319: tn The expression вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞ „Ґ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„У÷і„ЩвАО (derekh  њimmadi) means вАЬthe way with me,вАЭ i.e., вАЬthe way that I take.вАЭ The Syriac has вАЬmy way and my standing.вАЭ Several commentators prefer вАЬthe way of my standing,вАЭ meaning where to look for me. J. Reider offers вАЬthe way of my lifeвАЭ (вАЬSome notes to the text of the scriptures,вАЭ HUCA 3 [1926]: 115). Whatever the precise wording, Job knows that God can always find him.
n1802320: tn There is a perfect verb followed by an imperfect in this clause with the protasis and apodosis relationship (see GKC 493 ¬І159.b).
n1802323: tn The last clause, вАЬand I have not turned aside,вАЭ functions adverbially in the sentence. The form вАП„Р÷Є„ШвАО ( Њat) is a pausal form of вАП„Р÷Ј„™÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО ( Њatteh), the Hiphil of вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah, вАЬstretch outвАЭ).
n1802324: tc The form in the MT (вАП„Ю÷µ„Ч÷ї„І÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО, mekhuqqi) means вАЬmore than my portionвАЭ or вАЬmore than my law.вАЭ An expanded meaning results in вАЬmore than my necessary foodвАЭ (cf. Prov 30:8). HALOT 346 s.v. вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО 1 indicates that вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq) has the meaning of вАЬportionвАЭ and is here a reference to вАЬwhat is appointed for me.вАЭ The LXX and the Latin versions, along with many commentators, have вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ч÷µ„І÷і„ЩвАО (bekheqi, вАЬin my bosomвАЭ).
n1802325: tc The MT has вАЬBut he [is] in one.вАЭ Many add the word вАЬmindвАЭ to capture the point that God is resolute and unchanging. Some commentators find this too difficult, and so change the text from вАП„С÷∞„Р÷ґ„Ч÷Є„УвАО (ve Њekhad, here вАЬunchangeableвАЭ) to вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Є„®вАО (bakhar, вАЬhe has chosenвАЭ). The wording in the text is idiomatic and should be retained. R. Gordis (Job, 262) translates it вАЬhe is one, i.e., unchangeable, fixed, determined.вАЭ The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) is a bet essentiaeвАФвАЬand he [is] as one,вАЭ or вАЬhe is oneвАЭ (see GKC 379 ¬І119.i).
n1802328: tn The text has вАЬmy decree,вАЭ which means вАЬthe decree [plan] for/against me.вАЭ The suffix is objective, equivalent to a dative of disadvantage. The Syriac and the Vulgate actually have вАЬhis decree.вАЭ R. Gordis (Job, 262) suggests taking it in the same sense as in Job 14:5: вАЬmy limit.вАЭ.
n1802329:
tn Heb вАЬand many such [things] are with him.вАЭ
sn The text is saying that many similar situations are under GodвАЩs rule of the worldвАФhis plans are infinite.
n1802330: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷µ„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (herakh) means вАЬto be tenderвАЭ; in the Piel it would have the meaning вАЬto soften.вАЭ The word is used in parallel constructions with the verbs for вАЬfear.вАЭ The implication is that God has made Job fearful.
n1802331: tn This is a very difficult verse. The Hebrew text literally says: вАЬfor I have not been destroyed because of darkness, and because of my face [which] gloom has covered.вАЭ Most commentators omit the negative adverb, which gives the meaning that Job is enveloped in darkness and reduced to terror. The verb вАП„†÷і„¶÷∞„Ю÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (nitsmatti) means вАЬI have been silentвАЭ (as in Arabic and Aramaic), and so obviously the negative must be retainedвАФhe has not been silent.
n180241: tn The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) is used to express the cause (see GKC 389 ¬І121.f).
n180242: tc The LXX reads вАЬWhy are times hidden from the Almighty?вАЭ as if to say that God is not interested in the events on the earth. The MT reading is saying that God fails to set the times for judgment and vindication and makes good sense as it stands.
n180243: tn The line is short: вАЬthey move boundary stones.вАЭ So some commentators have supplied a subject, such as вАЬwicked men.вАЭ The reason for its being wicked men is that to move the boundary stone was to encroach dishonestly on the lands of others (Deut 19:14; 27:17).
n180244: tc The LXX reads вАЬand their shepherd.вАЭ Many commentators accept this reading. But the MT says that they graze the flocks that they have stolen. The difficulty with the MT reading is that there is no suffix on the final verbвАФbut that is not an insurmountable difference.
n180245: sn Because of the violence and oppression of the wicked, the poor and needy, the widows and orphans, all are deprived of their rights and forced out of the ways and into hiding just to survive.
n180246: tc The verse begins with вАП„Ф÷µ„ЯвАО (hen), but the LXX, Vulgate, and Syriac all have вАЬlike.вАЭ R. Gordis (Job, 265) takes вАП„Ф÷µ„ЯвАО (hen) as a pronoun вАЬtheyвАЭ and supplies the comparative. The sense of the verse is clear in either case.
n180248: tc The MT has вАЬin the working/labor of them,вАЭ or вАЬwhen they labor.вАЭ Some commentators simply omit these words. Dhorme retains them and moves them to go with вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО ( њaravah), which he takes to mean вАЬeveningвАЭ; this gives a clause, вАЬalthough they work until the evening.вАЭ Then, with many others, he takes вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo) to be a negative and finishes the verse with вАЬno food for the children.вАЭ Others make fewer changes in the text, and as a result do not come out with such a hopeless pictureвАФthere is some food found. The point is that they spend their time foraging for food, and they find just enough to survive, but it is a day-long activity. For Job, this shows how unrighteous the administration of the world actually is.
n180249:
tc Based on the text critical question in the previous note, some read this as a form related to the noun вАП„Ґ÷ґ„®÷ґ„СвАО ( њerev, вАЬeveningвАЭ). These same consonants occur as a verb in Isa 24:11, вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷∞„С÷Є„ФвАО ( њarevah) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav, вАЬbecome eveningвАЭ). This would give the time frame of their work rather than the location, but the location provides a parallel to вАЬwilderness.вАЭ
tn The rift valley (вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО,  њaravah) extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba, but the term normally refers only to a section of it. For the book of Job, the most likely section is that south of the Dead Sea, a section that is arid with only sparse vegetation.
n1802410: tn The verb is not included in the Hebrew text but is supplied in the translation.
n1802411: tc The word вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„Ь„Х÷євАО (belilo) means вАЬhis fodder.вАЭ It is unclear to what this refers. If the suffix is taken as a collective, then it can be translated вАЬthey gather/reap their fodder.вАЭ The early versions all have вАЬthey reap in a field which is not hisвАЭ (taking it as вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ „Ь„Х÷євАО, beli lo). A conjectural emendation would change the word to вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (ballaylah, вАЬin the nightвАЭ). But there is no reason for this.
n1802412: tn The verbs in this verse are uncertain. In the first line вАЬreapвАЭ is used, and that would be the work of a hired man (and certainly not done at night). The meaning of this second verb is uncertain; it has been taken to mean вАЬglean,вАЭ which would be the task of the poor.
n1802414: tn The verb with no expressed subject is here again taken in the passive: вАЬthey snatchвАЭ becomes вАЬ[child] is snatched.вАЭ
n1802415: tn This word is usually defined as вАЬviolence; ruin.вАЭ But elsewhere it does mean вАЬbreastвАЭ (Isa 60:16; 66:11), and that is certainly what it means here.
n1802416: tc The MT has a very brief and strange reading: вАЬthey take as a pledge upon the poor.вАЭ This could be taken as вАЬthey take a pledge against the poorвАЭ (ESV). Kamphausen suggested that instead of вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬagainstвАЭ) one should read вАП„Ґ„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО ( њul, вАЬsucklingвАЭ). This is supported by the parallelism. вАЬThey take as pledgeвАЭ is also made passive here.
n1802417: sn The point should not be missedвАФamidst abundant harvests, carrying sheaves about, they are still going hungry.
n1802418: tc The Hebrew term is вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„®÷є„™÷Є„ЭвАО (shurotam), which may be translated вАЬterracesвАЭ or вАЬolive rows.вАЭ But that would not be the proper place to have a press to press the olives and make oil. E. Dhorme (Job, 360-61) proposes on the analogy of an Arabic word that this should be read as вАЬmillstonesвАЭ (which he would also write in the dual). But the argument does not come from a clean cognate, but from a possible development of words. The meaning of вАЬolive rowsвАЭ works well enough.
n1802419: tn The final verb, a preterite with the вАП„ХвАО (vav) consecutive, is here interpreted as a circumstantial clause.
n1802420: tc The MT as pointed reads вАЬfrom the city of men they groan.вАЭ Most commentators change one vowel in вАП„Ю÷∞„™÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (metim) to get вАП„Ю÷µ„™÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (metim) to get the active participle, вАЬthe dying.вАЭ This certainly fits the parallelism better, although sense could be made out of the MT.
n1802421: tn Heb вАЬthe souls of the wounded,вАЭ which here refers to the wounded themselves.
n1802422: tc The MT has the noun вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (tiflah) which means вАЬfolly; tastelessnessвАЭ (cf. 1:22). The verb, which normally means вАЬto place; to put,вАЭ would then be rendered вАЬto impute; to charge.вАЭ This is certainly a workable translation in the context. Many commentators have emended the text, changing the noun to вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„§÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tefillah, вАЬprayerвАЭ), and so then also the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yasim, here вАЬchargesвАЭ) to вАП„Щ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (yishma њ, вАЬhearsвАЭ). It reads: вАЬBut God does not hear the prayerвАЭвАФreferring to the groans.
n1802424: tn The text simply has вАП„Ь÷Є„Р„Х÷є„®вАО (la Њor, вАЬat lightвАЭ or вАЬat daylightвАЭ), probably meaning just at the time of dawn.
n1802425: tn In a few cases the jussive is used without any real sense of the jussive being present (see GKC 323 ¬І109.k).
n1802426: sn The point is that he is like a thief in that he works during the night, just before the daylight, when the advantage is all his and the victim is most vulnerable.
n1802428: tn The phrase вАЬthe robberвАЭ has been supplied in the English translation for clarification.
n1802429: tc This is not the idea of the adulterer, but of the thief. So some commentators reverse the order and put this verse after v. 14.
n1802430: tc The verb вАП„Ч÷і„™÷Љ÷∞„Ю„Х÷ЉвАО (khittemu) is the Piel from the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„ЭвАО (khatam, вАЬto sealвАЭ). The verb is now in the plural, covering all the groups mentioned that work under the cover of darkness. The suggestion that they вАЬseal,вАЭ i.e., вАЬmarkвАЭ the house they will rob, goes against the meaning of the word вАЬseal.вАЭ
n1802431: tc Some commentators join this very short colon to the beginning of v. 17: вАЬthey do not know the light. For togetherвА¶вАЭ becomes вАЬfor together they have not known the light.вАЭ
n1802433: tc Many commentators find vv. 18-24 difficult on the lips of Job, and so identify this unit as a misplaced part of the speech of Zophar. They describe the enormities of the wicked. But a case can also be made for retaining it in this section. Gordis thinks it could be taken as a quotation by Job of his friendsвАЩ ideas.
n1802434: tn The verb вАЬsayвАЭ is not in the text; it is supplied here to indicate that this is a different section.
n1802436: sn The wicked person is described here as a spray or foam upon the waters, built up in the agitation of the waters but dying away swiftly.
n1802437: tn The text reads, вАЬhe does not turn by the way of the vineyards.вАЭ This means that since the land is cursed, he/one does not go there. Bickell emended вАЬthe way of the vineyardsвАЭ to вАЬthe treader of the vineyardвАЭ (see RSV, NRSV). This would mean that вАЬno wine-presser would turn towardsвАЭ their vineyards.
n1802438: tn Or вАЬdryness.вАЭ The term вАП„¶÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tsiyyah) normally refers to a dry region, a wilderness or desert. Here the focus is on dryness.
n1802441: tc Heb вАЬThe grave [] they have sinned.вАЭ The verb вАЬsnatch up/awayвАЭ is understood by parallelism. If the perfect verb is maintained, the line also implies the relative pronoun, вАЬthe grave [snatches] [those who] have sinned.вАЭ If the verb is emended from the perfect to a participle by deleting or moving the вАП„ХвАО (vav) from вАП„Ч„Ш„Р„ХвАО to вАП„Ч„Х„Ш„РвАО, it reads вАЬthe grave [snatches] one who sins.вАЭ
n1802442: tn Here вАЬwombвАЭ is synecdoche, representing oneвАЩs mother.
n1802443: tc The form in the text is the active participle, вАЬfeed; graze; shepherd.вАЭ The idea of вАЬpreyвАЭ is not natural to it. R. Gordis (Job, 270) argues that third he (вАП„ФвАО) verbs are often by-forms of geminate verbs, and so the meaning here is more akin to вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њa њ, вАЬto crushвАЭ). The LXX seems to have read something like вАП„Ф÷µ„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (hera њ, вАЬoppressedвАЭ).
n1802444: tn Heb вАЬthe childless [woman], she does not give birth.вАЭ The verbal clause is intended to serve as a modifier here for the woman. See on subordinate verbal clauses GKC 490 ¬І156.d, f.
n1802445: tn God has to be the subject of this clause. None is stated in the Hebrew text, but вАЬGodвАЭ has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1802446: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity. See the note on the word вАЬlifeвАЭ at the end of the line.
n1802447: tn This line has been given a number of interpretations due to its cryptic form. The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (yaqum) means вАЬhe rises up.вАЭ It probably is meant to have God as the subject, and be subordinated as a temporal clause to what follows. The words вАЬagainst himвАЭ are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation to specify the object and indicate that вАЬrise upвАЭ is meant in a hostile sense. The following verb вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷є„Р÷Њ„Щ÷Ј„Р÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (velo Њ ya Њamin), by its very meaning of вАЬand he does not believe,вАЭ cannot have God as the subject, but must refer to the wicked.
n1802448: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1802449: tn The expression вАП„Ь÷Є„С÷ґ„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (lavetakh, вАЬin securityвАЭ) precedes the verb that it qualifiesвАФGod вАЬallows him to take root in security.вАЭ For the meaning of the verb, see Job 8:15.
n1802451: sn The meaning of the verse is that God may allow the wicked to rest in comfort and security, but all the time he is watching them closely with the idea of bringing judgment on them.
n1802452: tn The Hebrew throughout this section (vv. 18-24) interchanges the singular and the plural. Here again we have вАЬthey are exaltedвА¶but he is not.вАЭ The verse is clear nonetheless: the wicked rise high, and then suddenly they are gone.
n1802453: tn The verb is the Hophal of the rare verb вАП„Ю÷Є„Ы÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (makhakh), which seems to mean вАЬto bend; to collapse.вАЭ The text would read вАЬthey are made to collapse like all others.вАЭ There is no reason here to change вАЬlike othersвАЭ just because the MT is banal. But many do, following the LXX with вАЬlike mallows.вАЭ The LXX was making a translation according to sense. R. Gordis (Job, 271) prefers вАЬlike grass.вАЭ
n1802454: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„§÷Ј„•вАО (qafats) actually means вАЬto shut in,вАЭ which does not provide exactly the idea of being gathered, not directly at least. But a change to вАП„І÷Є„Ш÷Ј„£вАО (qataf, вАЬpluckвАЭ) while attractive, is not necessary.
n1802455: sn This marks the end of the disputed section, taken here to be a quotation by Job of their sentiments.
n1802456: tn The word вАП„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО ( Њal, вАЬnotвАЭ) is used here substantivally (вАЬnothingвАЭ).
n180251: sn The third speech of Bildad takes up Job 25, a short section of six verses. It is followed by two speeches from Job; and Zophar does not return with his third. Does this mean that the friends have run out of arguments, and that Job is just getting going? Many scholars note that in chs. 26 and 27 there is material that does not fit JobвАЩs argument. Many have rearranged the material to show that there was a complete cycle of three speeches. In that light, 26:5-14 is viewed as part of BildadвАЩs speech. Some, however, take BildadвАЩs speech to be only ch. 25, and make 26:5-14 an interpolated hymn. For all the arguments and suggestions, one should see the introductions and the commentaries.
n180252: tn The word вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷µ„ЬвАО (hamshel) is a Hiphil infinitive absolute used as a noun. It describes the rulership or dominion that God has, that which gives power and authority.
n180253: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad) literally means вАЬfear; dread,вАЭ but in the sense of what causes the fear or the dread.
n180255: sn The line says that God вАЬmakes peace in his heights.вАЭ The вАЬheightsвАЭ are usually interpreted to mean the highest heaven. There may be a reference here to combat in the spiritual world between angels and Satan. The context will show that God has a heavenly host at his disposal, and nothing in heaven or on earth can shatter his peace. вАЬPeaceвАЭ here could also signify the whole order he establishes.
n180256: tn Heb вАЬIs there a number to his troops?вАЭ The question is rhetorical: there is no number to them!
n180257: tc In place of вАЬlightвАЭ here the LXX has вАЬhis ambush,вАЭ perhaps reading вАП„Р÷є„®÷∞„С„Х÷євАО ( Њorevo) instead of вАП„Р„Х÷є„®÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њorehu, вАЬhis lightвАЭ). But while that captures the idea of troops and warfare, the change should be rejected because the armies are linked with stars and light. The expression is poetic; the LXX interpretation tried to make it concrete.
n180258: sn Bildad here does not come up with new expressions; rather, he simply uses what Eliphaz had said (see Job 4:17-19 and 15:14-16).
n1802510: tn The text just has вАЬmaggotвАЭ and in the second half вАЬworm.вАЭ Something has to be added to make it a bit clearer. The terms вАЬmaggotвАЭ and вАЬwormвАЭ describe man in his lowest and most ignominious shape.
n180261: sn These two chapters will be taken together under this title, although most commentators would assign Job 26:5-14 to Bildad and Job 27:7-23 to Zophar. Those sections will be noted as they emerge. For the sake of outlining, the following sections will be marked off: JobвАЩs scorn for Bildad (26:2-4); a better picture of GodвАЩs greatness (26:5-14); JobвАЩs protestation of innocence (27:2-6); and a picture of the condition of the wicked (27:7-23).
n180262: tn The interrogative clause is used here as an exclamation, and sarcastic at that. Job is saying вАЬyou have in no way helped the powerless.вАЭ The verb uses the singular form, for Job is replying to Bildad.
n180263: tn The вАЬpowerlessвАЭ is expressed here by the negative before the word for вАЬstrength; powerвАЭвАФвАЬhim who has no powerвАЭ (see GKC 482 ¬І152.u, v).
n180264: tn Heb вАЬthe arm [with] no strength.вАЭ Here too the negative expression is serving as a relative clause to modify вАЬarm,вАЭ the symbol of strength and power, which by metonymy stands for the whole person. вАЬMan of armвАЭ denoted the strong in 22:8.
n180265: tc The phrase вАП„Ь÷Є„®÷є„СвАО (larov) means вАЬto abundanceвАЭ or вАЬin a large quantity.вАЭ It is also used ironically like all these expressions. This makes very good sense, but some wish to see a closer parallel and so offer emendations. Reiske and Kissane suggested вАЬto the tenderвАЭ for this word. But the timid are not the same as the ignorant and unwise. So Graetz supplied вАЬto the boorishвАЭ by reading вАП„Ь÷∞„С÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (leba њar). G. R. Driver did the same with less of a change: вАП„Ь÷Ј„С÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (labbor; HTR 29 [1936]: 172).
n180266: tn The verse begins with the preposition and the interrogative: вАП„Р÷ґ„™÷Њ„Ю÷і„ЩвАО ( Њet mi, вАЬwith who[se help]?вАЭ). Others take it as the accusative particle introducing the indirect object: вАЬfor whom did you utterвА¶вАЭ (see GKC 371 ¬І117.gg). Both are possible.
n180268: sn This is the section, Job 26:5-14, that many conclude makes better sense coming from the friend. But if it is attributed to Job, then he is showing he can surpass them in his treatise of the greatness of God.
n180269: tn The text has вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (harefa Њim, вАЬthe shadesвАЭ), referring to the вАЬdead,вАЭ or the elite among the dead (see Isa 14:9; 26:14; Ps 88:10 [11]). For further discussion, start with A. R. Johnson, The Vitality of the Individual, 88ff.
n1802610: tn The verb is a Polal from вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (khil) which means вАЬto tremble.вАЭ It shows that even these spirits cannot escape the terror.
n1802611: tc Most commentators wish to lengthen the verse and make it more parallel, but nothing is gained by doing this.
n1802614: tn The line has вАЬand there is no covering for destruction.вАЭ вАЬDestructionвАЭ here is another name for Sheol: вАП„Р÷≤„С÷Ј„У÷Љ„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( Њavaddon, вАЬAbaddonвАЭ).
n1802615: sn The Hebrew word is вАП„¶÷Є„§„Х÷є„ЯвАО (tsafon). Some see here a reference to Mount Zaphon of the Ugaritic texts, the mountain that Baal made his home. The Hebrew writers often equate and contrast Mount Zion with this proud mountain of the north. Of course, the word just means north, and so in addition to any connotations for pagan mythology, it may just represent the northern skiesвАФthe stars. Since the parallel line speaks of the earth, that is probably all that was intended in this particular context.
n1802616: sn There is an allusion to the creation account, for this word is вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (tohu), translated вАЬwithout formвАЭ in Gen 1:2.
n1802617: sn Buttenwieser suggests that Job had outgrown the idea of the earth on pillars, and was beginning to see it was suspended in space. But in v. 11 he will still refer to the pillars.
n1802618: tn The verb means вАЬto hold; to seize,вАЭ here in the sense of shutting up, enshrouding, or concealing.
n1802619: tc The MT has вАП„Ы÷і„°÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (khisseh), which is a problematic vocalization. Most certainly вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„°÷ґ„ФвАО (keseh), alternative for вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„°÷ґ„РвАО (kese Њ, вАЬfull moonвАЭ) is intended here. The MT is close to the form of вАЬthrone,вАЭ which would be вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„°÷Љ÷µ„РвАО (kisse Њ, cf. NLT вАЬhe shrouds his throne with his cloudsвАЭ). But here God is covering the face of the moon by hiding it behind clouds.
n1802620: tn The expression вАП„Ч÷є„І÷Њ„Ч÷Є„ТвАО (khoq khag) means вАЬhe has drawn a limit as a circle.вАЭ According to some the form should have been вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Њ„Ч„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (khaq khug, вАЬHe has traced a circleвАЭ). But others argues that the text is acceptable as is, and can be interpreted as вАЬa limit he has circled.вАЭ The Hebrew verbal roots are вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО (khaqaq, вАЬto engrave; to sketch out; to traceвАЭ) and вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (khug, вАЬdescribe a circleвАЭ) respectively.
n1802621: sn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 173) says these are the great mountains, perceived to hold up the sky.
n1802622: sn The idea here is that when the earth quakes, or when there is thunder in the heavens, these all represent GodвАЩs rebuke, for they create terror.
n1802623: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (raga њ) has developed a Semitic polarity, i.e., having totally opposite meanings. It can mean вАЬto disturb; to stir upвАЭ or вАЬto calm; to still.вАЭ Gordis thinks both meanings have been invoked here. But it seems more likely that вАЬcalmвАЭ fits the context better.
n1802624: tn Heb вАЬRahabвАЭ (вАП„®÷Є„Ф÷Ј„СвАО), the mythical sea monster that represents the forces of chaos in ancient Near Eastern literature. In the translation the words вАЬthe great sea monsterвАЭ have been supplied appositionally in order to clarify вАЬRahab.вАЭ
n1802625: sn Here again there are possible mythological allusions or polemics. The god Yam, вАЬSea,вАЭ was important in Ugaritic as a god of chaos. And Rahab is another name for the monster of the deep (see Job 9:13).
n1802626: tn Or вАЬwindвАЭ; or perhaps вАЬSpirit.вАЭ The same Hebrew word, вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh), may be translated as вАЬwind,вАЭ вАЬbreath,вАЭ or вАЬspirit/SpiritвАЭ depending on the context.
n1802627: sn Here too is a reference to pagan views indirectly. The fleeing serpent was a designation for Leviathan, whom the book will simply describe as an animal, but the pagans thought to be a monster of the deep. See the same Hebrew phrase in Isaiah 27:1. GodвАЩs power over nature is associated with defeat of pagan gods (see further W. F. Albright, Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan).
n1802628: tn Heb вАЬthe ends of his ways,вАЭ meaning вАЬthe fringes.вАЭ
n1802629: tn Heb вАЬhow little is the word.вАЭ Here вАЬlittleвАЭ means a вАЬfractionвАЭ or an вАЬecho.вАЭ
n180271: tn The Hebrew word вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Є„ЬвАО (mashal) is characteristically вАЬproverb; by-word.вАЭ It normally refers to a brief saying, but can be used for a discourse (see A. R. Johnson, вАЬMa≈°al,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 162ff.).
n180272: tn The expression вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷Њ„Р÷µ„ЬвАО (khay  Њel) is the oath formula: вАЬas God lives.вАЭ In other words, the speaker is staking GodвАЩs life on the credibility of the words. It is like saying, вАЬAs truly as God is alive.вАЭ
n180273: tn вАЬMy judgmentвАЭ would here, as before, be вАЬmy right.вАЭ God has taken this away by afflicting Job unjustly (A. B. Davidson, Job, 187).
n180274: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷µ„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (hemar) is the Hiphil perfect from вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (marar, вАЬto be bitterвАЭ) and hence, вАЬto make bitter.вАЭ The object of the verb is вАЬmy soul,вАЭ which is better translated as вАЬmeвАЭ or вАЬmy life.вАЭ
n180275: tn The adverb вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО ( њod) was originally a noun, and so here it could be rendered вАЬall the existence of my spirit.вАЭ The word comes between the noun in construct and its actual genitive (see GKC 415 ¬І128.e).
n180276: tn The word вАП„†÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (neshamah) is the вАЬbreathвАЭ that was breathed into Adam in Gen 2:7. Its usage includes the animating breath, the spiritual understanding, and the functioning conscienceвАФso the whole spirit of the person. The other word in this verse, вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh), may be translated as вАЬwind,вАЭ вАЬbreath,вАЭ or вАЬspirit/SpiritвАЭ depending on the context. Here, since it talks about the nostrils, it should be translated вАЬbreath.вАЭ
n180277: tn The verse begins with вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim), the formula used for the content of the oath (вАЬGod livesвА¶if I do/do notвА¶вАЭ). Thus, the content of the oath proper is here in v. 4.
n180278: tn The verb means вАЬto utter; to mumble; to meditate.вАЭ The implication is that he will not communicate deceitful things, no matter how quiet or subtle.
n180279: tn The text uses вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„Ь÷Є„Ф „Ь÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (khalilah li) meaning вАЬfar be it from me,вАЭ or more strongly, something akin to вАЬsacrilege.вАЭ
n1802710: tn In the Hebrew text вАЬyouвАЭ is pluralвАФa reference to Eliphaz, Zophar, and Bildad. To make this clear, вАЬthreeвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n1802712: tn The prepositional phrase вАЬfrom my daysвАЭ probably means вАЬfrom the days of my birth,вАЭ or вАЬall my life.вАЭ
n1802713: sn Of course, he means like his enemy when he is judged, not when he is thriving in prosperity and luxury.
n1802714: tn The form is the Hitpolel participle from вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum): вАЬthose who are rising up against me,вАЭ or вАЬmy adversary.вАЭ
n1802715: tc The LXX made a free paraphrase: вАЬNo, but let my enemies be as the overthrow of the ungodly, and they that rise up against me as the destruction of transgressors.вАЭ
