n1802716: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„С÷∞„¶÷Є„ҐвАО (yivtsa њ) means вАЬto cut off.вАЭ It could be translated transitively or intransitivelyвАФthe latter is better here (вАЬwhen he is cut offвАЭ). Since the next line speaks of prayer, some have thought this verse should be about prayer. Mandelkern, in his concordance (p. 228b), suggested the verb should be вАЬwhen he praysвАЭ (reading вАП„Щ÷і„§÷∞„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ҐвАО [yifga њ] in place of вАП„Щ÷і„С÷∞„¶÷Є„ҐвАО [yivtsa њ]).
n1802717: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷µ„©„Б÷ґ„ЬвАО (yeshel) is found only here. It has been related spoils [or sheaves]вАЭ); вАП„©„Б÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (sha Њal, вАЬto askвАЭ); вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto lift upвАЭ [i.e., pray]); and a host of others.
n1802718: tn See the note on 22:26 where the same verb is employed.
n1802719: tn The object suffix is in the plural, which gives some support to the idea Job is speaking to them.
n1802722: tn The interrogative uses the demonstrative pronoun in its emphatic position: вАЬWhy in the worldвА¶?вАЭ (IBHS 312-13 ¬І17.4.3c).
n1802723: tn The text has the noun вАЬvain thing; breath; vapor,вАЭ and then a denominative verb from the same root: вАЬto become vain with a vain thing,вАЭ or вАЬto do in vain a vain thing.вАЭ This is an example of the internal object, or a cognate accusative (see GKC 367 ¬І117.q). The LXX has вАЬyou all know that you are adding vanity to vanity.вАЭ
n1802724: tn The expression вАЬallotted by GodвАЭ interprets the simple prepositional phrase in the text: вАЬwith/from God.вАЭ
n1802725: tn R. Gordis (Job, 294) identifies this as a breviloquence. Cf. Ps 92:8 where the last two words also constitute the apodosis.
n1802726: tn Heb вАЬwill not be satisfied with bread/food.вАЭ
n1802727: tn The text says вАЬwill be buried in/by death.вАЭ A number of passages in the Bible use вАЬdeathвАЭ to mean the plague that kills (see Jer 15:2; Isa 28:3; and BDB 89 s.v. вАП„СвАО 2.a). In this sense it is like the English expression for the plague, вАЬthe Black Death.вАЭ
n1802728: tc The LXX has вАЬtheir widowsвАЭ to match the plural, and most commentators harmonize in the same way.
n1802729: tn The text simply repeats the verb from the last clause. It could be treated as a separate short clause: вАЬHe may store it up, but the righteous will wear it.вАЭ But it also could be understood as the object of the following verb, вАЬ[what] he stores up the righteous will wear.вАЭ The LXX simply has, вАЬAll these things shall the righteous gain.вАЭ
n1802730: tn Heb вАП„Ы÷Є„Ґ÷Є„©„БвАО (kha њash, вАЬlike a mothвАЭ), but this leaves room for clarification. Some commentators wanted to change it to вАЬbirdвАЩs nestвАЭ or just вАЬnestвАЭ (cf. NRSV) to make the parallelism; see Job 4:14. But the word is not found. The LXX has a double expression, вАЬas moths, as a spider.вАЭ So several take it as the spiderвАЩs web, which is certainly unsubstantial (cf. NAB, NASB, NLT; see Job 8:14).
n1802731: tn The Hebrew word is the word for вАЬbooth,вАЭ as in the Feast of Booths. The word describes something that is flimsy; it is not substantial at all.
n1802732: tc The verb is the Niphal вАП„Щ÷µ„Р÷Є„°÷µ„£вАО (ye Њasef), from вАП„Р÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО ( Њasaf, вАЬto gatherвАЭ). So, вАЬhe lies down rich, but he is not gathered.вАЭ This does not make much sense. It could mean вАЬhe will not be gathered for burial,вАЭ but that does not belong here. Many commentators accept the variant вАП„Щ÷є„Р„°÷і„£вАО (yo Њsif) stood for вАП„Щ„Х÷є„°÷і„Щ„£вАО (yosif, вАЬwill [not] addвАЭ). This is what the LXX and the Syriac have. This leads to the interpretive translation that вАЬhe will do so no longer.вАЭ
n1802733: tn Heb вАЬand he is not.вАЭ One view is that this must mean that he dies, not that his wealth is gone. R. Gordis (Job, 295) says the first part should be made impersonal: вАЬwhen one opens oneвАЩs eyes, the wicked is no longer there.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 396) has it more simply: вАЬHe has opened his eyes, and it is for the last time.вАЭ But the other view is that the wealth goes overnight. In support of this is the introduction into the verse of the wealthy. The RSV, NRSV, ESV, and NLT take it that вАЬwealth is gone.вАЭ
n1802734: tn Many commentators want a word parallel to вАЬin the night.вАЭ And so we are offered вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„ЭвАО (bayyom, вАЬin the dayвАЭ) for вАП„Ы÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (khammayim, вАЬlike watersвАЭ) as well as a number of others. But вАЬwatersвАЭ sometimes stand for major calamities, and so may be retained here. Besides, not all parallel structures are synonymous.
n1802735: tn The verb is once again functioning in an adverbial sense. The text has вАЬit hurls itself against him and shows no mercy.вАЭ
n1802736: tn If the same subject is to be carried through here, it is the wind. That would make this a bold personification, perhaps suggesting the force of the wind. Others argue that it is unlikely that the wind claps its hands. They suggest taking the verb with an indefinite subject: вАЬhe clapsвАЭ means вАЬone claps. The idea is that of people rejoicing when the wicked are gone. But the parallelism is against this unless the second line is changed as well. R. Gordis (Job, 296) has вАЬmen will clap their handsвА¶men will whistle upon him.вАЭ
n1802737: tn Or вАЬhisses at him from its placeвАЭ (ESV).
n180281: sn As the book is now arranged, this chapter forms an additional speech by Job, although some argue that it comes from the writer of the book. The mood of the chapter is not despair, but wisdom; it anticipates the divine speeches in the end of the book. This poem, like many psalms in the Bible, has a refrain (vv. 12 and 20). These refrains outline the chapter, giving three sections: there is no known road to wisdom (1-11); no price can buy it (12-19); and only God has it, and only by revelation can man posses it (20-28).
n180282: tn The poem opens with вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki). Some commentators think this should have been вАЬfor,вАЭ and that the poem once stood in another setting. But there are places in the Bible where this word occurs with the sense of вАЬsurelyвАЭ and no other meaning (cf. Gen 18:20).
n180283: tn The word вАП„Ю„Х÷є„¶÷Є„РвАО (motsa Њ, from вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО [yatsa Њ, вАЬgo outвАЭ]) is the word for вАЬmine,вАЭ or more simply, вАЬsource.вАЭ Mining was not an enormous industry in the land of Canaan or Israel; mined products were imported. Some editors have suggested alternative readings: Dahood found in the word the root for вАЬshineвАЭ and translated the MT as вАЬsmelter.вАЭ But that is going too far. P. Jo√Љon suggested вАЬplace of finding,вАЭ reading вАП„Ю÷і„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„РвАО (mimtsa Њ) for вАП„Ю„Х÷є„¶÷Є„РвАО (motsa Њ; see Bib 11 [1930]: 323).
n180284: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ц÷є„І÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yazoqqu) translated вАЬrefined,вАЭ comes from вАП„Ц÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО (zaqaq), a word that basically means вАЬto blow.вАЭ From the meaning вАЬto blow; to distend; to inflateвАЭ derives the meaning for refining.
n180286: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„¶„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (yatsuq) is usually translated as a passive participle вАЬis smeltedвАЭ (from вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ІвАО [yatsaq, вАЬto meltвАЭ]): вАЬcopper is smelted from the oreвАЭ (ESV) or вАЬfrom the stone, copper is poured outвАЭ (as an imperfect from вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„ІвАО [tsuq]). But the rock becomes the metal in the process. So according to R. Gordis (Job, 304) the translation should be: вАЬthe rock is poured out as copper.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 400), however, defines the form in the text as вАЬhard,вАЭ and simply has it вАЬhard stone becomes copper.вАЭ
n180287: sn The text appears at first to be saying that by opening up a mine shaft, or by taking lights down below, the miner dispels the darkness. But the clause might be more general, meaning that man goes deep into the earth as if it were day.
n180288: tn The verse ends with вАЬthe stone of darkness and deep darkness.вАЭ The genitive would be location, describing the place where the stones are found.
n180289: tc The first part of this verse, вАЬHe cuts a shaft far from the place where people live,вАЭ has received a lot of attention. The word for вАЬliveвАЭ is вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„®вАО (gar). Some of the proposals are: вАЬlimestone,вАЭ on the basis of the LXX; вАЬfar from the light,вАЭ reading вАП„†÷µ„®вАО (ner); вАЬby a foreign people,вАЭ taking the word to means вАЬforeign peopleвАЭ; вАЬa foreign people opening shaftsвАЭ; or taking gar as вАЬcraterвАЭ based on Arabic. Driver puts this and the next together: вАЬa strange people who have been forgotten cut shaftsвАЭ (see his вАЬProblems in Job,вАЭ AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 163). L. Waterman had вАЬthe people of the lampвАЭ (вАЬNote on Job 28:4, вАЭ JBL 71 [1952]: 167ff). And there are others. Since there is really no compelling argument in favor of one of these alternative interpretations, the MT should be preserved until shown to be wrong.
n1802810: tn Heb вАЬforgotten by the foot.вАЭ This means that there are people walking above on the ground, and the places below, these mines, are not noticed by the pedestrians above.
n1802811: sn This is a description of the mining procedures. Dangling suspended from a rope would be a necessary part of the job of going up and down the shafts.
n1802812: sn The verse has been properly understood, on the whole, as comparing the earth above and all its produce with the upheaval down below.
n1802813:
tn It is probably best to take вАЬplaceвАЭ in construct to the rest of the colon, with an understood relative clause: вАЬa place, the rocks of which are sapphires.вАЭ
sn The modern stone known as sapphire is thought not to have been used until Roman times, and so some other stone is probably meant here, perhaps lapis lazuli.
n1802814: sn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 181) suggests that if it is lapis lazuli, then the dust of gold would refer to the particles of iron pyrite found in lapis lazuli which glitter like gold.
n1802815: tn The вАЬpathвАЭ could refer to the mine shaft or it could refer to wisdom. The former seems more likely in the present context; the word вАЬhidden is supplied in the translation to indicate the mines are вАЬhiddenвАЭ from sharp-eyed birds of prey above.
n1802816: sn The kind of bird mentioned here is debated. The LXX has вАЬvulture,вАЭ and so some commentaries follow that. The emphasis on the sight favors the view that it is the falcon.
n1802817: tn Heb вАЬthe sons of pride.вАЭ In Job 41:26 the expression refers to carnivorous wild beasts.
n1802818: tn The Hebrew verb is simply вАЬto stretch out; to sendвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь„ЧвАО, shalakh). With вАП„Щ÷Є„У„Х÷євАО (yado, вАЬhis handвАЭ) the idea is that of laying oneвАЩs hand on the rock, i.e., getting to work on the hardest of rocks.
n1802819: tn The Hebrew вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷Љ÷є„®÷ґ„©„БвАО (mishoresh) means вАЬfrom/at [their] root [or base].вАЭ In mining, people have gone below ground, under the mountains, and overturned rock and dirt. It is also interesting that here in a small way humans do what God doesвАФoverturn mountains (cf. 9:5).
n1802820: tn Or вАЬtunnels.вАЭ The word is вАП„Щ÷∞„Р÷є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ye Њorim), the word for вАЬriversвАЭ and in the singular, the Nile River. Here it refers to tunnels or channels through the rocks.
n1802822: tc The translation вАЬsearchedвАЭ follows the LXX and Vulgate; the MT reads вАЬbinds upвАЭ or вАЬdams up.вАЭ This latter translation might refer to the damming of water that might seep into a mine (HALOT 289 s.v. вАП„Ч„С„©„БвАО; cf. ESV, NJPS, NASB, REB, NLT).
n1802823: tc The older translations had вАЬhe binds the streams from weeping,вАЭ i.e., from trickling (вАП„Ю÷і„С÷Љ÷∞„Ы÷і„ЩвАО, mibbekhi). But the Ugaritic parallel has changed the understanding, reading вАЬtoward the spring of the riversвАЭ ( њm mbk nhrm). Earlier than that discovery, the versions had taken the word as a noun as well. Some commentators had suggested repointing the Hebrew. Some chose вАП„Ю÷Ј„С÷Љ÷∞„Ы÷µ„ЩвАО (mabbekhe, вАЬsourcesвАЭ). Now there is much Ugaritic support for the reading (see G. M. Landes, BASOR 144 [1956]: 32f.; and H. L. Ginsberg, вАЬThe Ugaritic texts and textual criticism,вАЭ JBL 62 [1943]: 111).
n1802824: tc The LXX has вАЬits way,вАЭ apparently reading вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (darkah) in place of вАП„Ґ÷ґ„®÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО ( њerkah, вАЬplaceвАЭ). This is adopted by most modern commentators. But R. Gordis (Job, 308) shows that this change is not necessary, for вАП„Ґ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њerekh) in the Bible means вАЬorder; row; disposition,вАЭ and here вАЬplace.вАЭ An alternate meaning would be вАЬworthвАЭ (NIV, ESV).
n1802825: sn The вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ф„Х÷є„ЭвАО (tehom) is the вАЬdeepвАЭ of Gen 1:2, the abyss or primordial sea. It was always understood to be a place of darkness and danger. As remote as it is, it asserts that wisdom is not found there (personification). So here we have the abyss and the sea, then death and destructionвАФbut they are not the places that wisdom resides.
n1802826: tn The вАП„СвАО (bet) preposition is taken here to mean вАЬwithвАЭ in the light of the parallel preposition.
n1802827: tn The word actually means вАЬweighed,вАЭ that is, lifted up on the scale and weighed, in order to purchase.
n1802828: tn The exact identification of these stones is uncertain. Many recent English translations, however, have вАЬonyxвАЭ and вАЬsapphires.вАЭ
n1802829: tn The word is from вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (zakhakh, вАЬclearвАЭ). It describes a transparent substance, and so вАЬglassвАЭ is an appropriate translation. In the ancient world it was precious and so expensive.
n1802830: tc The MT has вАЬvase,вАЭ but the versions have a plural here, suggesting jewels of gold.
n1802831: tn The word вАП„Ю÷ґ„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (meshekh) comes from a root meaning вАЬto grasp; to seize; to hold,вАЭ and so the derived noun means вАЬgrasping; acquiring; taking possession,вАЭ and therefore, вАЬpriceвАЭ (see the discussion in R. Gordis, Job, 309). Gray renders it вАЬacquisitionвАЭ (so A. Cohen, AJSL 40 [1923/24]: 175).
n1802832: tn In Lam 4:7 these are described as red, and so have been identified as rubies (so NIV) or corals.
n1802833: tn Or вАЬEthiopia.вАЭ In ancient times this referred to the region of the upper Nile, rather than modern Ethiopia (formerly known as Abyssinia).
n1802834: tn The refrain is repeated, except now the verb is вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„С„Х÷є„РвАО (tavo Њ, вАЬcomeвАЭ).
n1802835: tn The vav on the verb is unexpressed in the LXX. It should not be overlooked, for it introduces a subordinate clause of condition (R. Gordis, Job, 310).
n1802837: tn Heb вАЬheard a report of it,вАЭ which means a report of its location, thus вАЬwhere it can be found.вАЭ
n1802838: tn Heb вАЬhe gave weight to the wind.вАЭ The form is the infinitive construct with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition. Some have emended it to change the preposition to the temporal вАП„СвАО (bet) on the basis of some of the versions (e.g., Latin and Syriac) that have вАЬwho made.вАЭ This is workable, for the infinitive would then take on the finite tense of the previous verbs. An infinitive of purpose does not work well, for that would be saying God looked everywhere in order to give wind its proper weight (see R. Gordis, Job, 310).
n1802839: tn The verb is the Piel perfect, meaning вАЬto estimate the measureвАЭ of something. In the verse, the perfect verb continues the function of the infinitive preceding it, as if it had a вАП„ХвАО (vav) prefixed to it. Whatever usage that infinitive had, this verb is to continue it (see GKC 352 ¬І114.r).
n1802841: tn Or вАЬthunderbolt,вАЭ i.e., lightning. Heb вАЬthe roaring of voices/sounds,вАЭ which describes the nature of the storm.
n1802842: tn Heb вАЬitвАЭ; the referent (wisdom) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1802843: tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„§÷Ј„®вАО (safar) in the Piel basically means вАЬto tell; to declare; to showвАЭ or вАЬto count; to number.вАЭ Many commentators offer different suggestions for the translation. вАЬDeclaredвАЭ (as in the RSV, NASB, and NRSV) would be the simplestвАФbut to whom did God declare it? Besides вАЬappraisedвАЭ which is the view of Pope, Dhorme and others (cf. NAB, NIV), J. Reider has suggested вАЬprobedвАЭ (вАЬEtymological studies in biblical Hebrew,вАЭ VT 2 [1952]: 127), Strahan has вАЬstudied,вАЭ and Kissane has вАЬreckoned.вАЭ The difficulty is that the line has a series of verbs, which seem to build to a climax, but without more details it is hard to know how to translate them when they have such a range of meaning.
n1802844: tc The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun) means вАЬto establish; to prepareвАЭ in this stem. There are several mss that have the form from вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin, вАЬdiscernвАЭ), giving вАЬhe discerned it,вАЭ making more of a parallel with the first colon. But the weight of the evidence supports the traditional MT reading.
n1802845: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО (khaqar) means вАЬto examine; to search out.вАЭ Some of the language used here is anthropomorphic, for the sovereign Lord did not have to research or investigate wisdom. The point is that it is as if he did this human activity, meaning that as in the results of such a search God knows everything about wisdom.
n1802846: tc A number of medieval Hebrew manuscripts have YHWH (вАЬLordвАЭ); BHS has вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬLordвАЭ). As J. E. Hartley (Job [NICOT], 383) points out, this is the only occurrence of вАП„Р÷≤„У÷є„†÷Є„ЩвАО ( Њadonay, вАЬLordвАЭ) in the book of Job, creating doubt for retaining it. Normally, YHWH is avoided in the book. вАЬFear ofвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Р÷Ј„™вАО, yir Њat) is followed by вАП„©„Б÷Ј„У÷Љ÷Ј„ЩвАО (shadday, вАЬAlmightyвАЭ) in 6:14вАФthe only other occurrence of this term for вАЬfearвАЭ in construct with a divine title.
n1802847: tc Many commentators delete this verse because (1) many read the divine name Yahweh (translated вАЬLordвАЭ) here, and (2) it is not consistent with the argument that precedes it. But as H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 185) points out, there is inconsistency in this reasoning, for many of the critics have already said that this chapter is an interpolation. Following that line of thought, then, one would not expect it to conform to the rest of the book in this matter of the divine name. And concerning the second difficulty, the point of this chapter is that wisdom is beyond human comprehension and control. It belongs to God alone. So the conclusion that the fear of the Lord is wisdom is the necessary conclusion. Rowley concludes: вАЬIt is a pity to rob the poem of its climax and turn it into the expression of unrelieved agnosticism.вАЭ
n180291: sn Now that the debate with his friends is over, Job concludes with a soliloquy, just as he had begun with one. Here he does not take into account his friends or their arguments. The speech has three main sections: JobвАЩs review of his former circumstances (29:1-25); JobвАЩs present misery (30:1-31); and JobвАЩs vindication of his life (31:1-40).
n180292: tn The verse uses a verbal hendiadys: вАЬand he added (вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷є„°÷ґ„£вАО, vayyosef)вА¶to raise (вАП„©„В÷∞„Р÷µ„™вАО, se Њet) his speech.вАЭ The expression means that he continued, or he spoke again.
n180293: tn The optative is here expressed with вАП„Ю÷і„Щ÷Њ„Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (mi yitteneni, вАЬwho will give meвАЭ), meaning, вАЬO that I [could be]вА¶вАЭ (see GKC 477 ¬І151.b).
n180294: tn The preposition вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) is used here in an expression describing the state desired, especially in the former time (see GKC 376 ¬І118.u).
n180295: tn The expression is literally вАЬmonths of before [or of old; or past].вАЭ The word вАП„І÷ґ„У÷ґ„ЭвАО (qedem) is intended here to be temporal and not spatial; it means days that preceded the present.
n180296: tn The construct state (вАЬdays ofвАЭ) governs the independent sentence that follows (see GKC 422 ¬І130.d): вАЬas the days of [вА¶] God used to watch over me.вАЭ
n180297: tn The imperfect verb here has a customary nuanceвАФвАЬwhen God would watch over meвАЭ (back then), or вАЬwhen God used to watch over me.вАЭ
n180298: tn This clause is in apposition to the preceding (see GKC 426 ¬І131.o). It offers a clarification.
n180299: tn The form вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ф÷і„Ь÷Љ„Х÷євАО (behillo) is unusual; it should be parsed as a Hiphil infinitive construct with the elision of a вАП„ФвАО (he). The proper spelling would have been вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ф÷≤„Ф÷і„Ь÷Љ„Х÷євАО (bahahillo). If it were Qal, it would just mean вАЬwhen his light shone.вАЭ
n1802910: sn Lamp and light are symbols of GodвАЩs blessings of life and all the prosperous and good things it includes.
n1802911: tn Here too the imperfect verb is customaryвАФit describes action that was continuous, but in a past time.
n1802912: tn The accusative (вАЬdarknessвАЭ) is here an adverbial accusative of place, namely, вАЬin the darkness,вАЭ or because he was successfully led by GodвАЩs light, вАЬthrough the darknessвАЭ (see GKC 374 ¬І118.h).
n1802913: tn Heb вАЬin the days of my ripeness.вАЭ The word вАП„Ч÷є„®÷ґ„£вАО (khoref) denotes the time when the harvest is gathered in because the fruit is ripe. Since this is the autumn, many translate that way hereвАФbut вАЬautumnвАЭ has a different connotation now. The text is pointing to a time when the righteous reaps what he has sown, and can enjoy the benefits. The translation вАЬmost productive timeвАЭ seems to capture the point better than вАЬautumnвАЭ or even вАЬprime.вАЭ
n1802914: tc The word вАП„°„Х÷є„УвАО (sod) in this verse is an infinitive construct, prefixed with the temporal preposition and followed by a subjective genitive. It forms a temporal clause. There is some disagreement about the form and its meaning. The confusion in the versions shows that they were paraphrasing to get the general sense. In the Bible the derived noun (from вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„УвАО, yasad) means (a) a circle of close friends; (b) intimacy. Others follow the LXX and the Syriac with a meaning of вАЬprotect,вАЭ based on a change from вАП„УвАО (dalet) to вАП„ЫвАО (kaf), and assuming the root was вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (sakhakh). This would mean, вАЬwhen God protected my tentвАЭ (cf. NAB). D. W. Thomas tries to justify this meaning without changing the text (вАЬThe Interpretation of BS≈МD in Job 29:4, вАЭ JBL 65 [1946]: 63-66).
n1802916: tc Some commentators suggest that вАП„Ґ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„У÷і„ЩвАО ( њimmadi, вАЬwith meвАЭ) of the second colon of v. 6 (which is too long) belongs to the second colon of v. 5, and should be pointed as the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Є„У„Х÷ЉвАО ( њamadu, вАЬthey stoodвАЭ), meaning the boys stood around him (see, e.g., E. Dhorme, Job, 417). But as R. Gordis (Job, 319) notes, there is a purpose for the imbalance of the metric pattern at the end of a section.
n1802917: tn The word is a hapax legomenon, but the meaning is clear enough. It refers to the walking, the steps, or even the paths where one walks. It is figurative of his course of life.
n1802918: tn The Hebrew word means вАЬto wash; to batheвАЭ; here it is the infinitive construct in a temporal clause, вАЬmy stepsвАЭ being the genitive: вАЬin the washing of my steps in butter.вАЭ
n1802920: tn The MT reads literally, вАЬand the rock was poured out [passive participle] for me as streams of oil.вАЭ There are some who delete the word вАЬrockвАЭ to shorten the line because it seems out of place. But olive trees thrive in rocky soil, and the oil presses are cut into the rock; it is possible that by metonymy all this is intended here (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 186).
n1802921: sn In the public square. The area referred to here should not be thought of in terms of modern western dimensions. The wide space, plaza, or public square mentioned here is the open area in the gate complex where legal and business matters were conducted. The area could be as small as a few hundred square feet.
n1802922: tn The verb means вАЬto hide; to withdraw.вАЭ The young men out of respect would withdraw or yield the place of leadership to Job (thus the translation вАЬstep asideвАЭ). The old men would rise and remain standing until Job took his seatвАФa sign of respect.
n1802923: tn The verb here is вАЬhiddenвАЭ as well as in v. 8. But this is a strange expression for voices. Several argue that the word was erroneously inserted from 8a and needs to be emended. But the word вАЬhideвАЭ can have extended meanings of вАЬwithdraw; be quiet; silentвАЭ (see Gen 31:27). A. Guillaume relates the Arabic habi Њa, вАЬthe fire dies out,вАЭ applying the idea of вАЬsilentвАЭ only to v. 10 (it is a form of repetition of words with different senses, called jinas). The point here is that whatever conversation was going on would become silent or hushed to hear what Job had to say.
n1802924: tn The words вАЬthese thingsвАЭ and вАЬthemвАЭ in the next colon are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1802925: tn The main clause is introduced by the preterite with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive (see GKC 327 ¬І111.h); the clause before it is therefore temporal and circumstantial to the main clause.
n1802926: tn The negative introduces a clause that serves as a negative attribute; literally the following clause says, вАЬand had no helperвАЭ (see GKC 482 ¬І152.u).
n1802927: tn The verb is simply вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬto come; to enterвАЭ). With the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬuponвАЭ) it could mean вАЬcame to me,вАЭ or вАЬcame upon me,вАЭ i.e., descended (see R. Gordis, Job, 320).
n1802928: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„†÷і„ЯвАО ( Њarnin) is from вАП„®÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (ranan, вАЬto give a ringing cryвАЭ) but here вАЬcause to give a ringing cry,вАЭ i.e., shout of joy. The rejoicing envisioned in this word is far greater than what the words вАЬsingвАЭ or вАЬrejoiceвАЭ suggest.
n1802929: tn Both verbs in this first half-verse are from вАП„Ь÷Є„С÷Ј„©„БвАО (lavash, вАЬto clothe; to put on clothingвАЭ). P. Jo√Љon changed the vowels to get a verb вАЬit adorned meвАЭ instead of вАЬit clothed meвАЭ (Bib 11 [1930]: 324). The figure of clothing is used for the character of the person: to wear righteousness is to be righteous.
n1802930: tn The word вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷і„ЩвАО (mishpati) is simply вАЬmy justiceвАЭ or вАЬmy judgment.вАЭ It refers to the decisions he made in settling issues, how he dealt with other people justly.
n1802931: sn The word вАЬfatherвАЭ does not have a wide range of meanings in the OT. But there are places that it is metaphorical, especially in a legal setting like this where the poor need aid.
n1802932: tn The word rendered вАЬfangsвАЭ actually means вАЬteeth,вАЭ i.e., the molars probably; it is used frequently of the teeth of wild beasts. Of course, the language is here figurative, comparing the oppressing enemy to a preying animal.
n1802933: tn вАЬI made [him] drop.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto throw; to cast,вАЭ throw in the sense of вАЬto throw away.вАЭ But in the context with the figure of the beast with prey in its mouth, вАЬdropвАЭ or вАЬcast awayвАЭ is the idea. Driver finds another cognate meaning вАЬrescueвАЭ (see AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 163).
n1802934: tc The expression in the MT is вАЬwith my nest.вАЭ The figure is satisfactory for the contextвАФa home with all the young together, a picture of unity and safety. In Isa 16:2 the word can mean вАЬnestlings,вАЭ and with the preposition вАЬwithвАЭ that might be the meaning here, except that his children had grown up and lived in their own homes. The figure cannot be pushed too far. But the verse apparently has caused enormous problems, because the versions offer a variety of readings and free paraphrases. The LXX has вАЬMy age shall grow old as the stem of a palm tree, I shall live a long time.вАЭ The Vulgate has, вАЬIn my nest I shall die and like the palm tree increase my days.вАЭ G. R. Driver found an Egyptian word meaning вАЬstrengthвАЭ (вАЬBirds in the Old Testament,вАЭ PEQ 87 [1955]: 138-39). Several read вАЬin a ripe old ageвАЭ instead of вАЬin my nestвАЭ (Pope, Dhorme; see P. P. Saydon, вАЬPhilological and Textual Notes to the Maltese Translation of the Old Testament,вАЭ CBQ 23 [1961]: 252). This requires the verb вАП„Ц÷Є„І÷Ј„ЯвАО (zaqan, вАЬbe oldвАЭ), i.e., вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Ц÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„†÷Ј„ЩвАО (bizqunay, вАЬin my old ageвАЭ) instead of вАП„І÷і„†÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (qinni, вАЬmy nestвАЭ). It has support from the LXX.
n1802935: tc For вАП„Ч„Х÷є„ЬвАО (khol, вАЬsandвАЭ) the LXX has a word that is вАЬlike the palm tree,вАЭ but which could also be translated вАЬlike the phoenixвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV). This latter idea was developed further in rabbinical teaching (see R. Gordis, Job, 321). See also M. Dahood, вАЬNest and phoenix in Job 29:18, вАЭ Bib 48 (1967): 542-44. But the MT yields an acceptable sense here.
n1802936: tn The word is вАЬmy glory,вАЭ meaning his high respect and his honor. Hoffmann proposed to read вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„У„Х÷є„ЯвАО (kidon) instead, meaning вАЬjavelinвАЭ (as in 1 Sam 17:6), to match the parallelism (RQ 3 [1961/62]: 388). But the parallelism does not need to be so tight.
n1802938: tn вАЬPeopleвАЭ is supplied; the verb is plural.
n1802939: tc The last verb of the first half, вАЬwait, hope,вАЭ and the first verb in the second colon, вАЬbe silent,вАЭ are usually reversed by the commentators (see G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 86). But if вАЬwaitвАЭ has the idea of being silent as they wait for him to speak, then the second line would say they were silent for the reason of his advice. The reading of the MT is not impossible.
n1802940: tn The verb simply means вАЬdropped,вАЭ but this means like the rain. So the picture of his words falling on them like the gentle rain, drop by drop, is what is intended (see Deut 32:2).
n1802941: tn The phrase вАЬpeople wait forвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation.
n1802942: sn The analogy is that they received his words eagerly as the dry ground opens to receive the rains.
n1802943: tn The вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) preposition is to be supplied by analogy with the preceding phrase. This leaves a double preposition, вАЬas forвАЭ (but see Job 29:2).
n1802944: tn The connection of this clause with the verse is difficult. The line simply reads: вАЬ[if] I would smile at them, they would not believe.вАЭ Obviously something has to be supplied to make sense out of this. The view adopted here makes the most sense, namely, that when he smiled at people, they could hardly believe their good fortune. Other interpretations are strained, such as KissaneвАЩs, вАЬIf I laughed at them, they believed not,вАЭ meaning, people rejected the views that Job laughed at.
n1802945: tn The meaning, according to Gordis, is that they did nothing to provoke JobвАЩs displeasure.
n1802946: tn All of these imperfects describe what Job used to do, and so they all fit the category of customary imperfect.
n1802948: tn The text simply has вАЬand I sat [as their] head.вАЭ The adverbial accusative explains his role, especially under the image of being seated. He directed the deliberations as a king directs an army.
n1802949: tc Most commentators think this last phrase is odd here, and so they either delete it altogether, or emend it to fit the idea of the verse. Ewald, however, thought it appropriate as a transition to the next section, reminding his friends that unlike him, they were miserable comforters. Herz made the few changes in the text to get the reading вАЬwhere I led them, they were willing to goвАЭ (ZAW 20 [1900]: 163). The two key words in the MT are вАП„Р÷≤„С÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„Э „Щ÷∞„†÷Ј„Ч÷µ„ЭвАО ( Њavelim yenakhem, вАЬhe [one who] comforts mournersвАЭ). Following Herz, E. Dhorme (Job, 422) has these changed to вАП„Р„Х÷є„С÷і„Щ„Ь÷µ„Э „Щ÷і„†÷Љ÷Ј„Ч„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њovilem yinnakhu). R. Gordis has вАЬlike one leading a camel trainвАЭ (Job, 324). But Kissane also retains the line as a summary of the chapter, noting its presence in the versions.
n180302: tn Heb вАЬwho I disdained their fathers to setвА¶,вАЭ meaning вАЬwhose fathers I disdained to set.вАЭ The relative clause modifies the young fellows who mock; it explains that Job did not think highly enough of them to put them with the dogs. The next verse will explain why.
n180303: sn Job is mocked by young fellows who come from low extraction. They mocked their elders and their betters. The scorn is strong hereвАФdogs were despised as scavengers.
n180304: tn The reference is to the fathers of the scorners, who are here regarded as weak and worthless.
n180305: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (kelakh) only occurs in Job 5:26, but the Arabic cognate gives this meaning вАЬstrength.вАЭ Others suggest вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (kalakh, вАЬold ageвАЭ), вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„Ь÷Њ„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО (kol khayil, вАЬall vigorвАЭ), вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„Ь÷Њ„Ь÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (kol leakh, вАЬall freshnessвАЭ), and the like. But there is no reason for such emendation.
n180306: tn This word, вАП„Т÷Ј÷Љ„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷Љ„УвАО (galmud), describes something as lowly, desolate, bare, gaunt like a rock.
n180307: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО ( њaqar) appears only here (and possibly in Job 30:17). Several translations render this as вАЬthey gnaw the dry groundвАЭ (NASB, ESV, NRSV), but it is not typical to gnaw on dirt. Suggested emendations include adding вАП„Щ÷∞„®÷Ј„ІвАО (yeraq from yereq, вАЬvegetation, greenery ofвАЭ) or вАП„Ґ÷і„І÷Є÷Љ„®÷µ„ЩвАО ( њiqqare from  њiqqar, вАЬroots of [the parched land]вАЭ), either of which could be a food to gnaw on. They propose to restore a word with letters so similar to the verb that it may have been omitted in copying due to haplography. But the verb in Aramaic can also mean вАЬto roamвАЭ (KJV вАЬfleeing into the wilderness;вАЭ NIV вАЬthey roamedвАЭ), making an emendation unnecessary (see J. Hartley, The Book of Job [NICOT], 396).
n180308: tn The MT has вАЬlast night desolate and waste.вАЭ The word вАП„Р÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„©„БвАО ( Њemesh, вАЬlast nightвАЭ or вАЬyesterdayвАЭ) is strange here. Among the proposals for вАП„Р÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„©„БвАО ( Њemesh), Duhm suggested вАП„Щ÷∞„Ю÷Ј„©÷∞÷Љ„Б„©„Б„Х÷ЉвАО (yemasheshu, вАЬthey gropeвАЭ), which would require darkness; Pope renders вАЬby night,вАЭ instead of вАЬyesterday,вАЭ which evades the difficulty; and Fohrer suggested with more reason вАП„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„•вАО ( Њerets, вАЬa desolate and waste landвАЭ). R. Gordis (Job, 331) suggests вАП„Щ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„©„Б„Х÷ЉвАО / вАП„Щ÷Є„Ю÷ї„©„Б„Х÷ЉвАО (yamishu/yamushu, вАЬthey wander offвАЭ).
n180309: tn Or вАЬthe leaves of bushesвАЭ (ESV), a possibility dating back to Saadia and discussed by G. R. Driver and G. B. Gray (Job [ICC], 2:209) in their philological notes.
n1803010: tn Here too the form is the participle with the article.
n1803011: tn Heb вАЬgather mallow,вАЭ a plant which grows in salt marshes.
n1803012: tn The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷µ„ХвАО (gev) is an Aramaic term meaning вАЬmidst,вАЭ indicating вАЬmidst [of society].вАЭ But there is also a Phoenician word that means вАЬcommunityвАЭ (DISO 48).
n1803013: tn The form simply is the plural verb, but it means those who drove them from society.
n1803014: tn The text merely says вАЬas thieves,вАЭ but it obviously compares the poor to the thieves.
n1803015: tn This use of the infinitive construct expresses that they were compelled to do something (see GKC 348-49 ¬І114.h, k).
n1803016: tn The adjectives followed by a partitive genitive take on the emphasis of a superlative: вАЬin the most horrible of valleysвАЭ (see GKC 431 ¬І133.h).
n1803017: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ф÷Ј„ІвАО (nahaq) means вАЬto bray.вАЭ It has cognates in Arabic, Aramaic, and Ugaritic, so there is no need for emendation here. It is the sign of an animalвАЩs hunger. In the translation the words вАЬlike animalsвАЭ are supplied to clarify the metaphor for the modern reader.
n1803018: tn The Pual of the verb вАП„°÷Є„§÷Ј„ЧвАО (safakh, вАЬto joinвАЭ) also brings out the passivity of these peopleвАФвАЬthey were huddled togetherвАЭ (E. Dhorme, Job, 434).
n1803019: tn The вАЬsons of the senselessвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„С÷Є„ЬвАО, naval) means they were mentally and morally base and defective; and вАЬsons of no-nameвАЭ means without honor and respect, worthless (because not named).
n1803020: tn Heb вАЬthey were whipped from the landвАЭ (cf. ESV) or вАЬthey were cast out from the landвАЭ (HALOT 697 s.v. вАП„†„Ы„РвАО). J. E. Hartley (Job [NICOT], 397) follows Gordis suggests that the meaning is вАЬbrought lower than the ground.вАЭ
n1803021: tn The idea is that Job has become proverbial, people think of misfortune and sin when they think of him. The statement uses the ordinary word for вАЬwordвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО, millah), but in this context it means more: вАЬproverb; byword.вАЭ
n1803023: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Ј„ЧвАО (patakh) means вАЬto untie [or undo]вАЭ a rope or bonds. In this verse вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„®„Х÷євАО (yitro, the Kethib, LXX, and Vulgate) would mean вАЬhis ropeвАЭ (see вАП„Щ÷ґ„™÷ґ„®вАО [yeter] in Judg 16:7-9). The Qere would be вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (yitri, вАЬmy rope [or cord]вАЭ), meaning вАЬme.вАЭ The word could mean вАЬrope,вАЭ вАЬcord,вАЭ or вАЬbowstring.вАЭ If the reading вАЬmy cordвАЭ is accepted, the cord would be something like вАЬmy tent cordвАЭ (as in Job 29:20), more than K&D 12:147 вАЬcord of life.вАЭ This has been followed in the present translation. If it were вАЬmy bowstring,вАЭ it would give the sense of disablement. If вАЬhis cordвАЭ is taken, it would signify that the restraint that God had in afflicting Job was loosenedвАФnothing was held back.
n1803024: sn People throw off all restraint in my presence means that when people saw how God afflicted Job, robbing him of his influence and power, then they turned on him with unrestrained insolence (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 193).
n1803025: tn This Hebrew word occurs only here. The word вАП„§÷Љ÷і„®÷∞„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (pirkhakh, вАЬyoung rabbleвАЭ) is a quadriliteral, from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ЧвАО (parakh, вАЬto budвАЭ) The derivative вАП„Р÷ґ„§÷∞„®÷є„Ч÷ЈвАО ( Њefroakh) in the Bible refers to a young bird. In Arabic farhun means both вАЬyoung birdвАЭ and вАЬbase man.вАЭ Perhaps вАЬyoung rabbleвАЭ is the best meaning here (see R. Gordis, Job, 333).
n1803026: tn Heb вАЬthey cast off my feetвАЭ or вАЬthey send my feet away.вАЭ Many delete the line as troubling and superfluous. E. Dhorme (Job, 438) forces the lines to say вАЬthey draw my feet into a net.вАЭ
n1803027: tn Heb вАЬpaths of their destructionвАЭ or вАЬtheir destructive paths.вАЭ
n1803029: tn This verb вАП„†÷Є„™÷∞„°„Х÷ЉвАО (natesu) is found nowhere else. It is probably a variant of the verb in Job 19:10. R. Gordis (Job, 333-34) notes the Arabic noun natsun (вАЬthornsвАЭ), suggesting a denominative idea вАЬthey have placed thorns in my path.вАЭ Most take it to mean they ruin the way of escape.
n1803030: tc The MT has вАЬthey further my misfortune.вАЭ The line is difficult, with slight textual problems. The verb вАП„Щ÷є„Ґ÷і„Щ„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (yo њilu) means вАЬto profit,вАЭ and so вАЬto succeedвАЭ or вАЬto set forward.вАЭ Good sense can be made from the MT as it stands, and many suggested changes are suspect.
n1803031: tn The sense of вАЬrestrainingвАЭ for вАЬhelpingвАЭ was proposed by Dillmann and supported by G. R. Driver (see AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 163).
n1803032: tn The MT has вАЬunder the crash,вАЭ with the idea that they rush in while the stones are falling around them (which is continuing the figure of the military attack). G. R. Driver took the expression to mean in a temporal sense вАЬat the moment of the crashвАЭ (AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 163-64). Guillaume, drawing from Arabic, has вАЬwhere the gap is made.вАЭ
n1803033: tn The verb, the Hitpalpel of вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (galal), means вАЬthey roll themselves.вАЭ This could mean вАЬthey roll themselves under the ruinsвАЭ (Dhorme), вАЬthey roll on like a stormвАЭ (Gordis), or вАЬthey roll onвАЭ as in waves of enemy attackers (see H. H. Rowley). This particular verb form is found only here (but see Amos 5:24).
n1803034: tn The passive singular verb (Hophal) is used with a plural subject (see GKC 388 ¬І121.b).
n1803035: tc This translation assumes that вАЬterrorsвАЭ (in the plural) is the subject. Others emend the text in accordance with the LXX, which has, вАЬmy hope is gone like the wind.вАЭ
n1803036: tn This line can either mean that Job is wasting away (i.e., his life is being poured out), or it can mean that he is grieving. The second half of the verse gives the subordinate clause of condition for this.
n1803037: tn The subject of the verb вАЬpiercesвАЭ can be the night (personified), or it could be God (understood), leaving вАЬnightвАЭ to be an adverbial accusative of timeвАФвАЬat night he pierces.вАЭ
n1803038: tc The MT concludes this half-verse with вАЬupon me.вАЭ That phrase is not in the LXX, and so many commentators delete it as making the line too long.
n1803039: tn Heb вАЬmy gnawers,вАЭ which is open to several interpretations. The NASB and NIV take it as вАЬgnawing painsвАЭ; cf. NRSV вАЬthe pain that gnaws me.вАЭ Some suggest worms in the sores (7:5). The LXX has вАЬmy nerves,вАЭ a view accepted by many commentators.
n1803040: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803041: tc This whole verse is difficult. The first problem is that this verb in the MT means вАЬis disguised [or disfigured],вАЭ indicating that JobвАЩs clothes hang loose on him. But many take the view that the verb is a phonetic variant of вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„©„БвАО (khavash, вАЬto bind; to seizeвАЭ) and that the Hitpael form is a conflation of the third and second person because of the interchange between them in the passage (R. Gordis, Job, 335). The commentaries list a number of conjectural emendations, but the image in the verse is probably that God seizes Job by the garment and throws him down.
n1803042: tn The phrase вАЬlike the collarвАЭ is difficult, primarily because their tunics did not have collars. A translation of вАЬneckвАЭ would suit better. Some change the preposition to вАП„СвАО (bet), getting a translation вАЬby the neck of my tunic.вАЭ
n1803043: sn The implication from the sentence is that this is a cry to God for help. The sudden change from third person (v. 19) to second person (v. 20) is indicative of the intense emotion of the sufferer.
n1803044: sn The verb is simple, but the interpretation difficult. In this verse it probably means he stands up in prayer (Jer 15:1), but it could mean that he makes his case to God. Others suggest a more figurative sense, like the English expression вАЬstand pat,вАЭ meaning вАЬremain silentвАЭ (see Job 29:8).
n1803045: tn If the idea of prayer is meant, then a pejorative sense to the verb is required. Some supply a negative and translate вАЬyou do not pay heed to me.вАЭ This is supported by one Hebrew ms and the Vulgate. The Syriac has the whole colon read with God as the subject, вАЬyou stand and look at me.вАЭ
n1803046: tn The idiom uses the Niphal verb вАЬyou are turnedвАЭ with вАЬto cruelty.вАЭ See Job 41:20b, as well as Isa 63:10.
n1803047: tc The LXX reads this verb as вАЬyou scourged/whipped me.вАЭ But there is no reason to adopt this change.
n1803048: sn Here Job changes the metaphor again, to the driving storm. God has sent his storms, and Job is blown away.
n1803049: tn The verb means вАЬto melt.вАЭ The imagery would suggest softening the ground with the showers (see Ps 65:10 [11]). The translation вАЬtossвА¶aboutвАЭ comes from the Arabic cognate that is used for the surging of the sea.
n1803050: tc The Qere is вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„©„Б÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tushiyyah, вАЬcounselвАЭ), which makes no sense here. The Kethib is a variant orthography for вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„©„Б÷ї„Р÷Є„ФвАО (teshu Њah, вАЬstormвАЭ).
n1803051: tn The imperfect verb would be a progressive imperfect, it is future, but it is also already underway.
n1803052: tc Here is another very difficult verse, as the differences among commentaries and translations attest. The MT has вАЬsurely not against a ruinous heap will he [God] put forth his [GodвАЩs] hand.вАЭ But A. B. Davidson takes Job as the subject, reading вАЬdoes not one stretch out his hand in his fall?вАЭ The RSV suggests a man walking in the ruins and using his hand for support. Dillmann changed it to вАЬdrowning manвАЭ to say вАЬdoes not a drowning man stretch out his hand?вАЭ Beer has вАЬhave I not given a helping hand to the poor?вАЭ Dhorme has, вАЬI did not strike the poor man with my hand.вАЭ Kissane follows this but retains the verb form, вАЬone does not strike the poor man with his hand.вАЭ
n1803053: tc The second colon is also difficult; it reads, вАЬif in his destruction to them he cries.вАЭ E. Dhorme (Job, 425-26) explains how he thinks вАЬto themвАЭ came about, and he restores вАЬto me.вАЭ This is the major difficulty in the line, and DhormeвАЩs suggestion is the simplest resolution.
n1803055: tn Heb вАЬmy loins,вАЭ вАЬmy bowelsвАЭ (archaic), вАЬmy innermost being.вАЭ The last option is reflected in the translation; some translations take the inner turmoil to be literal (NIV вАЬThe churning inside me never stopsвАЭ).
n1803057: tn The last clause reads вАЬand they [it] are not quietвАЭ or вАЬdo not cease.вАЭ The clause then serves adverbially for the sentenceвАФвАЬunceasingly.вАЭ
n1803058: tn The construction uses the word вАП„І÷є„У÷µ„®вАО (qoder) followed by the Piel perfect of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh, вАЬI go aboutвАЭ). The adjective вАЬblackenedвАЭ refers to JobвАЩs skin that has been marred by the disease. Adjectives are often used before verbs to describe some bodily condition (see GKC 374-75 ¬І118.n).
n1803059: sn The point of this figure is that JobвАЩs cries of lament are like the howls and screeches of these animals, not that he lives with them. In Job 39:13 the female ostrich is called вАЬthe wailer.вАЭ
n1803060: tn The MT has вАЬbecome dark from upon me,вАЭ prompting some editions to supply the verb вАЬfalls from meвАЭ (RSV, NRSV), or вАЬpeelsвАЭ (NIV).
n1803061: tn The word вАЬmy bonesвАЭ may be taken as a metonymy of subject, the bony framework indicating the whole body.
n1803062: tn The word вАП„Ч÷є„®÷ґ„СвАО (khorev) also means вАЬheat.вАЭ The heat in this line is not that of the sun, but obviously a fever.
n1803063: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah, вАЬto beвАЭ) followed by the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) means вАЬto serve the purpose ofвАЭ (see Gen 1:14ff., 17:7, etc.).
n180311: tn The idea of cutting a covenant for something may suggest a covenant that is imposed, except that this construction elsewhere argues against it (see 2 Chr 29:10).
n180312: tn This half-verse is the effect of the covenant. The interrogative вАП„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mah) may have the force of the negative, and so be translated вАЬnot to pay attention.вАЭ
n180313: tn Heb вАЬlot of Shaddai,вАЭ which must mean вАЬthe lot from Shaddai,вАЭ a genitive of source.
n180314: tn The normal approach is to take this as the protasis, and then have it resumed in v. 7 after a parenthesis in v. 6. But some take v. 6 as the apodosis and a new protasis in v. 7.
n180315: tn The вАЬifвАЭ is understood by the use of the consecutive verb.
n180316: sn The verbs вАЬwalkвАЭ and вАЬhastenвАЭ (referring in the verse to the foot) are used metaphorically for the manner of life Job lived.
n180317: tn вАЬGodвАЭ is undoubtedly the understood subject of this jussive. However, вАЬhimвАЭ is retained in the translation at this point to avoid redundancy since вАЬGodвАЭ occurs in the second half of the verse.
n180318: tn The word вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (tsedeq, вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ) forms a fitting genitive for the scales used in trade or justice. The вАЬscales of righteousnessвАЭ are scales that conform to the standard (see the illustration in Deut 25:13-15). They must be honest scales to make just decisions.
n180319: tn The verb is вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷µ„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (veyeda њ, вАЬand [then] he [God] will knowвАЭ). The verb could also be subordinated to the preceding jussive, вАЬso that God may know.вАЭ The meaning of вАЬto knowвАЭ here has more the idea of вАЬto come to know; to discover.вАЭ
n1803110: sn The meaning is вАЬbeen led by what my eyes see.вАЭ
n1803111: tc The word вАП„Ю÷ї„Р„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (mu Њum) could be taken in one of two ways. One reading is to represent вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (mum, вАЬblemish,вАЭ see the Masorah); the other is for вАП„Ю÷∞„Р„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (me Њumah, вАЬanything,вАЭ see the versions and the Kethib). Either reading fits the passage.
n1803112: tn The cohortative is often found in the apodosis of the conditional clause (see GKC 320 ¬І108.f).
n1803113: tn The word means вАЬwhat sprouts upвАЭ (from вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО [yatsa Њ] with the sense of вАЬsprout forthвАЭ). It could refer metaphorically to children (and so Kissane and Pope), as well as in its literal sense of crops. The latter fits here perfectly.
n1803114: tn Gordis notes that the word вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„™÷Ј„ЧвАО (petakh, вАЬdoorвАЭ) has sexual connotations in rabbinic literature, based on Prov 7:6ff. (see b. Ketubbot 9b). See also the use in Song 4:12 using a synonym.
n1803115: tn Targum Job interpreted the verb вАП„Ш÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЯвАО (takhan, вАЬgrindвАЭ) in a sexual sense, and this has influenced other versions and commentaries. But the literal sense fits well in this line. The idea is that she would be a slave for someone else (or someone elseвАЩs wife). The second line of the verse then might build on this to explain what kind of a slaveвАФa concubine (see A. B. Davidson, Job, 215).
n1803116:
tn Heb вАЬkneel down over her,вАЭ an idiom for sexual relations.
sn The idea is that if Job were guilty of adultery it would be an offense against the other womanвАЩs husband, and so by talionic justice another manвАЩs adultery with JobвАЩs wife would be an offense against him. He is not wishing something on his wife; rather, he is simply looking at what would be offenses in kind.
n1803117: tn Heb вАЬfor that [would be].вАЭ In order to clarify the referent of вАЬthat,вАЭ which refers to v. 9 rather than v. 10, the words вАЬI have committedвАЭ have been supplied in the translation.
n1803118: tn The word for вАЬshameful actвАЭ is used especially for sexual offenses (cf. Lev 18:27).
n1803119:
tc Some have deleted this verse as being short and irrelevant, not to mention problematic. But the difficulties are not insurmountable, and there is no reason to delete it. There is a Kethib-Qere reading in each half verse; in the first the Kethib is masculine for the subject but the Qere is feminine going with вАЬshameless deed.вАЭ In the second colon the Kethib is the feminine agreeing with the preceding noun, but the Qere is masculine agreeing with вАЬiniquity.вАЭ
tn The expression вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷є„Я „§÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( њavon pelilim) means вАЬan iniquity of the judges.вАЭ The first word is not spelled as a construct noun, and so this has led some to treat the second word as an adjective (with enclitic mem [вАП„ЭвАО]). The sense is similar in either case, for the adjective occurs in Job 31:28 meaning вАЬcalling for judgmentвАЭ (See GKC 427 ¬І131.s).
n1803121: tn The verb means вАЬto root out,вАЭ but this does not fit the parallelism with fire. Wright changed two letters and the vowels in the verb to get the root вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„£вАО (tsaraf, вАЬto burnвАЭ). The NRSV has вАЬburn to the root.вАЭ
n1803122: tn This construction is an adverbial clause using the temporal preposition, the infinitive from вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬcontendвАЭ), and the suffix which is the subjective genitive.
n1803123: tn Heb вАЬarises.вАЭ The LXX reads вАЬtakes vengeance,вАЭ an interpretation that is somewhat correct but unnecessary. The verb вАЬto riseвАЭ would mean вАЬto confront in judgment.вАЭ
n1803124: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) means вАЬto visit,вАЭ but with God as the subject it means any divine intervention for blessing or cursing, anything God does that changes a personвАЩs life. Here it is вАЬvisit to judge.вАЭ
n1803125: tn Heb вАЬhim,вАЭ but the plural pronoun has been used in the translation to indicate that the referent is the servants mentioned in v. 13 (since the previous вАЬhimвАЭ in v. 14 refers to God).
n1803127: tn Heb вАЬand an orphan did not eat from it.вАЭ
n1803128: tn Heb вАЬhe grew up with me.вАЭ Several commentators have decided to change the pronoun to вАЬI,вАЭ and make it causative.
n1803129: tn The expression вАЬfrom my motherвАЩs wombвАЭ is obviously hyperbolic. It is a way of saying вАЬall his life.вАЭ
n1803130: tn Heb вАЬI guided her,вАЭ referring to the widow mentioned in v. 16.
n1803131: tn The MT has simply вАЬif his loins did not bless me.вАЭ In the conditional clause this is another protasis. It means, вАЬif I saw someone dying and if he did not thank me for clothing them.вАЭ It is JobвАЩs way of saying that whenever he saw a need he met it, and he received his share of thanksвАФwhich prove his kindness. G. R. Driver has it вАЬwithout his loins having blessed me,вАЭ taking вАЬIfвА¶notвАЭ as an Aramaism, meaning вАЬexceptвАЭ (AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 164f.).
n1803132: tn This clause is interpreted here as a subordinate clause to the first half of the verse. It could also be a separate clause: вАЬwas he not warmedвА¶?вАЭ
n1803133: tn The expression вАЬraised my handвАЭ refers to a threatening manner or gesture in the court rather than a threat of physical violence in the street. Thus the words вАЬto voteвАЭ are supplied in the translation to indicate the setting.
n1803134: tn Heb вАЬgate,вАЭ referring to the city gate where judicial decisions were rendered in the culture of the time. The translation uses the word вАЬcourtвАЭ to indicate this to the modern reader, who might not associate a city gate complex with judicial functions.
n1803135: sn Here is the apodosis, the imprecation Job pronounces on himself if he has done any of these things just listed.
n1803136: tn The point is that if he has raised his arm against the oppressed it should be ripped off at the joint. The MT has вАЬlet fall my shoulder [вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„™÷µ„§÷і„ЩвАО, ketefi] from the nape of the neck [or shoulder blade (вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷Љ÷і„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО, mishikhmah)].вАЭ
n1803137: tn The word вАП„І÷Є„†÷ґ„ФвАО (qaneh) is вАЬreed; shaft; beam,вАЭ and here вАЬshoulder joint.вАЭ All the commentaries try to explain how вАЬreedвАЭ became вАЬsocket; joint.вАЭ This is the only place that it is used in such a sense. Whatever the exact explanationвАФand there seems to be no convincing viewвАФthe point of the verse is nonetheless clear.
n1803138: tc The LXX has вАЬFor the terror of God restrained me.вАЭ Several commentators changed it to вАЬcame upon me.вАЭ Driver had вАЬThe fear of God was burdensome.вАЭ I. Eitan suggested вАЬThe terror of God was mighty upon meвАЭ (вАЬTwo unknown verbs: etymological studies,вАЭ JBL 42 [1923]: 22-28). But the MT makes clear sense as it stands.
n1803139: tn The form is вАП„Х÷Љ„Ю÷і„©„В÷Љ÷∞„Р÷µ„™„Х÷євАО (umisse Њeto); the preposition is causal. The form, from the verb вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Є„РвАО (nasa Њ, вАЬto raise; to lift highвАЭ), refers to GodвАЩs exalted person, his majesty (see Job 13:11).
n1803140: tn Heb вАЬlight,вАЭ but parallel to the moon it is the sun. This section speaks of false worship of the sun and the moon.
n1803141: tn Heb вАЬand my hand kissed my mouth.вАЭ The idea should be that of вАЬmy mouth kissed my hand.вАЭ H. H. Rowley suggests that the hand was important in waving or throwing the kisses of homage to the sun and the moon, and so it receives the focus. This is the only place in the OT that refers to such a custom. Outside the Bible it was known, however.
n1803143: tn See v. 11 for the construction. In Deut 17:2ff. false worship of heavenly bodies is a capital offense. In this passage, Job is talking about just a momentary glance at the sun or moon and the brief lapse into a pagan thought. But it is still sin.
n1803144: tn The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„©„БвАО (kakhash) in the Piel means вАЬto deny.вАЭ The root meaning is вАЬto deceive; to disappoint; to grow lean.вАЭ Here it means that he would have failed or proven unfaithful because his act would have been a denial of God.
n1803145: tn The problem with taking this as вАЬif,вАЭ introducing a conditional clause, is finding the apodosis, if there is one. It may be that the apodosis is understood, or summed up at the end. This is the view taken here. But R. Gordis (Job, 352) wishes to take this word as the indication of the interrogative, forming the rhetorical question to affirm he has never done this. However, in that case the parenthetical verses inserted become redundant.
n1803146: sn The law required people to help their enemies if they could (Exod 23:4; also Prov 20:22). But often in the difficulties that ensued, they did exult over their enemiesвАЩ misfortune (Pss 54:7; 59:10 [11], etc.). But Job lived on a level of purity that few ever reach. Duhm said, вАЬIf chapter 31 is the crown of all ethical developments of the O.T., verse 29 is the jewel in that crown.вАЭ
n1803147: tn The Hitpael of вАП„Ґ„Х÷Љ„®вАО ( њur) has the idea of вАЬexult.вАЭ
n1803148: tn The word is вАП„®÷Є„ҐвАО (ra њ, вАЬevilвАЭ) in the sense of anything that harms, interrupts, or destroys life.
n1803149: tn This verse would then be a parenthesis in which he stops to claim his innocence.
n1803150: tn Heb вАЬI have not given my palate.вАЭ
n1803151: tn The infinitive construct with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition (вАЬby askingвАЭ) serves in an epexegetical capacity here, explaining the verb of the first colon (вАЬpermittedвА¶to sinвАЭ). To seek a curse on anyone would be a sin.
n1803152: tn Now Job picks up the series of clauses serving as the protasis.
n1803153: tn Heb вАЬthe men of my tent.вАЭ In context this refers to members of JobвАЩs household.
n1803154: sn The line is difficult to sort out. Job is saying it is sinful вАЬif his men have never said, вАШO that there was one who has not been satisfied from his food.вАЩвАЭ If they never said that, it would mean there were people out there who needed to be satisfied with his food.
n1803155: tn The optative is again expressed with вАЬwho will give?вАЭ
n1803156: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (Job) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803157: tn This verse forms another parenthesis. Job stops almost at every point now in the conditional clauses to affirm his purity and integrity.
n1803158: tn Or вАЬ[resident] foreigner.вАЭ The term вАП„Т÷µ÷Љ„®вАО (ger) refers to a foreign resident, but with different social implications in different settings. Here the вАЬstrangerвАЭ stands in need of the hospitality of lodgings.
n1803159: tn The word in the MT, вАП„Р÷є„®„Ч÷ЈвАО ( Њorakh, вАЬwayвАЭ), is a contraction from вАП„Р÷є„®÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО ( Њoreakh, вАЬwayfarerвАЭ); thus, вАЬtraveler.вАЭ The same parallelism is found in Jer 14:8. The reading here вАЬon/to the roadвАЭ is meaningless otherwise.
n1803161: sn Some commentators suggest taking the meaning here to be вАЬas Adam,вАЭ referring to the Paradise story of the sin and denial.
n1803162: tn The infinitive is epexegetical, explaining the first line.
n1803163: tn The MT has вАЬin my bosom.вАЭ This is the only place in the OT where this word is found. But its meaning is well attested from Aramaic.
n1803164: tn Here too the verb will be the customary imperfectвАФit explains what he continually did in past time.
n1803165: tn Heb вАЬthe great multitude.вАЭ But some commentators take вАП„®÷Ј„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (rabbah) adverbially: вАЬgreatlyвАЭ (see RSV).
n1803166: tn The optative is again introduced with вАЬwho will give to me hearing me?вАЭвАФвАЬO that someone would listen to me!вАЭ
n1803167: tn Heb вАЬhere is my вАШtavвАЩвАЭ (вАП„Ф÷µ„Я „™÷Љ÷Є„Х÷і„ЩвАО, hen tavi). The letter вАП„™вАО (tav) is the last letter of the alphabet in Hebrew. In paleo-Hebrew the letter was in the form of a cross or an вАЬX,вАЭ and so used for one making a mark or a signature. In this case Job has signed his statement and delivered it to the courtвАФbut he has yet to be charged. Kissane thought that this being the last letter of the alphabet, Job was saying, вАЬThis is my last word.вАЭ Others take the word to mean вАЬdesireвАЭвАФвАЬthis is my desire, that God would answer meвАЭ (see E. F. Sutcliffe, вАЬNotes on Job, textual and exegetical,вАЭ Bib 30 [1949]: 71-72; G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in Job,вАЭ AJSL 52 [1935/36]: 166; P. P. Saydon, вАЬPhilological and Textual Notes to the Maltese Translation of the Old Testament,вАЭ CBQ 23 [1961]: 252). R. Gordis (Job, 355) also argues strongly for this view.
n1803168: tn Heb вАЬa scroll,вАЭ in the context referring to a scroll containing the accusations of JobвАЩs legal adversary (see the next line).
n1803169: tn The last line is very difficult; it simply says, вАЬa scroll [that] my [legal] adversary had written.вАЭ The simplest way to handle this is to see it as a continuation of the optative (RSV).
n1803170: tn The clause begins with the positive oath formula, вАП„Р÷і„Э÷Њ„Ь÷є„РвАО ( Њim lo Њ).
n1803171: tn The word вАЬproudlyвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied (note the following line).
n1803172: tn This verb is only found in Prov 6:21. But E. Dhorme (Job, 470) suggests that (with metathesis) we have a derivative вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„У÷Ј„†÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (ma њadannot, вАЬbonds; tiesвАЭ) in 38:31.
n1803173: sn Many commentators place vv. 38-40b at the end of v. 34, so that there is no return to these conditional clauses after his final appeal.
n1803174: sn Some commentators have suggested that the meaning behind this is that Job might not have kept the year of release (Deut 15:1), and the law against mixing seed (Lev 19:19). But the context will make clear that the case considered is obtaining the land without paying for it and causing the death of its lawful owner (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 206). Similar to this would be the case of NabothвАЩs vineyard.
n1803176: tc The versions have the verb вАЬgrievedвАЭ here. The Hebrew verb means вАЬto breathe,вАЭ but the form is Hiphil. This verb in that stem could mean something of a contemptuous gesture, like вАЬsniffвАЭ in Mal 1:13. But with вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) in Job 11:20 it means вАЬto cause death,вАЭ i.e., вАЬto cause to breathe out; to expire.вАЭ This is likely the meaning here, although it is possible that it only meant вАЬto cause sufferingвАЭ to the people.
n1803177: tn There is some debate over the meaning of вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (be њaleha), usually translated вАЬits owners.вАЭ Dahood, following others (although without their emendations), thought it referred to вАЬlaborersвАЭ (see M. Dahood, Bib 41 [1960]: 303; idem, Bib 43 [1962]: 362).
n1803178: tn The word вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷∞„©„Б÷Є„ФвАО (bo Њshah, from вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Р÷Ј„©„БвАО [ba Њas, вАЬto have a foul smellвАЭ]) must refer to foul smelling weeds.
n180321: sn There are now four speeches from another friend of Job, Elihu. But Job does not reply to any of these, nor does the Lord. The speeches show a knowledge of the debate that has gone on, but they take a different approach entirely. ElihuвАЩs approach is that suffering is a discipline from God, to teach his people. In other words, Job was suffering to vindicate GodвАЩs confidence in him. His speeches are an interesting part of the book, but they too are irrelevant to JobвАЩs actual case. In the first speech, there is a short introduction (32:1-5), and then the speech proper with these sections: Elihu will speak because his youth is wiser (32:6-14), and his friends arguments failed (32:15-22); he calls for JobвАЩs attention (33:1-7), claims JobвАЩs case is wrong (33:8-13), and JobвАЩs argument that God does not answer is false (33:14-28), and then makes an appeal to Job (33:29-33). It becomes evident that Elihu correctly identified JobвАЩs determination to maintain his integrity at GodвАЩs expense as the primary problem in at least the latter stages of the dialogues (32:1-3; 34:37; 35:16; cf. 38:2; 40:8; 42:3). Elihu was respectful of his elders (32:4), but remained uninfected by their error (32:14). He sought to maintain impartiality (32:21-22) and to offer true wisdom (33:33), believed like Job that a mediator existed (33:23-24), and desired JobвАЩs vindication (33:32). In addition, Elihu focused on vindicating GodвАЩs actions (34:12; 35:10-11; 36:2-3, 22-26) and announced the coming theophany (37:1-5, 22). It appears that he was not included in the divine condemnation of JobвАЩs friends (42:7-9) and was excluded from JobвАЩs prayer of intercession (42:8-10)вАФboth perhaps implying divine approval of his behavior and words.
n180322: tn The form is the infinitive construct (вАЬanswerвАЭ) functioning as the object of the preposition; the phrase forms the complement of the verb вАЬthey ceased to answerвАЭ (= вАЬthey refused to answer furtherвАЭ).
n180323: tc The LXX, Syriac, and Symmachus have вАЬin their eyes.вАЭ This is adopted by some commentators, but it does not fit the argument.
n180324: tn The verse begins with вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Ч÷Ј„® „Р÷Ј„£вАО (vayyikhar  Њaf, вАЬand the anger became hotвАЭ), meaning Elihu became very angry.
n180325: tn The second comment about ElihuвАЩs anger comes right before the statement of its cause. Now the perfect verb is used: вАЬhe was angry.вАЭ
n180326: tn The explanation is the causal clause вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷∞„І„Х÷є „†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б„Х÷євАО ( њal-tsaddeqo nafsho, вАЬbecause he justified himselfвАЭ). It is the preposition with the Piel infinitive construct with a suffixed subjective genitive.
n180327: tc The LXX and Latin versions soften the expression slightly by saying вАЬbefore God.вАЭ
n180328: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (Job) has been specified in the translation to indicate whose friends they were.
n180329: tn The perfect verb should be given the category of potential perfect here.
n1803210: tc This is one of the eighteen вАЬcorrections of the scribesвАЭ (tiqqune sopherim); it originally read, вАЬand they declared God [in the wrong].вАЭ The thought was that in abandoning the debate they had conceded JobвАЩs point.
n1803211: tc This reading requires repointing the word вАП„С÷Љ÷і„У÷∞„С÷Є„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (bidevarim, вАЬwith wordsвАЭ) to вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„У÷Ј„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷Є„ЭвАО (bedabberam, вАЬwhile they spoke [with Job]вАЭ). If the MT is retained, it would mean вАЬhe waited for Job with words,вАЭ which while understandable is awkward.
n1803212: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ; the referent (the other friends) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803213: tn The first clause beginning with a vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive and the preterite can be subordinated to the next similar verb as a temporal clause.
n1803214: tn Heb вАЬthat there was no reply in the mouth of the three men.вАЭ
n1803216: tn The text has вАЬsmall in days.вАЭ
n1803217: tn The verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (zakhalti) is found only here in the OT, but it is found in a ninth century Aramaic inscription as well as in Biblical Aramaic. It has the meaning вАЬto be timidвАЭ (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 208).
n1803218: tn The Piel infinitive with the preposition (вАП„Ю÷µ„Ч÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷є„™вАО, mekhavvot) means вАЬfrom explaining.вАЭ The phrase is the complement: вАЬexplainвАЭ what Elihu feared.
n1803220: tn The imperfect here is to be classified as an obligatory imperfect.
n1803222: tn This is the word вАП„†÷∞„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (neshamah, вАЬbreathвАЭ); according to Gen 2:7 it was breathed into Adam to make him a living person (вАЬsoulвАЭ). With that divine impartation came this spiritual understanding. Some commentators identify the вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh) in the first line as the Spirit of God; this вАЬbreathвАЭ would then be the human spirit. Whether Elihu knew that much, however, is hard to prove.
n1803223: tn The MT has вАЬthe greatвАЭ or вАЬthe many,вАЭ meaning great in years according to the parallelism.
n1803224: tc In most Hebrew mss this imperative is singular, and so addressed to Job. But two Hebrew mss and the versions have the plural. Elihu was probably addressing all of them.
n1803226: tn The word means вАЬunderstanding.вАЭ It refers to the faculty of perception and comprehension, but it also can refer to what that produces, especially when it is in the plural (see Ps 49:4). See R. Gordis, Job, 368. Others translate it вАЬreasonings,вАЭ вАЬarguments,вАЭ etc.
n1803227: tn The verb again is from вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin, вАЬto perceive; to understandвАЭ); in this stem it means to вАЬto pay close attention.вАЭ
n1803228: tn The particle вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) has a deictic force here, calling attention to the thought that is now presented.
n1803229: tn The participle вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ы÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (mokhiakh) is from the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh) that has been used frequently in the book of Job. It means вАЬto argue; to contend; to debate; to prove; to dispute.вАЭ The usage of the verb shows that it can focus on the beginning of an argument, the debating itself, or the resolution of the conflict. Here the latter is obviously meant, for they did argue and contend and criticizeвАФbut could not prove Job wrong.
n1803230: tn Heb вАЬlest you say.вАЭ R. Gordis (Job, 368) calls this a breviloquence: вАЬbeware lest [you say].вАЭ He then suggests the best reading for their quote to be, вАЬWe have attained wisdom, but only God can refute him, not man.вАЭ H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 209) suggests the meaning is a little different, namely, that they are saying they have found wisdom in Job, and only God can deal with it. Elihu is in effect saying that they do not need God, for he is quite capable for this.
n1803231: tn The root is вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„£вАО (nadaf, вАЬto drive away; to drive offвАЭ). Here it is in the abstract sense of вАЬsucceed in doing something; confound,вАЭ and so вАЬrefute; rebut.вАЭ Dhorme wants to change the meaning of the word with a slight emendation in the text, deriving it from вАП„Р÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО ( Њalaf, вАЬinstructвАЭ) the form becoming вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷∞„§÷ґ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (yallefenu) instead of вАП„Щ÷і„У÷Љ÷∞„§÷ґ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yiddefenu), obtaining the translation вАЬGod will instruct us.вАЭ This makes a smoother reading, but does not have much support for it.
n1803232: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (Job) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803233: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Ј„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( њarakh) means вАЬto arrange in order; to set forth; to direct; to marshal.вАЭ It is used in military contexts for setting the battle array; it is used in legal settings for preparing the briefs.
n1803235: sn Elihu now will give another reason why he will speakвАФthe arguments of these friends failed miserably. But before he gets to his argument, he will first qualify his authority.
n1803236: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (khattu) is from вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„™вАО (khatat) which means вАЬto be terrified.вАЭ But here it stresses the resulting dilemma. R. Gordis (Job, 369) renders it, вАЬthey are shattered, beaten in an argument.вАЭ
n1803237: tn Heb вАЬwords have moved away from them,вАЭ meaning words are gone from them, they have nothing left to say.
n1803238: tn Some commentators take this as a question: вАЬAnd shall [or must] I wait because they do not speak?вАЭ (A. B. Davidson, R. Gordis). But this is not convincing because the silence of the friends is the reason for him to speak, not to wait.
n1803240: tn The verb вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (tsuq) means вАЬto constrain; to urge; to press.вАЭ It is used in Judg 14:17; 16:16 with the sense of wearing someone down with repeated entreaties. Elihu cannot restrain himself any longer.
n1803241: tn Heb вАЬin my belly I am like wine that is not openedвАЭ (a Niphal imperfect), meaning sealed up with no place to escape.
n1803242: tc The Hebrew text has вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Р÷є„С„Х÷є„™ „Ч÷≤„У÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ke Њovot khadashim), traditionally rendered вАЬlike new wineskins.вАЭ But only here does the phrase have this meaning. The LXX has вАЬsmithsвАЭ for вАЬnew,вАЭ thus вАЬlike smithвАЩs bellows.вАЭ A. Guillaume connects the word with an Arabic word for a wide vessel for wine shaped like a cup (вАЬArchaeological and philological note on Job 32:19, вАЭ PEQ 93 [1961]: 147-50). Some have been found in archaeological sites. The poor would use skins, the rich would use jars. The key to putting this together is the verb at the end of the line, вАП„Щ÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„І÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yibbaqea њ, вАЬthat are ready to burstвАЭ). The point of the statement is that Elihu is bursting to speak, and until now has not had the opening.
n1803243: tn The cohortative expresses ElihuвАЩs resolve to speak.
n1803244: tn The idiom is вАЬI will not lift up the face of a man.вАЭ Elihu is going to show no favoritism, but speak his mind.
n1803245: tn The verb means вАЬto confer an honorary title; to give a mark of distinction,вАЭ but it is often translated with the verb вАЬflatter.вАЭ Elihu will not take sides; he will not use pompous titles.
n1803246: tn The construction uses a perfect verb followed by the imperfect. This is a form of subordination equivalent to a complementary infinitive (see GKC 385-86 ¬І120.c).
n1803247: tn The words вАЬif I didвАЭ are supplied in the translation to make sense out of the two clauses.
n180331: tn Heb вАЬgive ear,вАЭ the Hiphil denominative verb from вАЬear.вАЭ
n180333: tn The perfect verbs in this verse should be classified as perfects of resolve: вАЬI have decided to openвА¶speak.вАЭ
n180334: sn H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 210) says, вАЬThe self-importance of Elihu is boundless, and he is the master of banality.вАЭ He adds that whoever wrote these speeches this way clearly intended to expose the character rather than exalt him.
n180335: tc This expression is unusual; R. Gordis (Job, 371) says it can be translated, вАЬthe purity of my heart [is reflected] in my words,вАЭ but that is far-fetched and awkward. So there have been suggestions for emending вАП„Щ÷є„©„Б÷ґ„®вАО (yosher, вАЬuprightnessвАЭ). KissaneвАЩs makes the most sense if a change is desired: вАЬshall revealвАЭ (an Arabic sense of yasher), although Holscher interpreted вАЬshall affirmвАЭ (yasher, with a Syriac sense). Dhorme has вАЬmy heart will repeatвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷Љ„®вАО, yashur), but this is doubtful. If KissaneвАЩs view is taken, it would say, вАЬmy heart will reveal my words.вАЭ Some commentators would join вАЬand knowledgeвАЭ to this colon, and read вАЬwords of knowledgeвАЭвАФbut that requires even more emendations.
n180336: tn More literally, вАЬand the knowledge of my lips they will speak purely.вАЭ
n180337:
tc Some commentators want to put this verse after v. 6, while others omit the verse entirely. Elihu is claiming here that he is inspired by God.
tn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷µ„†÷і„ЩвАО (tekhayyeni) is the Piel imperfect of the verb вАЬto live.вАЭ It can mean вАЬgives me life,вАЭ but it can also mean вАЬquickens me, enlivens me.вАЭ
n180338: tn The Hebrew text does not contain the term вАЬarguments,вАЭ but this verb has been used already for preparing or arranging a defense.
n180339: tn The verb means вАЬnipped off,вАЭ as a potter breaks off a piece of clay when molding a vessel.
n1803310: tc The noun means вАЬmy pressure; my burdenвАЭ in the light of the verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ы÷≤„£вАО ( Њakhaf, вАЬto press on; to grip tightlyвАЭ). In the parallel passages the text used вАЬhandвАЭ and вАЬrodвАЭ in the hand to terrify. The LXX has вАЬhandвАЭ here for this word. But simply changing it to вАЬhandвАЭ is ruled out because the verb is masculine.
n1803314: tn The word is a hapax legomenon; вАП„Ч÷Ј„£вАО (khaf) is from вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„£вАО (khafaf). It is used in New Hebrew in expressions like вАЬto washвАЭ the head. Cognates in Syriac and Akkadian support the meaning вАЬto wash; to clean.вАЭ
n1803316: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803317: tn The Hebrew means вАЬfrustrationsвАЭ or вАЬoppositions.вАЭ The RSV has вАЬdispleasure,вАЭ NIV вАЬfaults,вАЭ and NRSV вАЬoccasions.вАЭ Rashi chose the word found in Judg 14:4вАФwith metathesisвАФmeaning вАЬpretextsвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Р÷≤„†„Х÷є„™вАО, to Њanot); this is followed by NAB, NASB.
n1803319: tn The meaning of this verb is вАЬthis is my answer to you.вАЭ
n1803320: tc The LXX has вАЬhe that is above men is eternal.вАЭ Elihu is saying that God is far above JobвАЩs petty problems.
n1803321: tc The MT has вАЬall his words.вАЭ This must refer to вАЬmanвАЭ in the previous verse. But many wish to change it to вАЬmy words,вАЭ since it would be summarizing JobвАЩs complaint to God.
n1803322: tn The Syriac and the Vulgate have вАЬand he does not repeat it,вАЭ a reading of the text as it is, according to E. Dhorme (Job, 403). But his argument is based on another root with this meaningвАФa root which does not exist (see L. Dennefeld, RB 48 [1939]: 175). The verse is saying that God does speak to man.
n1803323: tn The idiom is вАЬhe uncovers the ear of men.вАЭ This expression means вАЬinformвАЭ in Ruth 4:4; 1 Sam 20:2, etc. But when God is the subject it means вАЬmake a revelationвАЭ (see 1 Sam 9:15; 2 Sam 7:27).
n1803324: tc Heb вАЬand seals their bonds.вАЭ The form of the present translation, вАЬand terrifies them with warnings,вАЭ is derived only by emending the text. Aquila, the Vulgate, Syriac, and Targum Job have вАЬtheir correctionвАЭ for вАЬtheir bond,вАЭ which is what the KJV used. But the LXX, Aquila, and the Syriac have вАЬterrifiesвАЭ for the verb. This involves a change in pointing from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷∞„™÷Љ÷є„ЭвАО (yakhtom) to вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЭвАО (yekhittem). The LXX has вАЬappearances of fearвАЭ instead of вАЬbonds.вАЭ The point of the verse seems to be that by terrifying dreams God makes people aware of their ways.
n1803325: tc The MT simply has вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„©„В÷ґ„ФвАО (ma њaseh, вАЬdeedвАЭ). The LXX has вАЬfrom his iniquityвАЭ which would have been вАП„Ю÷µ„Ґ÷Ј„Х÷∞„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (me њavlah). The two letters may have dropped out by haplography. The MT is workable, but would have to mean вАЬ[evil] deeds.вАЭ
n1803326: tc Here too the sense of the MT is difficult to recover. Some translations took it to mean that God hides pride from man. Many commentators changed вАП„Щ÷∞„Ы÷Ј„°÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (yekhasseh, вАЬcoversвАЭ) to вАП„Щ÷∞„Ы÷Ј„°÷Љ÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (yekhasseakh, вАЬhe cuts awayвАЭ), or вАП„Щ÷∞„Ы÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (yekhalleh, вАЬhe puts an end toвАЭ). The various emendations are not all that convincing.
n1803327: tn A number of interpreters and translations take this as вАЬthe pitвАЭ (see Job 17:14; cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n1803328: tc Here is another difficult line. The verb normally means вАЬto pass through; to pass over,вАЭ and so this word would normally mean вАЬfrom passing through [or over].вАЭ The word вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shelakh) does at times refer to a weapon, but most commentators look for a parallel with вАЬthe pit [or corruption].вАЭ One suggestion is вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (she Њolah, вАЬto SheolвАЭ), proposed by Duhm. Dhorme thought it was вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shalakh) and referred to the passageway to the underworld (see M. Tsevat, VT 4 [1954]: 43; and Svi Rin, BZ 7 [1963]: 25). See discussion of options in HALOT 1517-18 s.v. IV вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО. The idea of crossing the river of death fits the idea of the passage well, although the reading вАЬto perish by the swordвАЭ makes sense and was followed by the NIV.
n1803329: tc The MT has the passive form, and so a subject has to be added: вАЬ[a man] is chastened.вАЭ The LXX has the active form, indicating вАЬ[God] chastens,вАЭ but the object вАЬa manвАЭ has to be added. It is understandable why the LXX thought this was active, within this sequence of verbs; and that is why it is the inferior reading.
n1803330: tc The Kethib вАЬthe strife of his bones is continual,вАЭ whereas the Qere has вАЬthe multitude of his bones are firm.вАЭ The former is the better reading in this passage. It indicates that the pain is caused by the ongoing strife.
n1803331: tn Heb вАЬfood of desire.вАЭ The word вАЬrejectsвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1803332: tc Heb вАЬare laid bare.вАЭ This is the Qere reading; the Kethib means вАЬbare height.вАЭ Gordis reverses the word order: вАЬhis bones are bare [i.e., crushed] so that they cannot be looked upon.вАЭ But the sense of that is not clear.
n1803333: tn Heb вАЬhis soul [вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО, nefesh, вАЬlifeвАЭ] draws near.вАЭ
n1803334: tn The MT uses the Hiphil participle, вАЬto those who cause death.вАЭ This seems to be a reference to the belief in demons that brought about death, an idea not mentioned in the Bible itself. Thus many proposals have been made for this expression.
