Hoffmann and Budde divide the word into вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю„Х÷є „Ю÷µ„™÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (lemo metim) and simply read вАЬto the dead.вАЭ Dhorme adds a couple of letters to get вАП„Ь÷і„Ю÷∞„І„Х÷є„Э „Ю÷µ„™÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (limqom metim, вАЬto the place [or abode] of the deadвАЭ).
n1803335: sn The verse is describing the way God can preserve someone from dying by sending a messenger (translated here as вАЬangelвАЭ), who could be human or angelic. This messenger will interpret/mediate GodвАЩs will. By вАЬoneвА¶out of a thousandвАЭ Elihu could have meant either that one of the thousands of messengers at GodвАЩs disposal might be sent or that the messenger would be unique (see Eccl 7:28; Job 9:3).
n1803336: tn This is a smoother reading. The MT has вАЬto tell to a man his uprightness,вАЭ to reveal what is right for him. The LXX translated this word вАЬdutyвАЭ; the choice is adopted by some commentaries. However, that is too far from the text, which indicates that the angel/messenger is to call the person to uprightness.
n1803337: tn This verse seems to continue the protasis begun in the last verse, with the apodosis coming in the next verse.
n1803338: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803339: tc The verb is either taken as an anomalous form of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (pada њ, вАЬto rescue; to redeem,вАЭ or вАЬto exempt himвАЭ), or it is emended to some similar word, like вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (para њ, вАЬto let loose,вАЭ so Wright).
n1803340: sn This verse and v. 28 should be compared with Ps 49:7-9, 15 (8-10, 16 HT) where the same basic vocabulary and concepts are employed.
n1803341: tc The word вАП„®÷ї„Ш÷≤„§÷Ј„©„БвАО (rutafash) is found nowhere else. One suggestion is that it should be вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Ш÷Ј„СвАО (yirtav, вАЬto become freshвАЭ), connected to вАП„®÷Є„Ш÷Ј„СвАО (ratav, вАЬto be well watered [or moist]вАЭ). It is also possible that it was a combination of вАП„®÷Є„Ш÷Ј„СвАО (ratav, вАЬto be well wateredвАЭ) and вАП„Ш÷Є„§÷Ј„©„БвАО (tafash, вАЬto grow fatвАЭ). But these are all guesses in the commentaries.
n1803342: tn The word describes the period when the man is healthy and vigorous, ripe for what life brings his way.
n1803343: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803344:
tn Heb вАЬhis faceвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
sn This is usually taken to mean that as a worshiper this individual comes into the presence of the Lord in prayer, and in the sanctuary he sees GodвАЩs face, i.e., he sees the evidence of GodвАЩs presence.
n1803345: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803346: tc Many commentators think this line is superfluous and so delete it. The RSV changed the verb to вАЬhe recounts,вАЭ making the idea that the man publishes the news of his victory or salvation (taking вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ as a metonymy of cause).
n1803347: tc The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷є„®вАО (yashor) is unusual. The typical view is to change it to вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„®вАО (yashir, вАЬhe singsвАЭ), but that may seem out of harmony with a confession. Dhorme suggests a root вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„®вАО (shur, вАЬto repeatвАЭ), but this is a doubtful root. J. Reider reads it вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷µ„Щ„®вАО (yasher) and links it to an Arabic word вАЬconfessesвАЭ (ZAW 24 [1953]: 275).
n1803349: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (shavah) has the impersonal meaning here, вАЬit has not been requited to me.вАЭ The meaning is that the sinner has not been treated in accordance with his deeds: вАЬI was not punished according to what I deserved.вАЭ
n1803350: sn See note on вАЬhimвАЭ in v. 24.
n1803351: sn Elihu will repeat these instructions for Job to listen, over and over in painful repetition. See note on the heading to 32:1.
n1803352: tn The phrase вАЬin his dealingsвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1803354: tn The infinitive construct serves as the complement or object of вАЬI desire.вАЭ It could be rendered вАЬto justify youвАЭ or вАЬyour justification,вАЭ namely, вАЬthat you be justified.вАЭ
n180341: sn This speech of Elihu focuses on defending God. It can be divided into these sections: Job is irreligious (2-9), God is just (10-15), God is impartial and omniscient (16-30), Job is foolish to rebel (31-37).
n180343: tn The Hebrew word means вАЬthe men who know,вАЭ and without a complement it means вАЬto possess knowledge.вАЭ
n180345: tn Or вАЬpalateвАЭ; the Hebrew term refers to the tongue or to the mouth in general.
n180346: sn Elihu means вАЬchoose after careful examination.вАЭ
n180347: tn The word is вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat) again, with the sense of what is right or just.
n180348: tn Heb вАЬrighteous,вАЭ but in this context it means to be innocent or in the right.
n180349: tn The verb is the Piel imperfect of вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО (kazav), meaning вАЬto lie.вАЭ It could be a question: вАЬShould I lie [against my right?]вАФwhen I am innocent.вАЭ If it is repointed to the Pual, then it can be вАЬI am made to lie,вАЭ or вАЬI am deceived.вАЭ Taking it as a question makes good sense here, and so emendations are unnecessary.
n1803410: tn The Hebrew text has only вАЬmy arrow.вАЭ Some commentators emend that word slightly to get вАЬmy wound.вАЭ But the idea could be derived from вАЬarrowsвАЭ as well, the wounds caused by the arrows. The arrows are symbolic of GodвАЩs affliction.
n1803411: tn Heb вАЬwithout transgression,вАЭ but this is parallel to the first part where the claim is innocence.
n1803412: tn Heb вАЬhe drinks,вАЭ but coming after the question this clause may be subordinated.
n1803413: tn The scorn or derision mentioned here is not against Job, but against God. Job scorns God so much, he must love it. So to reflect this idea, Gordis has translated it вАЬblasphemyвАЭ (cf. NAB).
n1803414: tn The perfect verb with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive carries the sequence forward from the last description.
n1803415: tn The word вАП„Ч÷ґ„С÷∞„®÷Ј„ФвАО (khevrah, вАЬcompanyвАЭ) is a hapax legomenon. But its meaning is clear enough from the connections to related words and this context as well.
n1803416: tn The infinitive construct with the вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition may continue the clause with the finite verb (see GKC 351 ¬І114.p).
n1803417: tn Heb вАЬmen of wickednessвАЭ; the genitive is attributive (= вАЬwicked menвАЭ).
n1803418: tn Gordis, however, takes this expression in the sense of вАЬbeing in favor with God.вАЭ
n1803419: tn Heb вАЬmen of heart.вАЭ The вАЬheartвАЭ is used for the capacity to understand and make the proper choice. It is often translated вАЬmind.вАЭ
n1803421: tn Heb вАЬfor the work of man, he [= God] repays him.вАЭ
n1803422: tn Heb вАЬhe causes it to find him.вАЭ The text means that God will cause a man to find (or receive) the consequences of his actions.
n1803423: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) means вАЬto visit; to appoint; to number.вАЭ Here it means вАЬto entrustвАЭ for care and governing. The implication would be that there would be someone higher than GodвАФwhich is what Elihu is repudiating by the rhetorical question. No one entrusted God with this.
n1803424: tn The preposition is implied from the first half of the verse.
n1803425: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803426: tc This is the reading following the Qere. The Kethib and the Syriac and the LXX suggest a reading вАП„Щ÷Є„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (yasim, вАЬif he [God] recallsвАЭ). But this would require leaving out вАЬhis heart,вАЭ and would also require redividing the verse to make вАЬhis spiritвАЭ the object. It makes better parallelism, but may require too many changes.
n1803427: tn Or perhaps вАЬSpirit,вАЭ though this may be less likely in close proximity with вАЬbreath.вАЭ As a reference to the human spirit cf. Ps. 104:29; as a reference to GodвАЩs Spirit originating with him, cf. Ps 104:30.
n1803428: tn The phrase вАЬyou haveвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied.
n1803430: tn The force of вАП„Ф÷Ј„Р÷Ј„£вАО (ha Њaf) is вАЬIs it truly the case?вАЭ The point is being made that if Job were right God could not be judging the world.
n1803431: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„©„БвАО (khavash) has the basic idea of вАЬto bind,вАЭ as in binding on the yoke, and then in the sense of subduing people under authority (cf. Assyrian absanu). The imperfect verb here is best expressed with the potential nuance.
n1803432: tn The two words could be taken separately, but they seem to form a fine nominal hendiadys, because the issue is GodвАЩs justice. So the word for power becomes the modifier.
n1803433: tc Heb вАЬDoes one say,вАЭ although some smooth it out to say вАЬIs it fit to say?вАЭ For the reading вАЬwho says,вАЭ the form has to be repointed to вАП„Ф÷Ј„Р÷є„Ю÷µ„®вАО (ha Њomer) meaning, вАЬwho is the one saying.вАЭ This reading is supported by the LXX, Vulgate, and Syriac. Also it seems to flow better with the following verse. It would be saying that God is over the rulers and can rebuke them. The former view is saying that no one rebukes kings, much less Job rebuking God.
n1803434: tn The word вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (beliyya њal) means both вАЬworthlessвАЭ and вАЬwicked.вАЭ It is common in proverbial literature, and in later writings it became a description of Satan. It is usually found with вАЬson of.вАЭ
n1803435: tn The verb means вАЬto give recognition; to take note ofвАЭ and in this passage with вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne, вАЬbeforeвАЭ) it means to show preferential treatment to the rich before the poor. The word for вАЬrichвАЭ here is an unusual word, found parallel to вАЬnobleвАЭ (Isa 32:2). P. Jo√Љon thinks it is a term of social distinction (Bib 18 [1937]: 207-8).
n1803436: tn Dhorme transposes вАЬin the middle of the nightвАЭ with вАЬthey pass awayвАЭ to get a smoother reading. But the MT emphasizes the suddenness by putting both temporal ideas first. E. F. Sutcliffe leaves the order as it stands in the text, but adds a verb вАЬthey expireвАЭ after вАЬin the middle of the nightвАЭ (вАЬNotes on Job, textual and exegetical,вАЭ Bib 30 [1949]: 79ff.).
n1803437: tn R. Gordis (Job, 389) thinks вАЬpeopleвАЭ here mean the people who count, the upper class.
n1803438: tn The verb means вАЬto be violently agitated.вАЭ There is no problem with the word in this context, but commentators have made suggestions for improving the idea. The proposal that has the most to commend it, if one were inclined to choose a new word, is the change to вАП„Щ÷і„Т÷∞„Х÷Є„Ґ„Х÷ЉвАО (yigva њu, вАЬthey expireвАЭ; so Ball, Holscher, Fohrer, and others).
n1803439: tn Heb вАЬnot by hand.вАЭ This means without having to use force.
n1803440: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (a person) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803441: tn The construction of this colon uses the Niphal infinitive construct from вАП„°÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО (satar, вАЬto be hidden; to hideвАЭ). The resumptive adverb makes this a relative clause in its usage: вАЬwhere the evildoers can hide themselves.вАЭ
n1803442: tn Heb вАЬfor he does not put upon man yet.вАЭ This has been given a wide variety of interpretations, all of which involve a lot of additional thoughts. The word вАП„Ґ„Х÷є„УвАО ( њod, вАЬyet, stillвАЭ) has been replaced with вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„УвАО (mo њed, вАЬan appointed time,вАЭ Reiske and Wright), with the вАП„ЭвАО (mem) having dropped out by haplography. This makes good sense. If the MT is retained, the best interpretation would be that God does not any more consider (from вАЬplace upon the heartвАЭ) man, that he might appear in judgment.
n1803444: tn The direct object вАЬthemвАЭ is implied and has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1803445: tn The Hebrew term вАЬnightвАЭ is an accusative of time.
n1803446: tn Heb вАЬunder wicked men,вАЭ or вАЬunder wickednesses.вАЭ J. C. Greenfield shows that the preposition can mean вАЬamongвАЭ as well (вАЬPrepositions B Tachat in Jes 57:5, вАЭ ZAW 32 [1961]: 227). That would allow вАЬamong wicked men.вАЭ It could also be вАЬinstead ofвАЭ or even вАЬin return for [their wickedness]вАЭ which is what the RSV does.
n1803447: tn The text simply uses вАП„®÷є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (ro Њim): вАЬ[in the place where there are] seers,вАЭ i.e., spectators.
n1803448: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷і„©„В÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (hiskilu) means вАЬto be prudent; to be wise.вАЭ From this is derived the idea of вАЬbe wise in understanding GodвАЩs will,вАЭ and вАЬbe successful because of prudenceвАЭвАФi.e., successful with God.
n1803449: tn The verse begins with the infinitive construct of вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬgoвАЭ), showing the result of their impious actions.
n1803450: tn The verb here is an imperfect; the clause is circumstantial to the preceding clause, showing either the result, or the concomitant action.
n1803451: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803452: tn The verb in this position is somewhat difficult, although it does make good sense in the sentenceвАФit is just not what the parallelism would suggest. So several emendations have been put forward, for which see the commentaries.
n1803453: tn The line simply reads вАЬand over a nation and over a man together.вАЭ But it must be the qualification for the points being made in the previous lines, namely, that even if God hides himself so no one can see, yet he is still watching over them all (see H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 222).
n1803454: tn The word translated вАЬalikeвАЭ (Heb вАЬtogetherвАЭ) has bothered some interpreters. In the reading taken here it is acceptable. But others have emended it to gain a verb, such as вАЬhe visitsвАЭ (Beer), вАЬhe watches overвАЭ (Duhm), вАЬhe is compassionateвАЭ (Kissane), etc. But it is sufficient to say вАЬhe is over.вАЭ
n1803455: tn This last verse is difficult because it is unbalanced and cryptic. Some have joined the third line of v. 29 with this entire verse to make a couplet. But the same result is achieved by simply regarding this verse as the purpose of v. 29. But there still are some words that must be added. In the first colon, вАЬ[he is over the nations]вА¶preventing from ruling.вАЭ And in the second colon, вАЬlayingвАЭ has to be supplied before вАЬsnares.вАЭ
n1803456: tn The Hebrew text has only вАЬI lift upвАЭ or вАЬI bearвАЭ (= I endure). The reading вАЬI have been led astrayвАЭ is obtained by changing the vowels to read a passive. If the MT is retained, an object has to be supplied, such as вАЬchastisementвАЭ (so RSV, NASB) or вАЬpunishmentвАЭ (NRSV). If not, then a different reading would be followed (e.g., вАЬI was misguidedвАЭ [NAB]; вАЬI am guiltyвАЭ [NIV]).
n1803457: tn Heb вАЬwhat I do not see,вАЭ more specifically, вАЬapart from [that which] I see.вАЭ
n1803458: tn Heb вАЬis it from with you,вАЭ an idiomatic expression meaning вАЬto suit youвАЭ or вАЬaccording to your judgment.вАЭ
n1803459: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803460: tn There is no object on the verb, and the meaning is perhaps lost. The best guess is that Elihu is saying Job has rejected his teaching.
n1803461: tn Adding вАЬthatвАЭ in the translation clarifies ElihuвАЩs indirect citation of the wise individualsвАЩ words.
n1803462: tn The Hiphil infinitive construct is here functioning as a substantive. The word means вАЬprudence; understanding.вАЭ
n1803463: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷Є„С÷і„ЩвАО ( Њavi, вАЬmy fatherвАЭ), which makes no sense. Some follow the KJV and emend the word to make a verb вАЬI desireвАЭ or use the noun вАЬmy desire of it.вАЭ Others follow an Arabic word meaning вАЬentreat, I prayвАЭ (cf. ESV, вАЬWould that Job were triedвАЭ). The LXX and the Syriac versions have вАЬbutвАЭ and вАЬsurelyвАЭ respectively. Since this is the only ms support, albeit weak, it may be the best choice. In this sense Elihu would be saying that because of JobвАЩs attitude God will continue to test him.
n1803464: tn Although frequently translated вАЬrebellion,вАЭ the basic meaning of this Hebrew term is вАЬtransgression.вАЭ
n1803465: tc If this reading stands, it would mean that Job shows contempt, meaning that he mocks them and accuses God. It is a bold touch, but workable. Of the many suggested emendations, Dhorme alters some of the vowels and obtains a reading вАЬand casts doubt among us,вАЭ and then takes вАЬtransgressionвАЭ from the first colon for the complement. Some commentators simply delete the line.
n180351: sn This short speech falls into two sections: Elihu refutes JobвАЩs claim that goodness avails nothing (35:2-8), asserting that when the cry of the afflicted goes unanswered they have not learned their lesson (35:9-16).
n180352: tn The line could be read as вАЬdo you reckon this for justice? Here вАЬto beвАЭ is understood.
n180353: tn The word вАЬwhenвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is implied.
n180354: tn The brief line could be interpreted in a number of ways. The MT simply has вАЬmy right from God.вАЭ It could be вАЬI am right before God,вАЭ вАЬI am more just/right than GodвАЭ (identifying the preposition as a comparative min (вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО); cf. J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 463), вАЬI will be right before God,вАЭ or вАЬMy just cause against God.вАЭ
n180355: tn The referent of вАЬyouвАЭ is usually understood to be God.
n180356: tn The Hebrew text merely says, вАЬWhat do I gain from my sin?вАЭ But Job has claimed that he has not sinned, and so this has to be elliptical: вАЬmore than if I had sinnedвАЭ (H. H. Rowley, Job [NCBC], 224). It could also be, вАЬWhat do I gain without sin?вАЭ
n180357: tn The emphatic pronoun calls attention to Elihu who will answer these questions.
n180358: tn The Hebrew text adds, вАЬwith words,вАЭ but since this is obvious, for stylistic reasons it has not been included in the translation.
n180359: tn The preposition is taken here as a comparative min (вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО). The line could also read вАЬthat are high above you.вАЭ This idea has appeared in the speech of Eliphaz (22:12), Zophar (11:7-8), and even Job (9:8-11).
n1803510: tn Heb вАЬhimвАЭ (also in v. 7); the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803512:
tn The phrase вАЬaffects onlyвАЭ is supplied in the translation of this nominal sentence.
sn According to Strahan, вАЬElihu exalts GodвАЩs greatness at the cost of His grace, His transcendence at the expense of His immanence. He sets up a material instead of a spiritual stand of profit and loss. He does not realize that God does gain what He desires most by the goodness of men, and loses what He most loves by their evil.вАЭ
n1803513: tn Heb вАЬand to [or for] a son of man, your righteousness.вАЭ
n1803514: tn The word вАЬpeopleвАЭ is supplied, because the sentence only has the masculine plural verb.
n1803515: tn The final noun is an abstract plural, вАЬoppression.вАЭ There is no reason to change it to вАЬoppressorsвАЭ to fit the early versions. The expression is literally вАЬmultitude of oppression.вАЭ
n1803516: tn Heb вАЬthe arm,вАЭ a metaphor for strength or power.
n1803517: tn Or вАЬof the manyвАЭ (see HALOT 1172 s.v. I вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО 6.a).
n1803518: tn There have been several attempts to emend the line, none of which are particularly helpful or interesting. H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 225) says, вАЬIt is a pity to rob Elihu of a poetic line when he creates one.вАЭ
n1803519: tn The form in the text, the Piel participle from вАП„Р÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО ( Њalaf, вАЬteachвАЭ) is written in a contracted form; the full form is вАП„Ю÷∞„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷∞„§÷µ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (me Њallefenu).
n1803520: tn Some would render this вАЬteaches us by the beasts.вАЭ But Elihu is stressing the unique privilege humans have.
n1803521: tn The adverb вАП„©„Б÷Є„ЭвАО (sham, вАЬthereвАЭ) connects this verse to v. 11. вАЬThereвАЭ can be locative or temporalвАФand here it is temporal (= вАЬthenвАЭ).
n1803522: tn Heb вАЬsurelyвАФvanity, he does not hear.вАЭ The cry is an empty cry, not a prayer to God. Dhorme translates it, вАЬIt is a pure waste of words.вАЭ
n1803523: sn The point is that if God does not listen to those who do not turn to him, how much less likely is he to turn to one who complains against him.
n1803524: tn The expression вАЬand nowвАЭ introduces a new complaint of ElihuвАФin addition to the preceding. Here the verb of v. 14, вАЬyou say,вАЭ is understood after the temporal ki (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО).
n1803525: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) means вАЬto visitвАЭ (also вАЬto appoint; to muster; to numberвАЭ). When God visits, it means that he intervenes in oneвАЩs life for blessing or cursing (punishing, destroying).
n1803526: tn The word вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„©„БвАО (pash) is a hapax legomenon. K&D 12:275 derived it from an Arabic word meaning вАЬbelch,вАЭ leading to the idea of вАЬoverflow.вАЭ BDB 832 s.v. defines it as вАЬfolly.вАЭ Several define it as вАЬtransgressionвАЭ on the basis of the versions (Theodotion, Symmachus, Vulgate). The RSV took it as вАЬgreatly heed,вАЭ but that is not exactly вАЬgreatly know,вАЭ when the text beyond that requires вАЬnot know at all.вАЭ The NIV has вАЬhe does not take the least notice of wickedness.вАЭ
n1803527: tn The word вАП„Ф÷ґ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (hevel) means вАЬvanity; futility; to no purpose.вАЭ
n180361: sn This very lengthy speech can be broken down into the following sections: the discipline of suffering (36:2-25), the work and wisdom of God (36:26-37:24).
n180362: tn The use of вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷є„°÷ґ„£вАО (vayyosef) is with the hendiadys construction: вАЬand he added and said,вАЭ meaning вАЬand he said again, further.вАЭ
n180363: tn The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„™÷Љ÷Ј„®вАО (kattar) is the Piel imperative; in Hebrew the word means вАЬto surroundвАЭ and is related to the noun for crown. But in Syriac it means вАЬto wait.вАЭ This section of the book of Job will have a few Aramaic words.
n180364: tn The Hebrew text simply has вАЬfor yet for God words.вАЭ
n180365: tn Heb вАЬI will carry my knowledge to-from afar.вАЭ The expression means that he will give a wide range to knowledge, that he will speak comprehensively.
n180366: tn This line gives the essence of all of ElihuвАЩs speechвАФto give or ascribe righteousness to God against the charges of Job. Dhorme translates this вАЬI will justify my Maker,вАЭ and that is workable if it carries the meaning of вАЬdeclaring to be right.вАЭ
n180367: tn The word is вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (temim), often translated вАЬperfect.вАЭ It is the same word used of Job in 2:3. Elihu is either a complete stranger to modesty or is confident regarding the knowledge that he believes God has revealed to him for this situation. See the note on the heading before 32:1.
n180368: tn The object вАЬpeopleвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text but is implied.
n1803610: tn The last two words are simply вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„Ч÷Ј „Ь÷µ„СвАО (koakh lev, вАЬstrong in heartвАЭ), meaning something like вАЬstrong; firm in his decisions.вАЭ
n1803611: tc There are several problems in this verse: the repetition of вАЬmighty,вАЭ the lack of an object for вАЬdespise,вАЭ and the meaning of вАЬstrength of heart.вАЭ Many commentators reduce the verse to a single line, reading something like вАЬLo, God does not reject the pure in heartвАЭ (Kissane). Dhorme and Pope follow Nichols with: вАЬLo, God is mighty in strength, and rejects not the pure in heart.вАЭ This reading moved вАЬmightyвАЭ to the first line and took the second to be вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„®вАО (bar, вАЬpureвАЭ).
n1803612: tn Or вАЬhe does not keep the wicked alive.вАЭ
n1803613: tc Many commentators accept the change of вАЬhis eyesвАЭ to вАЬhis rightвАЭ (reading вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„†„Х÷євАО [dino] for вАП„Ґ÷µ„Щ„†÷Є„Щ„ХвАО [ њenayv]). There is no compelling reason for the change; it makes the line commonplace.
n1803614: tn Heb вАЬthemвАЭ; the referent (the righteous) has been repeated from the first part of the verse for clarity.
n1803615: tn Heb вАЬhe seats them forever and exalts them.вАЭ The last verb can be understood as expressing a logical consequence of the preceding action (cf. GKC 328 ¬І111.l = вАЬhe seats them forever so that he exalts themвАЭ). Or the two verbs can be taken as an adverbial hendiadys whereby the first modifies the second adverbially: вАЬhe exalts them by seating them foreverвАЭ or вАЬwhen he seats them foreverвАЭ (cf. GKC 326 ¬І111.d). Some interpret this verse to say that God seats kings on the throne, making a change in subject in the middle of the verse. But it makes better sense to see the righteous as the subject matter throughoutвАФthey are not only protected, but are exalted.
n1803616: tn Dhorme thinks that the verse is still talking about kings, who may be in captivity. But this diverts attention from ElihuвАЩs emphasis on the righteous.
n1803617: tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„Т÷Ј„УвАО (nagad) means вАЬto declare; to tell.вАЭ Here it is clear that God is making known the sins that caused the enslavement or captivity, so вАЬrevealвАЭ makes a good interpretive translation.
n1803619: tn The idiom once again is вАЬhe uncovers their ear.вАЭ
n1803620: tn The revelation is in the preceding verse, and so a pronoun must be added to make the reference clear.
n1803621: tn The verb вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto turn; to returnвАЭ) is one of the two major words in the OT for вАЬrepentвАЭвАФto return from evil. Here the imperfect should be obligatoryвАФthey must do it.
n1803622: tc Some commentators delete this last line for metrical considerations. But there is no textual evidence for the deletion; it is simply the attempt by some to make the meter rigid.
n1803623: tn This is a similar expression to the one in Job 33:18, where the suggestion was made by many that it means crossing over the canal or river of death. Some retain the earlier interpretation of вАЬperish by the swordвАЭ (cf. NIV).
n1803624: tn The expression вАЬgodless [or hypocrite] in heartвАЭ is an intensification of the description. It conveys that they are intentionally godless. See Matt 23:28.
n1803625: tn Heb вАЬthey put anger.вАЭ This is usually interpreted to mean they lay up anger, or put anger in their hearts.
n1803626: tn The text expresses this with вАЬtheir soul dies.вАЭ
n1803627: tn Heb вАЬamong the male prostitutesвАЭ who were at the templeвАФthe вАЬholy ones,вАЭ with вАЬholyвАЭ being used in that sense of вАЬseparated to that form of temple service.вАЭ So uncleanness and shame are some of the connotations of the reference. Some modern translations give the general sense only: вАЬtheir life ends in shameвАЭ (NRSV); вАЬand perish among the reprobateвАЭ (NAB); вАЬdieвА¶after wasting their lives in immoral livingвАЭ (NLT).
n1803628: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) in these two lines is not location but instrument, not вАЬinвАЭ but вАЬby means of.вАЭ The affliction and the oppression serve as a warning for sin, and therefore a means of salvation.
n1803631: tn The Hebrew verb means вАЬto entice; to lure; to allure; to seduce,вАЭ but these have negative connotations. The English вАЬto persuade; to drawвАЭ might work better. The verb is the Hiphil perfect of вАП„°„Х÷Љ„™вАО (sut). But the nuance of the verb is difficult. It can be equivalent to an English present expressing what God is doing (Peake). But the subject is contested as well. Since the verb usually has an evil connotation, there have been attempts to make the вАЬplazaвАЭ the subjectвАФвАЬthe wide place has led you astrayвАЭ (Ewald).
n1803632: tn Heb вАЬa broad place where there is no cramping beneath [or under] it.вАЭ
n1803633: tn The word вАП„†÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nakhat) could be translated вАЬsetвАЭ if it is connected with the verb вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh, вАЬto rest,вАЭ but then вАЬto lay to rest, to setвАЭ). Kissane translates it вАЬcomfort.вАЭ Dhorme thinks it could come from вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh, вАЬto restвАЭ) or вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nakhat, вАЬto descendвАЭ). But his conclusion is that it is a dittography after вАЬunder itвАЭ (p. 545).
n1803635: tn The first expression is idiomatic: the text says, вАЬbecause wrath lest it entice youвАЭвАФthus, beware.
n1803636: tn The word is вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„§÷ґ„®вАО (kofer), often translated вАЬransom,вАЭ but frequently in the sense of a bribe.
n1803637: tn The form in the MT is вАЬyour cry (for help).вАЭ See J. E. Hartley (Job [NICOT], 472-73) and E. Dhorme (Job, 547-48) on the difficulties.
n1803638: tn This part has only two words вАП„Ь÷є„Р „С÷∞„¶÷Є„®вАО (lo Њ betsar, вАЬnot in distressвАЭ). The negated phrase serves to explain the first colon.
n1803639: tc For the many suggestions and the reasoning here, see the commentaries.
n1803640: tn The meaning of this line is difficult. There are numerous suggestions for emending the text. Kissane takes the first verb in the sense of вАЬoppress,вАЭ and for вАЬthe nightвАЭ he has вАЬbelonging to you,вАЭ meaning вАЬyour people.вАЭ This reads: вАЬOppress not them that belong not to you, that your kinsmen may mount up in their place.вАЭ
n1803641: tn Normally вАЬtestedвАЭ would be the translation for the Niphal of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (bakhar). Although the Qal is employed here, the context favors вАЬtestedвАЭ rather than вАЬchose.вАЭ
n1803642: tn The word вАП„Ю„Х÷є„®÷ґ„ФвАО (moreh) is the Hiphil participle from вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„ФвАО (yarah). It is related to the noun вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah, вАЬwhat is taughtвАЭ i.e., the law).
n1803643: tn The expression is вАЬthat you extol,вАЭ serving as an object of the verb.
n1803644: tn The last part has the verbal construction, вАЬand we do not know.вАЭ This clause is to be used adverbially: вАЬbeyond our understanding.вАЭ
n1803645: tn The verb means вАЬto filter; to refine,вАЭ and so a plural subject with the drops of water as the subject will not work. So many read the singular, вАЬhe distills.вАЭ
n1803646: tn This word вАП„Ґ÷µ„УвАО ( њed) occurs also in Gen 2:6. The suggestion has been that instead of a mist it represents an underground watercourse that wells up to water the ground.
n1803648: tn The word actually means вАЬto spread,вАЭ but with lightning as the object, вАЬto scatterвАЭ appears to fit the context better.
n1803649: tn The word is вАЬlight,вАЭ but taken to mean вАЬlightning.вАЭ Theodotion had вАЬmistвАЭ here, and so most commentators follow that because it is more appropriate to the verb and the context.
n1803651: tn The verb is вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (yadin, вАЬhe judgesвАЭ). Houbigant proposed вАП„Щ÷Є„Ц„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (yazun, вАЬhe nourishesвАЭ). This has found wide acceptance among commentators (cf. NAB). G. R. Driver retained the MT but gave a meaning вАЬenrichesвАЭ to the verb (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 88ff.).
n1803652: tn R. Gordis (Job, 422) prefers to link this word with the later Hebrew word for вАЬarch,вАЭ not вАЬhands.вАЭ
n1803653: tn Because the image might mean that God grabs the lightning and hurls it like a javelin (cf. NLT), some commentators want to change вАЬcoversвАЭ to other verbs. Dhorme has вАЬliftsвАЭ (вАП„†÷і„©„В÷Љ÷Є„РвАО [nissa Њ] for вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„°÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО [kissah]). This fit the idea of God directing the lightning bolts.
n1803654: tn Peake knew of over thirty interpretations for this verse. The MT literally says, вАЬHe declares his purpose [or his shout] concerning it; cattle also concerning what rises.вАЭ Dhorme has it: вАЬThe flock which sniffs the coming storm has warned the shepherd.вАЭ Kissane: вАЬThe thunder declares concerning him, as he excites wrath against iniquity.вАЭ Gordis translates it: вАЬHis thunderclap proclaims his presence, and the storm his mighty wrath.вАЭ Many more could be added to the list.
n1803655: tn The meaning of this verse is disputed; it may refer to cattle sensing the approaching storm.
n180371: tn The imperative is followed by the infinitive absolute from the same root to express the intensity of the verb.
n180372: tn The word is the usual word for вАЬto meditate; to murmur; to groanвАЭ; here it refers to the low building of the thunder as it rumbles in the sky. The thunder is the voice of God (see Ps 29).
n180373: tn Heb вАЬwings,вАЭ and then figuratively for the extremities of garments, of land, etc.
n180374: tn The verb simply has the pronominal suffix, вАЬthem.вАЭ The idea must be that when God brings in all the thunderings he does not hold back his lightning bolts either.
n180375: tn The form is the Niphal participle, вАЬwonders,вАЭ from the verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„РвАО (pala Њ, вАЬto be wonderful; to be extraordinaryвАЭ). Some commentators suppress the repeated verb вАЬthunders,вАЭ and supply other verbs like вАЬshowsвАЭ or вАЬworks,вАЭ enabling them to make вАЬwondersвАЭ the object of the verb rather than leaving it in an adverbial role. But as H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 236) notes, no change is needed, for one is not surprised to find repetition in ElihuвАЩs words.
n180377: tn The verb actually means вАЬbeвАЭ (found here in the Aramaic form). The verb вАЬto beвАЭ can mean вАЬto happen, to fall, to come about.вАЭ
n180378: tn Heb вАЬand [to the] shower of rain and shower of rains, be strong.вАЭ Many think the repetition grew up by variant readings; several Hebrew mss delete the second pair, and so many editors do. But the repetition may have served to stress the idea that the rains were heavy.
n1803710: tn Heb вАЬby the hand of every man he seals.вАЭ This line is intended to mean that with the heavy rains God suspends all agricultural activity.
n1803711: tc This reading involves a change in the text, for in MT вАЬmenвАЭ is in the construct. It would be translated вАЬall men whom he madeвАЭ (i.e., вАЬall men of his makingвАЭ). This is the translation followed by the NIV and NRSV. Olshausen suggested that the word should have been вАП„Р÷≤„†÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њanashim) with the final вАП„ЭвАО (mem) being lost to haplography.
n1803712: tn D. W. Thomas suggested a meaning of вАЬrestвАЭ for the verb, based on Arabic. He then reads вАП„Р÷±„†„Х÷є„©„БвАО ( Њenosh) for man, and supplies a вАП„ЭвАО (mem) to вАЬhis workвАЭ to get вАЬthat every man might rest from his work [in the fields].вАЭ
n1803713: tn The вАЬdriving windsвАЭ reflects the Hebrew вАЬfrom the scatterers.вАЭ This refers to the north winds that bring the cold air and the ice and snow and hard rains.
n1803714: tn The word вАЬmoistureвАЭ is drawn from вАП„®÷і„ЩвАО (ri) as a contraction for вАП„®÷∞„Х÷і„ЩвАО (revi). Others emended the text to get вАЬhailвАЭ (NAB) or вАЬlightning,вАЭ or even вАЬthe Creator.вАЭ For these, see the various commentaries. There is no reason to change the reading of the MT when it makes perfectly good sense.
n1803715: tn The words вАЬthe cloudsвАЭ are supplied from v. 11; the sentence itself actually starts: вАЬand it goes round,вАЭ referring to the cloud.
n1803717: tn Heb вАЬrod,вАЭ i.e., a rod used for punishment.
n1803718: tn This is interpretive; Heb вАЬhe makes find it.вАЭ The lightning could be what is intended here, for it finds its mark. But R. Gordis (Job, 429) suggests man is the subjectвАФlet him find what it is for, i.e., the fate appropriate for him.
n1803719: tn The verb is вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„©„В„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (besum, from вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО [sim, вАЬsetвАЭ]), so the idea is how God lays [or sets] [a command] for them. The suffix is proleptic, to be clarified in the second colon.
n1803720: tn Dhorme reads this вАЬand how his stormcloud makes lightning to flash forth?вАЭ
n1803721: tn As indicated by HALOT 618 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷Є„©„ВвАО, the concept of вАЬbalancingвАЭ probably refers to вАЬfloatingвАЭ or вАЬsuspensionвАЭ (cf. NIVвАЩs вАЬhow the clouds hang poisedвАЭ and J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 481-82, n. 2).
n1803722: tn The verb means вАЬto beat out; to flatten,вАЭ and the analogy in the next line will use molten metal. From this verb is derived the word for the вАЬfirmamentвАЭ in Gen 1:6-8, that canopy-like pressure area separating water above and water below.
n1803723: tn The imperfect verb here carries the obligatory nuance, вАЬwhat we should say?вАЭ
n1803724: tn The verb means вАЬto arrange; to set in order.вАЭ From the context the idea of a legal case is included.
n1803725: tn This imperfect works well as a desiderative imperfect.
n1803726: tn The light here must refer to the sun in the skies that had been veiled by the storm. Then, when the winds blew the clouds away, it could not be looked at because it was so dazzling. ElihuвАЩs analogy will be that God is the sameвАФin his glory one cannot look at him or challenge him.
n1803727: tn The verb has an indefinite subject, and so should be a passive here.
n1803728: tn Heb вАЬand cleaned them.вАЭ The referent is the clouds (v. 18), which has been supplied in the translation for clarity. There is another way of reading this verse: the word translated вАЬbrightвАЭ means вАЬdark; obscuredвАЭ in Syriac. In this interpretation the first line would mean that they could not see the sun, because it was darkened by the clouds, but then the wind came and blew the clouds away. Dhorme, Gray, and several others take it this way, as does the NAB.
n1803729: tn The MT has вАЬout of the north comes gold.вАЭ Left in that sense the line seems irrelevant. The translation вАЬgolden splendorвАЭ (with RV, RSV, NRSV, NIV) depends upon the context of theophany. Others suggest вАЬgolden raysвАЭ (Dhorme), the aurora borealis (Graetz, Gray), or some mythological allusion (Pope), such as BaalвАЩs palace. Golden rays or splendor is what is intended, although the reference is not to a natural phenomenonвАФit is something that would suggest the glory of God.
n1803730: tn The name вАЬAlmightyвАЭ is here a casus pendens, isolating the name at the front of the sentence and resuming it with a pronoun.
n1803731: tn The MT places the major disjunctive accent (the atnach) under вАЬpower,вАЭ indicating that вАЬand justiceвАЭ starts the second half of the verse as a disjunctive clause (with ESV, NASB, NIV, NLT). Ignoring the Masoretic accent, NRSV has вАЬhe is great in power and justice.вАЭ
n1803732: sn The phrase вАЬwise of heartвАЭ was used in Job 9:4 in a negative sense.
n180381: sn This is the culmination of it all, the revelation of the Lord to Job. Most interpreters see here the style and content of the author of the book, a return to the beginning of the book. Here the Lord speaks to Job and displays his sovereign power and glory. Job has lived through the sufferingвАФwithout cursing God. He has held to his integrity, and nowhere regretted it. But he was unaware of the real reason for the suffering, and will remain unaware throughout these speeches. God intervenes to resolve the spiritual issues that surfaced. Job was not punished for sin. And JobвАЩs suffering had not cut him off from God. In the end the point is that Job cannot have the knowledge to make the assessments he made. It is wiser to bow in submission and adoration of God than to try to judge him. The first speech of God has these sections: the challenge (38:1-3), the surpassing mysteries of earth and sky beyond JobвАЩs understanding (4-38), and the mysteries of animal and bird life that surpassed his understanding (38:39-39:30).
n180382: sn Throughout the book of Job exchanges between speakers are stated as вАЬ[someone] answered and said.вАЭ However, when the Lord speaks, the formula is usually just вАЬhe said.вАЭ The rhetorical function in Job is likely to show that God initiates and others respond to him. The text only describes the Lord as вАЬansweringвАЭ when he responds to Job in 38:1; 40:1, 6. That God вАЬrespondsвАЭ to Job shows his merit.
n180383: sn This is not the storm described by ElihuвАФin fact, the Lord ignores Elihu. The storm is a common accompaniment for a theophany (see Ezek 1:4; Nah 1:3; Zech 9:14).
n180384: tn The demonstrative pronoun is used here to emphasize the interrogative pronoun (see GKC 442 ¬І136.c).
n180385: sn The referent of вАЬcounselвАЭ here is not the debate between Job and the friends, but the purposes of God (see Ps 33:10; Prov 19:21; Isa 19:17). Dhorme translates it вАЬProvidence.вАЭ
n180386: tn Heb вАЬGird up your loins.вАЭ This idiom basically describes taking the hem of the long garment or robe and pulling it up between the legs and tucking it into the front of the belt, allowing easier and freer movement of the legs. вАЬGirding the loinsвАЭ meant the preparation for some difficult task (Jer 1:17), or for battle (Isa 5:27), or for running (1 Kgs 18:46). C. Gordon suggests that it includes belt-wrestling, a form of hand-to-hand mortal combat (вАЬBelt-wrestling in the Bible World,вАЭ HUCA 23 [1950/51]: 136).
n180387: tn The construction is the infinitive construct in a temporal clause, using the preposition and the subjective genitive suffix.
n180388: tn The verb is the imperative; it has no object вАЬmeвАЭ in the text.
n180389: tn The particle вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) is taken here for a conditional clause, вАЬif you knowвАЭ (see GKC 498 ¬І159.dd). Others take it as вАЬsurelyвАЭ with a biting irony.
n1803810: tn For the interrogative serving as a genitive, see GKC 442 ¬І136.b.
n1803811: sn The world was conceived of as having bases and pillars, but these poetic descriptions should not be pressed too far (e.g., see Ps 24:2, which may be worded as much for its polemics against Canaanite mythology as anything).
n1803812: sn The expression вАЬmorning starsвАЭ (Heb вАЬstars of the morningвАЭ) is here placed in parallelism to the angels, вАЬthe sons of God.вАЭ It may refer to the angels under the imagery of the stars, or, as some prefer, it may poetically include all creation. There is a parallel also with the foundation of the temple which was accompanied by song (see Ezra 3:10, 11). But then the account of the building of the original tabernacle was designed to mirror creation (see M. Fishbane, Biblical Text and Texture).
n1803813: tn The construction, an adverbial clause of time, uses вАП„®÷Є„†÷Є„ЯвАО (ranan), which is often a ringing cry, an exultation. The parallelism with вАЬshout for joyвАЭ shows this to be enthusiastic acclamation. The infinitive is then continued in the next colon with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive preterite.
n1803814: tn Heb вАЬtogether.вАЭ This is DhormeвАЩs suggestion for expressing how they sang together.
n1803816: tn The MT has вАЬand he shut up.вАЭ The Vulgate has вАЬWho?вАЭ and so many commentaries and editions adopt this reading, if not from the Vulgate, then from the sense of the sequence in the text itself.
n1803817: tn The line uses two expressions, first the temporal clause with вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (giakh, вАЬwhen it burst forthвАЭ) and then the finite verb вАП„Щ÷µ„¶÷µ„РвАО (yetse Њ, вАЬgo outвАЭ) to mark the concomitance of the two actions.
n1803818: tn The temporal clause here uses the infinitive from вАП„©„В÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (sim, вАЬto place; to put; to makeвАЭ). It underscores the sovereign placing of things.
n1803819: tn This noun is found only here. The verb is in Ezek 16:4, and a related noun is in Ezek 30:21.
n1803820: tc The MT has вАЬand I broke,вАЭ which cannot mean вАЬset, prescribedвАЭ or the like. The LXX and the Vulgate have such a meaning, suggesting a verb вАП„Ґ÷≤„©„Б÷і„Щ„™вАО ( њashit, вАЬplan, prescribeвАЭ). A. Guillaume finds an Arabic word with a meaning вАЬmeasured it by span by my decree.вАЭ Would God give himself a decree? R. Gordis simply argues that the basic meaning вАЬbreakвАЭ develops the connotation of вАЬdecide, determineвАЭ (2 Sam 5:24; Job 14:3; Dan 11:36).
n1803821: tn Dhorme suggested reversing the two verbs, making this the first, and then вАЬshatterвАЭ for the second colon.
n1803822: tn The imperfect verb receives the permission nuance here.
n1803823: tn The text has вАП„™÷є„°÷і„Щ„£вАО (tosif, вАЬand you may not addвАЭ), which is often used idiomatically (as in verbal hendiadys constructions).
n1803824: tn The MT literally says, вАЬhere he will put on the pride of your waves.вАЭ The verb has no expressed subject and so is made a passive voice. But there has to be some object for the verb вАЬput,вАЭ such as вАЬlimitвАЭ or вАЬboundaryвАЭ; the translations вАЬconfined; halted; stoppedвАЭ all serve to paraphrase such an idea. The LXX has вАЬbrokenвАЭ at this point, suggesting the verse might have been confusedвАФbut вАЬbreaking the prideвАЭ of the waves would mean controlling them. Some commentators have followed this, exchanging the verb in v. 11 with this one.
n1803825: tn The Hebrew idiom is вАЬhave you from your days?вАЭ It means вАЬnever in your lifeвАЭ (see 1 Sam 25:28; 1 Kgs 1:6).
n1803826: tn The verb is the Piel of вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ) with a double accusative.
n1803827: sn The poetic image is that darkness or night is like a blanket that covers the earth, and at dawn it is taken by the edges and shaken out. Since the wicked function under the cover of night, they are included in the shaking when the dawn comes up.
n1803828: sn The verse needs to be understood in the context: as the light shines in the dawn, the features of the earth take on a recognizable shape or form. The language is phenomenological.
n1803829: tn Heb вАЬtheyвАЭ; the referent (the objects or features on the earth) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803830: tc The MT reads вАЬthey stand up like a garmentвАЭ (NASB, NIV) or вАЬits features stand out like a garmentвАЭ (ESV). The reference could be either to embroidered decoration on a garment or to the folds of a garment (REB вАЬuntil all things stand out like the folds of a cloakвАЭ; cf. J. E. Hartley, Job [NICOT], 497, вАЬthe early light of day makes the earth appear as a beautiful garment, exquisite in design and glorious in colorвАЭ). Since this is thought to be an odd statement, some suggest with Ehrlich that the text be changed to вАП„™÷Љ÷і„¶÷Љ÷Є„С÷Љ÷Ј„ҐвАО (titsabba њ, вАЬis dyed [like a garment]вАЭ). This reference would be to the colors appearing on the earthвАЩs surface under daylight. The present translation follows the emendation.
n1803831: tn Heb вАЬthe raised arm.вАЭ The words вАЬin violenceвАЭ are not in the Hebrew text, but are supplied in the translation to clarify the metaphor.
n1803832: sn What is active at night, the violence symbolized by the raised arm, is broken with the dawn. G. R. Driver thought the whole verse referred to stars, and that the arm is the navigatorвАЩs term for the line of stars (вАЬTwo astronomical passages in the Old Testament,вАЭ JTS 4 [1953]: 208-12).
n1803833: tn Heb вАЬthe springs of the sea.вАЭ The words вАЬthat fillвАЭ are supplied in the translation to clarify the meaning of the phrase.
n1803835: tn Some still retain the traditional phrase вАЬshadow of deathвАЭ in the English translation (cf. NIV). The reference is to the entrance to Sheol (see Job 10:21).
n1803836: tn The interrogative with вАП„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh) means вАЬin what roadвАЭ or вАЬin what direction.вАЭ
n1803837: tn The suffixes are singular (вАЬthat you may take it to its borderвА¶to its homeвАЭ), referring to either the light or the darkness. Because either is referred to, the translation has employed plurals, since singulars would imply that only the second item, вАЬdarkness,вАЭ was the referent. Plurals are also employed by NAB and NIV.
n1803838: tn The imperfect verb after the adverb вАП„Р÷Є„ЦвАО ( Њaz, вАЬthenвАЭ) functions as a preterite (вАЬyou were bornвАЭ); вАП„Р÷Є„ЦвАО has been left untranslated because the past tense translation itself suffices to reflect the idiom. The pronoun вАЬthem,вАЭ referring to light and darkness mentioned in v. 19, has been added to clarify the sense of the statement. Clearly the line is sarcastic.
n1803839: sn Snow and ice are thought of as being in store, brought out by God for specific purposes, such as times of battle (see Josh 10:11; Exod 9:2ff.; Isa 28:17; Isa 30:30; Ps 18:12 [13]).
n1803840: tn The same Hebrew term (вАП„Р„Х÷є„¶÷Є„®вАО,  Њotsar), has been translated вАЬstorehouseвАЭ in the first line and вАЬarmoryвАЭ in the second. This has been done for stylistic variation, but also because вАЬhail,вАЭ as one of GodвАЩs вАЬweaponsвАЭ (cf. the following verse) suggests military imagery; in this context the word refers to GodвАЩs вАЬammunition dumpвАЭ where he stockpiles hail.
n1803841: sn The terms translated war and battle are different Hebrew words, but both may be translated вАЬwarвАЭ or вАЬbattleвАЭ depending on the context.
n1803842: tn Because the parallel with вАЬlightвАЭ and вАЬeast windвАЭ is not tight, Hoffmann proposed вАП„Ґ÷µ„УвАО ( њed) instead, вАЬmist.вАЭ This has been adopted by many. G. R. Driver suggests вАЬparching heatвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 91-92).
n1803844: tn Heb вАЬa desert, no man in it.вАЭ
n1803845: tn Heb вАЬto cause to sprout a source of vegetation.вАЭ The word вАП„Ю÷є„¶÷Є„РвАО (motsa Њ) is rendered вАЬmineвАЭ in Job 28:1. The suggestion with the least changes is WrightвАЩs: вАП„¶÷Є„Ю÷µ„РвАО (tsame Њ, вАЬthirstyвАЭ). But others choose вАП„Ю÷і„¶÷Љ÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (mitsiyyah, вАЬfrom the steppeвАЭ).
n1803846: tn Or вАЬheavens.вАЭ The Hebrew term вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (shamayim) may be translated вАЬheaven(s)вАЭ or вАЬskyвАЭ depending on the context.
n1803847: tn Several suggest that the verb is not from вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Є„РвАО (khava Њ, вАЬto hideвАЭ) but from a homonym, вАЬto congeal.вАЭ This may be too difficult to support, however.
n1803848: tn This word is found here and in 1 Sam 15:32. Dhorme suggests, with others, that there has been a metathesis (a reversal of consonants), and it is the same word found in Job 31:36 (вАЬbindвАЭ). G. R. Driver takes it as вАЬclusterвАЭ without changing the text (вАЬTwo astronomical passages in the Old Testament,вАЭ JTS 7 [1956] :3).
n1803849: tn The word вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ц÷Љ÷Є„®„Х÷є„™вАО (mazzarot) is taken by some to refer to the constellations (see 2 Kgs 23:5), and by others as connected to the word for вАЬcrown,вАЭ and so вАЬcorona.вАЭ
n1803851: tc The LXX has вАЬanswer you,вАЭ and some editors have adopted this. However, the reading of the MT makes better sense in the verse.
n1803852: tn This verse is difficult because of the two words, вАП„Ш÷ї„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (tukhot, rendered here вАЬheartвАЭ) and вАП„©„В÷ґ„Ы÷∞„Х÷і„ЩвАО (sekhvi, here вАЬmindвАЭ). They have been translated a number of ways: вАЬmeteorвАЭ and вАЬcelestial appearanceвАЭ; the stars вАЬProcyonвАЭ and вАЬSiriusвАЭ; вАЬinward partвАЭ and вАЬmindвАЭ; even as birds, вАЬibisвАЭ and вАЬcock.вАЭ One expects them to have something to do with natureвАФclouds and the like. The RSV accordingly took them to mean вАЬmeteorвАЭ (from a verb вАЬto wanderвАЭ) and вАЬa celestial appearance.вАЭ But these meanings are not well-attested.
n1803853: tn The word actually means вАЬto cause to lie down.вАЭ
n1803854: tn The word means вАЬto flowвАЭ or вАЬto castвАЭ (as in casting metals). So the noun developed the sense of вАЬhard,вАЭ as in cast metal.
n1803856: tn The verse is difficult, making some suspect that a line has dropped out. The little birds in the nest hardly go wandering about looking for food. Dhorme suggests вАЬand stagger for lack of food.вАЭ
n180391: tn The text uses the infinitive as the object: вАЬdo you know the giving birth of?вАЭ
n180393: tn Here the infinitive is again a substantive: вАЬthe time of their giving birth.вАЭ
n180394: tc The Hebrew verb used here means вАЬto cleave,вАЭ and this would not have the object вАЬtheir young.вАЭ Olshausen and others after him change the вАП„ЧвАО (khet) to вАП„ШвАО (tet) and get a verb вАЬto drop,вАЭ meaning вАЬdrop [= give birth to] youngвАЭ as used in Job 21:10. G. R. Driver holds out for the MT, arguing it is an idiom, вАЬto breach the wombвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew text of Job,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 92-93).
n180395: tn Heb вАЬthey cast forth their labor pains.вАЭ This word usually means вАЬbirth pangsвАЭ but here can mean what caused the pains (metonymy of effect). This fits better with the parallelism, and the verb (вАЬcast forthвАЭ). The words вАЬtheir offspringвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarity; direct objects were often omitted when clear from the context, although English expects them to be included.
n180396: tn The idea is that of the open countryside. The Aramaism is found only here.
n180398: sn The animal is happier in open countryside than in a busy town, and on its own rather than being driven by a herdsman.
n180399: tn Some commentators think that the addition of the вАЬwild oxвАЭ here is a copyistвАЩs error, making the stich too long. They therefore delete it. Also, binding an animal to the furrow with ropes is unusual. So with a slight emendation Kissane came up with вАЬWill you bind him with a halter of cord?вАЭ While the MT is unusual, the sense is understandable, and no changes, even slight ones, are absolutely necessary.
n1803911: tn The word is normally translated вАЬbelieveвАЭ in the Bible. The idea is that of considering something dependable and acting on it. The idea of reliability is found also in the Niphal stem usages.
n1803912: tc There is a textual problem here: вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (yashuv) is the Kethib, meaning вАЬ[that] he will returnвАЭ; вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (yashiv) is the Qere, meaning вАЬthat he will bring in.вАЭ This is the preferred reading, since the object follows it. For commentators who think the line too unbalanced for this, the object is moved to the second colon, and the reading вАЬreturnsвАЭ is taken for the first. But the MT is perfectly clear as it stands.
n1803913: tn Heb вАЬyour seedвАЭ; this must be interpreted figuratively for what the seed produces.
n1803914: tn Heb вАЬgather itвАЭ; the referent (the grain) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1803915: tn Simply, the MT has вАЬand your threshing floor gather.вАЭ The вАЬthreshing floorвАЭ has to be an adverbial accusative of place.
n1803916: tc This whole section on the ostrich is not included in the LXX. Many feel it is an interpolation and should therefore be deleted. The pattern of the chapter changes from the questions being asked to observations being made.
n1803917: tn The word occurs only here and means вАЬshrill cries.вАЭ If the MT is correct, this is a poetic name for the ostrich (see Lam 4:3).
n1803918: tn Many proposals have been made here. The MT has a verb, вАЬexult.вАЭ Strahan had вАЬflap joyously,вАЭ a rendering followed by the NIV. The RSV uses вАЬwave proudly.вАЭ
n1803919: tn The point of this statement would be that the ostrich cannot compare to the stork. But there are many other proposals for this lineвАФjust about every commentator has a different explanation for it. Of the three words here, the first means вАЬpinion,вАЭ the third вАЬplumage,вАЭ and the second probably вАЬstork,вАЭ although the LXX has вАЬheron.вАЭ The point of this whole section is that the ostrich is totally lacking in parental care, whereas the stork is characterized by it. The Hebrew word for вАЬstorkвАЭ is the same word for вАЬloveвАЭ: вАП„Ч÷≤„°÷і„Щ„У÷Є„ФвАО (khasidah), an interpretation followed by the NASB. The most likely reading is вАЬor are they the pinions and plumage of the stork?вАЭ The ostrich may flap about, but cannot fly and does not care for its young.
n1803920: tn The meaning may have the connotation of вАЬlays; places,вАЭ rather than simply abandoning (see M. Dahood, вАЬThe Root  њzb II in Job,вАЭ JBL 78 [1959]: 307f.).
n1803922: sn This verb, вАЬto deal harshly; to harden; to treat cruelly,вАЭ is used for hardening the heart elsewhere (see Isa 63:17).
n1803923: tn The colon poses a slight problem here. The literal meaning of the Hebrew verb translated вАЬsprings upвАЭ (i.e., вАЬlifts herself on highвАЭ) might suggest flight. But some of the proposals involve a reading about readying herself to run.
n1803924: tn The second half of the verse contains this hapax legomenon, which is usually connected with the word вАП„®÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (ra њmah, вАЬthunderвАЭ). A. B. Davidson thought it referred to the quivering of the neck rather than the mane. Gray thought the sound and not the movement was the point. But without better evidence, a reading that has вАЬquivering maneвАЭ may not be far off the mark. But it may be simplest to translate it вАЬmaneвАЭ and assume that the idea of вАЬquiveringвАЭ is part of the meaning.
n1803925: sn The same ideas are found in Joel 2:4. The leaping motion is compared to the galloping of the horse.
n1803926: tn The word could mean вАЬsnortingвАЭ as well (see Jer 8:16). It comes from the root вАЬto blow.вАЭ If the horse is running and breathing hard, this could be the sense here.
n1803927: tc The Hebrew text has a plural verb, вАЬthey paw.вАЭ For consistency and for stylistic reasons this is translated as a singular.
n1803928: tn The armies would prepare for battles that were usually fought in the valleys, and so the horse was ready to charge. But in Ugaritic the word  њmk means вАЬforceвАЭ as well as вАЬvalley.вАЭ The idea of вАЬforceвАЭ would fit the parallelism here well (see M. Dahood, вАЬValue of Ugaritic for textual criticism,вАЭ Bib 40 [1959]: 166).
n1803930: tn This may be the scimitar (see G. Molin, вАЬWhat is a kidon?вАЭ JSS 1 [1956]: 334-37).
n1803931: tn вАЬSwallow the groundвАЭ is a metaphor for the horseвАЩs running. Gray renders the line: вАЬquivering and excited he dashes into the fray.вАЭ
n1803932: tn The use of вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman) in the Hiphil in this place is unique. Such a form would normally mean вАЬto believe.вАЭ But its basic etymological meaning comes through here. The verb means вАЬto be firm; to be reliable; to be dependable.вАЭ The causative here would mean вАЬto make firmвАЭ or вАЬto stand firm.вАЭ
n1803933: tn This word occurs only here. It is connected to вАЬpinionsвАЭ in v. 13. Dhorme suggests вАЬclad with feathers,вАЭ but the line suggests more the use of the wings.
n1803935: tn Heb вАЬupon the tooth of a rock.вАЭ
n1803936: tn The word could be taken as the predicate, but because of the conjunction it seems to be adding another description of the place of its nest.
n1803937: tn The word means вАЬsearch,вАЭ but can be used for a wide range of matters, including spying.
n1803939: tn The word вАП„Ч÷≤„Ь÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khalalim) designates someone who is fatally wounded, literally the вАЬpierced one,вАЭ meaning anyone or thing that dies a violent death.
n180402: tn The form вАП„®÷є„СвАО (rov) is the infinitive absolute from the verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬcontendвАЭ). Dhorme wishes to repoint it to make it the active participle, the вАЬone who argues with the Almighty.вАЭ
n180403: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„°÷Љ„Х÷є„®вАО (yissor) is found only here, but comes from a common root meaning вАЬto correct; to reprove.вАЭ Several suggestions have been made to improve on the MT. Dhorme read it вАП„Щ÷Є„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО (yasur) in the sense of вАЬto turn aside; to yield.вАЭ Ehrlich read this emendation as вАЬto come to an end.вАЭ But the MT could be read as вАЬto correct; to instruct.вАЭ
n180404: tn The word вАП„І÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷є„™÷і„ЩвАО (qalloti) means вАЬto be light; to be of small account; to be unimportant.вАЭ From this comes the meaning вАЬcontemptible,вАЭ which in the causative stem would mean вАЬto treat with contempt; to curse.вАЭ Dhorme tries to make the sentence a conditional clause and suggests this meaning: вАЬIf I have been thoughtless.вАЭ There is really no вАЬifвАЭ in JobвАЩs mind.
n180405: tn The perfect verb here should be classified as an instantaneous perfect; the action is simultaneous with the words.
n180406: tn The words вАЬto silence myselfвАЭ are supplied in the translation for clarity.
n180408: sn The speech can be divided into three parts: the invitation to Job to assume the throne and rule the world (40:7-14), the description of Behemoth (40:15-24), and the description of Leviathan (41:1-34).
n1804011: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (parar) means вАЬto annul; to break; to frustrate.вАЭ It was one thing for Job to claim his own integrity, but it was another matter altogether to nullify GodвАЩs righteousness in the process.
n1804012: tn Heb вАЬdo you have an arm like God?вАЭ The words вАЬas powerful asвАЭ have been supplied in the translation to clarify the metaphor.
n1804013: tn The verb was used for scattering lightning (Job 37:11). God is challenging Job to unleash his power and judge wickedness in the world.
n1804015: tn The word was just used in the positive sense of excellence or majesty; now the exalted nature of the person refers to self-exaltation, or pride.
n1804016: tn The expression translated вАЬon the spotвАЭ is the prepositional phrase вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (takhtam, вАЬunder themвАЭ). вАЬUnder themвАЭ means in their place. But it can also mean вАЬwhere someone stands, on the spotвАЭ (see Exod 16:29; Jos 6:5; Judg 7:21, etc.).
n1804017: tn The word вАЬdustвАЭ can mean вАЬgroundвАЭ here, or more likely, вАЬgrave.вАЭ
n1804018: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„©„БвАО (khavash) means вАЬto bind.вАЭ In Arabic the word means вАЬto bindвАЭ in the sense of вАЬto imprison,вАЭ and that fits here.
n1804020: tn The word is вАЬsecret place,вАЭ the place where he is to hide them, i.e., the grave. The text uses the word вАЬsecret placeвАЭ as a metonymy for the grave.
n1804021: tn The verb is usually translated вАЬpraise,вАЭ but with the sense of a public declaration or acknowledgment. It is from вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (yadah, in the Hiphil, as here, вАЬgive thanks, laudвАЭ).
n1804022: tn The imperfect verb has the nuance of potential imperfect: вАЬcan save; is able to save.вАЭ
n1804023: sn The next ten verses are devoted to a portrayal of Behemoth (the name means вАЬbeastвАЭ in Hebrew). It does not fit any of the present material very well, and so many think the section is a later addition. Its style is more like that of a textbook. Moreover, if the animal is a real animal (the usual suggestion is the hippopotamus), then the location of such an animal is Egypt and not Palestine. Some have identified these creatures Behemoth and Leviathan as mythological creatures (Gunkel, Pope). Others point out that these creatures could have been dinosaurs (P. J. Maarten, NIDOTTE, 2:780; H. M. Morris, The Remarkable Record of Job, 115-22). Most would say they are real animals, but probably mythologized by the pagans. So the pagan reader would receive an additional impact from this point about GodвАЩs sovereignty over all nature.
n1804024: sn By form the word is the feminine plural of the Hebrew word for вАЬbeast.вАЭ Here it is an abstract wordвАФa title.
n1804025: tn Heb вАЬwith you.вАЭ The meaning could be temporal (вАЬwhen I made youвАЭ)вАФperhaps a reference to the sixth day of creation (Gen 1:24).
n1804026: tn In both of these verses вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (hinneh, вАЬbeholdвАЭ) has the deictic force (the word is from Greek ќіќµбљЈќЇќљѕЕќЉќє, deiknumi, вАЬto showвАЭ). It calls attention to something by pointing it out. The expression goes with the sudden look, the raised eye, the pointing handвАФвАЬO look!вАЭ
n1804027: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„•вАО (khafats) occurs only here. It may have the meaning вАЬto make stiff; to make tautвАЭ (Arabic). The LXX and the Syriac versions support this with вАЬerects.вАЭ But there is another Arabic word that could be cognate, meaning вАЬarch, bend.вАЭ This would give the idea of the tail swaying. The other reading seems to make better sense here. However, вАЬstiffвАЭ presents a serious problem with the view that the animal is the hippopotamus.
n1804028:
tn Heb вАЬthe ways of God.вАЭ
sn This may be a reference to Gen 1:24, where the first of the animal creation was the cattleвАФbehemah (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ф÷µ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО).
n1804029: tc The literal reading of the MT is вАЬlet the one who made him draw near [with] his sword.вАЭ The sword is apparently a reference to the teeth or tusks of the animal, which cut vegetation like a sword. But the idea of a weapon is easier to see, and so the people who favor the mythological background see here a reference to GodвАЩs slaying the Beast. There are again many suggestions on how to read the line. The RV probably has the safest: вАЬHe that made him has furnished him with his swordвАЭ (the sword being a reference to the sharp tusks with which he can attack).
n1804030: tn The word вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (bul) probably refers to food. Many take it as an abbreviated form of вАП„Щ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„ЬвАО (yevul, вАЬproduce of the fieldвАЭ). The vegetation that is produced on the low hills is what is meant.
n1804031: tn The suffix is singular, but must refer to the treesвАЩ shade.
n1804032: tn The word ordinarily means вАЬto oppress.вАЭ So many commentators have proposed suitable changes: вАЬoverflowsвАЭ (Beer), вАЬgushesвАЭ (Duhm), вАЬswells violentlyвАЭ (Dhorme, from a word that means вАЬbe strongвАЭ).
n1804034: tn The idea would be either (1) catch it while it is watching, or (2) in some way disabling its eyes before the attack. But others change the reading; Ball suggested вАЬwith hooksвАЭ and this has been adopted by some modern English versions (e.g., NRSV).
n1804035: tn Ehrlich altered the MT slightly to get вАЬwith thorns,вАЭ a view accepted by Driver, Dhorme and Pope.
n180411: sn Beginning with 41:1, the verse numbers through 41:9 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 41:1 ET = 40:25 HT, 41:2 ET = 40:26 HT, etc., through 41:34 ET = 41:26 HT. The Hebrew verse numbers in the remainder of the chapter differ from the verse numbers in the English Bible. Beginning with 42:1 the verse numbers in the ET and HT are again the same.
n180412: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (mashakh) means вАЬto extract from the water; to fish.вАЭ The question here includes the use of a hook to fish the creature out of the water so that its jaws can be tied safely.
n180413: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷Ј„ҐвАО (shaqa њ) means вАЬto cause to sink,вАЭ if it is connected with the word in Amos 8:8 and 9:5. But it may have the sense of вАЬto tie; to bind.вАЭ If the rope were put around the tongue and jaw, binding tightly would be the sense.
n180414: tn The line asks if the animal, when caught and tied and under control, would keep on begging for mercy. Absolutely not. It is not in the nature of the beast. The construction uses вАП„Щ÷Ј„®÷∞„С÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (yarbeh, вАЬ[will] he multiplyвАЭ [= вАЬmake numerousвАЭ]), with the object, вАЬsupplicationsвАЭ i.e., prayers for mercy.
n180415: tn The rhetorical question again affirms the opposite. The poem is portraying the creature as powerful and insensitive.
n180417: tn The imperfect verb serves to express what the covenant pact would cover, namely, вАЬthat you take.вАЭ
n180418: tn The Hebrew verb is вАП„©„В÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (sakhaq, вАЬto sport; to trifle; to play,вАЭ Ps 104:26).
n180419: tn The idea may include putting Leviathan on a leash. D. W. Thomas suggested on the basis of an Arabic cognate that it could be rendered вАЬtie him with a string like a young sparrowвАЭ (вАЬJob XL 29b: Text and Translation,вАЭ VT 14 [1964]: 114-16).
n1804110: tn The word вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Љ÷Ј„®вАО (khabbar) is a hapax legomenon, but the meaning is вАЬto associateвАЭ since it is etymologically related to the verb вАЬto join together.вАЭ The idea is that fishermen usually work in companies or groups, and then divide up the catch when they come ashoreвАФwhich involves bargaining.
n1804111: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ФвАО (karah) means вАЬto sell.вАЭ With the preposition вАП„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њal, вАЬuponвАЭ) it has the sense вАЬto bargain over something.вАЭ
n1804112: tn The verb means вАЬto cut up; to divide upвАЭ in the sense of selling the dead body (see Exod 21:35). This will be between them and the merchants (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„†÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, kena њanim).
n1804113: tn The verse uses two imperatives which can be interpreted in sequence: do this, and then this will happen.
n1804114: tc The LXX reads вАЬYou will lay a hand on it, [though] remembering the battle that [be]comes in its body, donвАЩt let it happen again.вАЭ The LXX appear to have read the first verb as an imperfect, implying the addition of a yod, rather than an imperative. If that is correct, it could be read as another question in the series. Also the LXX reading вАЬa handвАЭ rather than вАЬyour handвАЭ The LXX could imply a different verb division with the вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) of the pronominal suffix going instead with the following word. This doesnвАЩt work in the MT, which reads an imperative, but the LXX assumes different vowels for the second verb, treating it as a participle. If these cues are correct, the verse may have originally read, вАЬWill you lay a hand on it? Like one remembering the fight, do not do it again.вАЭ
n1804115: sn Job 41:9 in the English Bible is 41:1 in the Hebrew text (BHS). From here to the end of the chapter the Hebrew verse numbers differ from those in the English Bible, with 41:10 ET = 41:2 HT, 41:11 ET = 41:3 HT, etc. See also the note on 41:1.
n1804116: tn The line is difficult. вАЬHis hope [= expectation]вАЭ must refer to any assailant who hopes or expects to capture the creature. Because there is no antecedent, Dhorme and others transpose it with the next verse. The point is that the man who thought he was sufficient to confront Leviathan soon finds his hopeвАФhis expectationвАФfalse (a derivative from the verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО [kazav, вАЬlieвАЭ] is used for a mirage).
n1804117: tn There is an interrogative particle in this line, which most commentators ignore. But others freely emend the MT. Gunkel, following the mythological approach, has вАЬhis appearance casts down even a god.вАЭ Cheyne likewise has: вАЬeven divine beings the fear of him brings lowвАЭ (JQR 9 [1896/97]: 579). Pope has, вАЬWere not the gods cast down at the sight of him?вАЭ There is no need to bring in this mythological element.
n1804118: sn The description is of the animal, not the hunter (or fisherman). Leviathan is so fierce that no one can take him on alone.
n1804119: tc MT has вАЬbefore meвАЭ and can best be rendered as вАЬWho then is he that can stand before me?вАЭ (ESV, NASB, NIV, NLT, NJPS). The following verse (11) favors the MT since both express the lesson to be learned from Leviathan: If a man cannot stand up to Leviathan, how can he stand up to its creator? The translation above has chosen to read the text as вАЬbefore himвАЭ (cf. NRSV, NJB).
n1804120: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„У÷Ј„ЭвАО (qadam) means вАЬto come to meet; to come before; to confrontвАЭ to the face.
n1804121: sn The verse seems an intrusion (and so E. Dhorme, H. H. Rowley, and many others change the pronouns to make it refer to the animal). But what the text is saying is that it is more dangerous to confront God than to confront this animal.
n1804122: tn This line also focuses on the sovereign God rather than Leviathan. H. H. Rowley, however, wants to change вАП„Ь÷і„Щ÷Њ„Ч„Х÷Љ„РвАО (li hu Њ, вАЬit [belongs] to meвАЭ) into вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ф„Х÷Љ„РвАО (lo Њ hu Њ, вАЬthere is no oneвАЭ). So it would say that there is no one under the whole heaven who could challenge Leviathan and live, rather than saying it is more dangerous to challenge God to make him repay.
n1804123: tn Dhorme changes the noun into a verb, вАЬI will tell,вАЭ and the last two words into вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„Я „Ґ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њen  њerekh, вАЬthere is no comparisonвАЭ). The result is вАЬI will tell of his incomparable might.вАЭ
n1804124: tn Heb вАЬthe face of his garment,вАЭ referring to the outer garment or covering. Some take it to be the front as opposed to the back.
n1804125: tc The word вАП„®÷ґ„°÷ґ„ЯвАО (resen) has often been rendered вАЬbridleвАЭ (cf. ESV), but that leaves a number of unanswered questions. The LXX reads вАП„°÷і„®÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„ЯвАО (siryon), with the transposition of letters, but that means вАЬcoat of armor.вАЭ If the metathesis stands, there is also support from the cognate Akkadian.
n1804127: tc The MT has вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„Р÷≤„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ga Њavah, вАЬhis prideвАЭ), but the LXX, Aquila, and the Vulgate all read вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„Х÷Љ„Х÷євАО (gavvo, вАЬhis backвАЭ). Almost all the modern English versions follow the variant reading, speaking about вАЬhis [or its] back.вАЭ
n1804128: tn Instead of вАП„¶÷Є„®вАО (tsar, вАЬcloselyвАЭ) the LXX has вАП„¶÷є„®вАО (tsor, вАЬstoneвАЭ) to say that the seal was rock hard.
n1804129: tn The expression вАЬeach oneвА¶to the nextвАЭ is literally вАЬone with one.вАЭ
n1804131: tn Heb вАЬthe eyelids,вАЭ but it represents the early beams of the dawn as the cover of night lifts.
n1804132: sn For the animal, the image is that of pent-up breath with water in a hot steam jet coming from its mouth, like a stream of fire in the rays of the sun. The language is hyperbolic, probably to reflect the pagan ideas of the dragon of the deep in a polemical wayвАФthey feared it as a fire breathing monster, but in reality it might have been a steamy crocodile.
n1804133: tn The word вАЬburningвАЭ is supplied. The Syriac and Vulgate have вАЬa seething and boiling potвАЭ (reading вАП„Р÷є„Т÷µ„ЭвАО [ Њogem] for вАП„Р÷Ј„Т÷∞„Ю÷є„ЯвАО [ Њagmon]). This view is widely accepted.
n1804134: tn This word, вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (de Њavah) is a hapax legomenon. But the verbal root means вАЬto languish; to pine.вАЭ A related noun talks of dejection and despair in Deut 28:65. So here вАЬdespairвАЭ as a translation is preferable to вАЬterror.вАЭ
n1804136: tn The last clause says вАЬit cannot be moved.вАЭ But this part will function adverbially in the sentence.
n1804137: tn The description of his heart being вАЬhardвАЭ means that he is cruel and fearless. The word for вАЬhardвАЭ is the word encountered before for molten or cast metal.
n1804138: tc This verse has created all kinds of problems for the commentators. The first part is workable: вАЬwhen he raises himself up, the mighty [the gods] are terrified.вАЭ The mythological approach would render вАП„Р÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њelim) as вАЬgods.вАЭ But the last two words, which could be rendered вАЬat the breaking [crashing, or breakers] they fail,вАЭ receive much attention. E. Dhorme (Job, 639) suggests вАЬmajestyвАЭ for вАЬraising upвАЭ and вАЬbillowsвАЭ (вАП„Т÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, gallim) for вАП„Р÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њelim), and gets a better parallelism: вАЬthe billows are afraid of his majesty, and the waves draw back.вАЭ But H. H. Rowley (Job [NCBC], 263) does not think this is relevant to the context, which is talking about the creatureвАЩs defense against attack. The RSV works well for the first part, but the second part need some change; so Rowley adopts вАЬin their dire consternation they are beside themselves.вАЭ
n1804139: tn This is the clearest reading, following A. B. Davidson, Job, 285. The versions took different readings of the construction.
n1804140: tn The verb вАП„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (qum, вАЬstandвАЭ) with вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (beli, вАЬnotвАЭ) has the sense of вАЬdoes not hold firm,вАЭ or вАЬgives way.вАЭ
n1804142: tn The verb is plural, but since there is no expressed subject it is translated as a passive here.
n1804144: tn Here only the word вАЬsharpвАЭ is present, but in passages like Isa 41:15 it is joined with вАЬthreshing sledge.вАЭ Here and in Amos 1:3 and Isa 28:27 the word stands alone, but represents the вАЬsledge.вАЭ
n1804145: sn The idea is either that the sea is stirred up like the foam from beating the ingredients together, or it is the musk-smell that is the point of comparison.
n1804147: tn Heb вАЬthe sons of pride.вАЭ Dhorme repoints the last word to get вАЬall the wild beasts,вАЭ but this misses the point of the verse. This animal looks over every proud creatureвАФbut he is king of them all in that department.
n180421: tn The expression вАЬyou askedвАЭ is added here to clarify the presence of the line to follow. Many commentators delete it as a gloss from Job 38:2. If it is retained, then Job has to be recalling GodвАЩs question before he answers it.
n180422: tn The word вАП„Ь÷Є„Ы÷µ„ЯвАО (lakhen) is simply вАЬbut,вАЭ as in Job 31:37.
n180423: tn Heb вАЬand I do not understand.вАЭ The expression serves here in an adverbial capacity. It also could be subordinated as a complement: вАЬI have declared [things that] I do not understand.вАЭ
n180424: tn The last clause is вАЬand I do not know.вАЭ This is also subordinated to become a dependent clause.
n180425: tn This phrase, вАЬyou said,вАЭ is supplied in the translation to introduce the recollection of GodвАЩs words.
n180426: sn This statement does not imply there was a vision. He is simply saying that this experience of God was real and personal. In the past his knowledge of God was what he had heardвАФhearsay. This was real.
n180427: tn Or вАЬdespise what I said.вАЭ There is no object on the verb; Job could be despising himself or the things he said (see L. J. Kuyper, вАЬRepentance of Job,вАЭ VT 9 [1959]: 91-94).
n180428: tn Heb вАЬthe Lord.вАЭ The title has been replaced by the pronoun (вАЬheвАЭ) in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1804210: tn The form вАП„†÷∞„Ы„Х÷є„†÷Є„ФвАО (nekhonah) is from вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬto be firm; to be fixed; to be establishedвАЭ). Here it means вАЬthe right thingвАЭ or вАЬtruth.вАЭ The Akkadian cognate kƒЂnu means вАЬtrue, just, honest, firmвАЭ (CAD K: 389).
n1804211: tn The imperatives in this verse are plural, so all three had to do this together.
n1804212: tn The verb вАЬprayвАЭ is the Hitpael from the root вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (palal). That root has the main idea of arbitration; so in this stem it means вАЬto seek arbitration [for oneself],вАЭ or вАЬto pray,вАЭ or вАЬto intercede.вАЭ
n1804213: tn Heb вАЬI will lift up his face,вАЭ meaning, вАЬI will regard him.вАЭ
n1804214: tn This clause is a result clause, using the negated infinitive construct.
n1804215: tn The word вАЬfollyвАЭ can also be taken in the sense of вАЬdisgrace.вАЭ If the latter is chosen, the word serves as the direct object. If the former, then it is an adverbial accusative.
n1804216: sn The difference between what they said and what Job said, therefore, has to do with truth. Job was honest, spoke the truth, poured out his complaints, but never blasphemed God. For his words God said he told the truth. He did so with incomplete understanding, and with all the impatience and frustration one might expect. Now the friends, however, did not tell what was right about God. They were not honest; rather, they were self-righteous and condescending. They were saying what they thought should be said, but it was wrong.
n1804217: tn The expression вАЬhad respect for JobвАЭ means God answered his prayer.
n1804218: tn The paragraph begins with the disjunctive vav, вАЬNow as for the Lord, heвА¶.вАЭ
n1804219: sn The expression here is interesting: вАЬhe returned the captivity of Job,вАЭ a clause used elsewhere in the Bible of Israel (see e.g., Ps 126). Here it must mean вАЬthe fortunes of Job,вАЭ i.e., what he had lost. There is a good deal of literature on this; for example, see R. Borger, вАЬZu ≈°ub ≈°b(ƒЂ)t,вАЭ ZAW 25 (1954): 315-16; and E. Baumann, ZAW 6 (1929): 17ff.
n1804220: tn This is a temporal clause, using the infinitive construct with the subject genitive suffix. By this it seems that this act of Job was also something of a prerequisite for restorationвАФto pray for them.
n1804221: tn The construction uses the verb вАЬand he addedвАЭ with the word вАЬrepeatвАЭ (or вАЬtwiceвАЭ).
n1804223:
tn The Hebrew word вАП„І÷∞„©„В÷і„Щ„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (qesitah) is generally understood to refer to a unit of money, but the value is unknown.
sn The Hebrew word refers to a piece of silver, yet uncoined. It is the kind used in Gen 33:19 and Josh 24:32. It is what would be expected of a story set in the patriarchal age.
n1804224: sn This gold ring was worn by women in the nose, or men and women in the ear.
n1804225: tn The word for вАЬsevenвАЭ is spelled in an unusual way. From this some have thought it means вАЬtwice seven,вАЭ or fourteen sons. Several commentators take this view, but it is probably not warranted.
n1804228: sn The Hebrew name Keren-Happuch means вАЬhorn of eye-paint.вАЭ
